title: Special Weather Statement until WED 7:00 AM MDT
description: A mixture of snow and rain has developed into patchy freezing rain early this morning. While significant accumulation is not expected, icy conditions are possible.
_full_text:
Patchy Freezing Rain Creating Hazardous Travel Conditions This Morning

What’s Happening:

A mixture of snow and rain has developed into patchy freezing rain early this morning. While significant accumulation is not expected, icy conditions are possible.

What to Expect:

Patchy freezing rain mixed with snow and rain.
Bridges and overpasses are the first to become icy.
Sudden changes in weather conditions may occur.

Impacts:

Hazardous travel conditions, especially on bridges and overpasses.
Potential for minor traffic disruptions due to icy surfaces.

Safety Tips:

Drive with caution and reduce speed on icy roads.
Avoid sudden braking or sharp turns to prevent skidding.
Check local traffic reports for road conditions before traveling.

When to Check Back:

Monitor weather.gov or rapidcityjournal.com/weather for updates.

This weather alert was produced with AI assistance using National Weather Service data.

title: Youth can learn gardening basics at Rapid City Parks & Rec class
description: The Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department is hosting an upcoming "Bloom With Us" event, providing local youth with an opportunity to learn the basics of gardening.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department is hosting an upcoming "Bloom With Us" event, providing local youth with an opportunity to learn the basics of gardening.

The event will be held Saturday, April 11 from 8-10 a.m. at the Parks Greenhouse, 2915 Canyon Lake Drive. The event is open to youth 5-13 years old. Event fee is $5.

Each participating child will receive a terracotta pot and choose two flowers to pot and take home. Youth will learn about Celosia, Marigold and Snapdragon flowers as part of the event.

“This hands-on class is perfect for young gardeners who love to learn, explore and get a little messy,” said City Recreation Specialist Lindsey Myers. “It’s a bright and cheerful way to grow curiosity one flower at a time!”

For more information, contact Myers at 605-394-4175.

title: Rapid City and Optimist Club team up on Saturday's Easter egg hunt
description: In just a few days, the Easter Bunny – with assistance from helpers from the Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department and the Rapid City Optimist Club – will be dropping hundreds of plastic eggs on the Sioux Park Soccer Fields.
_full_text:
In just a few days, the Easter Bunny – with assistance from helpers from the Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department and the Rapid City Optimist Club – will be dropping hundreds of plastic eggs on the Sioux Park Soccer Fields.

The 62nd annual City/Optimists Easter Egg Hunt is scheduled for 9 a.m. this Saturday, April 4 at Sioux Park. The event is free and open to youth 13 years of age and under. Participants are advised to bring their own basket.

This year’s event is the first time in which the two hosting groups are combining efforts.

Thousands of eggs will be laid out on the Sioux Park grass for the event. The area will be divided into various age groups and it’s expected the candy- and surprise-filled eggs will be collected in a manner of minutes.

For more information about this year’s event, contact Myers at 605-394-4175 or lindsey.myers@rcgov.org.

 PHOTOS: Rapid City kids hunt down 4,000 eggs
Dressed in coats and hats to combat the brisk Saturday morning air and armed with buckets and bags for their loot, a throng of children gathered at the Omaha Street Soccer Fields in Rapid City and plucked up 4,000 Easter eggs in a matter of minutes. Lindsey Meyers with the Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department said families showed up more than an hour before the 9 a.m. kickoff. The parks department hosted the egg hunt, which catered to children 13 and under.

title: Piedmont man dies in crash near Deadwood, 2 others severely injured
description: Authorities have released the name of a Piedmont man killed in a four-vehicle crash near Deadwood.
_full_text:
Authorities have released the name of a Piedmont man killed in a four-vehicle crash near Deadwood.

The South Dakota Highway Patrol said Robert Allen Erickson, 92, died from injuries suffered in a crash that occurred just after 8 p.m. on Friday, March 27, one mile east of Deadwood.

The patrol said a preliminary investigation determined Erickson, driving a 2019 Chevrolet Suburban, was traveling east on US Highway 14A and crossed the centerline, sideswiping an oncoming 2010 Ford Flex driven by Joanna Silvia Barlow, 41, of Lead, causing the Ford to spin and come to rest on the centerline.

The Suburban then struck an oncoming Ford F-150 head-on, driven by Justin Weaver, 44, which went off the road and into the ditch. The Suburban then struck a GMC Sierra, driven by Cody Oliver, 42, of Deadwood. The Suburban came to rest in the westbound passing lane.

Erickson died at the scene, the patrol said. Justin Weaver and passenger Lindsey Weaver, 41, both of Rapid City, in the F-150, were transported to Monument Health Rapid City Hospital with life-threatening injuries. Joanna Barlow and a 2-year-old passenger in the Ford Flex, and Cody Oliver in the GMC Sierra, were not injured, the patrol said.

Erickson was not wearing a seat belt, while Barlow and Oliver were wearing seat belts, the patrol said. The Weavers' use of seat belts has not been determined.

An investigation into the crash continues.

title: Oglala, South Dakota's Cody Two Bulls honored by Congressional Medal of Honor Society
description: Cody Two Bulls, Oglala Lakota, has been recognized nationally for the support he's provided to his community.
_full_text:
Cody Two Bulls, Oglala Lakota, has been recognized nationally for the support he's provided to his community. 

The 18-year-old Mahpíya Lúta High School senior founded Backpacks & Baseball four years ago and hosts baseball camps on the Pine Ridge Reservation. He is on the school's basketball team, which took the 2025 Lakota Nation Invitational boys championship. He also competed at state in cross country. In his spare time, he invests in his community. 

 Two Bulls and his parents
Cody Two Bulls, left, with his parents Robbie Two Bulls and Lloyd Two Bulls in Washington, D.C. 

"To be honored by the Medal of Honor Society has been an overwhelming and humbling experience," Two Bulls said in a statement to the Journal provided by his mother. "I never expected wanting to help a few kids find a backpack or teaching a kid to play baseball would lead to all of this."

On March 25, National Medal of Honor Day, the Society presented its annual Citizen Honors Awards at the Andrew W. Mellon Auditorium in Washington, D.C. Two Bulls is the 2026 Youth Service Honoree. He was among just half a dozen "extraordinary Americans whose actions embody the values of courage, sacrifice, and service that define the nation’s highest military award for valor in combat."

"Through tireless personal effort, Cody has distributed thousands of backpacks, meals, holiday gifts, and essential supplies while creating safe, joyful community events for children and families. His self-initiated, year-round service exemplifies leadership, generosity, and a deep commitment to uplifting his community," the Congressional Medal of Honor Society said about Two Bulls in a March 24 press release.

 Cody Two Bulls (copy)
Cody Two Bulls, 17, of Rapid City stands outside the northside Walmart on Aug. 9, 2024, during a school supply drive hosted by the Rapid City Police Department. The drive supported his project to fill backpacks and provide them to students on the Pine Ridge Indian Reservation. A similar drive was held in 2025. 

Just last year, a Backpacks & Baseball drive held at both Rapid City Walmarts, with the help of the Rapid City Police Department, netted about 400 backpacks filled with supplies. Some were distributed at a baseball clinic Two Bulls hosted in Oglala. Others went to the Rapid City Police Department Community Outreach Team, which distributed them to Rapid City youth.

Two Bulls flew to Washington with his mother, Robbie Two Bulls, and his father, Lloyd Two Bulls.

"He was very, very humble about the award. I don't think he realized how big it was until we go to D.C.," Robbie Two Bulls told the Journal. "...And I was like, 'Yeah, because of your service work, you did a great thing.'"

Robbie Two Bulls said the trip was a "great experience for all of us." It included a trip to Arlington Cemetery and a private tour of the Capitol building courtesy of Sen. John Thune's office. She said their flights, hotel and food were all covered. 

 Thune
Cody Two Bulls, right, shakes hands with Sen. John Thune, R-South Dakota, at his office in D.C.  

"My trip to Washington, D.C., was crazy. I think the one thing that will actually stick out in my mind was being able to be a part of the wreath laying ceremony," Cody Two Bulls said. "Precision and respect is the only way to explain it. Coming from a family of veterans, I had some knowledge of the respect required by I really had no idea the precision involved."

Robbie Two Bulls said both of her brothers and her dad — Cody Two Bull's uncle and grandfather — served in the military. They prepared him for his trip, telling him to show respect and shake everyone's hand, "holding true to that respect that his uncles and grandfathers had prepared him for, but also keeping true to himself as a Lakota."

 Cody Two Bulls
Cody Two Bulls' mother said he wanted to wear his feathers to D.C. and his beaded ties. "He wanted to honor his people back home by doing those little details," she said.   

"He wanted to wear his feathers. The ties he wore were beaded. He wanted to honor his people back home by doing those little details," she said. "He wanted to represent his people in a good way, and I think he did that by the way he presented himself to everybody, how he wore his feathers proudly. We gave him two feathers, one for this service work and one for this award."

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

Robbie Two Bulls explained parents give their children feathers for great deeds. 

National Medal of Honor Day was established by Congress in 1991 to foster public appreciation and recognition of Medal of Honor Recipients. March 25 was selected to commemorate the date in 1863 when the first Medals of Honor were presented to six members of Andrews’ Raiders during the Civil War.

title: Kori Dianne Wallace
description: September 8, 1961 - March 24, 2026
_full_text:
September 8, 1961 - March 24, 2026

Kori D. (Determan) Wallace, 64, passed away March 24, 2026, from lung cancer at Brigham and Women's Hospital in Boston, Mass. A celebration of life and burial will be held at a later date in Emery, S.D.

Kori Diane Determan was born September 8, 1961, to Robert and Cecilia (Edzards) Determan in Mitchell, S.D. She grew up in Emery and attended a one-room country schoolhouse for two years, before starting in the Emery school system. She loved swimming and spent her summers riding the five miles into town to swim every day at the Emery pool. She was a 1979 graduate of Emery High School, where she played basketball and ran track. She also was a spirited softball player.

Wanting to see the world, she enlisted in the Air Force in August 1979. She and her former spouse, Keith Wallace, had two children, Scott in 1981 and Lyndsey in 1983. She served for 20 years in the Air Force, with assignments to North Dakota, England, Utah, Texas, and Germany. She and her family were visiting Berlin when the Wall fell, and she helped hammer chunks out of it with the celebrating Germans. After Germany, she returned home to South Dakota, spending the last eight years of her Air Force career at Ellsworth AFB, ensuring top notch training for a whole generation of Air Force leaders.

Every major holiday and long weekend (and sometimes short weekends) you could find Kori and her kids (and sometimes some extra kids) at her parents' farm in Emery or at Werner's Pasture in Chamberlain on a fishing trip.

Following her retirement from the Air Force, she took up Property Management in Rapid City, S.D. After a few years, she followed her kids out to the East Coast and managed properties in Salem, Mass. She bought a condo that had previously been a church and painted the cathedral ceilings and sky-high walls every shade you could think of from the Crayola box.

Kori loved games and puzzles, and was an avid card player with a strong competitive streak. She spent hours with her family around the farm kitchen table every holiday, playing rounds upon rounds of Pinochle and loudly shaking the Boggle cube first thing in the mornings with her Mother during Christmas vacations.

She loved creamer in her coffee and a little Baileys with milk during the holidays. Kori never met a cat she didn't want to pet (or tame) and she spent many a weekend with her kids or her Mother at auctions collecting goods from decades past, be it salt and pepper shakers or historic farm equipment.

In her later years, she spent her time between South Dakota and the East Coast before moving out to the Boston area to live with her daughter and daughter's family in 2021. She adored her new role as Grandma, and relished spending time with all four of her grandchildren. She loved being on the East Coast for the contact naps with Gia and Anna and watching their first steps, and she delighted in her active granddaughters Maisie and Annie, watching them as they ice skated, performed gymnastics, rode horses, and learned to love games, too. She had an enthusiastic cackle, devilish sense of humor, and heart filled with love. While she might say her children were her greatest accomplishment, her grandchildren were her greatest delight.

She is survived by her mother, Cecilia Determan of Emery; her favorite son, Scott and wife, Lisa of Silver Spring, Md. and their children, Margaret "Maisie" and Marian "Annie"; and her favorite daughter, Lyndsey Liberti, and husband, Michael of Wilmington, Mass. and their children, Giavanna "Gia" and Adrianna "Anna". Also surviving her are her siblings, Gordon (Kay) Determan of Alexandria, Steven Determan of Emery and Kevin Determan of Emery, and multiple nephews, nieces and cousins. Preceding her in death was her father, Robert Determan, in 2014.

The family would like to give a heartfelt thank you to the Dana Farber Cancer Institute of Boston for the world class care over the past four and a half years – allowing her last years to be defined by love and not cancer.

title: Rapid City man sentenced for 2024 sawed-off shotgun shooting incident
description: A Rapid City man was sentenced to prison for a September 2024 shooting incident that left one man injured after getting shot in the head with a sawed off shot gun.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was sentenced to prison for a September 2024 shooting incident that left one man injured after getting shot in the head with a sawed off shot gun.

Trevon Spotted Eagle appeared for his sentencing before Circuit Court Judge Joshua Hendrickson on Monday, March 30, at the Pennington County Courthouse.

Spotted Eagle pleaded guilty to two counts of aggravated assault on February 23. He was sentenced to 12 and a half years in prison for each count, 25 total, with the sentences to run consecutively.

Spotted Eagle was initially indicted for attempted first-degree murder, one count of domestic aggravated assault, possession of a firearm by a felon, possession of a controlled weapon and possession of a loaded firearm while intoxicated. 

However, through an agreement with the state, Spotted Eagle pleaded guilty to two aggravated assault counts, dismissing all other charges. 

Senior Deputy Pennington County State's Attorney Jason Thomas said at sentencing the case was all about "control and fear" as Spotted Eagle held three people, one a pregnant woman, at gunpoint. 

Police responded to the 700 block of East North Street for a report of a gunshot on Sept. 6, 2024. Once they arrived, they found a man with an apparent gunshot wound to the head. The man was still conscious and able to speak to police about the shooting. 

Thomas criticized how Spotted Eagle claimed self-defense initially, stating one of the three victims had a machete. However, there was no evidence indicating that there was a machete. 

Thomas asked the court consider a 12 and half year sentence for each count and to run consecutively, "to ensure the community's safety."

Spotted Eagle's attorney, Gregory Sperlich, highlighted that Spotted Eagle had a long history of drug and alcohol abuse. Sperlich noted the gun used during the incident wasn't a traditional handgun and was "a difficult, unreliable weapon to operate."

Sperlich asked the court to suspend some of the sentence because of Spotted Eagle's close family ties that can help him get back on track. 

Before sentencing, Hedrickson described Spotted Eagle's actions that night as "extremely violent, unnecessary and disturbing." 

Hedrickson said he found the state's request fair, especially considering Spotted Eagle's "violent history." According to federal court documents, Spotted Eagle was previously convicted in March 2014 for an assault.

title: School of Mines 64th annual Cultural Expo celebrates food, entertainment and language
description: The aroma of foods spanning cultures and continents filled the Surbeck Center's Beck Ballroom at the South Dakota School of Mines on Saturday, March 28.
_full_text:
The aroma of foods spanning cultures and continents filled the Surbeck Center's Beck Ballroom at the South Dakota School of Mines on Saturday, March 28.

The 64th annual Cultural Expo brought together international students sharing elements of their home countries with campus and community members. 

Ticket booklets for food tasting were available for purchase at the door. Attendees meandered to try flavors not frequently found on a Midwestern plate.

 033126-nws-expo-p3.JPG
A young boys enjoy a hibiscus drink at the African Countries table.  

Kollette Medicine, a Lakota language teacher in Rapid City, attended the event with her two children.

"I want to expose my kids to different cultures in the Black Hills," Medicine said. "...It's just nice to see, nice to try, nice to taste."

The African Countries booth offered chicken and plantains, rice and peanut sauce, puff-puff pastries and a hibiscus drink. A&D Jamaican Restaurant brought jerk chicken. The Bangladesh table offered polao rice, chicken curry, payesh and a sweet rice pudding. 

The Society of Asian Scientists and Engineers served pancit bihon and ube brownie. Advancing Indigenous Science and Engineering Society offered fry bread and wojapi — mixed berries with corn starch and water. 

The Nepal table offered palau rice, chicken curry and achar (pickle). The Japan table served miso soup. The China table offered egg rolls, pearl meatballs and dumplings. 

 033126-nws-expo-p10.JPG
A woman performs a Chinese opera song.  

But food wasn't the only thing. Entertainment included Chinese opera, Indian and multicultural dancing. Group games included men trying their best to tie a sari, which is a traditional Indian garment, and songs in various languages played over the speakers, with audience members trying their best to guess the language. 

Mehzabeen Mannan, who is from Bangladesh and works as a research scientist and lecturer at Mines, said if there's anything she would want someone to know about her home country, it's the hospitality, the food and the people. 

"You are welcome to visit Bangladesh, and you will find so many friendly people there," Mannan said. "...People enjoy Bangladeshi food, and we love to prepare food for them, show the culture here."

"We want people to know about our culture and how we are," Mannan said. 

 PHOTOS: Food, dancing and fun at the School of Mines Cultural Expo
The South Dakota School of Mines hosted the 64th Annual Cultural Expo on Saturday, March 28, in the Surbeck Center's Beck Ballroom. International students shared elements of their home countries with campus and community members in the form of food, entertainment and games.

title: Pennington County Department of Equalization to present on agricultural classified properties
description: The Pennington County Department of Equalization will provide a public presentation on agricultural classified properties during the regularly scheduled County Commission meeting on April 7 at 9 a.m. The meeting will be held in the Commission Chambers at 900 Concourse Drive.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Department of Equalization will provide a public presentation on agricultural classified properties during the regularly scheduled County Commission meeting on April 7 at 9 a.m. The meeting will be held in the Commission Chambers at 900 Concourse Drive.

The presentation will offer an overview of how agricultural (Ag) status is determined for properties within Pennington County. Department staff will explain the state laws governing Ag classification, as well as the formulas used to assess qualifying properties, including soil and productivity factors.

In addition, the presentation will address how these laws and formulas are applied in practice and outline the process property owners must follow to qualify for agricultural status. Staff will also discuss the broader impact on the county if classification standards are not applied consistently across properties, emphasizing the importance of uniform application.

This informational session is intended to help residents better understand agricultural property classification and its implications for both property owners and the county as a whole.

Members of the public are encouraged to attend the April 7 meeting to learn more about the topic and hear directly from Department of Equalization staff.

For more information, please contact the Pennington County Department of Equalization at 605-394-2175.

title: Martin man sentenced for head-on collision with elderly couple on Pine Ridge
description: A Martin man was sentenced to five years and three months in federal prison, followed by three years of supervised release for vehicular assault of an elderly man on Pine Ridge Reservation.
_full_text:
A Martin man was sentenced to five years and three months in federal prison, followed by three years of supervised release for vehicular assault of an elderly man on Pine Ridge Reservation. 

Jose Jimenez, 40, appeared for his sentencing before U.S. District Judge Camela Theeler on Monday, March 23. 

According to the press release from the South Dakota's U.S. Attorney's Office, Jimenez was indicted for two counts of assault resulting in serious bodily injury and two counts of assault with a dangerous weapon by a federal grand jury in September 2024.

Through a plea agreement with the government, Jimenez pleaded guilty to one count of assault resulting in serious bodily injury. In exchange, all other charges would be dismissed.

According to the factual basis, on July 24, 2024, Jimenez was traveling westbound into the city of Pine Ridge, going 77 mph in a 65 mph zone. In a no passing zone near a hill, Jimenez attempted to pass another car and ended up striking a maroon Ford F-150 head-on.

After crashing into the 78-year-old man's car, the government alleged Jimenez discarded a bottle of alcohol from his car. In the truck, police found one empty bottle and another unopened bottle of alcohol.

The 78-year-old man and a 66-year-old woman were transported to Monument Health Rapid City Hospital for their injuries. The man suffered from several fractures and skin abrasions. The woman suffered from several lacerations and sustained a partial amputation of her left thumb. 

Though Jimenez tested positive for alcohol at the scene, it was determined his BAC was under the legal limit. At sentencing, Jimenez asked for leniency due to his substance abuse history and because he was "hungover" at the time of the accident.

title: 2 suspects in Rapid City encampment homicide plead not guilty
description: The two people accused of killing 43-year-old Randall Lonehill at an improvised encampment in Rapid City pleaded not guilty on Monday, March 30.
_full_text:
The two people accused of killing 43-year-old Randall Lonehill at an improvised encampment in Rapid City pleaded not guilty on Monday, March 30. 

Celeste Conquering Bear, 30, and Artie Black Bear, 41, appeared for their arraignment before Judge Stacy Wickre at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

According to court documents, Black Bear and Conquering Bear are each indicted on aiding and abetting second-degree murder. 

In South Dakota, both charges are Class B felonies that carry a mandatory minimum sentence of life in prison and the possibility of up to a $50,000 fine. 

An autopsy report revealed Lonehill's death was caused by trauma associated with a physical assault and stabbing.

Police said officers were called around 4:35 p.m. on Monday, Jan. 26, to the 100 block of East North Street for a report of an unresponsive male. On arrival, officers found Lonehill dead in an improvised outdoor encampment set up near the railroad tracks. 

Black Bear and Conquering Bear's bond is set at $500,000 cash only. Their next court appearance is set for April 27 at 10:45 a.m.

title: Updated: Rapid City implements water restrictions early
description: Effective Wednesday, April 1, the City of Rapid City is elevating the storage condition status for Pactola Reservoir from “normal status” to “concern status.”
_full_text:
Effective Wednesday, April 1, the City of Rapid City is elevating the storage condition status for Pactola Reservoir from “normal status” to “concern status.”

As a result, City Public Works officials are implementing water conservation measures — commonly referred to as watering restrictions — two months early this season.

“We made this decision based off of the levels in Pactola. But also the National Weather Service predicts that this year is going to be lower precipitation, higher temperature for all summer,” Rapid City Water Superintendent Eric Boyda said at a press conference on Monday, March 30.

The action is due to current water levels and inflows at Pactola Reservoir. Last week, the reservoir level was at 47,532 acre feet, which is below the “concern status" threshold of 48,950 acre feet. The lower water level, coupled with the lack of snowpack remaining in the Pactola watershed areas, has led Rapid City officials to implement water restrictions beginning April 1.

These measures will affect residents' watering usage from April through the end of September. No watering is allowed between 9 a.m. and 6 p.m. daily. Odd-numbered addresses are allowed to water, outside those time restrictions, on odd-numbered calendar days. Even-numbered addresses are allowed to water on even-numbered calendar days. No outside watering is allowed on the 31st day of any month.

Manual watering with a handheld hose or with a bucket, sprinkling can or other similar container is allowed at all times.

 Eric Boyda
On March 30, Rapid City Water Superintendent Eric Boyda spoke to the press about upcoming water restrictions for April through September. 

This is the second consecutive summer Rapid City officials have instituted early water restrictions. Last summer, City officials implemented water conservation measures May 1, a month ahead of the annual water restriction period of June 1-Aug. 31.

“We do not come to this conclusion lightly and (it) likely comes as no surprise to anyone,” Public Works Director Mike Theis said in a release. “One of our main concerns is there’s basically no snowpack left due to the extremely warm temperatures and lack of precipitation. Ground conditions are very dry, so unless we get significant moisture, much of any moisture we receive will be absorbed into the ground and will provide minimal runoff."

Boyda has previously told the Journal the Rapid City water portfolio is made up of a mix of surface and stored water at 83%, and groundwater making up just 13% of the water the city uses.

“In addition, the extended forecast continues to feature little to no significant precipitation. Given all of these factors, we are implementing water restrictions earlier than normal to protect our water supplies, and they will remain in effect through the summer,” Theis said in the release.

Since the 1990s, the City of Rapid City has instituted mandatory water conservation measures, generally implemented June 1-Aug. 31 each year under "normal status" conditions. Status conditions include normal, concern, alert and critical stages and are determined by the water levels and inflows to Pactola Reservoir.

The “concern status” designation means whenever Pactola Reservoir contains less than 48,950 acre feet of water or the inflow to Pactola Reservoir is less than 90% of the mean monthly average, conservation measures will be implemented earlier and continue through Sept. 30.

Under the "alert status" designation, water conservation measures shall apply throughout the entire year and under the "critical access" designation, no outside watering is permitted. The status designations are determined by water levels and inflows at Pactola Reservoir.

Boyda said further information on Pactola water levels can be tracked through the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.

title: Rapid City woman sentenced for gun incident at grocery store
description: A Rapid City woman was sentenced to 12 years in prison for aggravated assault for a gun incident at a local grocery store.
_full_text:
A Rapid City woman was sentenced to 12 years in prison for aggravated assault for a gun incident at a local grocery store. 

Talisse Fighting Bear appeared for her sentencing before Circuit Court Judge Stacy Wickre on Monday, March 30, at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

The state alleged that on April 16, 2025, Fighting Bear entered the store armed with a loaded firearm. She pointed the gun at two employees and instructed them to call law enforcement.

Before entering the store, she reportedly discharged a round into the air. When police arrived, Fighting Bear pointed the gun at her own head, however, eventually surrendered the weapon without further incident.

At the time of the offense, Fighting Bear was on parole for two prior felony convictions and out on bond for other pending charges. Due to being a convicted felon, Fighting Bear is prohibited from possessing a firearm. 

Fighting Bear pleaded guilty to one count of aggravated assault and one count of possession of a firearm by a felon in February. 

Fighting Bear was also sentenced to two years in prison for possession of a firearm by a felon. That sentence will run concurrently with the 12 year aggravated assault sentence. 

According to the press release from the State's Attorney's Office, Assistant Chief Deputy State's Attorney Olivia Siglin acknowledged that Fighting Bear may have been experiencing mental health challenges but emphasized the seriousness of the offense. Siglin requested the court consider a 15-year sentence. 

Wickre told Fighting Bear that regardless of her intent, her actions carried serious consequences. Wickre highlighted her extensive criminal history and conduct while in custody, including a simple assault charge, as evidence of an ongoing pattern of behavior.

title: 'We want her story to get out': Community gathers for vigil for woman allegedly murdered at Ellsworth
description: On what should have been another milestone for Sahela Toka Win (Different Cheyenne Woman) Sangrait-Dubray, the Cheyenne River Lakota woman's family and community gathered to remember her and to seek answers for her death on Aug. 11, 2024. A similar gathering was held a year ago.
_full_text:
On what should have been another milestone for Sahela Toka Win (Different Cheyenne Woman) Sangrait-Dubray, the Cheyenne River Lakota woman's family and community gathered to remember her and to seek answers for her death on Aug. 11, 2024. A similar gathering was held a year ago. 

 Sahela's grandmothers
Sahela Toka Win (Different Cheyenne Woman) Sangrait-Dubray's grandmothers described her as "beautiful" inside and out.  

Despite the cold and wet weather, attendees huddled together holding candles in remembrance of Sangrait-Dubray on what would have been her 23rd birthday on Thursday, March 26. She died when she was 21. Each flickering flame serves as a demand for justice that has yet to find its way to the Black Hills.

"It's for justice," Red Ribbon Skirt Society Member Frances Dupris said. "The people who do bad things need to be held accountable."

 Sign
Signs lamented Sangrait-Dubray joining the list of murdered Indigenous women.  

Sangrait-Dubray was allegedly murdered in August 2025 on Ellsworth Air Force Base. Quinterius Charles Chappelle, a 24-year-old airman, is charged with first-degree murder in federal court. His jury trial has not been scheduled at this time.  According to federal court documents, Drew Durand of Rapid City is charged with accessory after the fact and misprision of a felony in connection to the case.

Chappelle was someone Sangrait trusted, her friends said. 

 Sangrait's mother
Sangrait-Dubray's mother, Hillary Dubray, at her daughter's vigil.  

About 30 community members gathered at the Journey Museum in Rapid City on Thursday with candles, flowers and balloons to not only celebrate another missed birthday of Sangrait-Dubray's, but to call for justice. Among those in attendance were local Missing and Murdered Indigenous People advocates the Red Ribbon Skirt Society and the Medicine Wheel Riders. Friends and family of Sangrait-Dubray also attended from across the nation.

Among the crowd, Hillary Dubray, Sangrait-Dubray's mother, wore a red hoodie bearing her daughter's face and name. As each person took their turn speaking about Sangrait-Dubray and the epidemic of Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women the nation faces, Dubray clung onto her other daughter in a tearful embrace.

 Vonda High Hawk
Vonda High Hawk, one of Sangrait-Dubray's grandmothers, speaks during the vigil.  

Dubray said Sangrait-Dubray moved to Rapid City when she was 18 years old and began working to help raise money for youth programs. Dubray described her daughter as "productive" and was just starting her adult life.

"We want a lot of her story to get out cause she was just turning 21, getting her life started, exploring her world and stuff," Dubray said. "It was just cut short."

Sangrait-Dubray’s grandmothers Vonda High Hawk and Phyllis Bald Eagle said she lived with them for a time when she was a child. Toka Win, Sangrait-Dubray’s Lakota name, was given to her by her great-grandmother.

 Vigil goers
Signs called for change. 

“We just want people to know that she mattered to us,” Bald Eagle said.

Both came out to speak about how beautiful of a person Sangrait-Dubray was and about her lineage.

“She’s the greatest person I’ve ever come to know,” said Kaylee Bores a Hole, who was best friends with Sangrait-Dubray since kindergarten. “She lights up the world wherever she goes.”

Bores a Hole said Sangrait-Dubray was outgoing and ambitious, with a dream to travel the world.

Dubray said her daughter spent a lot of her time speaking out against domestic violence and trying to help her community. Dubray said she only met Chappelle once.

Sangrait-Dubray's body was found March 4, 2025, outside of Hill City, 40 miles from Box Elder. Slightly over a year since she was found, she is finally being returned to her family. Dubray said they will be holding a funeral for her on April 17.

The Medicine Wheel Ride is organizing a demonstration in front of Ellsworth Air Force Base in mid-April.

Journal reporter Frank Espinoza contributed to this report. 

 Photos from Sahela Sangrait-Dubray's vigil in Rapid City
Community members gathered at the Journey Museum with candles, flowers and balloons to not only celebrate another missed birthday of Sangrait-Dubray's, but to call for justice.

title: A victim services conference in Box Elder highlighted how to help and not harm
description: BOX ELDER — The scenarios aren’t uncommon: Someone goes missing, maybe for the first time, maybe for the dozenth time. Their face and name is posted by a local or federal law enforcement agency and the comments roll in — worry, speculation and even blame on substance abuse issues that may or may not exist.
_full_text:
BOX ELDER — The scenarios aren’t uncommon: Someone goes missing, maybe for the first time, maybe for the dozenth time. Their face and name is posted by a local or federal law enforcement agency and the comments roll in — worry, speculation and even blame on substance abuse issues that may or may not exist.

Biting words rebuke irresponsible parents, whether that’s a factor or not. Someone is killed or abused, and the community responds with a mix of empathy and pointed questions like, “Why didn’t they leave sooner?”

“If I could leave with you anything, it’s just to be mindful of the ripple effect of MMIP (Murdered and Missing Indigenous People). It’s to be cognizant of those Facebook posts. I know sometimes it’s hard to see the same name or the same face show up,” Tanya Grassel-Krietlow, a program manager with the South Dakota Network Against Family Violence and Sexual Assault, said during a West River conference on multi-disciplinary response to victim services.

“We have to ask ourselves, 'What’s going on?' not 'Why isn’t he in school? Stop judging and start supporting.”

 Tanya Grassel-Krietlow
Tanya Grassel-Krietlow, a program manager with the South Dakota Network Against Family Violence and Sexual Assault speaks during the conference on Murdered and Missing Indigenous People. 

On Thursday, March 26, members of law enforcement agencies, a South Dakota Supreme Court justice, prosecutors, social services nonprofits, the medical field shelters and others gathered at The Box Event Center in Box Elder for the conference.

Although the all-day event was geared toward those working with victims, valuable information surfaced about the complexities of victimization and how community members can “show up” for each other, or at least not make a bad situation worse.

Grassel-Krietlow’s talk focused on Missing and Murdered Indigenous People, a topic she is intimately familiar with both professionally and personally. She grew up on the Lower Brule Reservation and primarily works with tribal partners.

When Grassel-Krietlow was born on May 29, 1970, in Mitchell, her mother had a 17-year-old little brother named Weldon who was eager to meet her. The conversation her mother had with Weldon was light-hearted. They talked about how big of a baby she was and joked that she was a walking toddler.

“He couldn’t wait to meet me, and then he was going to go fishing. Weldon was never seen (alive) again,” Grassel-Krietlow said.

Her newly-postpartum mother spent the next week looking for Weldon. He was ultimately found on the Crow Creek side of the Missouri River, up river from where his fishing equipment was found. He went from missing to what the family considered murdered. Grassel-Krietlow said there were issues with the investigation and some of his belongings were found on people in the years after his death, “suggesting that he had been with others,” she said.

When Grassel-Krietlow was 26, she began work as a federal probation officer and received an apology about how her uncle’s investigation went.

“We just kind of chalked it up to another drunk Indian,” Grassel-Krietlow recalled being told by federal law enforcement on her first day. “So my family grew up under the cloud of MMIP.”

 Gusinsky
South Dakota Supreme Court Justice Robert Gusinsky listens to a presenter during the victims services conference.  

“Everybody that is in Indian Country, I can confidently say everybody, has a connection to the MMIP space,” she added after telling the story of how her son, a game warden on the reservation, was one of the first responders on the scene when her cousin Amy was found dead, and when one of Amy’s children committed suicide after her death.

“I just gave you … three examples: my uncle, my cousin and my cousin's son, and that's only because I only have a half an hour,” she said.

Grassel-Krietlow pointed out the ripple effects these situations have on people, and the gaps in victim services that emerge when it’s not clear if there’s a criminal element to a disappearance.

“How are we supporting families when someone goes missing, and there's a bias that there may just be a substance abuse issue involved? How do we support the siblings of that person who's missing, who carries guilt that they weren't there when their brother went missing?” Grassel-Krietlow asked.

 Crowd listens to Tanya Grassel-Krietlow
The crowd gathered at the event center listens to Tanya Grassel-Krietlow speak.  

The Journal spoke with Grassel-Krietlow after her presentation about how the average person who’s not working in the system can help.

“Well, the first would be to, you know, not be a keyboard warrior,” she said. “The second would be when there are those volunteer programs that are there to help youth and to help adults in situations where they might need a helping hand, show up for those spaces, because then you'll get to know the advocates. You'll get to know the services that are available in your community, and then you'll even know how to help in a deeper level.”

She referenced Working Against Violence Incorporated, Journey On and Red Ribbon Skirt Society as local organizations community members could help out with. All are based in Rapid City.

Tifanie Petro, victims assistance program director at the Pennington County State’s Attorney’s Office and one of the primary coordinators of the event, has worked in victim advocacy for decades. Previous to her position at the State’s Attorney’s Office, Petro worked for the Children’s Home Society of South Dakota’s Child Adcocacy Center in Rapid City. She has also worked for Child Protective Services, Working Against Violence Inc. and Youth and Family Services.

Petro warned against the belief that only certain people experience some form of victimization.

“It really knows no bounds,” Petro said. “And on the other side of that, anybody can be a support to somebody who's experienced harm. You don’t need any letters behind your name. That’s my favorite thing to say. You just got to know how to show up and show up in a way that doesn't cause any harm, but allows us to have this connection, this relationship with people, and just meet them where they're at.”

 Booths
Organizations manned booths at the conference.  

Petro said some of the best ways to do that is to acknowledge what the person has gone through and “that it really sucks."

“Just stay in a space of warmth and genuineness,” she said. “People are going to respond to that.”

The event was brought together by the Interagency Training Council and a variety of community partners. Sponsors included the Children’s Home Society, the Supreme Court of South Dakota, the South Dakota Network Against Family Violence and Sexual Assault, Children’s Advocacy Centers of South Dakota, South Dakota State University and the Center for the Prevention of Child Mistreatment.

“It really is about bringing everybody together in one room to learn the same thing,” she said.

Other presentations included information about how trauma affects a child’s brain, trauma-informed approaches to domestic violence, child maltreatment, strategies to thrive through change and uncertainty and a multidisciplinary case review of a Pennington County rape and murder case.

title: Black Hills agencies encourage wildfire safety, preparedness
description: As the Coyote Flats Fire reaches 100% containment and the Qury Fire following close behind with 90% containment, Pennington County, Rapid City and Rockerville emergency officials gathered together to launch a wildfire awareness campaign.
_full_text:
As the Coyote Flats Fire reaches 100% containment and the Qury Fire following close behind with 90% containment, Pennington County, Rapid City and Rockerville emergency officials gathered together to launch a wildfire awareness campaign.

Despite grass already sprouting beneath the ash after the Coyote Flats wildfire, Rapid City Fire Department Fire Operations Division Chief Brian Povandra said the Black Hills isn't out of the woods just yet.

"This year we've had some really dry conditions throughout the winter," Povandra said during a campaign announcement on Thursday, March 26. "We haven't had the snow pack or moisture that we're used to and it's led to very erratic and extreme fire behavior very early in the year. So we thought it would be good to get everybody together and get this campaign out well in advance (of) what our normal fire season is."

 RCFD Division Chief of Fire Operations
Rapid City Fire Department Fire Operations Division Chief Brian Povandra explained fire season started early this year. 

From Monday, March 30, to Saturday, April 4, Pennington County Emergency Management, Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Fire Department and Rockerville Volunteer Fire Department will be hosting a social media campaign to discuss how to stay informed in your area during a fire, what to do during evacuation orders, what to do with your animals, citations for starting fires and defensible space tips to protect your property.

Povandra recommends no matter the person, everyone should be prepared for the abnormally early fire season. He said this year significantly lacked moisture, which accelerated the fire season. Fire season typically starts early summer.

"This is going to be an extreme year if we don't get some moisture," Povandra said.

Povandra said the public should be mindful of fuels, such as vegetation or stick piles, near the home and consider having them at least 5 feet clear of the residence.

 Pennington County Sheriff's Office about evacuations
Pennington County Sheriff's Office Lt. Jason Mitzel said during the Coyote Flats wild fire two deputies were nearly hit due to residents trying to veer around a roadblock established by the PCSO. 

Another consideration Povandra asked people to keep in mind is to clear out their gutters, as it only takes one small ember to ignite an entire roof.

Something brought to light with the most recent fires was how the public handled evacuations. Pennington County Sheriff's Office Lt. Jason Mitzel said during the Coyote Flats Fire two deputies were nearly hit due to residents trying to veer around the roadblock established by the PCSO.

Mitzel said it causes a dangerous situation for everyone involved if people don't follow evacuation orders.

"I can appreciate and empathize with the public wanting to come home during events like this. However, it really causes a lot of chaos," Mitzel said. "We had some fire equipment that was not able to get to where it was supposed to go in a timely manner."

 Surrounded house
Scorched earth, caused by the Coyote Flats wildfire, surrounds a home near Rockerville on Thursday, March 19. 

With fire season starting earlier than expected, and one of Rapid City's biggest celebrations rounding the corner this summer, Pennington County Emergency Management's Adam Davis said local law enforcement and fire departments are completely prepared to handle America's 250th celebration.

"That doesn't change a whole lot for us. We prepare for the events that are presented to us,' Davis said. "We have an amazing public safety community in Pennington County. We work together really well, so we're pretty effective in planning meetings to make sure we have our bases covered."

 Burnt Tree outside of Rockerville Fire Station 2
The Coyote Flats Fire is 100% contained. The fire burned 385 acres in the Rockerville area. 

For the Rapid Valley area, known to have limited firework restrictions compared to the rest of the Black Hills, Davis advised residents to take extra precautions in maintaining fire safety this summer.

"If you know it's a higher-than-normal fire season, be smart about what you're doing," Davis said. "Certainly, if you're going to be doing anything that has fire risk or spark risk, have resources immediately available to deal with that situation."

If there are any questions about conditions or about evacuation areas, the public is encouraged to look at the county's website, pennco.org/evacuation.

title: 'No Kings' protest in Rapid City lines Omaha Street, joining nationwide series of rallies
description: Nicole Preble hopes Saturday's "No Kings" protest inspires people.
_full_text:
Nicole Preble hopes Saturday's "No Kings" protest inspires people.

Preble, an organizer with Indivisible Rapid City, said the event is an effort "to show everybody that we're a community, that we're not alone and there are people in this town that are not OK with what's going on" with the administration of Republican President Donald Trump.

 033026-nws-protest-p27.JPG
About 1,300 people attended a "No Kings" rally on Saturday, March 28, in Rapid City. 

"It's a chance for people to find their people and get involved," she said. "Some people say, 'Why are you out there protesting? It's not going to change anything.' It galvanizes us, and it sends a loud message to people in office and people who want to run for office."

The March 28 protest in Rapid City was part of a series of rallies nationwide and beyond. The Associated Press reported organizers said more than 3,100 events have been registered in all 50 states and several other countries, with more than 9 million people expected to participate.

Indivisible Rapid City organized the local event, which ran along Omaha Street from Fifth Street to about Mount Rushmore Road on the downtown side and to about Seventh Street on the Memorial Park side. Police on scene estimated about 1,300 people attended.

The event featured a nonperishable food drive benefiting One Spirit, a nonprofit that provides food and essential supplies to Lakota communities on the Pine Ridge Reservation. A number of candidates held petition drives at the rally as well, with the deadline for state offices on Tuesday, March 31.

There were also rallies in Spearfish and Sturgis.

Jeff Jacobsen of Rapid City said he came out because of "the problems with our government." Signs at the event called out the actions of Trump, Immigration and Customs Enforcement and other agencies.

 033026-nws-protest-p7.JPG
The "No Kings" protest on Saturday, March 28, was the third since President Donald Trump returned to office. 

Jacobsen said as opposed to an increase in executive power, he's worried about a decrease in legislative and judicial power.

"So that's the problem. If he's violating the laws or skirting the laws it's up to the judicial and legislative to put a rein on it. And they're not," he said, before turning to the war in Iran. "It was a war of choice. There's no reason to do it right now."

Jacobsen pointed to a 2015 deal between the U.S. and Iran (which also involved Russia, France, China and the United Kingdom) that featured Iran agreeing not to pursue nuclear weapons and allowed monitoring of its compliance. In return, the major powers lessened economic sanctions against Iran.

Trump withdrew the U.S. from the deal during his first term, calling it "a horrible, one-sided deal," according to the New York Times.

"We, the United States, had a treaty with Iran to keep an eye on their radioactive material, and Trump killed it," Jacobsen said. "This is a totally unnecessary war."

Jacobsen said that arguments in favor of removing the repressive regime in Iran don't hold up.

 033026-nws-protest-p14.JPG
The event featured a nonperishable food drive benefiting One Spirit, a nonprofit that provides food and essential supplies to Lakota communities on the Pine Ridge Reservation. 

"It is a repressive regime, but they still had a treaty with us, and they still were following it. So it is a repressive regime, it's a bad regime," Jacobsen said. "But are we, the world's policemen? We're supposed to go out and make sure everybody becomes a democracy like us? No."

Preble, with Indivisible Rapid City, drew a line from the war in Iran to domestic issues, questioning the priorities of those in power.

"I hope this is going to galvanize people to demand a reset, and to say there is no reason why the richest country on the planet does not have universal health care. We've been given crumbs all this time, for decades, it's what we've been conditioned to receive," she said. "It's ridiculous and enraging to me that we can't feed the poor, we can't fund SNAP benefits, we can't have Medicaid, we can't have universal health care. You're just sink or swim, pal, but oh my God, we've got to spend at least a billion dollars a day on an illegal war and put our sons' and daughters' lives on the line."

 033026-nws-protest-p2.JPG
"I hope this is going to galvanize people to demand a reset," Nicole Preble with Indivisible Rapid City said about the event. 

Karen Psiaki of Summerset, standing near City Hall twirling an umbrella emblazoned with "No Kings" on it, said she's worried about executive power in the country.

"If I were a board, if I were a board president, my job would be to oversee the work of my CEO. If you're the president of the country, you're the CEO of the country. You're a hired hand. You answer to a board, the board is the Congress. You answer to them for us. They do oversight to make sure your work is being done correctly on our behalf," she said. "Somebody's dropping the ball, somebody's not doing their job. It's oversight that's not being done."

Psiaki also lamented the Trump's family profits during his time in office. Forbes reported Trump's net worth has increased roughly $3 billion since his return to office, much of that coming from Trump cryptocurrency.

The left-leaning Brennan Center for Justice notes his financial entanglements aren't illegal "on their face, because the president — like members of Congress and the Supreme Court — is exempt from ethics rules that bind most other federal officials. But by erasing the distinction between public office and private business and systematically weaving personal profit into official policy, Trump has enriched himself financially on a scale that dwarfs even the most infamous scandals in American history."

 PHOTOS: 'No Kings' protest along Omaha Street in Rapid City
The third nationwide "No Kings" protest featured a rally along Omaha Street in Rapid City. Police on scene estimated about 1,300 people attended.

title: Rapid City prepares for Community Cleanup Week
description: Homeowners have less than four weeks to clean out their garages, attics and closets as the 54th annual City-Wide Cleanup Week is just around the corner.
_full_text:
Homeowners have less than four weeks to clean out their garages, attics and closets as the 54th annual City-Wide Cleanup Week is just around the corner.

This year’s Cleanup Week is scheduled for April 20-24 with Community Cleanup Day set for Saturday, April 25. Highlights of the week include fees waived for disposal of many items at the Rapid City Landfill Monday through Friday, 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. and the annual community cleanup day set for Saturday of cleanup week, April 25. The waiver fee does not include tires, mattresses and box springs.

The City’s Solid Waste Division is organizing volunteer groups and individuals to help clean up public spaces. The City will provide trash bags and interested groups are encouraged to email ria.hannon@rcgov.org to determine available spaces.

“We’re expecting another busy community cleanup week,” said Ria Hannon, City Solid Waste Outreach coordinator. “There are areas with a lot of trash that collected from December’s wind storm event, not to mention fence lines, ditches, areas along Rapid Creek and areas with thick grass that always seem to collect a lot of material in a year’s time.

“Once again, we’ll have the landfill available Monday through Friday of Cleanup Week with fees waived for disposal of many items excluding tires, mattresses and box springs. Cleanup Week is always a great way to get rid of a lot of items that have piled up around the home, in the yard and in the garage.”

The relaxing of fees will be available for private pickups, cars and small trailers Monday to Friday of Cleanup Week but not commercial vehicles. Commercial vehicles will still be charged normal tipping fees. All loads must be secured. The Landfill will not accept Freon-based appliances or hazardous waste items or paint and there will be a fee for disposal of tires.

On Saturday, April 25, Community Cleanup Day teams will collect trash and items and leave them curbside at their respective cleanup areas for collection. There will be no admittance to the Landfill for cleanup items on Saturday. Regular paid access from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m.

“Community Cleanup Week serves as a great community service project for groups, especially youth groups and teams,” Hannon said. “It’s also a great opportunity to get businesses to form teams of workers to spend a few hours during the week or on a Saturday morning cleaning up an area of town. It’s a great way to help clean up our community.”

For more information about Cleanup Week, contact the Rapid City Solid Waste Division at 355-3496.

A look back at Cleanup Week 2025

City residents picked up, collected and hauled nearly 850 tons of material during last April’s 54th annual Community Cleanup Week. The total includes approximately seven tons of material collected in pink bags by 114 volunteer teams that fanned out through the community during the week’s cleanup activities.

Rapid City Solid Waste Division officials also reported a total of 2,315 residential vehicles passed through the scales at the Rapid City Landfill during the cleanup week event. In addition, officials at ReStore in Rapid City report 640 pounds of materials were recovered rather than buried in the Landfill.

As a comparison, if the average elephant weighs four tons, the tonnage of material crossing the landfill scales during last year’s cleanup week represents the weight of nearly 213 elephants!

title: 10-year-old dies from injuries suffered in accidental shooting in Pennington County
description: Law enforcement reported a 10-year-old died on Sunday, March 22, due to an accidental shooting.
_full_text:
Law enforcement reported a 10-year-old died on Sunday, March 22, due to an accidental shooting.

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said deputies responded to an area near Owanka on Sunday for a report of a juvenile who had been shot. 

During the initial investigation, it was determined that the 10-year-old juvenile had sustained a gunshot wound during what is believed to be "an accidental family-related incident."

Despite response efforts, the juvenile died from injuries suffered in the shooting, the sheriff's office said.

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said it is actively investigating the fatal shooting.

title: Rapid City offices closed on Good Friday
description: Rapid City administrative offices will be closed next Friday, April 3, in observance of Good Friday. The holiday closure will affect city transit and trash collection services. Some City facilities will be open and services operating on Good Friday but closed on Easter Sunday.
_full_text:
Rapid City administrative offices will be closed next Friday, April 3, in observance of Good Friday. The holiday closure will affect city transit and trash collection services. Some City facilities will be open and services operating on Good Friday but closed on Easter Sunday.

• City offices, including City Hall, will be closed on Good Friday, April 3.

• Rapid Transit Services, including RapidRide and Dial-A-Ride, will not be operating on Good Friday, April 3 but will operate on a normal Saturday schedule on Saturday, April 4.

• The City Landfill will be closed and there will be no collections Good Friday, April 3. City garbage collection will be adjusted the week of March 30-April 3 with normal collection next Monday (March 30), with Tuesday through Friday collections (March 31-April 3) scheduled a day earlier (Tuesday on Monday, Wednesday on Tuesday, etc.). All routes will be completed by Thursday afternoon. Residents are asked to set out trash and recycling containers by 7 a.m. and leave out until collected. The Landfill will be open with normal hours of operation on Saturday, April 4.

• The Rapid City Library will be open Good Friday and Saturday but closed Easter Sunday.

• The Monument’s administrative offices will be closed on Good Friday, April 3. The complex and Bluepeak ticket offices will remain open for scheduled events and will continue normal event operations throughout the weekend.

title: Thomas Parson
description: December 26, 1934 - March 19, 2026
_full_text:
December 26, 1934 - March 19, 2026

Thomas J. Parson, age 91, died on March 19, 2026, at Monument Health Home + Hospice House in Rapid City.

Thomas was born December 26, 1934, in Fiatt, Ill., the son of Thomas and Jeanne (Pilgrim) Parson.

Tom entered the United States Air Force on January 14, 1954, and retired on June 1, 1977. During his tenure in the Air Force he was stationed with the 307th Bomb Wing at Lincoln AFB, Lincoln, Neb.; the 389th and the 90th Strategic Missile Wings at F.E. Warren AFB, Cheyenne, Wyo.; the 455th Strategic Missile Wing at Minot AFB, N.D.; the 44th Strategic Missile Wing Ellsworth AFB, Rapid City, S.D.; and the 321st Strategic Missile Wing at Grand Forks AFB, N.D. He also spent a year in Southeast Asia in 1972-1973 with the 366th Tactical Fighter Wing and the 432nd Tactical Fighter Wing. While stationed at Lincoln AFB, Tom met the girl of his dreams. He and Pauline (Lori) Schmidt were married on January 7, 1956.

After retiring from the Air Force, Tom worked for Rockwell International and The Boeing Company for 20 years as Technical Representative for the Minuteman II and Minuteman III Missile systems at F.E. Warren, Ellsworth, and Minot Air Force Bases.

Survivors include daughters, Lorilynn (Thomas) Hahn of Sheridan, Wyo., and Yvonne (Brian) Gour of Littleton, Colo.; son, Thomas (Melanie Karp) Parson of Rapid City, S.D.; 10 grandchildren and 15 great-grandchildren.

Tom was preceded in death by his wife, Lori; and his daughter, Aimee (John) Pister of Kief, N.D.

Tom was a lifetime member of many organizations associated with his military career. He also held many offices within various levels of the Veterans of Foreign Wars in Rapid City, S.D. He was also an active member of the Honor Guard for VFW Post 1273.

A memorial service for both Tom and Lori will be held at 10:30 a.m. on Friday, May 1, 2026, at Osheim & Schmidt Funeral Home. They will be laid to rest together at 12:30 p.m. following the service at Black Hills National Cemetery.

Please share your memories of Tom at: www.OsheimSchmidt.com.

title: Travis Clarke
description: October 26, 1972 - December 1, 2025
_full_text:
October 26, 1972 - December 1, 2025

It is with a heavy heart that I am posting this. Us Navy Seabee EOC (retired) Travis Dean Clarke, age 53 Dover, OK. was taken from us on Dec. 1, 2025, after a tragic accident in OK. Travis was born in Gordon, Ne. on Oct. 26, 1972. He lived in Rapid City, SD and enjoyed spending time in Buffalo Gap and Custer SD. He graduated from Central High School in Rapid City in 1991. He then joined the US Navy Seabees in 1992, retiring in 2012. His 20 years of service took him around the world. After the Navy he worked in the Oklahoma oil fields. He is survived by his children, son Steven Clarke ,daughter Magen (Larry) Reylea, Granddaughters Genevieve and Evangelina Reylea of Virginia Beach, Va., son Troy Clarke Hermosa, SD, daughter Faith Clarke, Guthrie OK. . Parents Peggy (Cody) Champion, Rapid City, SD and Wallie (Caroline) Clarke, Guthrie OK. Siblings Shannon Champion (David & Ruby) Rapid City SD. Thomas (Kristin) Champion (Mckynlee and Wyatt) of Saratoga Springs Utah. Tad Clarke, Hot Sprigs SD. Aunts, uncles, cousins and many friends he considered family. He was preceded in death by grandparents, Roy and Mary Sawyer, Hot Springs SD, Grandfather Tad Clarke, Rushville Ne., Grandmother Millie Clarke Buffalo Gap SD, Grandparents Tom and Jean Champion, Custer SD., Uncles Ernest Martinez, Kennel SD, and Steve Clarke , Buffalo Gap SD. Full Military Honors will be at the Black Hills National Cemetery, Sturgis SD April 10, 2026 at 11.00 AM. Following will Be A celebration Of life with a lunch at the Ramkota Inn, 2111 N. Lacrosse St. Rapid City SD 57701
