title: Wildfire burns 43 acres in Pennington County
byline: SHALOM BAER GEE and MADISON WILLIS Journal staff
description: A wildfire sparked about 6 miles east of New Underwood on Friday, March 27, ultimately burning about 43 acres. The fire is contained, as of a Friday evening update.
_full_text:
A wildfire sparked about 6 miles east of New Underwood on Friday, March 27, ultimately burning about 43 acres. The fire is contained, as of a Friday evening update.

The New Underwood Volunteer Fire Department said it responded to a 2 p.m. report of a wildfire in the 22000 block of Sharpe Road in unincorporated Pennington County. The area is a couple miles from Interstate 90, closest to Exit 84.

 New Underwood Fire
A Friday, March 27, wildfire near New Underwood left behind charred fields. 

Upon arrival, firefighters observed a wind-driven fire burning in cured vegetation under critical fire weather conditions and immediately struck a second alarm.

The fire threatened infrastructure and caused damage to ranching assets, the New Underwood department said. There were no reported injuries to firefighters, civilians, livestock or domestic animals.

Agencies that were part of the response were Pennington County 911, New Underwood Volunteer Fire Department, Folsom Volunteer Fire Department, Rapid Valley Volunteer Fire Department, Box Elder Volunteer Fire Department, Wall Volunteer Fire Department, Wasta Volunteer Fire Department, Ellsworth Air Force Base Fire Department, local ranchers and neighbors.

The fire continues the start of an early and active fire season in West River, with wildfires already having burned thousands of acres in the Black Hills near Custer. 

In preparation for the season, Black Hills first responder agencies are launching a wildfire awareness campaign.

From Monday, March 30, to Saturday, April 4, Pennington County Emergency Management, Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Fire Department and Rockerville Volunteer Fire Department will be hosting a social media campaign to discuss how to stay informed in your area during a fire, what to do during evacuation orders, what to do with your animals, citations for starting fires and defensible space tips to protect your property.

 Fire vehicle
A New Underwood Volunteer Fire Department vehicle responds to a wildfire on Friday, March 27.

title: Pennington County Sheriff's Office investigates shooting in Rapid Valley
description: The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said it is investigating a shooting in Rapid Valley that left one man injured.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said it is investigating a shooting in Rapid Valley that left one man injured.

Deputies were called to a residence on Teewinot Drive at around 11:56 p.m. on Thursday, March 26, on a report of a disturbance, the sheriff's office said in a Friday, March 27, release.

Deputies made contact with two men and a woman in the residence. One of the men suffered from a gunshot wound that is not considered life-threatening, the sheriff's office said. Medics took the man to the hospital for treatment and he remains there in stable condition. The other two individuals at the residence exhibited injuries consistent with a physical altercation.

An investigation into the incident continues as the sheriff's office works with the Rapid City Police Department. All individuals believed to be involved in this incident have been identified and accounted for, the sheriff's office said. Investigators have determined that the parties involved are known to one another.

The sheriff's office did not release the names of those involved or who fired the weapon. The Journal has reached out for additional information.

title: South Dakota Mines invites community to experience a world of culture at Cultural Expo
description: For more than six decades, South Dakota Mines international students have shared a little of their home countries with the community during the annual Cultural Expo, an event that brings people together to celebrate global cultures.
_full_text:
For more than six decades, South Dakota Mines international students have shared a little of their home countries with the community during the annual Cultural Expo, an event that brings people together to celebrate global cultures.

The event, which began 64 years ago with the university’s first international students, has grown immensely over the years and has become a must-attend tradition.

“South Dakota Mines' Cultural Expo is a place for global exchange, curiosity, community, and learning from different cultures — values that align well with STEM fields,” said Beth Riley, director of the university’s Ivanhoe International Center. “As the world becomes a global society, engaging with different cultures expands professional and personal perspectives, sparks curiosity and intellectual exploration beyond the classroom environment, and is a wonderful event celebrating many cultures together.”

 The 64th annual Cultural Expo
The 64th annual Cultural Expo will be held on Saturday, March 28, from 11 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. in the Surbeck Center’s Beck Ballroom. There will be food tasting, entertainment and country displays. The cost is $15 per person, $5 for children 10 and under, and includes a set of sampling tickets to be used at various booths. Tickets can be purchased in advance here.   

The 64th annual Cultural Expo will be held on Saturday, March 28, from 11 a.m. to 1:30 p.m. in the Surbeck Center’s Beck Ballroom. There will be food tasting, entertainment and country displays. The cost is $15 per person, $5 for children 10 and under, and provides participants with a set of sampling tickets to be used at various booths. Tickets can be purchased in advance at eventbrite.com/e/south-dakota-mines-cultural-expo-tickets-1984337820839?aff=oddtdtcreator.

The doors open at 11 a.m. with a lineup of free entertainment featuring traditional music, vibrant dance and a world-class fashion show. Food sampling beings at 11:30 a.m. and allows participants to enjoy a variety of authentic international foods while the performances continue on stage.

Five countries and two organizations will be represented at this year’s event, which is hosted by groups from Mines and the local community and is sponsored by the Ivanhoe International Center, international students, faculty, and staff, as well as support from community partners, International Students, Inc., and Black Hills Energy.

Signature events like the Cultural Expo connect communities, celebrate traditions, strengthen community bonds and educate younger generations about diverse cultures.

 The Cultural Expo
The Cultural Expo features food and entertainment from around the world.

title: Black Hills communities host three protests as part of nationwide 'No Kings' rallies
description: Rapid City, Sturgis and Spearfish will host peaceful protests as part of the nationwide “No Kings” rallies on Saturday, March 28. This will be the third coordinated nationwide “No Kings” rally, and organizers predict it could be the largest nonviolent protest in United States history.
_full_text:
Rapid City, Sturgis and Spearfish will host peaceful protests as part of the nationwide “No Kings” rallies on Saturday, March 28. This will be the third coordinated nationwide “No Kings” rally, and organizers predict it could be the largest nonviolent protest in United States history.

Organizers request that no weapons of any kind, including those legally permitted, be brought to these events.

Rapid City

Indivisible Rapid City and 50501 Rapid City are organizing a “No Kings 3 Rally” and food drive from noon to 1:30 p.m. Saturday, March 28, at City Hall, 300 Sixth St.

Organizers said “No Kings 3 Rally” seeks to "give voice to those who oppose these unilateral and unpopular actions," the President Donald Trump Administration, Indivisible Rapid City said in a news release.

 Street corner protest .JPG (copy)
Rapid City's "No Kings" protest flooded Omaha Street near City Hall with signs and blaring horns Saturday, June 14, 2025. More than 2,000 people came out, according to some estimates, though no official number was clocked. The events were organized by Indivisible Rapid City and 50501 South Dakota. "No Kings" nationwide protests were held in June and October 2025. Rapid City, Spearfish and Sturgis will host peaceful protests as part of the upcoming nationwide "No Kings" rallies on Saturday, March 28. 

“While lawless, militarized policing agencies like ICE tear families apart, U.S. oligarchs are starting wars and building ballrooms,” said Lesleigh Owen, outreach coordinator for Indivisible Rapid City. “We the people need to come together to show them we are watching, and we are not going to let this continue.”

Community members are encouraged to bring non-perishable food items for the food drive that will benefit One Spirit, a nonprofit that provides food and essential supplies to Lakota communities on the Pine Ridge Reservation.

Spearfish

A peaceful “No Kings” protest will take place from noon to 1 p.m. at Main Street and Jackson Boulevard.

Sturgis

A peaceful “No Kings” protest will take place from 2 to 3 p.m. at Lazelle and Second Streets.

The Spearfish and Sturgis “No Kings” rallies are organized by Lawrence County Democrats, Belle Fourche Pro-Democracy Squad, Meade County Democrats, and Spearfish Defenders of Democracy.

“Join us as we demand the Trump administration stop its attacks on free speech, involvement in illegal wars, and corruption of our Constitutional systems. Our representatives must be held accountable,” the organizations said in a news release about the upcoming “No Kings” events. “We have the power and are claiming it together. No thrones. No crowns. No kings.”

“No Kings” rallies are a response to growing public outrage over recent federal actions, according to Ezra Levin, co-founder of Indivisible. Previous "No Kings" nationwide rallies were held in June and October 2025.

Organizers said all “No Kings” events are committed to nonviolent action. All participants are encouraged to de-escalate any potential confrontation with those who disagree, and to act lawfully at “No Kings” events.

title: Dinosaurs loose in the Rapid City Public Library
description: A few prehistoric friends — or at least casts of their bones — have moved into the Rapid City Public Library .
_full_text:
A few prehistoric friends — or at least casts of their bones — have moved into the Rapid City Public Library.

A Denversaurus, Tyrannosaurus rex skull and neck and a Brachiosaurus skull are on display at the library, located at 610 Quincy St., on loan from the Black Hills Institute of Geological Research.

"Our dinosaur guest(s) joined us as part of our preparation for Summer Reading, the library’s summer learning loss initiative," said Laurinda Tapper, the library's public relations coordinator. 

This year’s Summer Reading theme is "Unearth a Story," which will focus on paleontology and archeology. 

"We felt this would be a great way for us to engage the community with something different for them to enjoy while they’re using the library," Tapper said. "We reached out to the Black Hills Institute of Geological Research and they were eager to partner with us to display some of their dinosaurs here."

For children, the library asks they read or be read to for 15 minutes each day in June and July to earn a small prize and a free book for each month they do so. If they read in both June and July, they’ll also be entered to win a grand prize. They’ll color in each day that they read to keep track and turn in their completed logs at the library.

Teens and adults also have their own programs, with the outline is similar: 30 minutes of reading a day. Each week, they turn in a tracking bookmark for a small prize and to be entered in a weekly drawing for a prize basket.

All the prizes are either community donations or are funded by the Friends of the Library.

 T-Rex lurks
A Tyrannosaurus rex lurks among the books at the Rapid City Public Library.  

The dinos arrived on March 17. It's not clear how long they'll be there, but Tapper said she suspects they'll stick around for the duration of Summer Reading.

"We’ve had a tremendous response from our community about the dinosaurs. People of all ages are enjoying them, but it’s especially rewarding to see children engaged and taking them in," Tapper said. "For those who don’t know they’re here, it’s such an exciting surprise to see them when they visit us —come to get a book, and wow, see some really cool dinosaurs!"

Tapper said the dinosaurs align not only with Summer Reading, but with the library's mission to encourage learning.

 "Tank" the Denversaurus schlessmani
"Tank" the Denversaurus schlessmani is the most complete nodosaurid skeleton from the Maastrichtian-aged sediments of North America. This 66-million-year-old specimen is preserved with a large portion of the original bony plates that lined the skin and protected this herbivore from attacks by predators like Tyrannosaurus rex, per the informational plaque accompanying the "Tank" cast at the library. 

"Having dinosaurs on display at the library is another way where we can connect our community with education and inspire people of all ages," she added. "I‘ve heard our next dinosaur will be a stegosaurus, but stay tuned!"

In a social media post, the Natural History Museum of the Black Hills said, "This unique collaboration aims to inspire curiosity of all ages, connecting the ancient world of dinosaurs with the pursuit of knowledge found within the library's shelves."

The Black Hills Institute has purchased the former Prairie Berry Winery in Hill City and plans to reopen it in 2027 as the Natural History Museum of the Black Hills. The Institute currently operates the Museum at Black Hills Institute on Main Street in Hill City. After the former winery is renovated, the new museum will contain nearly 40,000 square feet of space.

 Photos of dinosaurs in the Rapid City Public Library
A Denversaurus, T-rex skull and neck and a Brachiosaurus are on display at the library on loan from the Black Hills Institute of Geological Research.

title: Ellsworth to welcome B-21 in 2027 as funding accelerates bomber production
description: President Donald Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" included $4.5 billion in funding for the production of the B-21 Raider, which now puts the aircraft on track to be stationed at Ellsworth Air Force Base next year, according to a press release by the U.S. Air Force .
_full_text:
President Donald Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" included $4.5 billion in funding for the production of the B-21 Raider, which now puts the aircraft on track to be stationed at Ellsworth Air Force Base next year, according to a press release by the U.S. Air Force.

In 2021, the Air Force formally announced Ellsworth would be the first Main Operating Base for the new B-21, with the expectation to have the bombers stationed in the mid-2020s.

Per the recent press release: "The Department of the Air Force and Northrop Grumman Corp. have reached an agreement to expand production capacity for the B-21 Raider, accelerating delivery of the Air Force’s next-generation stealth bomber fleet."

Included in the agreement is $4.5 billion in funding from the “One Big Beautiful Bill," which the Air Force said will increase the annual production capacity by 25% for the bomber.

"This is what disciplined acquisition delivers," Gen. Dale R. White, director for critical major weapons systems and direct reporting portfolio manager to the Deputy Secretary of War, said in the release. "This decision reflects our confidence in the program's performance and the stability of the industrial base. By increasing production capacity now, we are responsibly accelerating delivery of a critical, combat-effective capability to the warfighter."

The releases state the B-21 remains on track to be "on the ramp" at Ellsworth Air Force Base in South Dakota in 2027.

Gov. Larry Rhoden's press secretary, Josie Harms, told the Journal the governor is committed to strengthening national security in the state and nationally.

"Ellsworth Air Force Base is uniquely positioned to play a crucial role in national security. Its location in the interior of the United States is particularly advantageous; it's harder for adversaries to spy in a rural area far in America's interior," Harms said.

According to a press release from Rhoden, the Ellsworth base has played key roles in major global events, the most recent being "Operation Epic Fury," which Trump authorized for the bombing of Iran, and included the killing of its leader Ali Khamenei on Feb. 28.

"When actions like this happen on the international level, they impact us here at home. South Dakota’s own Ellsworth Air Force Base played a key role in Operation Epic Fury, and we thank the brave men and women who spent long hours flying our military ordnance to deliver a crippling blow to our adversary," Rhoden said in the release.

The B-21 is set to replace the B-1 Lancer and B-2 Spirit bombers, which have provided global strike capabilities.

The new aircraft is a long-range, stealth bomber capable of delivering a mix of conventional and nuclear munitions.

Ellsworth will be the first main operating base to receive the new aircraft, with Whiteman Air Force Base in Missouri and Dyess Air Force Base in Texas to follow.

Apart from this update, an estimated $2 billion worth of construction has been committed to the base in anticipation of the new stealth bomber planes and for expansion needs for the increase in servicemen to the base that will come with the plane.

 B-21 video
Col. Brian Buschur, 28th Operations Group commander at Ellsworth Air Force Base, stands while showing the crowd a video about the B-21 Raider program. 

South Dakota Ellsworth Development Authority Executive Director Scott Landguth gave an update on March 19 to the Journal on the base's expansion.

"To date, at Ellsworth, there's been almost $2 billion in construction awarded and about 18 projects awarded out there, with a total of that being around 35 projects that will be awarded by the time all this expansion is done," Landguth said.

"I can say one of the projects that was completed last year, the runway improvements and reconstruction, was done all within the calendar year of 2025 .... So significant runway reconstruction all done within one year," Landguth said.

As for current projects under way, he said there is construction on a weapons generation facility, mission planning facility, hangars for the use of maintenance and support work on the bomber simulator and a building for training simulators for the aircraft.

Additionally, Harms referenced further investments into the community of Box Elder. She cited Senate Bill 6, which provides a no-interest loan for 20 years of an amount of up to $15 million to the Douglas School District, as necessary for the community.

Harms said South Dakota is a state veterans like to retire to.

"That means service members stationed at Ellsworth have a strong opportunity to put down roots and continue participating in our communities long after their service is done," Harms said.

According to a South Dakota Legislature Ellsworth Impact report, the base has an annual economic impact of approximately $886.8 million (2023), supporting roughly 9,100 personnel (active duty, civilians and dependents).

The report estimates the total number of personnel the base will support following the bombers' arrival will be 11,792.

The base is the largest employer in western South Dakota and the second-largest employer in the state.

title: For first time since 2002, every ward contested in Rapid City Council election
description: The Rapid City Council election is set.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Council election is set.

This year every ward was contested for the first time since 2002, according to Finance Director Daniel Ainslie.

The current seats up for election on June 2 are held by Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2), Kevin Maher (Ward 3), John Roberts (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5).

 Voting
On March 25 the city finance department conducted a random drawing for the placement of names on the city's municipal election on June 2. 

Biberdorf, Seachris, Maher and Roberts have turned in their required 50 valid signatures to qualify for the ballot on the June 2 election. Petitions were due on Tuesday, March 24.

Pettigrew is not seeking reelection.

Ainslie told the Journal only one petitioner, Brandon Ferguson, didn't meet the 50 valid signatures threshold when he turned his paperwork in on Tuesday.

Ainslie said after candidates turn in petitions, the finance office checks to ensure those who signed were registered to vote, registered to vote within the correct ward, have the correct address and filled out information in its entirety.

On Wednesday, March 25, the city drew names to determine how they'd be listed on the ballot.

The official order goes:

Ward one: Josh Biberdorf, Murray Lee

Ward two: Christopher Vanderhoof, Lindsey Seachris

Ward three: Andrea Schaefer, Kevin Maher

Ward four: Valeriah Big Eagle, John Roberts, Ardin Jay Cychosz

Ward five: Pat Roseland, Laura Armstrong

The city will send the official order to the Pennington County's Auditor's Office.

Absentee voting begins on April 17 and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  

Voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

 Drawing cup
A cup filed with folded paper that was cut out containing candidates' names for a random drawing for the placement of names for the race of city council. 

"On average, our municipal elections have been in the teens, maybe 20%," Ainslie said about voter turnout for a municipal election.

He said the city and county spilt some of the costs of the municipal election, but estimated it will cost $40,000 to $50,000.

Those officially throwing their hat in the race who are not currently on the council are Murray Lee (Ward 1), Christopher Vanderhoof (Ward 2), Andrea Schaefer (Ward 3), Ardin Jay Cychosz (Ward 4) and Valeriah Big Eagle (Ward 4) and Laura Armstrong (Ward 5).

Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) said of Pettigrew's departure, "I have really enjoyed working with President Pettigrew over the last year, and serving as his vice president has been a great experience. He is a thoughtful leader who cares deeply about this community, and I respect the time he has put into this community."

Regarding the election, Tamang said he strongly encourages residents to pay attention to the candidates in their ward and make an informed decision.

"Local government affects our lives in profound ways, and who we choose to serve on City Council has a significant impact on the direction of our city," Tamang said.

Although the city does not host any candidate forums, Ainslie said residents should expect to hear other organizations that will be hosting candidates, as well as marketing from each campaign.

"You have some candidates who invest very little, and maybe it'll be a couple $1,000 for a few yard signs. And then there are other candidates that will advertise in media, whether it's radio, social media, television, newspapers, you know, they'll invest in all those things and billboards ... It can cost $10s of $1,000s of dollars, especially if they start doing mailers," Ainslie said.

For more information or questions, contact the City Finance Department at 605-394-4143.

title: Rapid City man arrested for starting a fire near Roosevelt Park
description: A Rapid City man was arrested on Wednesday, March 25, after he allegedly started a fire under the East Omaha Bridge near Roosevelt Park.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was arrested on Wednesday, March 25, after he allegedly started a fire under the East Omaha Bridge near Roosevelt Park. 

Tony Hessler, 38, was placed under arrest on suspicion of city park fire violation and a bond revocation. 

At around 12:20 p.m. on Wednesday, police were contacted by a city park ranger about a man who had started a fire on a pile of leaves under the East Omaha Bridge near Roosevelt Park. Police contacted Hessler shortly after. 

The park ranger ensured the fire was extinguished. 

Hessler is being held at the Pennington County Jail with no bond set.

title: Rapid City man arrested after rollover crash
description: A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes.

Robin Masters, 67, was arrested on suspicion of reckless driving. 

At around 1:05 p.m. on Wednesday, March 25, police responded to the 2400 block of Canyon Lake Drive for a report of a crash involving several vehicles. 

After speaking with witnesses at the scene, police determined a Fiat 500, driven by Masters, was driving eastbound on Canyon Lake Drive at a high rate of speed. Masters entered the lane of oncoming traffic, then swerved back into the eastbound lane, striking a motorcycle and causing a Buick passenger vehicle to swerve. 

 032826-nws-masters-p1.jpg
A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes. 

The Fiat and the Buick both left the roadway and rolled. The driver of the Buick was transported to the hospital for minor injuries. The motorcyclist was uninjured. 

As of Thursday, March 26, Masters was not found on the Pennington County Jail roster. He appeared in court on Thursday, March 26, and is set to appear for a dispositional conference on May 4. Prosecutors and defense discuss a potential resolution of a case, sometimes through a plea deal, at such conferences.

Court records show Masters was charged in November 2025 with failure to stop and provide information after a property damage accident, reckless driving and following too closely.

title: Missing Pine Ridge man found dead, cause under investigation
description: Nearly a month after last being seen, the Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said it found a missing Pine Ridge man dead.
_full_text:
Nearly a month after last being seen, the Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said it found a missing Pine Ridge man dead.

Dale Glenn Warrior Sr., 64, was last seen on Feb. 27 driving a silver 2006 Pontiac Grand Prix GT2 in the Wounded Knee area. The department said on Thursday, March 26, that Warrior and his vehicle had been found.

Warrior's family has been notified. 

"We would like to express our gratitude to the community for your assistance and support throughout this search," the department said on Facebook. "...We extend our sincerest condolences to his family and loved ones during this difficult time."

The department said the case remains under investigation and investigators are currently waiting on autopsy reports to determine an official cause of death.

The department didn't say when or where Warrior was found. Attempts to reach the department for additional information weren't immediately successful on Thursday.

title: Pennington County Auditor’s Office shares information on new voting bill
description: The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is informing residents of a new state law, Senate Bill 175, which is now in effect and introduces updated requirements for voter registration in South Dakota.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is informing residents of a new state law, Senate Bill 175, which is now in effect and introduces updated requirements for voter registration in South Dakota.

SB 175 requires individuals registering to vote for the first time, or those who have been removed from the state’s voter rolls, to provide documentary proof of United States citizenship at the time of registration.

The new law does not impact individuals who are already registered to vote. Voters who are currently registered, or those making updates to their existing registration—such as a change of address, name, or political party—are not required to provide proof of citizenship under this law.

Acceptable forms of documentation for new voter registrations include:

A South Dakota driver’s license or nondriver identification card issued after July 1, 2025, which indicates citizenship status on the back of the card;
A driver’s license or nondriver identification card from another state that indicates citizenship has been verified;
A tribal identification card; or
Photocopy’s of official documents such as a birth certificate or U.S. passport. Other forms of official documents are outlined in section 3 of SB 175

“This new law brings changes to the voter registration process, and our office is here to help residents understand what it means for them,” said Sabrina Green, Pennington County Auditor. “For current voters, nothing will change. However, individuals registering for the first time should be prepared to provide the required documentation. We encourage anyone with questions to contact our office for assistance.”

The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is working to ensure a smooth implementation of SB 175 and remains available to assist residents with voter registration questions or concerns.

Residents can also verify their voter registration status through the South Dakota Secretary of State’s website at https://vip.sdsos.gov/VIPLogin.aspx.

title: Indian Health Service is digging out of decades-old construction backlog for medical buildings
description: SANTA ANA PUEBLO, N.M. (AP) — An empty lot between a fire station and a soccer field just outside Albuquerque soon will be the home of a federal medical center first promised to Native American patients more than 30 years ago.
_full_text:
SANTA ANA PUEBLO, N.M. (AP) — An empty lot between a fire station and a soccer field just outside Albuquerque soon will be the home of a federal medical center first promised to Native American patients more than 30 years ago.

Earlier this month, Santa Ana Pueblo Gov. Myron Armijo took officials from the U.S. Indian Health Service and the Department of Health and Human Services on a tour of the location where patients are to receive everything from dialysis and diabetes care to optometry services.

“This will definitely change the game for health care in our area,” Armijo said.

Set to break ground in 2027, the 235,000-square-foot (22,000-square-meter) center will be run by the IHS, the U.S. agency that provides health care to Native Americans. Tribal leaders hope it will relieve pressure on the aging and overextended Albuquerque Indian Health Center, a federal facility originally built 90 years ago where some patients report waiting months for an appointment.

The Albuquerque facility was among more than 60 clinics and hospitals the agency identified for replacement in 1993 due to their age, condition and inability to serve a growing population. It remains on the list along with six other projects scattered around Arizona and New Mexico. IHS officials say it will eventually be replaced by two new facilities in the Albuquerque area, including the center planned at Santa Ana Pueblo.

In February, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. pledged $1 billion toward those long-delayed projects, including $22 million for the Santa Ana Pueblo center. The agency estimates $8 billion is needed to tackle all remaining projects on the 1993 list that, under federal law, must be complete before the IHS can address other major construction needs.

A.C. Locklear, CEO of the nonprofit National Indian Health Board, said the $1 billion is the single largest financial investment by any administration in addressing the aging facilities. Yet, he said, it also shows the federal government has neglected its legal duty to provide adequate health care to tribal nations.

“It’s a drop in the bucket in terms of what’s needed to modernize these facilities,” Locklear said.

Aging infrastructure impacts access, quality of care

The IHS serves 2.8 million Native American and Alaska Native patients at 21 hospitals and 78 smaller health centers nationwide. The average age of those facilities is around 40 years old and one-third are in “poor” physical condition, according to a 2023 U.S. Government Accountability Office report.

That isn't lost on Theresa Nelson, a 62-year-old Navajo Nation citizen who started relying on the Albuquerque Indian Health Center after retiring and losing her health insurance.

“It felt like going back in time,” she said, describing everything from the X-ray machines to exam rooms and waiting room furniture as outdated.

Nelson said the center relies on a complex system of outside referrals for treatments and tests that were easier to access in the private sector. She has been waiting for eight weeks for IHS to approve a referral for a 3D mammogram, a tool the Mayo Clinic says is offered at most U.S. health care facilities.

The Indian Health Service said appointment wait times at the Albuquerque center are less than 14 days for patients who are established with a primary care provider. But Nelson and other patients report going years without being assigned a doctor and waiting months to be seen for preventive care.

Farther west, the Gallup Indian Medical Center operates out of a mashup of modular buildings and piecemeal renovations. The hospital, which opened over six decades ago and is on the 1993 list, serves a population that includes the Navajo Nation. Tribal lawmaker Vince James said constant construction and a disjointed layout make it difficult for elderly and disabled patients to navigate the hospital and for providers to do their jobs.

“These are Band-Aid fixes,” James said. “Eventually the GIMC campus will become unsafe.”

An “unacceptable” backlog

Senior HHS adviser Mark Cruz urged Congress to make a special appropriation to complete the remaining projects that are in various stages of planning and design.

Without that funding, he said, it could take another 40 years to get through the priority list.

“It’s really unacceptable that we’re still working off of that 33-year-old construction list,” Cruz said during the Santa Ana Pueblo tour.

Federal law requires the Indian Health Service to complete that list before replacing clinics and hospitals that have fallen into disrepair since 1993. That includes two nearly 90-year-old hospitals in Montana and Minnesota. The agency also can't build new facilities to meet patient demand, which has grown and shifted geographically in recent decades.

“I can’t get to additional projects that have merit across Indian Country or Alaska because I have a statutory obligation to get through the 1993 list first,” Cruz said.

In 2023 the IHS crossed a project in Rapid City, South Dakota off its priority list. The replacement of the aging and troubled Sioux San Hospital has been “transformational,” said Jerilyn Church, CEO of the Great Plains Tribal Leader’s Health Board.

The renamed Oyate Health Center is three times larger than the former hospital and equipped with far more modern medical equipment. But demand for care at the new center is already outstripping available space.

“That’s what happens when you work from a backlog,” Church said. “In the time between identifying the need and the money finally becoming available, the population grows.”

title: Arthur Charles Fredrickson
_full_text:
July 5, 1925 - March 15, 2026

Arthur Charles Fredrickson, known lovingly as "Grandpa" passed away peacefully on March 15, 2026 at Hearthstone Nursing Home in York, Nebraska, having lived a full and remarkable life of over a Century.

He was born on July 5, 1925 in Hamill, South Dakota. Art grew up with love of God, a quiet strength and steadfast character that would define his many years.

After graduating from High School in Spencer, NE, he served two years of active combat in the infantry in the Italian Campaign during World War II.

After coming home, he married Marian Fahk and began working for the Chicago Northwestern Railroad where he worked in both operations as a dispatcher, and then in communications. The family moved to Chadron, NE where they raised their two children, Robert and Shari. Art retired from the railroad and after Marian passed, he married Bernice Amy Grove. They eventually moved to York, Nebraska to be near his daughter Shari, her husband Phil and their girls Kimberly and Christina.

He always enjoyed playing his organ for one and all. For many years before covid hit, he played at the York nursing homes and Wendy's.

He is survived by his son Robert, (wife Starla) and their two boys John and Gregory Fredrickson of Sterling, CO and by Shari and her two daughters Kimberly and Christina Hallisey of York, NE. Also, by two younger brothers, Keith Fredrickson of Bluffdale, TX and Cliff Fredrickson of Escondido, CA, and many nieces and nephews.

He was preceded in death by his wives, Marian and Amy, his son-in-law Phil, his parents John and Lilly, a brother Wally, and two sisters Mable and Betty.

title: Donaldeen Mary Connealy
description: August 5, 1935 - March 18, 2026
_full_text:
August 5, 1935 - March 18, 2026

MULLEN, NE - Donaldeen Mary Connealy died at age 90 on March 18, 2026, in Omaha, Nebraska.

Born August 5, 1935, in Gordon, NE as the youngest daughter of Hop and Helen Vinton, Donnie was raised on their ranch in Cherry County. After attending middle school at St. Agnes Academy in Alliance, and high school at St. Mary's Academy in O'Neill, Donnie then took undergraduate studies at St. Mary of the Woods in Terre Haute, Indiana, before marrying Marty Connealy on July 6, 1955, at St. Leo's Catholic Church in Gordon, NE. In the spring of 1962, Donnie and Marty moved to the ranch a few miles south of Whitman, NE where they raised their seven children. Later in life, the couple spent portions of their time in Lincoln, NE where they had a second home and many friends. After Marty's death in 2011, Donnie spent an increasing amount of time in Lincoln, and later at Elk Ridge Village, a senior living community in Elkhorn.

Donnie is survived by sons, Bill (Renee) of Phoenix, Jerry (Sharon) of Whitman, Jack (Jane) of Lincoln, Mick (Kathy) and Terry (Lisa) both of Omaha; daughters, Susan (Bob) Huffman of Bassett, and Kathy (Steve) Cleveland of Chadron. She is also survived by 25 grandchildren and 46 great-grandchildren.

A Holy Rosary will be prayed for Donnie on the evening of Sunday, March 29, 2026, in Mullen at 6 p.m. (MST) at St. Mary's Catholic Church with visitation prior from 5 p.m. to 6 p.m. (MST). The Mass of Christian Burial will be held for Donnie at St. Mary's Catholic Church on Monday, March 30, 2026, at 10 a.m. with burial immediately following in Cedarview Cemetery.

The family asks that in lieu of flowers, memorials may be offered to All Saints Altar Society in Hyannis, NE. Arrangements are being handled by Govier Brothers Mortuary/Mullen Funeral Home. Online words of remembrance or condolences may be left at www.govierbrothers.com.

title: Mark Sheldon Kjellerson
_full_text:
July 25, 1957 - March 21, 2026

Mark Sheldon Kjellerson went to be with the Lord on March 21, 2026 at Monument Hospice House in Rapid City, SD.

Mark was born on July 25, 1957 in Minot, ND to Fred and Gladys "Maggie" (Mattson) Kjellerson. Mark had polio when he was a baby, but it didn't hold him back…he thrived!

He married his "Jewel", Julie K. Sauter, on October 12, 1991 at Harvey, ND. This union was blessed with two sons, Jacob and Joshua, who he was so very proud of! He was beyond grateful for the support of his family.

Mark held several jobs as a teenager and young man, and then got a job as a civilian at Minot Air Force Base. This began a 35-year-long civil service career with the Air Force in Minot, ND, Cheyenne, WY, Rapid City, SD, and Mildenhall, England. During this time, he was recognized with many awards, and at Mildenhall, his office was named the best education office in the European Command. In 1998, Mark started the Black Hills Regional Job Fair.

Mark's secondary education culminated in his doctorate in Adult and Higher Education from the University of South Dakota in 2009. Mark was an adjunct instructor at National American University, South Dakota School of Mines and Technology, and Western Dakota Technical College.

Mark loved traveling and seeing new places, woodworking, riding his 3-wheel Can-Am Spyder, teaching college classes, and especially going out camping in the Black Hills with his family.

Mark was avidly involved in Boy Scouts of America, becoming an Eagle Scout himself, and leading both his sons as well to become Eagle Scouts. He was a member of the Jaycees in Williston and Minot, ND and received the Outstanding Jaycee of the Year Award for North Dakota and the ND Jaycee Roughrider award, plus he represented the ND Jaycees at the Jerry Lewis Labor Day Telethon. Mark was a member of the Zion Lutheran Church, School, and Preschool in Rapid City, SD.

Grateful for sharing his life are his wife, Julie; sons: Jacob and Joshua; his brothers: Jim (Karen), Craig (Vicki), and Matthew (Lucy); and many, many extended family and friends.

He was preceded in death by his parents, Fred and Gladys (Mattson) Kjellerson, and by his grandparents.

Mark left us with two important gifts. First, he left us a good example of how to live life abundantly in spite of challenges. His optimism, courage, enthusiasm, values, and sense of humor will always be with us. And second, he left us with the knowledge that we can see him again. "We do not grieve like those who have no hope." I Thessalonians 4:13 We can just imagine him running into the arms of Jesus for a big hug!

"But those who wait on the Lord shall renew their strength;

They shall mount up with wings like eagles.

They shall run and not be weary,

They shall walk and not faint."

Isaiah 40:31

Funeral Services will be at 10:30 a.m. Saturday March 28, 2026 at Zion Lutheran Church. Visitation, also at the church, will be Friday evening from 6:00 p.m. to 8:00 p.m. Interment will be at Pine Lawn Memorial Park.

Kirk Funeral Home is assisting the family with arrangements.
