title: Updated: Coyote Flats wildfire at 50% containment; has destroyed 3 structures, but no homes
description: ROCKERVILLE — The leader of the effort against the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville said no one's been injured in the blaze, but multiple structures have been destroyed. Thankfully, incident commander Tom Roerick said, no homes have been destroyed.
_full_text:
ROCKERVILLE — The leader of the effort against the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville said no one's been injured in the blaze, but multiple structures have been destroyed. Thankfully, incident commander Tom Roerick said, no homes have been destroyed.

 5 wildfire terms to know as fires burn in the Black Hills
The Qury fire near Custer has swelled to over 9,000 acres since it ignited Thursday, March 12. The Coyote Flats fire, near Rockerville, is about 385 acres.

"Right now, we (have) three structures lost," Roerick told media on Thursday, March 19, outside of Rockerville Fire Station 2. "All of those were outbuildings... Not part of a primary residence."

 Blue heeler
Two women and a blue heeler inspect the fallout of the Coyote Flats wildfire on Thursday, March 19, outside Rockerville. 

Roerick said some other properties were damaged. An undetermined number of wildlife were affected by the fire.

It started on Wednesday, March 18, in the evening, about 2 miles southeast of Rockerville. The fireline surrounds Station 2. The cause remains under investigation, Roerick said. The fire was about 385 acres as of Thursday, March 19.

Roerick hesitated to call a containment percentage earlier in the day on Thursday because "there's so much heat and fire activity around the perimeter." Around 4:20 p.m., however, officials declared 50% containment on the fire.

 "We have the forward progress stopped, we've got good line all around the fire that we're confident in being able to hold today," Roerick said earlier in the day.

He explained containment isn't called until, "it's really good and cold ... you can put your hand in there and feel there are no hot spots. There's no smoke showing, folks have gone through and made sure there's no hot embers hiding in that grass or duff layer, there's no possibility of anything rekindling."

 Fire
The fire started on Wednesday, March 18, in the evening, about 2 miles southeast of Rockerville. The cause remains under investigation. 

Crews continue mop up operations along the fire’s perimeter and around private residences.

Personnel, engines and bulldozers are on scene, while aircraft were on standby as of around 10 a.m. The interagency team handling the fire said later in the day, "Air attack will be overhead by midday. One Type 3 helicopter and two National Guard Blackhawks are currently supporting the nearby Qury Fire in Custer but are available to the Coyote Flats Fire if needed."

 Fire
The fireline abuts a home off Neck Yoke Road near Rockerville. 

"Huge team effort, we have great interagency cooperation," said Roerick, who's with the U.S. Forest Service's Mystic Ranger District in Rapid City. "We have a great community of firefighters here in the Black Hills who are always willing to step up. It was a huge effort and a great catch by everybody involved yesterday."

Thursday, March 19, brings a Red Flag Warning from 11 a.m. to 7 p.m., with warm weather, windy and low humidity bringing high fire danger, according to the National Weather Service. The minimum relative humidity is 13% and winds 10-15 mph with gusts of 20 to 25 mph are possible, fire officials said.

 Surrounded
The leader of the effort against the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville said no one's been injured in the blaze, but multiple structures have been destroyed. Thankfully, incident commander Tom Roerick said, no homes have been destroyed. 

"The weather is definitely a concern for us," Roerick said. "We're in a prolonged dry spell here, we haven't had a whole lot of moisture. With this really warm weather and wind, it dries this grass out. It's very dry, very receptive to fire."

Roerick expressed confidence that fire crews in the area have enough resources to battle both the Coyote Flats fire and the Qury wildfire near Custer.

In an social media post, officials said  fire crews are well supported with food and drinks at this time. Those wishing to assist are encouraged to coordinate donations through their local fire departments.

The post concluded with, "Thank you to our community for your continued support."

 Tom Roerick
"The weather is definitely a concern for us," incident commander Tom Roerick said. "We're in a prolonged dry spell here, we haven't had a whole lot of moisture. With this really warm weather and wind, it dries this grass out. It's very dry, very receptive to fire." 

 47 photos from the Coyote Flats fire outside Rapid City
Crews continued to work on the Coyote Flats wildfire, which started on Wednesday, on Thursday, March 19.

title: Incident commander 'fairly confident' Qury wildfire acreage won't increase
description: CUSTER — It's been a week since the Qury wildfire started outside Custer on a warm and windy day. Since then, the fire's footprint has increased to 9,161 acres, over 14 square miles. Containment is at 44%. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.
_full_text:
CUSTER — It's been a week since the Qury wildfire started outside Custer on a warm and windy day. Since then, the fire's footprint has increased to 9,161 acres, over 14 square miles. Containment is at 44%. The cause of the fire is still under investigation.

But, at a press briefing on Thursday, March 19, Todd Hoover, the fire's incident commander, said, "We're feeling fairly confident now that we have the final acreage of the fire."

 5 wildfire terms to know as fires burn in the Black Hills
The Qury fire near Custer has swelled to over 9,000 acres since it ignited Thursday, March 12. The Coyote Flats fire, near Rockerville, is about 385 acres.

There are still a few more days of "critical fire weather coming up" as Red Flag Warnings pop up across western South Dakota. Those warnings will be a "great test" for the fire to make sure that the control lines keep Qury from spreading. Firefighters will continue to work on the containment lines, ensuring they are "good and solid," he said, as well as monitoring the structures within the fire lines to protect them from damage.

 Augusta Hotshots
The Augusta Interagency Hotshot Crew travelled across state lines to help fight the Qury fire. 

The firefighters are working the blaze 24/7, Hoover said, with the primary work being done during the day. Day shift is from roughly 6 a.m. until 8 p.m. and night shift is from 7 p.m. to 9 a.m., allowing for an overlap between shifts. 

"We do try to do things, little things, for the firefighters, to try to keep them warm and help with their morale. That could be something as simple as a hot cup of coffee or, you know, maybe feeding them a little extra snack or something like that," Hoover said.

The community has rallied to help support evacuees and firefighters, from churches providing shelter to community members providing food and supplies, like toothbrushes, to ranchers providing space for displaced animals. 

It could be a long, smoky summer

Hoover said the conditions that led to the Qury fire started last fall.

"The precipitation over western South Dakota has shut off, as I like to say. We had a snow drought. There has been very minimal snow over the winter that has allowed the old, decadent fuels to dry out. And so we are setting record levels and fuel dryness right now for this time of the year," Hoover said. "And so all those factors are contributing to the fire spread."

 Todd Hoover
Todd Hoover, incident commander on the Qury fire, speaks with press at a Thursday, March 19, briefing.  

The lack of moisture hasn't allowed for any green up, meaning everything is "available to burn."

When asked what that means for this summer, Hoover said, "We need to get some rain." 

In the next few weeks, temperatures are forecast to be warmer than normal, with lower-than-normal precipitation, during a time of year that's normally the wettest. 

"If we do not start picking up significant precipitation here, we could be in for a very long fire season here in the Black Hills and the entire western United States," Hoover said.

 Burnout operation 2
A torch is used to light fire to fuel in burnout operations. Burnouts create firelines to stop uncontrolled spread of a wildfire. 

Because of the forecasted fire-friendly conditions, Hoover plans to keep some of the resources that have gathered to fight the Qury fire around to boost the capacity of the local area, at least through the weekend.

"We're not going to release a bunch of folks on a whim," he said.

The National Weather Service is forecasting temperatures in Custer to reach the mid-70s on Friday, the low-80s on Saturday, and the mid-50s on Sunday. There is a slight chance of showers on Saturday night, but it will take more than a sprinkle to protect the hills from fire.

The burros are safe

Easing some fears, Lydia Austin, visitor services supervisor at Custer State Park, said the park's begging burros are safe and sound. The Qury encroached into the western side of the park, up to Mount Coolidge, but the park is open. That includes the Wildlife Loop Road and the visitor centers.

The herds manager and the resource team moved the buffalo and the burros to the southeast corner of the park. 

"We closed the fence and gates behind them so they are in essence locked into the southeast corner park. The reason we did that is twofold. We wanted to keep them out of the fire if it did continue to make its run. And then second, we did burn about three miles of fence on our western boundary, and want to keep the wildlife within Custer State Park," Austin said.

Despite the fire, the park is ready for visitors, Austin said. There is one closure in the park, Highway 87 South is closed, as are some of the trails in the area.

 Lydia Austin
Lydia Austin, visitor services supervisor at Custer State Park, at a Thursday, March 19, press conference said the park is open.  

What's open includes Iron Mountain Road and Needles Highway. Austin urged park visitors not to cancel their camping reservations. If they have questions, they can call the park at 605-394-2693 or check in on the park's Facebook page at www.facebook.com/CusterStatePark.

"This park has weathered storms before. She's weathered fires, droughts, wind storms, and she's still a beautiful place. So definitely come visit," Austin said.

Current Road Closures:

• Highway 87 South: From the Highway 16A junction to Blue Bell Lodge.

• Lower French Creek Road

• Stockade Lake Drive and Stockade Lake Day Use Area

Trail and Area Closures:

• Centennial Trail

• Heady Draw Trail

• Stockade Lake Trail Area

Highway 16A remains open for travel through the park. The Park Office and Visitor Center are also open during normal operating hours.

 Photos from the Qury wildfire as work continues near Custer
Late Thursday morning, March 19, the Qury fire outside Custer sat at 9,161 acres burned and 44% containment. The Qury fire started a week ago and the cause is undetermined.

title: Reclaiming the Lakota language: South Dakota woman weaves indigenous culture, history into Nebraska classes
description: SCOTTSBLUFF, Nebraska — Growing up, Beverly Running Bear remembers her parents and grandparents speaking a language that she was forbidden to use.
_full_text:
SCOTTSBLUFF, Nebraska — Growing up, Beverly Running Bear remembers her parents and grandparents speaking a language that she was forbidden to use.

 Free Lakota language classes in Scottsbluff weave in Indigenous culture and history
Beverly Running Bear reviews a lesson on how to ask and answer the greeting, "Good day, how are you?" in Lakota, at her community language class at Scottsbluff High School on March 12. 

“I love my culture and my language,” Running Bear said. “Lakota language was my first language until I went to boarding school. We were forbidden to talk about our culture or use our language, and so we lost them. My generation and older really lost a lot of that.”

Running Bear, Oglala Lakota, attended boarding school in the 1960s, where she was harshly prohibited from speaking her native tongue. Even after leaving the school, her memory of the Lakota language faded gradually over several decades, until the harrowing realization that it had slipped away entirely.

“I lost it for a minute there, but it was in my early 40s, when I went back to school,” Running Bear said. “I started relearning when I took the language courses, and that's where I learned to read and write. … I think that was healing for me.”

Realizing what she had nearly lost, Running Bear busied herself with studying and recapturing her instincts for the Lakota language. Some time later, after graduating with degrees in business and American Indian Studies, she began teaching the language to others.

“I had a great friend who was also a professor at Black Hills State, and she had approached me about teaching the Lakota language class, and I kind of balked at it,” Running Bear said. “Unfortunately, (she) passed away, and upon that happening, a group of Lakota elders got together and said that I was to carry on her legacy. And with that, I have to obey my elders, so I started teaching.”

Running Bear has been teaching the Lakota language at Black Hills State University in Spearfish, South Dakota, for about a decade now, but she said her commitment to preserving and sharing the language goes further.

In partnership with Empowering Families, Running Bear has been traveling to Scottsbluff each year for about five years, teaching free community classes on the basics of the Lakota language.

“Wherever it takes me, I'll go,” Running Bear said.

Running Bear said she teaches more than vocabulary and grammar. With each lesson, she weaves stories of the people who spoke the language, so students can understand it more fully.

“You need to understand the culture because it teaches you how the people interact with each other; our language is a descriptive language,” Running Bear said. “Because if you go in there and just teach the language itself, you have really no clue of where it's coming from.

 Free Lakota language classes in Scottsbluff weave in Indigenous culture and history
Beverly Running Bear teaches a lesson on how to refer to oneself and others in Lakota during her class at Scottsbluff High School on March 12. 

“People need to understand and know how the language was taken,” she continued. “I would select a video of past boarding schools that shows how the children were restricted and what kind of cruelty was bestowed upon them; punishment for speaking the language. And then, they have an understanding of how we almost lost the language.”

Running Bear says that this method of instruction has proved useful to both Indigenous and non-Indigenous students alike.

“Even native people aren't aware of some of this history,” Running Bear said. “And so, that's an eye-opener for them. That makes me feel good that they know that now.”

 Free Lakota language classes in Scottsbluff weave in Indigenous culture and history
Members of the community Lakota language repeat after the instructor and take notes during the class at Scottsbluff High School on March 12. 

Running Bear said the goal of the class isn’t necessarily for students to become fluent in the Lakota language — she openly admits she isn't fluent herself. Sometimes, she said, exposure to the basics of the language and its cultural context can be enough for students to venture out on their own to seek further information.

“To be fluent, you have to constantly speak the language, which isn't available for them at home,” Running Bear said. “This is just the tip of the iceberg … if you choose to continue to really want to speak it, you'll reach out to elders who are always speaking the language. Like in our Scottsbluff group, there's some elders who know the language, and this is really helping them to open up, and I just love that.”

Looking ahead, Running Bear said she is willing to go wherever she is needed to teach the Lakota language, culture and history for years to come.

 Free Lakota language classes in Scottsbluff weave in Indigenous culture and history
A member of the community Lakota language class flips through her papers to find the correct reference sheet during the class at Scottsbluff High School on March 12. 

“Somebody asked me if I plan on retiring. I said, ‘What's that?’” Running Bear said, laughing. “(If I were to retire), there's many young people out there who are really adamant about relearning the language. (They) are in line, so to speak. … They enjoy learning it, then that makes me happy, because that's the intent: To get the word out there, and have an understanding.”

This year, Running Bear's classes are running each Thursday in March, April and the first two weeks of May. More information can be found at the Empowering Families online events calendar, empoweringfamiliesne.org/events.

title: Evacuation order lifted for Coyote Wildfire near Rockerville
byline: MADISON WILLIS and SHALOM BAER GEE Journal staff
description: Officials have lifted an evacuation order related to the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville.
_full_text:
Officials have lifted an evacuation order related to the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville.

The Coyote Flats wildfire is now estimated at 385 acres and is 0% contained, officials said in an update on Thursday, March 19. Firefighters staffed the fire overnight, providing structure protection. The U.S. Forest Service has jurisdiction.

 032026-nws-flats-p3.jpg

The fire's about 2 miles southeast of Rockerville. The cause remains under investigation.

It's burning on federal and private lands, officials said during a 6 p.m. press conference on Wednesday.

In its Thursday update, the interagency team handling the fire said, "Objectives today include working the fire’s edge, mapping the fire’s footprint, mopping up around structures, and being prepared to respond to potential new incidents as today is another critical fire weather day. Federal, state, and local resources including personnel and equipment are working together to meet those objectives.

"Air attack will be overhead by midday. One Type 3 helicopter and two National Guard Blackhawks are currently supporting the nearby Qury Fire in Custer but are available to the Coyote Flats Fire if needed."

 032026-nws-flats-p2.jpg

Thursday, March 19, brings a Red Flag Warning from 11 a.m. to 7 p.m., with warm weather, windy and low humidity bringing high fire danger, according to the National Weather Service. 

The Rockerville Volunteer Fire Department said Thursday morning, "There is heavy smoke in the area of the Coyote Flats Fire this morning. As you head off to school and work, PLEASE drive slowly and safely through the area. Heavy fire traffic will exist on Neck Yoke Rd between Coyote Flats and Pine Grove Rd throughout the day."

 032026-nws-flats-p1.jpg
Fire crews worked overnight Wednesday, March 18, into Thursday, March 19, on the Coyote Flats wildfire. 

 Photos from the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville
Crews are on scene at the Coyote Flats wildfire near Rockerville, battling it by ground and air.

title: Buy a sandwich, support Special Olympics on March 25
description: Jersey Mike’s Subs in Rapid City is raising money to send athletes to the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games.
_full_text:
Jersey Mike’s Subs in Rapid City is raising money to send athletes to the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games.

Jersey Mike's Subs nationwide are participating in this fundraiser to support Special Olympics.

On Wednesday, March 25, Jersey Mike Subs will donate 100% of sales to help 43 Team South Dakota athletes attend the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games in Minneapolis-St. Paul from June 20 to 26. All Jersey Mike’s purchases in store, online or on the app will benefit the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games and the state programs attending the event.

Nationwide, Jersey Mike’s hopes to raise enough money to help every qualifying athlete across the country attend the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games.

 Jersey Mike's fundraiser
Jersey Mike's Subs in Rapid City and nationwide will donate all sales on March 25 to Special Olympics.  

Throughout the month of March, which is Jersey Mike’s 16th annual Month of Giving, customers can also support Special Olympics by rounding up the cost of their orders and making donations at Jersey Mike’s.

The 2026 Special Olympics USA Games 2026 will bring together 3,000 athletes, 1,500 coaches, 10,000 volunteers, and 75,000 fans from all 50 states. Athletes will compete in 16 sports including athletics, gymnastics, swimming, and basketball. New this year, pickleball and cornhole are joining the sports lineup at Special Olympics.

In Rapid City, Jersey Mike’s Subs is located at 1745 Eglin St., Suite 120. According to the Jersey Mike’s website, a second Rapid City location will be opening at 855 Omaha St. No date for the opening of the second location has been announced.

title: Mines raises over $50 million for scholarships to help remove financial barriers for students
description: More than $50 million in scholarships raised through the recently completed New Heights Campaign at South Dakota Mines is helping remove financial barriers and open doors for current and future students pursuing careers in STEM fields.
_full_text:
More than $50 million in scholarships raised through the recently completed New Heights Campaign at South Dakota Mines is helping remove financial barriers and open doors for current and future students pursuing careers in STEM fields.

Last month, Mines’ Center for Alumni Relations and Advancement (CARA) announced it had surpassed its original $100 million goal, raising more than $120 million, the most successful capital campaign in university history. A significant portion of the funds raised, more than $50 million, is dedicated to scholarships, ensuring talented students can focus on learning rather than the financial burden of paying for college.

“Scholarships were at the heart of the New Heights Campaign. Raising more than $50 million for student support reflects what our donors care about most — making a Mines education accessible to students who will go on to solve the world's hardest problems,” said Marc Vaillancourt, CARA’s chief executive officer. “The generosity of the Hardrocker community is extraordinary, and we're grateful. Scholarship demand at Mines continues to grow, and so does our commitment to meeting it."

For Cindy Eng, a freshman geology major, scholarships have already made a difference.

 Cindy Eng
More than $50 million in scholarships raised through the recently completed New Heights Campaign at South Dakota Mines is helping remove financial barriers and open doors for current and future students, including Cindy Eng, freshman geology major from Blaine, Minnesota. 

Eng, who grew up in Blaine, Minnesota, said she was drawn to Mines for its location in the Black Hills and its hands-on learning opportunities. While she didn't realize geology could be a career path until high school, her fascination with earth science began much earlier. “I remember reading an assignment about Yellowstone and thinking, ‘Wow, this is amazing.’”

At Mines, Eng has already taken part in hands-on field experiences, including a trip last semester to Wind Cave National Park, where students spent a few days camping and studying the area's unique geological formations.

In addition to her studies, Eng also participates as a percussionist in the university’s music program and recently performed with the Black Hills Symphony, playing student-engineered instruments.

Scholarship support has allowed her to fully embrace these opportunities.

“These scholarships help me focus on my studies instead of worrying about how I’m going to pay for school,” said Eng, who received both academic and department scholarship support. “Mines is a challenging school and having that financial burden lifted means I can put my energy into learning and preparing for my future career,” she said. “I know there are a lot of people who struggle financially, have to work on top of school, and then if they fail a class, they have to pay for it again. With that burden off, I can focus on my education.”

She is already preparing for her next step. She will be spending the summer interning with the Bureau of Land Management in Montana.

Stories like Eng’s illustrate the impact of donor support.

“Many of our students come from middle-class families who have worked hard but can’t always make the finances work to attend South Dakota Mines,” said Molly Moore, associate vice president for enrollment management. “We often see students who want to be here but need that extra support to make it possible. Scholarships help bridge that gap and can truly be the difference between attending and not attending. “

By expanding scholarship support, the campaign strengthened the university’s ability to recruit and educate the next generation of engineers, scientists and innovators, graduates who will go on to power industries, advance research and technology and support communities around the world.

With scholarship demand continuing to grow, donor support remains essential to maintaining access to a Mines education.

“I would tell anyone thinking about donating that scholarships really do make a difference,” Eng said. “I believe it really does take the burden off of students by reducing the stress of finances so they can focus on their education.”

title: 'Helping our neighbors': Black Hills restaurant and inn owners assist during Coyote Flats fire
byline: MADISON WILLIS and SHALOM BAER GEE Journal staff
description: As the "GO NOW" orders crackled across Rockerville's Neck Yoke/Coyote Flats area the usual dinner rush at Shipwrecks Bar and Grill took on a desperate new meaning.
_full_text:
As the "GO NOW" orders crackled across Rockerville's Neck Yoke/Coyote Flats area the usual dinner rush at Shipwrecks Bar and Grill took on a desperate new meaning. 

 Shipwrecks owners
Owners Andrea and Adam Weber said they will be preparing their restaurant to feed firefighters about 150 meals per day until the Coyote Flats fire is resolved.  

While the Coyote Flats Fire swelled to 200 acres nearby on Wednesday, March 18, owners of the restaurant and the adjacent Sweetgrass Inn Bed and Breakfast, Adam and Andrea Weber, raced to contact vendors and prepare their kitchen to feed firefighters who will be battling the flames, while also preparing any guest suites for families fleeing the encroaching flames. 

"If we're not helping out our fellow man, then what are we doing here?" Adam Weber told the Journal. "We're here for each other, we're all one species. All these guys, the firefighters, they're putting their lives on the line. Why can't we feed them?"

 031926-nws-rockervillefire-p7.JPG
People pulled off along US Highway 16 to watch the fire. 

Their establishments are about 5 miles from the fire and as the flames progressed throughout the afternoon, the Webers began to notice a swell of their friends and neighbors coming to the restaurant after not being able to reach their homes.

Though the approaching dinner rush wasn't unusual for them, Andrea said she knew something was wrong as soon as she saw smoke out in the horizon from the porch of their house earlier in the day. 

According to the Facebook page Coyote Flats Fire 2026:

Neck Yoke Road is closed in both directions:

• East side: Spring Creek to Neck Yoke Junction

• West side: South Rockerville Road to Neck Yoke

"Those will be (closed) until we get a better assessment on the status of the fire," Jason Mitzel with the Pennington County Sheriff's Office said during the press conference.

At the media availability, Brandon Sanchez, duty officer at the Mystic Ranger District with the U.S. Forest Service, said numerous engines are on scene, in addition to three National Guard helicopters, two air tankers, on air attack plane and one lead plane "over the fire as we speak."

The Webers were contacted by local emergency personnel shortly after their restaurant began to swell with families waiting to go home. Officials ask the restaurant to be on standby to prepare about 150 meals per day for the firefighters at the scene.

Though the Webers would be compensated for their efforts, the married couple said they never thought twice about helping their community. 

"We need to be there for our first responders and make sure that they are well taken care of because they are taking care of us," Adam said. "And that's what creates that ultimate sense of community is everyone coming together in times of need. No matter what, doing whatever it takes to be there for your fellow human."

For Andrea their mission was deeply personal; as she prepares guests rooms for the displaced, she is also bracing to welcome her own family, who are among those waiting to learn if their homes will still be standing when the smoke clears.

 Smoke
Smoke from the Coyote Flats fire rises above trees in the area, as seen from Highway 16.  

"They're all of our neighbors as well," Andrea said. "The whole community that's sitting out in our parking lot right now, they don't know if they have a house to go back to yet. So if they want to come in and have some dinner or whatever, we're happy to feed them. If we can offer them peace of mind through this challenging time, we'll do everything we can do to help." 

Area resident Aaron Skramstad said he lives on Jill Road and received a notice to evacuate around 1:45 p.m. on Wednesday. He was at work in Rapid City at the time and tried to go home to get his dog, Snoop, but was turned around by Pennington County Sheriff's Office personnel about a mile from his house.

 Skramstad
Aaron Skramstad, foreground, stands with his girlfriend and watches the fire that broke out in Rockerville. Skramstad lives on Jill Road and received a notice to evacuate around 1:45 p.m. on Wednesday, March 18. He was at work in Rapid City at the time and tried to go home to get his dog, Snoop, but was turned around by Pennington County Sheriff's Office personnel about a mile from his house. "I am worried," he said. "But the way the wind's blowing, I think it's OK." 

"I am worried," he said. "But the way the wind's blowing, I think it's OK."

 Helicopter
A helicopter carries a load of water to pour on the Coyote Flats wildfire. 

Coverage of the Coyote Flats Fire 

The fire is estimated at 200 acres, according to fire officials. It's burning on federal and private lands. During the press conference, officials said they'd issue an update later on whether there are injuries or structural damage related to the fire.

Jim Gubbels, district ranger at the Mystic Ranger District in Rapid City, called firefighters' work a "heck of a good effort this afternoon to keep the fire hemmed in at a couple hundred acres and minimize damage to a lot of private property."

"Just a tremendous effort out there today, and we're getting a handle on this thing," he said. "We should be able to really mop things up tomorrow (Thursday), and hopefully the weather cooperates, and we keep it at a couple hundred acres."

Areas to the south of the current evacuation area, shown in the map, are instructed to get ready to evacuate, according to Pennington County Emergency Management.

 go now map.jpg
From Pennington County Emergency Management: Anyone in the GO NOW (in red) area should leave NOW. Travel away from smoke and fire. Be aware of emergency vehicles and firefighters in the area. Get SET to GO - Areas to the south of the current GO NOW area, as depicted on the map above, have been placed in a Get SET to GO status. 

A Red Cross Reception Center has been established at 8722 S. Highway 16 (Lil' Harvesters Pumpkin Patch at Harvest Acres Venues), across Highway 16 from Reptile Gardens, officials said.

"I want to say thank you to all the resources," said Jay Wickham, director of South Dakota Wildland Fire. "A lot of people don't understand what goes into maybe a fire this size, the interagency cooperation between the volunteers, state, (U.S.) Forest Service, local sheriff and local volunteers. It's critical for us to be able to be safe and to be successful on a fire like this."
Local residents with livestock concerns can speak with law enforcement at the two road closure locations. If safe, a law enforcement escort will be provided to retrieve livestock from a known and specific location. Livestock can be sheltered at the Central States Fairgrounds.

 Coyote Flats wildfire
A look from afar at the Coyote Flats wildfire.

title: Two weeks for youth to submit art for 150th anniversary contest
description: Local youth are reminded of the upcoming deadline to submit art works as part of Rapid City’s 150th anniversary.
_full_text:
Local youth are reminded of the upcoming deadline to submit art works as part of Rapid City’s 150th anniversary.

The theme for the City’s Youth Art Contest is ‘My Favorite Thing About the Community I Live In’. Young artists are encouraged to let their creative side shine through the art contest. Contest age groups are 6-10, 11-14 and 15-18. Entries will be displayed in the lobby of City Hall in Rapid City and winning entries will be included in the 2076 time capsule to be buried in Founders Park in May.

Deadline for submission of entries is March 31. The public will be asked to vote on the contest entries April 6-30. The contest is open to Rapid City public, private and home school students. The City is partnering with the Rapid City School District, encouraging students to submit entries to their art teachers by the deadline. Private and home school students are encouraged to contact the Rapid City Mayor’s Office for contest materials.

For more information, contact Buchanan in the Rapid City Mayor’s Office at 605-394-4110.

title: William Jeffrey Shroll
description: February 19, 1958 - March 11, 2026
_full_text:
February 19, 1958 - March 11, 2026

William Jeffrey (Bill) Shroll (68) passed away on March 11, 2026 at Monument Hospital in Rapid City, S.D.

Services will be at Osheim Schmidt Funeral Home with Visitation on March 22 from 4:00-6:00 pm and Funeral on March 23, at 10:30. Burial will follow immediately at Mountain View Cemetery.

Bill Shroll was born in Huron, South Dakota on February 19, 1958 to Bernell and Audrey Shroll. He married Linda (Shreeve) Shroll in 1980. Bill graduated from Stevens High School and then earned his BA and Master's in Secondary Education with a Specialist Degree in School Administration. He served as teacher, coach, principal and superintendent at Crow Creek Tribal Schools. Bill stated this was when he was happiest and where he met his friend and mentor, Cody Russell, whom he never forgot. Bill always treated everyone with incredible kindness and respect. During his retirement years he volunteered as a Foster Grandparent. He spoke often of the friendship and support he received and gave to his many friends at West Park Apartments, his last residence.

Bill was an avid sports fan and could recite stats for every player and team in pro basketball and football. He had an amazing memory and was witty with a wry sense of humor. Bill struggled with addiction and mental illness. He was a member of AA and NAMI and tried to reduce the stigma attached to these illnesses.

He is survived by his partner, Linda Shroll; his children Amie (Shaun) Skinner, James (Marcy) Shroll and Dylan (Carrie) Shroll, the three of which he says are his greatest success; his sisters, Victoria Leonard and Cynthia (Craig) Angel. Grandchildren: Brandon (Kassandra) Williams, Kaylee Williams, Logan Williams, Jaden Shroll, Jimmy Shroll, Jonah Shroll and Jude Shroll and great grandson, Russell Williams. Bill's children and grandchildren were his greatest joy and source of pride. He loved being with them.

Bill's final act was to donate his organs. He really was an incredible man. We will always remember him for his kindness, his respect, his support and unconditional love for his family. We will never forget him.

title: Joyce Richardson
description: March 12, 2026
_full_text:
March 12, 2026

RAPID CITY - Joyce Richardson, 71, died 3/12/2026 Funeral Services: 12:30PM Mon., 3/23 at Kirk Funeral Home. Visitation: Sunday from 3PM to 6PM at Kirk Funeral Home. Kirk Funeral Home is assisting the family with arrangements.

title: Qury fire update: Fire covers more than 9,000 acres, weather continues to present a challenge
description: The Qury wildfire near Custer has ticked above 9,000 acres, with officials putting it at 9,059 acres in a Wednesday, March 18, morning update. The fire is 27% contained.
_full_text:
The Qury wildfire near Custer has ticked above 9,000 acres, with officials putting it at 9,059 acres in a Wednesday, March 18, morning update. The fire is 27% contained.

The Qury fire, which started Thursday afternoon, March 12, is located about 2 miles southeast of Custer. 

The interagency team battling the fire said containment lines held Tuesday and overnight despite intense wind conditions around the entire perimeter of the fire. On Wednesday, firefighters continue to strengthen lines through mop-up operations. Interior pockets of unburned fuel will continue to burn, creating a safer black edge. Firefighters will monitor lines and burning fuels to ensure they hold, the team said. Crews have started rehabilitating identified dozer lines that were put in during firefighting operations.

 pio_8x11_land_20260317_1939_Qury_SDSDS260142_0318day.jpg
A map provided by South Dakota Wildland Fire on Wednesday, March 18, of the Qury wildfire. 

Critical fire weather began today at 10 a.m., as the temperature was around 70, the team said. This will bring the potential for more intense fire activity than the last few days. Smoke and flames may be visible on the interior of the fire as larger fuels burn to completion. Firefighters are confident the work completed over the last few days will continue to hold throughout these critical weather conditions.

"These anomalously warm temperatures coupled with gusty west-northwest winds (gusts 30 to 40 mph) and relative humidity falling to around 15% will result in critical fire weather conditions," the National Weather Service office in Rapid City said in a Red Flag Warning.

 031926-nws-fireupdate-p3.jpg
The Qury wildfire near Custer has ticked above 9,000 acres, with officials putting it at 9,059 acres in a Wednesday, March 18, morning update. The fire is 27% contained. 

The Red Flag warning affects northeast Wyoming and southwest South Dakota, with the northern portion of the warning area including Custer.

The team noted the warming trend is expected to continue throughout the week with windy conditions and lower relative humidity, the team noted. The weather service forecast predicts highs in the 70s through Saturday before a cool down to a high of around 49 on Sunday, March 22.

Additional information from the Wednesday morning update

The team said about 350 personnel are working on the fire. At a community meeting in Custer on Saturday, March 14, incident commander Todd Hoover said they've brought firefighters from as far as Virginia, Oregon and New Mexico.

Public Closures: Lower French Creek Road from Highway 16A to Highway 87, Hazelrodt, Glen Erin, Flynn Creek, Herbert Draw, Highway 87 from 16A to the north boundary of Wind Cave National Park (the park remains open).

 031926-nws-fireupdate-p2.jpg
A warming trend in the area is expected to continue throughout the week with windy conditions and lower relative humidity, which heightens fire risk. 

Residential reopenings: Repopulation efforts have happened across the entire fire. Access is for residential traffic only. Residents must have the Orange Card issued by the Custer County Emergency Management. Visit Custer County Sheriff for most up-to-date information on road closures www.facebook.com/custercountysd.

Temporary Flight Restrictions: There is a Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) over the perimeter of the Qury Fire. "Please do not fly any unauthorized aircraft over the fire. If you fly, we can’t."

Portion of Black Hills National Forest closed

The U.S. Forest Service said Tuesday afternoon it has closed portions of Black Hills National Forest because of the Qury fire.

 5 wildfire terms to know as fires burn in the Black Hills
The Qury fire near Custer has swelled to over 9,000 acres since it ignited Thursday, March 12. The Coyote Flats fire, near Rockerville, is about 385 acres.

The closure order, which the Forest Service said is for public and firefighter safety, is in effect March 17 to April 16, "unless modified or rescinded sooner." Closed areas include Forest Service lands contained within an area west of the intersection of U.S. Highway 385 and U.S. Highway 16A to the boundary of Custer State Park, and south of the intersection of U.S. 385 and U.S. Highway 16A to the boundary of Wind Cave National Park.

 031926-nws-fireupdate-p4 qury.jpg
The Qury fire, which started Thursday afternoon, March 12, is located about 2 miles southeast of Custer. 

The U.S. Forest Service said exemptions to this closure include residents and landowners with a RapidTag ID issued by Custer County to access their private properties, persons with a permit for use of roads, trails, or areas specifically exempting them from this order or a written determination by a Forest Service authorized officer, and any federal, state, or local officer, or member of an organized rescue or firefighting force performing official duties.

 Black Hills National Forest
A Black Hills National Forest map, released Tuesday, March 17, showing closures enforced because of the Qury fire near Custer. 

 Qury fire
A brief video from the Qury wildfire near Custer, South Dakota.

title: Rapid City population continues to climb
description: Rapid City's 2025 population estimate is in. As expected, it's up, although not at the same rate as last time.
_full_text:
Rapid City's 2025 population estimate is in. As expected, it's up, although not at the same rate as last time. 

City officials estimate Rapid City’s population at the end of 2025 was at 86,484, compared to 84,930 in 2024 and 82,418 in 2023.

The 2024 to 2025 increase is 1.83%. From 2023 to 2024, the estimated population grew by 3.05%, the second-largest percentage increase in Rapid City since 2000, surpassed only by the 3.14% increase from the 2021 to 2022.

 Rapid City east view
A view of Rapid City looking east. The east side of the city has seen growth in recent years with new neighborhoods and businesses popping up.  

The estimate, released on March 31, comes from the city's Long Range Planning Division. Each year, the Division prepares a population estimate based on approved building permits, housing demolitions, the 2020 Census occupancy rate and the 2020 Census persons per household rate for Rapid City and Pennington County. The methodology is approved by the United States Census Bureau.

“The data reflects continued increases in population for our community,” said City Long Range Planner Kelly Brennan in a statement. “We base these projections on the available data. The estimates are important as the information assists City leaders and department staff as we make plans for the future. The information is helpful in determining projected growth as it relates to infrastructure, community planning, transportation, housing and other areas in terms of policies, guidelines and planning.”

title: 'This is our food': A hands-on buffalo harvest brings together Pine Ridge community
description: On the cool Friday morning of March 27, at the break of dawn, a rifle cracks against the still wind. In the distance, two bruting buffalo standing over 200 yards away hunch and fall over from the impact of rifle rounds.
_full_text:
On the cool Friday morning of March 27, at the break of dawn, a rifle cracks against the still wind. In the distance, two bruting buffalo standing over 200 yards away hunch and fall over from the impact of rifle rounds. 

A group of Lakota men walked up to the Tatanka — buffalo in Lakota — and began to pray, thanking them and honoring their lives as the animals transitioned from this world into the next.

"We are preserved because we have that relationship, and their sacrifice of life betters our life understanding, because that's the relationship we made a millennium in our historical relationship," Makoce Agriculture Development CEO Nick Hernandez (Lakota) said.

Arlo Iron Cloud (Lakota), a community member experienced in buffalo harvesting, attended the event.

Iron Cloud praised Hernandez's ability to shoot the buffalo with clean shots, as he said the buffalo is a relative that they wanted to make sure experienced as little trauma as possible.

"It was actually really an amazing thing. What my brother (Hernandez) did today, because when we were there, we had to take two of these guys down. That's always really difficult, because usually one takes off, and it's really dangerous," Iron Cloud said. "So Nick took the shot and he fell down ... it was a very special event."

 Makoce
Makoce members tending to the buffalo, as they get ready for a community harvest that welcomes all. 

Hernandez told the Journal the buffalo are central to the Oceti Sakowin (Seven Council Fires made up of the Lakota, Nakota and Dakota people), as they have thrived in much of the plains because of the buffalo. 

The point of this harvest, Hernandez said, is to both welcome the spring equinox, which marks the new year for the Lakota people, and to connect the community to the buffalo through the harvest and learning process.

"Buffalo have been the keystone species of the ecosystem, but for us, our relationship is every element," Hernandez said.

Hernandez is the founder of Makoce, meaning "homeland" in Lakota, a nonprofit working to return regenerative food systems rooted in Indigenous values, based on the Pine Ridge Reservation.

They do this through five initiatives: Food Systems Institute, Oceti Sakowin Food Alliance Systems, Regenerative Production Farms, Biobased Solutions and Food Hubs.

'This is a caretaker of the land'

The two buffalo harvested on March 27 came from Wounded Knee on the Black Feather Ranch. One was taken to the Makoce commercial kitchen in Porcupine, where it was greeted by members of the community. The other buffalo didn't belong to Makoce, so it was not a part of the community harvest.

"We do it a couple times a year as kind of a community thing. We get members of the community to come. It's kind of a forgotten art, how to butcher a buffalo just right there on the ground," Makoce Community Engagement Manager Jonathan Red Owl (Lakota) said. "It's one of the ways we try to reach the community to get back and say, 'This is our food.'"

Red Owl said they invite local schools to join and participate to get the younger generation hands-on experience with harvesting. They leave nothing to waste from the buffalo.

Hernandez and Red Owl said there are more than 100 uses from the buffalo body, such as clothing, blankets, teepees, tools, toys and roles in ceremony (using the skull in inipi, a Lakota purification ceremony).

Hernandez said this harvest is an initiative to bring the buffalo back to the people, as historically they had been targeted.

 Nick Hernandez
Hernandez speaks to participants before the buffalo is harvested in a group learning experience.   

In the 1850s, as Western expansion carried over to the Great Plains, the federal government had a goal to remove Native Americans to designated reservations. The buffalo were targeted as they were essential for food and cultural significance.

The “scorched earth” policy was utilized, giving military officials the license to kill as many buffalo as possible in the plains. These killings caused food scarcity for many tribes, including the Lakota, which then contributed to tribes seeking government support and signing treaties for the survival of their people.

According to the National Park Service, from 1820 to 1880, the population of bison dramatically decreased from 30-60 million to 1,000 in the 1890s.

"Killing the buffalo killed the Indigenous community, killed our food source for the Indigenous community. So, there was an act of genocide, but we're still here as Indigenous peoples. We're still here because we have that relationship to our most traditional foods," Hernandez said.

 Buffalo Harvest
A woman teaches the youth beginner safety precautions before cutting into the buffalo. 

Anyone from the community is invited to the harvest and can take meat with them. The rest of the body parts are given out as well, Hernandez said.

"The majority of us were taught was that this isn't just an animal that we go and harvest and eat. This is a caretaker of the land. The buffalo's natural instincts, the way that they kind of graze across the land, they take care of it. whereas if you had a bunch of head of cattle, they would just eat it until it turns to dirt," Red Owl said.

 Buffalo harvest
A child breaks down a buffalo leg during the Makoce-led harvest. 

Hernandez credits his grandmother for inspiring Makoce

Hernandez said the idea for Makoce came in 2007 from speaking with his grandmother, Roberta Iron Cloud, on possible efforts to help his community. He said she helped to inspire the nonprofit initiative.

After grabbing fruits from his grandmother's garden, Hernandez said, "I want to utilize food to be able to connect myself back to my community, back to my family, back to my culture ... I said, 'Hey, grandma, can you teach me these ways?' And she stood up and she said, 'Turn around grandson, look out here.' It was just an open field and she's like, 'You have access to 40 acres you can do with whatever you want.'"

He continued saying she told him, "'I can't actually teach you how to do the things that you want to do. You actually have to go out and do them,'" Hernandez said. "Those were her words to me. It was go out and learn these things and (she'll) be here to support."

He said since establishing Makoce in 2019, he now has 15 full-time staff working not just on a community level, but on a national level.

Pine Ridge is a food desert with little access to healthy, quality and sustainable foods, such as fruits, vegetables and even meats like poultry. Red Owl said they raise chickens and harvest them to sell to the community.

"That's why we raise chickens, because they're kind of economical. They grow in a short period of time, and they don't require a lot of space. Showing people that we can grow our own food. The USDA classifies us as a food desert, because 95% of the food is shipped onto the reservation," Red Owl said.

He said they are also looking to connect the local fruits and vegetables back to the community through growing buffalo berries, choke cherries and ground plums.

Food for construction 

Another venture Makoce is exploring pertains to meeting a need with housing on the reservation.

The 2018 Farm Bill featured a federal reclassification of hemp that legalized industrial hemp farming, which Hernandez said has allowed the tribe to regulate hemp farming. This allows Makoce to grow hemp on the reservation for use in materials for the construction of housing.

 Makoce
Founded in 2019, the nonprofit Makoce ("homeland" in Lakota) has been working toward developing a local food system to support a self-sufficient Oglala Lakota nation. 

"The biobased (initiative) derived from my passion from the interest of cannabis hemp plant. It revolutionized the world. During the world wars they used to raise hemp to be able to make fibers to build sails and clothes and the flags. But it's also it also has serves a number of industrial properties." Hernandez said. "So, for us, we want to be able to research those industrial properties."

He said the plant's fiber can be used in construction material to provide local sourcing for those looking for an affordable way to build a home on the reservation. 

Hernandez said Dionne Holmquist, the Biobased Solutions director, is currently working with the Lower Sioux Indian Reservation as they opened an industrial hemp facility for the use of hempcrete, a mixture of hemp, water and binder that's extracted from limestone. They are also using hemp in insulation for housing.

"We can build homes with hemp, and they're doing it over there. There's a housing shortage here on the reservation. I think that's something that is systematic," Red Owl said.

Hernandez also added the hemp plant can also be utilized in more than one manner, as it can be harvested for uses such as seed oil, and benefit the local soil ecology.

"Biobased is looking at how do we take an energy and transfer it into many, many uses," Hernandez said

title: Mines graduate student helps advance early detection research for Alzheimer’s disease
description: For Hunter Paxton, the path to cutting-edge Alzheimer’s research began long before he became a student at South Dakota Mines.
_full_text:
For Hunter Paxton, the path to cutting-edge Alzheimer’s research began long before he became a student at South Dakota Mines.

Raised in a family deeply connected to assisted living care, Paxton spent much of his childhood alongside his mother and grandmother, both facility managers, chatting up residents and building relationships. It was during those interactions that he also noticed the effects of cognitive decline.

“Some days they don’t remember you,” said Paxton, a Mines computer science and engineering graduate student. “It has become so personal for me to work on this project.”

 rotary_club_awards.jpg
Hunter Paxton with his grandmother, Gloria Kaiser, left, one of the original founders of the nursing home Devonshire Acres, and his mother, Adria Paxton, who worked at the center for more than 30 years. 

Those early experiences are what inspired him to get involved with research currently being done at Colorado State University.

“When this project came along, it was perfect for Hunter,” said Prasoon Diwakar, Ph.D., associate professor in the Leslie A. Rose Department of Mechanical Engineering. He could put his degree and knowledge to something he cares about.”

Diwakar brought Paxton into the mechanical engineering department as an undergraduate and introduced him to Neha Lodha, Ph.D., Colorado State University (CSU) associate professor and director of the Movement Neuroscience and Rehabilitation Lab. Lodha’s team is researching how subtle changes in mobility, such as how people drive, can be an early detection of Alzheimer’s disease and other forms of dementia.

“Alzheimer’s disease and related dementia pose a growing global health challenge with early detection still being a major hurdle for effective treatment,” Paxton said.

Over the past three years, Paxton has been working with CSU researchers to develop a model to analyze data from the I-Drive study, which tracks real-world driving behavior among older adults.

“CSU is doing a lot for Alzheimer’s research, looking at older individuals and how they progress with certain dementias,” Paxton said.

His research explores whether subtle changes in everyday driving habits, such as speed, navigation patterns and trip frequencies, can signal the onset of mild cognitive impairment (MCI), often a precursor to Alzheimer’s disease. MCI can develop years before a formal diagnosis.

“On a daily basis, these subtle changes in behavior can go unnoticed and undiagnosed,” Paxton said.

That is where the research comes in.

“The idea is if we can pick up on mild cognitive impairment, then we can get these individuals the treatment they need to help them before it gets to dementia or Alzheimer’s,” he said.

Participants in the study have a small sensor installed in their vehicles that collects data every 30 seconds. That data is analyzed across multiple timeframes, from individual trips to weekly behaviors and a variety of destinations visited.

The data is collected across the current 51 participants with Paxton cleaning up the data and developing a model that uses machine learning and artificial intelligence to figure out patterns.

 Hunter Paxton
Hunter Paxton with South Dakota Mines president Brian Tande, Ph.D., earned first place for his Alzheimer’s research during the 2025 Research Symposium. 

“In our study, we are seeing how well we can rank people – how likely are these individuals to have MCI, the probability they will have it or do have it,” Paxton said.

With this information, intervention can start earlier.

“The biggest issue that comes into play just globally with MCI cases is that symptoms are so subtle that people aren’t getting tested because they have no idea they have it,” Paxton said.

And the current diagnostic measures, such as cerebrospinal fluid analysis, are invasive.

“We are trying to offer a method that is accurate and non-invasive,” Paxton said. “You just put it in your vehicle, and it runs in the background.”

While the current study includes data from 51 participants over the past two years, the goal is to conduct larger-scale research to improve accuracy and expand real-world applications.

Paxton graduates this May and is headed to Austin, Texas, to start his career in AI and machine learning, but his involvement with this research will continue to shape his path.

“It is super rewarding to work on such an impactful project. These tools are interesting and cool, but they are nothing if we can’t apply them to helping people,” Paxton said. “If we can’t see the human impact from these tools, they are not valuable beyond novel research.”

title: Update: Mayor Salamun confirms 'it was my decision' to oust city attorney
description: Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun confirmed he fired Joel Landeen from his position as city attorney. Landeen has served in the city attorney’s office since 2004 and as city attorney since 2012.
_full_text:
Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun confirmed he fired Joel Landeen from his position as city attorney. Landeen has served in the city attorney’s office since 2004 and as city attorney since 2012.

The announcement of Landeen's departure came on Wednesday, April 1, through the city's public information officer, Darrell Shoemaker. 

“We would like to thank Joel for his many years of public service to the City of Rapid City,” Salamun said in a statement on Wednesday, April 1. “We wish him well in his future endeavors.”

Shoemaker later told the Journal Landeen's last day with the city was March 31. He said Salamun is looking for leadership changes in city government, but did not specify what prompted this decision.

Salamun told the Journal, "It was my decision to make a change in leadership in the City Attorney’s Office," confirming Landeen did not resign. "I determined it was time to bring fresh perspective, renewed energy, and stronger execution to our priorities."

 Daniel Ainslie and Joel Landeen (copy)
Joel Landeen at a July 2024 press conference.  

To ensure continuity of operations and steady legal leadership for the city, Carla Cushman, who currently serves as deputy city attorney, will assume the role of interim city attorney effective immediately. She is a graduate of Augustana College and the University of South Dakota Knudson School of Law, and has been with the city attorney’s office in Rapid City since 2012.

"We will begin our search for a new city attorney soon, but we will not settle until we find the right leader for the job. In the meantime, we are fortunate to have solid interim leadership in Carla Cushman and the outstanding team in the City Attorney’s Office," Salamun told the Journal. "Again, we are grateful for the contributions of Joel Landeen and wish him the best moving forward."

title: Division of Criminal Investigation investigates officer involved shooting in Sioux Falls
description: The Division of Criminal Investigation, per the request of the Sioux Falls Police Department, is investigating an officer-involved shooting that occurred on Tuesday, March 31, in Sioux Falls.
_full_text:
The Division of Criminal Investigation, per the request of the Sioux Falls Police Department, is investigating an officer-involved shooting that occurred on Tuesday, March 31, in Sioux Falls.

This incident marks the second officer-involved shooting in South Dakota this year. 

Richard Paul Olson, Jr., 36, Sioux Falls, was wanted for violation of his federal probation for possession of a firearm by a felon. Olson was found at a Sioux Falls gas station by the Sioux Falls Police Department's Violent Crime Unit on Tuesday.

According to the press release from the attorney general's office, Olson exited a car and later pulled out a weapon. Two officers fired their guns. A weapon believed to be in Olson's possession was later retrieved. 

Olson then allegedly attempted to flee, where he was stopped by a police service dog. Olson sustained injuries due to dog bites. No one was injured by the gunfire.

"DCI investigates all officer-involved shootings fairly in an unbiased manner," Attorney General Marty Jackley said in a press release. "After all of the facts are gathered and reviewed, DCI will issue a final report." 

DCI will process the crime scene, conduct forensic examination of all collected evidence, interview those involved and review all available video. The division will then issue a summary once the investigation is complete.

The Minnehaha County Sheriff's Office and the South Dakota Highway Patrol are assisting DCI. The Sioux Falls Police Department is cooperating in the investigation.

title: Billy Franklin Hughes
description: September 18, 1937 - March 28, 2026
_full_text:
September 18, 1937 - March 28, 2026

Billy F Hughes born in September 1937 died Saturday, March 28, 2026.

Bill was born on a farm in Sutherland NE to Almer and Acenath Hughes. He graduated from Sutherland High School and left for Longview Texas to attend LaTourneau College. Graduating with a degree in Electrical Engineering. Joined the USAF in 1960 as a navigator. Beginning his career flying B57 doing nuclear testing WB47 with Air Weather Reconnaissance, B57 and C47 in Viet Nam, B52 with Strategic Air Command, EC 135 ACCS Airborne Command and Control squadron. Retiring from the Air Force in Rapid City in 1962. He then started is own business mowing soccer fields for the city and was recognizable by the yellow lab accompanying him.

While a youth he attended Bible Camp and excepted Christ as his savior. He was a real soldier for the Lord. Sharing his testimony with people, teaching adult bible studies, being an Elder and Deacon in his church. He also belonged to Christian Business Men in Rapid City, SD. His grandma Hughes was a good influence and he remembers her favorite Bible verse Lamentations 3:23 Great is his faithfulness; his mercies begin afresh each morning.

Bill married Evelyn Hallett in 1962. They had two daughters, Carrie and Robin. He loved his family and spent many weekends taking them all camping and fishing for Walleye. Bill enjoyed Skiing with Carrie and soccer with Robin. He loved children and before his girls were born, the neighbor kids would knock on the door and ask if Bill could come play. When four grandchildren arrived, he was more than ready to play with Jazmine, Aliza, Azra and Boaz.

Left to mourn his life are his wife, daughters Carrie and son in law Bryon Gaul, Robin and son in law Brian Edwards, Grandchildren Jazmine, Aliza, Azra, Boaz Edwards.

He will be rejoicing in heaven with his brother Dave Hughes, Sisters Ramona Powell, Betty Priel, and Joyce Boyer. Many friends and family.

He will be remembered for his love for Jesus, family, good sense of humor, fishing, Husker football and apple pie.

title: Kent DeWitt Murtland
_full_text:
Nov 22, 1955 - Mar 29, 2026

Kent DeWitt Murtland, from Rapid City, S.D., loving husband and father, passed away peacefully, on Palm Sunday, March 29, 2026, at Edgewood Memory Care in Spearfish, S.D.

He was 70 years old. He will be remembered for his quick wit, love of a good joke or prank, and for his passion of helping people, kindness to friends, and family and his love of Jesus.

Born November 22, 1955, in North Carolina to Dr. Richard and Jane Murtland and was raised in Carmel, Calif. He graduated from Robert Louis Stevenson School in Pebble Beach, Calif., in 1973 and completed University of the Pacific College in Stockton, Calif., in 1978.

Upon returning home from UOP with a business degree, he began working at MJ Murphy Lumberyard in Carmel Valley Village. While attending church at Carmel Valley Baptist Church, he met Kathleen Elizabeth Hall (Kathy). They were married on November 8, 1980, at the First Baptist Church, Terrell, Texas. Kent and Kathy set up their first home in the Carmel Valley Village close to Kent's work at MJ Murphy and welcomed their first child, a son, Taylor Mason, in 1982.

Kent accepted a management position with Hayward Lumber in the summer of 1982, and they moved to Pacific Grove, Calif., about 30 miles down the road. They enjoyed Pacific Grove, the Butterfly Capital, and life was good. They welcomed a daughter, Tiffany Michelle, in 1985 and another son, Richard Lee, in 1989.

Kent enjoyed managing lumber yards and accepted a position with United Building Centers, Pierre, S.D., in 1993. Life in the Midwest offered many new opportunities for Kent such as kayaking the Missouri River, field dressing pheasants, and many new places in the beautiful Black Hills, South Dakota, to hike.

Kent loved playing guitar, leading worship, and was ordained as a deacon at Capital Heights Baptist Church, Pierre. He served as a Young Life leader in California and was a member of the Gideons Ministry helping place Bibles where needed. He continued to encourage friends to be active in church and the community.

Kent is survived by his wife, Kathy; his children, Taylor Murtland, (Brenda Bray), Tiffany Smart and Richard Murtland (Ushashi), along with his sisters, Candace Grant (Gary) and Cynthia Hebert (Jim).

In lieu of flowers, please donate to the Alzheimer's Association nearest you; or to Landmark Community Church, 3777 Anderson Road, Rapid City, SD 57703.

A Celebration of Life is being planned for April 15, 2026; 1 p.m., at Landmark Community Church, 3777 Anderson Road, Rapid City, SD 57703. Burial will be a family event at Point Lobos, Big Sur, Calif., in late summer.

Cremation arrangements are under the care of Fidler-Isburg Funeral Chapel & Isburg Crematory of Spearfish. Online condolences may be written at https://www.fidler-isburgfuneralchapels.com/.

title: Barbara Culey
description: Barbara J. Heylens Culey, age 94, passed away peacefully surrounded by family on March 23, 2026. She was born July 17, 1931, in Volga, S.D., to Con and Margaret Heylens.
_full_text:
Barbara J. Heylens Culey, age 94, passed away peacefully surrounded by family on March 23, 2026. She was born July 17, 1931, in Volga, S.D., to Con and Margaret Heylens.

Barbara started her teaching career at age 18, teaching all grades in a one room schoolhouse. She went back to school and received her degree with honors in 1977 from Black Hills State College. Barbara finally retired from teaching in 1994. Barbara married Robert (Bob) Culey on June 8, 1952. They lived in Castlewood, Worthington and Mobridge before settling in Rapid City in 1967. In 1965 they were blessed with a son, Jim. The family was completed in 1968 with daughter Connie. Having children was always very important to them and spending time with their East River nephews helped fulfill and prepare them for parenthood.

She was preceded in death by her husband, Robert (Bob) Culey; her parents, Con and Margaret Heylens; her brother, Bill Heylens. She is survived by her son, Jim (Elaine) Culey; grandchildren, Ethan, Lane (Clayton), Ashton and daughter Connie (Dan) Even, grandchildren Katie, Hannah (Austin), Megan (Logan) and Greg. Also, great-grandchildren Emma, Ayden, Austin JR., Jefferson, Ophelia, Ivy, Wren and Eve, her sister Connie Culey and nephews and nieces that held a very special place in her heart.

Barbara lived the kind of life we should all strive for, a life of dignity and grace.

Funeral services will be held at 12 p.m. Thursday, April 2, 2026, at Canyon Lake United Methodist Church. Interment services will follow at 2:30 p.m. at Black Hills National Cemetery near Sturgis, S.D.

In lieu of flowers donations can be made in Barbara's name to: www.stjude.org or one of your favorite charities.

Friends and family are invited to sign the online guest register and leave written condolences at WestRiverfd.com.

title: Shriner's Children to host screening clinic for children's bone, muscle and joint issues
description: Families in western South Dakota are invited to a free pediatric orthopedic and scoliosis screening clinic from 2 to 7 p.m. Tuesday, April 7, at Naja Shrine Center, 4091 Sturgis Road, Rapid City. The clinic is hosted by Shriner’s Children Twin Cities, a children’s hospital in Woodbury, Minn.
_full_text:
Families in western South Dakota are invited to a free pediatric orthopedic and scoliosis screening clinic from 2 to 7 p.m. Tuesday, April 7, at Naja Shrine Center, 4091 Sturgis Road, Rapid City. The clinic is hosted by Shriner’s Children Twin Cities, a children’s hospital in Woodbury, Minn.

This screening clinic is for children who may be experiencing bone, muscle or joint concerns. Initial screenings are free for conditions including: in-toeing, toe walking or flat feet; bowed legs or knocked knee; ore, painful, or stiff joints; limb deficiency or limb length discrepancy; curved spine indicating possible scoliosis; club feet and other orthopedic concerns.

 Shriner's Children screening clinic
Shriner's Children Twin Cities will host a free screening clinic on Tuesday, April 7, from 2 to 7 p.m. at Naja Shrine Center, 4091 Sturgis Road, Rapid City. 

Appointments for screenings can be scheduled through the Naja Shrine Center by calling 605-343-4279 or emailing patientservices@najashriners.org. The appointment office is open Monday through Thursday from 9 a.m. to 2 p.m., or leave a message if calling before or after office hours. Walk-ins are welcome, too.

The clinic gives families an opportunity to learn whether their children could benefit from evaluation or care at Shriner’s Children Twin Cities. Shriner’s Children Twin Cities provides specialized pediatric orthopedic care regardless of a family’s ability to pay or insurance status.

If follow-up care is recommended, families will receive information about next steps and available support services.

title: Rapid City man enters guilty pleas after a day of shooting and burglary in Rapid Valley
description: A Rapid City man entered guilty pleas on Tuesday, March 31, after an eventful day in October 2024 when he shot someone and also engaged in a burglary.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man entered guilty pleas on Tuesday, March 31, after an eventful day in October 2024 when he shot someone and also engaged in a burglary.

Travis Chipps, 19, appeared before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse and pleaded guilty to aggravated assault, first-degree burglary and assault with human bodily fluids or waste at the jail, also known as sliming.

According to court documents, Chipps was initially indicted for attempted first-degree murder for shooting a man on Oct. 4, 2024.

Authorities were called to the 4200 block of Apple Tree Road around 12:15 a.m. that morning for a report of a man knocking on doors asking for help after being shot. The victim was later found with gunshot wounds. An investigation led officials to believe he had been shot at a different location. He was sent to the hospital and declared stable.

During the investigation into the shooting, an attempted burglary was reported around the 6200 block of East Highway 44.

The state said the shooting occurred earlier in the day, while the separate burglary incident happened later that evening. 

In exchange for Chipps' guilty pleas, the state agreed to dismiss all other charges and request a 15-year sentence. Despite the state's agreement to recommend 15 years, the sentencing judge can go lower or higher. Legally, Chipps faces the potential of a combined 42 years in prison. 

Aggravated assault is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. First-degree burglary is a Class 2 felony with a maximum sentence of 25 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. Sliming is a Class 6 felony with a maximum sentence of two years in prison and a $4,000 fine. 

Chipps next court appearance is set for May 5 at 8:30 a.m.

title: Let's give them something to cluck about: Rapid City chicken ordinance pecking its way along
description: Legalization of backyard chickens in Rapid City is on its way to city council after the ordinance was approved by Rapid City's Legal and Finance Committee on Wednesday, April 1. Community members voiced support of legalized poultry at the meeting.
_full_text:
Legalization of backyard chickens in Rapid City is on its way to city council after the ordinance was approved by Rapid City's Legal and Finance Committee on Wednesday, April 1. Community members voiced support of legalized poultry at the meeting. 

The committee voted 3-1 in favor of the ordinance, with Councilman Lance Lehmann (Ward 4) voting no.

 Stephen Tamang
Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1), right, a supporter for the legalization of backyard chickens, continued his efforts at the April 1 Legal and Finance Committee meeting. Councilman Lance Lehmann (Ward 4), left, voted against. 

Before the meeting, Lehmann told the Journal residents of his ward voiced their opposition of the ordinance, and his vote would reflect their concerns.

Currently, the Rapid City Municipal Code prohibits the keeping of backyard hens except on land that is zoned for agricultural uses. 

According to city documents, the Rapid City Urban Hens Group along with City Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) brought the issue before City Council during the Jan. 20 council meeting, and advocated for city staff to draft an ordinance. 

As currently written, the ordinance would allow for a maximum number of six chickens per residential parcel. Roosters would not be allowed under the proposed ordinance.

The ordinance requires containment for chickens through an enclosed coop and chicken run, an enclosed outdoor space, to keep predators out.

The coops must be ventilated and provide a minimum area of at least 2 square feet per chicken. Runs must be attached to the coop and have a minimum area of at least 10 square feet per chicken. 

The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines. 

"It is unlawful to keep hens where the provisions of § 6.08.021 are not met. Each day a violation occurs is a separate offense. In addition to any other remedies or causes of action the city may possess, the keeping or maintaining of a hen in a manner inconsistent with § 6.08.021 is declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated by the city as provided by state law and City ordinance," the ordinance reads.

The violations include owning roosters, not providing feed and water at all times and or keeping poultry to be used for commercial use.

All five speakers who made public comment at the committee meeting were in support of the ordinance.

 Dave Holland
Resident and urban chicken advocate Dave Holland addresses concerns over backyard chickens, saying the ordinance brought further restrictions.   

David Holland, one of the members of the urban hen group, said, "Our group started meeting a little over a year ago, and we looked at previous attempts to pass this ordinance. We made a list of the reasons why it failed. We tried to listen to the citizens that were against it."

Holland said he supports the ordinance as it is drafted, as the city added additional restrictions from the original draft ordinance that came before the council in January.

This included a setback for the construction and location of any coop, chicken run, or exercise yard having to be 25 feet from any residential dwelling, church, school, or place of business and 15 feet from any lot line.

Rapid City resident Susan Martin said she did not plan on speaking at the meeting, but out of support decided she felt like she had to.

 Chickens
Rapid City resident Susan Martin tells the committee her child will cry if the ordinance isn't passed.  

"My family is highly in favor. She'll cry if she doesn't get hens in her backyard," Martin said with a grin about her child. 

She supported her stance by stating Rapid City is mostly an agriculture-based community with ample educational opportunities to learn how to raise chickens and care for chickens.

 Chickens
Sara Parkison, speaks in favor of the ordinance for backyard chickens on April 1, 2026. 

Sara Parkison with Trash Lilly Farm, which raises chickens, voiced support for the community to learn sustainable practices by reaching out to their farm.

Parkison said, "I know that this is kind of talked about as a secondary thing, the fact that hens eat this much food, and this is what we can do with our food waste. I think it goes beyond food waste. It goes from food waste, you get rid of that, that methane, that's great but you also are producing neural soil."

Before voting, Tamang said, "Mr. Holland presented a lot of great facts. One thing he glossed over a little bit is the list of other communities across our state that allow backyard hands, and the very little amount of problems it causes, but the great joy it brings."

Councilman Bill Evans (Ward 2) followed up Tamang's comments saying he has previously voted down the backyard chickens' ordinance, but because of the overwhelming support he's heard and little opposition, he would vote in favor.

"I've received two or three negative things; I've received dozens of positive. So I'm willing to go with the people," Evans said.

Councilmember Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2) said she has been supportive of the ordinance, although she well not have chickens in her yard.

"I am going to support this if we move this forward. And just wanted to thank the city for their work on this. I know this is probably our fifth time in the last couple of decades that they've took a stab at this," Seachris said.

Tamang told the Journal the ordinance will be on city council's agenda for consideration on April 6.
