title: South Dakota governor signs law that allows school boards to remove disruptive children
description: South Dakota Gov. Larry Rhoden signed into law a bill that provides an avenue for school boards to remove children from traditional learning settings.
_full_text:
South Dakota Gov. Larry Rhoden signed into law a bill that provides an avenue for school boards to remove children from traditional learning settings.

House Bill 1017 allows school boards to "assign a student to receive instruction in an alternative setting for aggressive or violent behaviors that disrupt the school or that affect a health or safety factor of the school or its program."

The bill breezed its way through the South Dakota Legislature with unanimous approval in the House and just one dissenter in the Senate. Rhoden signed it on March 27.

"It's necessary because public schools in America, including in South Dakota, have seen dramatic increases in student misbehavior involving extreme examples of violence and disruption," South Dakota Secretary of Education Joe Graves said during the legislative session. "The situations have become not only more frequent, but also more explosive. These are not one-offs, and they are not playground dust-ups or a little boy throwing a rock at the little girl he likes."

Graves introduced the bill.

Earlier versions of the bill included definitions of "aggressive or violent behavior," "alternative setting" and "disrupt."

The version Rhoden approved and that passed House with a 67-0 vote and the Senate with a 33-1 vote is more bare-bones. Sen. Red Dawn Foster, D-27, Pine Ridge, was the lone nay vote. 

The act essentially does four things: 

Allows the school board to place children in an alternative setting for behavior. 
Does not apply to children who are protected by the federal Individuals with Disabilities Act or the Rehabilitation Act of 1973.
Requires school boards to annually report to the state students who were assigned instruction in alternative settings. 
Allows the state to disclose the information in the report only if the children's names are removed and information is aggregated with data reported by all other districts. 

Rhoden also signed into law House Bill 1262, which allots $2 million from the general fund for a grant to support non-residential, school-based therapeutic services for students receiving instruction in an alternative setting. The Children's Home Society of South Dakota advocated for the passage of HB 1262 and HB 1017.

CHS has residential schools in Rockerville and Sioux Falls and day schools in Sioux Falls and Rapid City that provide alternative instruction. The ability to take on more children, however, "is certainly challenged right now," Jennifer Stalley, a spokesperson for CHS, told legislators during the session.

Stalley advocated for the formation of a day school in Aberdeen that can serve northeastern South Dakota.

title: Former City Attorney Landeen responds to firing. Council member says 'he needed to go'
description: Former Rapid City Attorney Joel Landeen said "I wish everybody at the city the best" while discussing his recent departure from the office.
_full_text:
Former Rapid City Attorney Joel Landeen said "I wish everybody at the city the best" while discussing his recent departure from the office.

On April 1, Rapid City announced Landeen's final day was March 31. Mayor Jason Salamun told the Journal, "It was my decision to make a change in leadership in the City Attorney’s Office," confirming Landeen did not resign.

Landeen told the Journal, "It wasn't antagonistic. I know everybody always wants to make this like there's some conspiracy, or there's something going on. A lot of times this stuff is a lot more benign than people think, and there isn't any hard feelings or animosity."

He added, "I enjoyed my time at the city. All of the director positions served at the pleasure of the mayor. I knew that when I started and the mayor decided to go a different direction, and that's his prerogative. And I don't hold ill will. I wish everybody at the city the best."

 Former City attorney Joel Landeen
Rapid City Attorney Joel Landeen's last day in the position was March 31. 

Landeen said he was notified of his termination by Salamun on March 31 but declined to give specifics on what was discussed. He worked for the city for 22 years and as city attorney since 2012. According to the Rapid City Human Resources Department, Landeen was making $182,988 a year as of late 2025.

Landeen said he's looking forward to his next venture.

“We would like to thank Joel for his many years of public service to the City of Rapid City,” Salamun said in a statement on Wednesday, April 1. “We wish him well in his future endeavors.”

Carla Cushman, who currently serves as deputy city attorney, assumed the role of interim city attorney on April 1. She is a graduate of Augustana College and the University of South Dakota Knudson School of Law, and has been with the city attorney’s office in Rapid City since 2012.

The Journal reached out to three city council members for comment on Landeen's departure from the city.

Council President Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) provided the Journal with a statement reviewed by council members and his personal attorney, “President of (the) City Council Pettigrew is sure the mayor gave it proper consideration prior to deciding a change was needed. Pettigrew and other council members support the move and wish Joel all the best on his future endeavors. It is a good practice for any organization to be evaluating its employees and overall performance and make necessary and difficult changes when and where appropriate to improve performance for the city.”

Councilman John Roberts (Ward 4) said, "he needed to go," about Landeen.

Councilman Lance Lehmann (Ward 4) said, "Mr. Landeen was with the city a long time and his service to our community is greatly appreciated. I do feel that change within government is a healthy thing. When fresh people are moved into positions, it plants fresh ideas into the organization. I'm looking forward to seeing what candidates apply for the position and am hopeful I'm part of the hiring committee."

Department directors, including the city attorney, are appointed by the mayor and confirmed by the City Council. All other personnel decisions, including termination, fall under the mayor.

title: Snow doesn't stop children from enjoying Easter egg hunt at Sioux Park
description: Hoards of children trudged through the snow at Sioux Park on Saturday, April 4, in search of the Easter Bunny's hidden treasures.
_full_text:
Hoards of children trudged through the snow at Sioux Park on Saturday, April 4, in search of the Easter Bunny's hidden treasures.

The mad dash for the bounty the soccer fields held was over in a matter of minutes. The snowy field went from dotted with 6,000 colorful Easter eggs to covered in footprints, and the children filed away with their loot in baskets, buckets and bags. The Easter Bunny was also present so families could take photos.

 040526-nws-egghunt-p24.JPG
The Easter Bunny greets a youngster during an egg hunt at Sioux Park hosted by Rapid City and the local Optimist Club oon Saturday, April 4. 

The Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department and the Rapid City Optimist Club teamed up to host the 62nd annual easter egg hunt. It's the first year the two groups have joined forces. In previous years, both entities hosted separate events on different days and at different locations.

 040526-nws-egghunt-p2.JPG
Snow on the ground didn't deter youngsters at the egg hunt. 

Mark Kline, vice president of the Optimist Club, said Sioux Park is one of the only places in town with enough space for everyone. Kline said sponsors, such as Scheels and Target, helped pay for the event, and the city bought the eggs. The total cost was between $1,500 and $2,000, Kline estimated. 

 040526-nws-egghunt-p7.JPG
Hoards of children trudged through the snow at Sioux Park on Saturday, April 4, in search of the Easter Bunny's hidden treasures. 

He said the snow is a bit of hindrance. The club usually puts out candy on the grass in addition to the eggs, but the snow prevented that. It didn't prevent the hundreds of people who came out for the event. 

"It's an awesome turnout today," Kline said. 

 PHOTOS: The Easter Bunny hid eggs in the snow at Sioux Park in Rapid City
The Rapid City Parks and Recreation Department and the Rapid City Optimist Club teamed up to host the 62nd annual City Easter Egg Hunt on Saturday, March 4, at Sioux Park. The 6,000 eggs place in the snowy soccer fields were gone in a matter of minutes.

title: 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 6,000 acres
description: The 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 6,000 acres, an interagency team responding to the fire said in a Sunday, April 12 update.
_full_text:
The 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 6,000 acres, an interagency team responding to the fire said in a Sunday, April 12 update.

The fire's located about 3 miles north of Buffalo Gap, the update said. According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, it is located just east of Highway 79 along East French Creek Road in eastern Custer County.

 79 Fire Operations A.M. Map 4.12.26.png
A map of the 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap released on Sunday, April 12. 

The fire officials update said the footprint of the fire reaches about 6 miles from Highway 79 to 144th Avenue in eastern Custer County. No structures have been lost.

Firefighters have it 40% contained and the cause remains under investigation, officials said in the update.

"Fire lines overnight held steady with no new growth," the update said, noting a red flag warning remains in effect until 9 p.m. tonight. The high in the area is around 79 degrees, with wind speeds up to 29 mph possible, according to the National Weather Service's Rapid City office. The forecast doesn't show a chance of precipitation until Tuesday afternoon.

Gov. Larry Rhoden has declared a state of emergency for portions of southwest South Dakota affected by the 79 wildfire. The emergency declaration allows fire suppression costs to be covered by the state fire suppression fund in accordance with SDCL 41-20A-8, DPS said in a release.

 041326-nws-fire-p3.jpg
According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, the 79 wildfire is located just east of Highway 79 along East French Creek Road in eastern Custer County. 

Due to the extreme lack of moisture in vegetation and in the ground there is a potential for heat to remain beneath the surface. This is is what resources will seek to locate and extinguish today. Doing so will keep the fire from expanding or reigniting," officials said in the update, noting "fire activity has lessened significantly since the fire began."

The effort includes state, federal and local resources, who "will continue actively working to mop up along the perimeter to prevent the fire from escaping or expanding the current perimeter."

"The objective today is to keep the fire in it's current footprint thereby lessening threat to the line," officials said in the update.

 041326-nws-fire-p2.jpg
The 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 6,000 acres, an interagency team responding to the fire said in a Sunday, April 12 update. 

The fire was reported around 11:35 a.m. on Saturday, April 11, according to Watch Duty, a nonprofit wildfire tracking site run by active and retired firefighters, dispatchers, and first responders, according to its website.

The Bureau of Indian Affairs Forest and Wildland Management agency said Saturday it's tracking firefighting efforts, as the wildfire is west of Pine Ridge Reservation, but noted there is no threat to the reservation at this time. 

Additional information in 79 wildfire update

Weather: Critical fire weather continues today with warm temperatures in the mid to upper 70s, very low relative humidity, with a moderate breeze and faster gusts possible.

Public Closures: Road closures have been lifted in the area. However, the public is asked to avoid the area due to ongoing active fire suppression activity including personnel and equipment.

Residential Impact: There are no evacuations in place.

Temporary Flight Restrictions: There is a Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) over the perimeter of the 79 Fire. "Please do not fly any unauthorized aircraft over the fire. If you fly, we can’t."

title: Rapid City police issue alert on missing 12-year-old
description: The Rapid City Police Department said it is seeking the public's help to locate 12-year-old runaway Kyson Whiteplume.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department said it is seeking the public's help to locate 12-year-old runaway Kyson Whiteplume.

In an alert, police said numerous locations have been checked since he was reported as a runaway Sunday morning, April 12. He was last seen Saturday morning in the 600 block of North Seventh Street wearing a black sweater, black pants and bright yellow shoes.

 041326-nws-missing-p1.jpg
Kyson Whiteplume 

Anyone with any information about his whereabouts should contact police immediately at 605-394-4131.

title: Every ward contested in Rapid City Council election
description: The Rapid City Council election is set.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Council election is set.

This year every ward was contested for the first time since 2002, according to Finance Director Daniel Ainslie.

The current seats up for election on June 2 are held by Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2), Kevin Maher (Ward 3), John Roberts (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5).

Biberdorf, Seachris, Maher and Roberts have turned in their required 50 valid signatures to qualify for the ballot on the June 2 election. Petitions were due on Tuesday, March 24.

Pettigrew is not seeking reelection.

Ainslie told the Journal only one petitioner, Brandon Ferguson, didn't meet the 50 valid signatures threshold when he turned his paperwork in on Tuesday.

Ainslie said after candidates turn in petitions, the finance office checks to ensure those who signed were registered to vote, registered to vote within the correct ward, have the correct address and filled out information in its entirety.

On Wednesday, March 25, the city drew names to determine how they'd be listed on the ballot.

The official order goes:

Ward one: Josh Biberdorf, Murray Lee

Ward two: Christopher Vanderhoof, Lindsey Seachris

Ward three: Andrea Schaefer, Kevin Maher

Ward four: Valeriah Big Eagle, John Roberts, Ardin Jay Cychosz

Ward five: Pat Roseland, Laura Armstrong

The city will send the official order to the Pennington County's Auditor's Office.

Absentee voting begins on April 17 and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  

Voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

"On average, our municipal elections have been in the teens, maybe 20%," Ainslie said about voter turnout for a municipal election.

He said the city and county spilt some of the costs of the municipal election, but estimated it will cost $40,000 to $50,000.

Those officially throwing their hat in the race who are not currently on the council are Murray Lee (Ward 1), Christopher Vanderhoof (Ward 2), Andrea Schaefer (Ward 3), Ardin Jay Cychosz (Ward 4) and Valeriah Big Eagle (Ward 4) and Laura Armstrong (Ward 5).

Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) said of Pettigrew's departure, "I have really enjoyed working with President Pettigrew over the last year, and serving as his vice president has been a great experience. He is a thoughtful leader who cares deeply about this community, and I respect the time he has put into this community."

Regarding the election, Tamang said he strongly encourages residents to pay attention to the candidates in their ward and make an informed decision.

"Local government affects our lives in profound ways, and who we choose to serve on City Council has a significant impact on the direction of our city," Tamang said.

Although the city does not host any candidate forums, Ainslie said residents should expect to hear other organizations that will be hosting candidates, as well as marketing from each campaign.

"You have some candidates who invest very little, and maybe it'll be a couple $1,000 for a few yard signs. And then there are other candidates that will advertise in media, whether it's radio, social media, television, newspapers, you know, they'll invest in all those things and billboards ... It can cost $10s of $1,000s of dollars, especially if they start doing mailers," Ainslie said.

For more information or questions, contact the City Finance Department at 605-394-4143.

title: 3 seats on Pennington County Commission up for grabs
description: The Pennington County Commission race is on.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Commission race is on.

Three of the five seats on the board are up for election on June 2. The current seats up for election are held by Commissioners Ron Rossknecht (District 1), Deb Hadcock (District 3) and Gary Drewes (District 5).

Hadcock is the only commissioner seeking re-election.

Rossknecht and Drewes shared with the Journal their reason for stepping away.

"I live near Sheridan Lake, which is in District 1. I had no background in county government back in 2018, but I interfaced with county departments on a weekly basis as I appraised real estate ... I thought with my 30 years of appraisal experience and familiarity with the Black Hills National Forest, I could bring something valuable to the table," Rossknecht said.

He said some of his goals in the position were to complete the Sheridan Lake Road reconstruction, South Rochford Road paving, Rochford Road (chipped sealed), and Mystic Road (mag watered). 

He said these county projects are now complete.

"I want to be a snowbird and go where it's warm in the winter, and you can’t do that and serve as a commissioner," Rossknecht said.

He noted parts of district one include the Black Hills National Forest, which has its own ongoing challenges with mining, recreation, population growth, road systems, infrastructure and forestry.

Drewes told the Journal he did not seek a seat on the county commission to make it a career.

"When I see new voices and interest in performing public service, I am more than willing to step aside," Drewes said.

On Monday, April 6, the Pennington County Auditor's office drew names to determine how names will be listed on the June 2 primary ballot.

The official ballot goes:

District one: Zachary Bush, Mike Mueller (both Republicans)

District three: Randy Decker, Deb Hadcock (both Republicans)

District 5: Jefferey Heines (running uncontested as a Republican) and Tim Williams (running uncontested as a Democrat).

Absentee voting begins on April 17 and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  

Voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

title: Red Flag Warning until SUN 9:00 PM MDT
description: A Red Flag Warning is in effect until 9 PM MDT this evening. Warm, dry, and breezy conditions are creating critical fire weather conditions, expected to persist through Monday.
_full_text:
Critical Fire Weather Conditions in Northeastern Wyoming and Western South Dakota

What's Happening:

A Red Flag Warning is in effect until 9 PM MDT this evening. Warm, dry, and breezy conditions are creating critical fire weather conditions, expected to persist through Monday.

Affected Areas:

Northern Campbell (Zone 314)
Southern Campbell (Zone 315)
Weston County Plains (Zone 317)
Central Black Hills (Zone 320)
Southern Black Hills (Zone 321)
Fall River County Area (Zone 322)
Eastern Foot Hills (Zone 324)
Custer County Plains (Zone 325)
Pine Ridge Area (Zone 326)
West Central Plains (Zone 329)
Badlands Area (Zone 332)

What to Expect:

Winds from the west at 10 to 20 mph, with gusts up to 35 mph.
Relative humidity as low as 10%.

Impacts:

The combination of gusty winds and low humidity will create conditions conducive to rapid fire spread. Extreme fire behavior is possible.

Safety Tips:

Avoid activities that could spark a fire, such as outdoor burning or using machinery that can create sparks.
Stay informed about local fire conditions and restrictions.
Report any fires immediately to local authorities.

When to Check Back:

Monitor weather.gov or rapidcityjournal.com/weather for updates.

This weather alert was produced with AI assistance using National Weather Service data.

title: Trump approves disaster requests for at least 7 states. Others await aid decisions
byline: By GABRIELA AOUN ANGUEIRA - Associated Press
description: The Trump administration approved major disaster declaration requests for at least seven states this week, according to information released Saturday by the Federal Emergency Management Agency , allowing affected communities to access federal support. About 15 requests for assistance from others states and tribes for extreme weather events this year and last seem to be pending, along with three appeals of previous denials.
_full_text:
The Trump administration approved major disaster declaration requests for at least seven states this week, according to information released Saturday by the Federal Emergency Management Agency, allowing affected communities to access federal support. About 15 requests for assistance from others states and tribes for extreme weather events this year and last seem to be pending, along with three appeals of previous denials.

Alaska, Idaho, Montana, Oregon, South Carolina, South Dakota and Washington were granted major disaster declarations, which can unlock federal support and funding for recovery needs such as public infrastructure repairs and aid for survivors.

The announcement, in a FEMA daily briefing document, comes weeks into Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin’s tenure overseeing the disaster relief agency and is the latest signal that the former Republican senator from Oklahoma could ease some of the turmoil from the leadership of his predecessor, Kristi Noem, who was fired by President Donald Trump in March.

Nonetheless, FEMA’s work could be undermined by the ongoing DHS shutdown, now eight weeks long. While disaster response and recovery can continue through a shutdown because FEMA’s Disaster Relief Fund does not lapse, that money is running low as the funding impasse drags on. The DHS appropriations bill would replenish the fund with more than $26 billion.

Mullin said Tuesday that he planned to brief Trump that day on the pending declaration requests, affirming his intention to speed up work on past disasters in the run-up to Atlantic hurricane season, which begins June 1.

“We’re trying to push this stuff forward as fast as possible,” Mullin said after surveying Hurricane Helene recovery work in North Carolina on his first official visit as DHS secretary, acknowledging that “disasters are happening constantly.”

White House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson said Saturday that Trump responds to such requests “with great care and consideration, ensuring American tax dollars are used appropriately and efficiently by the states to supplement — not substitute — their obligation to respond to and recover from disasters.” She said an administration goal is having state and local governments "invest in their own resilience before disaster strikes, making response less urgent and recovery less prolonged.”

While Mullin assured fellow senators during his confirmation hearing that he believed in FEMA's mission, the agency's future is uncertain. Trump has expressed a desire to push more responsibility for disasters down to states. The FEMA Review Council he appointed last year has not released a recommendation report expected to include sweeping changes to how the federal government supports disaster resilience, response and recovery.

It was not immediately clear whether other states or tribes had also been told of approvals or denials that were not yet announced publicly. Hawaii Governor Josh Green, a Democrat, said Wednesday said his state had received a disaster declaration for devastating March flooding.

Trump also amended past disaster declarations for Tennessee and Mississippi, adding more counties for individual assistance after a severe winter storm in January.

Some communities have experienced unprecedented long waits for answers on their disaster requests during Trump's second term. An analysis by The Associated Press in September found approvals were taking more than a month on average.

It took less than two weeks on average for a governor’s disaster declaration request to be granted by presidents in the 1990s and early 2000s. That rose to about three weeks during the past decade under presidents from both major parties.

Arizona has been waiting nearly three months for an answer to its appeal after being denied support for severe storms and flooding that occurred in September.

Some Democrat-led states have complained about being denied disaster declarations despite proving need. Maryland Gov. Wes Moore called Trump's decision “deeply frustrating” after the president twice denied the state's request for support for May 2025 flooding despite a FEMA assessment showing over $33 million in damages.

While FEMA assesses damage and uses a specific formula to analyze the possible impact on states and local jurisdictions, disaster declarations are ultimately at the president’s discretion.

None of the approvals made this week includes hazard mitigation funding, a once-typical add on to disaster declaration support that helped communities build back with more resilience. Trump has not approved a hazard mitigation request for more than a year.

title: Tensions rise over Pennington County TIF at Rapid City Council meeting
description: On Monday, April 6, Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach challenged City Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) over concerns about a Tax Increment Financing District resolution that is going through the council approval process.
_full_text:
On Monday, April 6, Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach challenged City Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) over concerns about a Tax Increment Financing District resolution that is going through the council approval process.

Weifenbach noted the county took on the TIF after the city had initially wanted it, and chided Tamang for his social media posts about the project. Rapid City Finance Director Daniel Ainslie admitted fault for miscommunication regarding TIF information shared with council members.

 Stephen Tamang
Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) listens as Ron Weifenbach addresses concerns over Tamang's online posts about a county TIF during the Rapid City Council meeting on Monday, April 6. 

Pennington County is looking to create a Tax Increment Financing District on an estimated 172-acre lot located generally south of Catron Boulevard, west of Fifth Street and east of Black Hills Boulevard.
Part of the TIF area is within Rapid City limits, and the county has requested permission for that area to be used in the TIF district.

According to city documents, Black Hills Marketplace would be used for major retail and restaurant developments, as well as single- and multi-family residential developments. The city limits section is on Catron Boulevard.

The Rapid City Council voted 9-0 to delay action to its April 20 meeting. Council President Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) was absent from the meeting.

Ainslie could not give an exact figure of the city's property in the project site, but estimated it at less than 5% of the total 172-acre site. Ainslie also said it's too early in the TIF process for a project cost valuation.

 Ron Weifenbach
Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach takes to the podium at the Monday, April 6 Rapid City Council meeting. 

 Residents
Residents of Rapid City listen to Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach address Rapid City Councilman Stephan Tamang during the council meeting on Monday, April 7. 

Ainslie said if the TIF resolution is denied by the city, the county could still pass the TIF, excluding the area that is in city limits. 
Weifenbach took to the podium at the council meeting on Monday to address issues he had with Tamang's online comments regarding the TIF.
"I'm basically here to talk about the TIF that the Rapid City administration sent to the county commission because they couldn't get it done ... So next thing I know, I started getting comments on Facebook ... I had a conversation with Mr. Tamang, told him straight exactly what was happening. He chose not to listen to me. He's worried about losing control of the TIF that you guys actually originated," Weifenbach said of Tamang's online statements regarding the TIF.
Tamang took to Substack on April 3, to address his concerns over the proposed TIF boundary. He wrote that because the TIF project plans will be approved by the county, the city will have limited say.
"If this moves forward in its current form, the City Council would be giving up meaningful control over a project that is expected to come back into the city through annexation. In practical terms, that means neither I nor the rest of the City Council would be in a position to vote on or defend against a TIF plan that we believe falls short of providing adequate community benefit," Tamang said in his Substack post.
"He's worried about losing control of the TIF that you guys (the city) actually originated. And so, I want to make sure that everybody in this audience and everybody in Rapid City knows that Dr. Tamang did not know what he was talking about," Weifenbach said.
Finance Director Daniel Ainslie confirmed with the Journal the city did in fact receive a TIF request from the developer of this TIF, but because the TIF area falls mostly within the county, the city reached out to the county for them to consider the TIF.
"That's where I called up Commissioner Weifenbach and asked would the county be willing to (take on the project). He said that they would be willing to consider, that's not a guarantee that the county would agree, but they would be willing to look at a TIF application, and see if they would approve it," Ainslie told the Journal.
Ainslie also took blame for not providing all council members with a briefing on the TIF request, as is typical. He said he did speak to some council members, but due to a busy schedule, he wasn't able to provide an extensive timeline history of the request. 
"You should have took the time, you got my phone number. You could have called me. You could have called Daniel Ainslie, you could have called Jason Salamun. All these people knew what's going on," Weifenbach said in comments directed to Tamang at the meeting.
Ainslie clarified at issue is a request for the city to join in the TIF boundary with the county. There is no approval of a TIF project plan at this time.
Ainslie also said the city would have some oversight of the project. He said if the city does approve of the TIF, the city still controls the planning and zoning aspects of the project.
"So the council is still going to have a lot of opportunities to weigh in. The only time the council will not have an opportunity to weigh in is going to be the actual particulars of the project plan," Ainslie said.
Tamang did not respond to Weifenbach's comments at the meeting.
Before voting for a delay in the resolution approval, Tamang said, "I think we had a very good discussion regarding what degree of control the city should have over this process, and we still seek to be good partners with the county and work together as a whole. So I think we have more time to learn about this."
Kevin Maher (Ward 3) and John Roberts (Ward 4) both said they support the TIF.
Maher said, "I will be supporting this. I would like two more weeks, (as) I haven't had a chance to meet with the developers."
Roberts concluded the discussion before voting, saying, "We're getting all the benefit of this TIF now, Mr. Tamang, you're going to lose a little control over it, but really you're not, because we still have control of all the zoning, we control all the planning. We control every step of this going forward, except for the TIF plan itself."

title: Rapid City man pleads guilty to possessing child sexual abuse material
description: A Rapid City man pleaded guilty on Tuesday, April 7, to several counts of possession of child sexual abuse material.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man pleaded guilty on Tuesday, April 7, to several counts of possession of child sexual abuse material.

Austin Michaelson, 31, appeared for his change of plea hearing before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

According to court documents, Michaelson was indicted for distributing child pornography and four counts of possessing child pornography. The indictment states the incidents occurred on Sept. 14 and June 29.  

In South Dakota, distribution of child pornography is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. While possessing child pornography is a Class 4 felony with a maximum sentence of 10 years in prison and a $20,000 fine. 

Per the plea agreement, Michaelson pleaded guilty to three counts of possessing child sexual abuse material and the state dismissed all other charges. Both sides are free to comment. 

Michaelson's sentencing is set for 8:30 a.m. on June 16.

title: Red Flag Warning until SUN 9:00 PM MDT
description: A Red Flag Warning is in effect until 9 PM MDT Sunday for northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota. Warm, dry, and breezy conditions are creating critical fire weather. Isolated dry thunderstorms may occur, potentially bringing erratic wind gusts over 50 mph.
_full_text:
Critical Fire Weather Conditions Persist Through Sunday

What’s Happening:

A Red Flag Warning is in effect until 9 PM MDT Sunday for northeastern Wyoming and western South Dakota. Warm, dry, and breezy conditions are creating critical fire weather. Isolated dry thunderstorms may occur, potentially bringing erratic wind gusts over 50 mph.

Affected Areas:

Northern and Southern Campbell
Weston County Plains
Central and Southern Black Hills
Fall River County Area
Eastern Foot Hills
Custer County Plains
Pine Ridge Area
West Central Plains
Badlands Area

What to Expect:

Southwest winds 10 to 20 mph, gusting up to 35 mph.
Relative humidity as low as 10%.
Potential for isolated dry thunderstorms with erratic wind gusts over 50 mph.

Impacts:

High risk of wildfires due to gusty winds and low humidity.
Potential for rapid fire spread and extreme fire behavior.

Safety Tips:

Avoid activities that could start a fire.
Stay informed about local fire restrictions.
Report any signs of smoke or fire to authorities immediately.

When to Check Back:

Monitor weather.gov or rapidcityjournal.com/weather for updates.

This weather alert was produced with AI assistance using National Weather Service data.

title: Rapid City Rotary clubs joining clean up events, Earth Day Expo
description: Rotary District 5610's Unite for Water Initiative has begun, highlighting two of Rotary’s most important causes — clean water and sanitation. Members of Rushmore Rotary and Rapid City Rotary clubs are volunteering at local community events in April as part of the Unite for Water Initiative.
_full_text:
Rotary District 5610's Unite for Water Initiative has begun, highlighting two of Rotary’s most important causes — clean water and sanitation. Members of Rushmore Rotary and Rapid City Rotary clubs are volunteering at local community events in April as part of the Unite for Water Initiative.

Clean up Rapid Creek at 10 a.m. Saturday, April 11. Meet at East St. Louis Street near Breadroot Natural Foods Co-op in Rapid City. This clean-up is sponsored by Black Hills Flyfishers and Breadroot. All ability groups are needed. Supplies will be provided. Please dress for weather and wear gloves.

Clean up on 44th Street near Rapid City Stevens High School on Saturday, April 25. Volunteers will meet at 10:30 a.m. in the overflow parking lot. This activity is part of the City of Rapid City's Community Cleanup Week, April 20-24, and the annual citywide Cleanup Day on April 25.

The City of Rapid City’s Solid Waste Division is organizing volunteer groups and individuals to help clean up public spaces throughout Community Cleanup Week and on the annual Cleanup Day. The City will provide trash bags. Anyone interested in participating is encouraged to email ria.hannon@rcgov.org to determine available spaces. For more information about Cleanup Week, contact the Rapid City Solid Waste Division at 355-3496.

 Rapid Creek (copy)
Rapid Creek flows calmly through Founders Park in this February 2025 file photo. Black Hills Flyfishers and Breadroot Co-op are sponsoring a cleanup of Rapid Creek starting at 10 a.m. Saturday, April 11, 2026. 

Earth Day Expo 2026, 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. Saturday, April 25, at Western Dakota Tech in Rapid City. Rotary club members will do water testing during the expo from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. in EET Lab L126. Anyone can submit a water sample for testing. The cost is $10 per sample. Pick up sample bottles and instructions at Hay Camp Brewing Company on Friday, April 24, between 4:30 and 7 p.m. Drop off samples to be tested at WDT between 10 a.m. and 1 p.m. Students from WDT, South Dakota Mines and area high schools will assist Rotary members with water testing.

Earth Day Expo 2026 is sponsored by WDT, South Dakota Mines and Dakota Rural Action. The expo will provide opportunities for the community to attend workshops, participate in a clothing swap, recycle old electronics, and visit dozens of booths from vendors such as eco-friendly businesses, local producers, and organizations focused on sustainability.

From April through June, Rotary clubs across six states and five districts will participate in events that support the Unite for Water Initiative. For a complete list of local events Rotary clubs will take part in, go to rushmorerotary.org.

title: 40 years of service, friendship: Rushmore Rotary Club celebrates anniversary with kindness
description: Days before Christmas, winds as strong as 100 mph battered the region. When the storms subsided, Rapid City Rushmore Rotary Club members got to work cleaning up debris and branches left behind.
_full_text:
Days before Christmas, winds as strong as 100 mph battered the region. When the storms subsided, Rapid City Rushmore Rotary Club members got to work cleaning up debris and branches left behind.

In honor of its 40th anniversary in March, Rushmore Rotary Club set a goal to perform 40 acts of service, including four storm cleanup events, between Oct. 31, 2025, and March 12, 2026. The club not only achieved their goal but outdid themselves by completing 57.

 Rushmore Rotary cleanup 1
Rushmore Rotary Club members hauled away branches and debris on Jan. 3 on Dan and Arlette Schweitzer's property near Hill City. After the Dec. 18 storm that caused winds as strong as 100 mph, the Schweitzers had 56 downed trees on their property.  

“The beauty of Rotary is our motto is ‘Serving Above Self,’ and we truly live that. We think of opportunities to serve our community and throughout the world,” said Roger Heacock, Rushmore Rotary Club president.

 Rushmore Rotary cleanup 2
Rushmore Rotary Club members load a truck with branches and debris on Jan. 3. Club members organized four different storm clean-up projects after the Dec. 18, 2025, windstorms battered Rapid City and the Black Hills. 

Since Rushmore Rotary Club’s first meeting on March 12, 1986, club members have completed thousands of service projects and fundraisers. The club has grown from 25 members at its first meeting to 92 now. Opportunities to do good and to build friendships attract Heacock and others to the Rotary International organization and have kept Rushmore Rotary Club going strong for four decades.

Initiating 40 acts of kindness in honor of the club’s anniversary was “so much fun,” Heacock said.

The 40 acts of kindness goal demonstrates the club’s creativity, teamwork, and dedication to serving the Rapid City community.

 Rushmore Rotary cleanup 3
Rushmore Rotary Club members saw a fallen tree on Jan. 3. Club members organized four cleanup projects as part of their 40 acts of kindness campaign in honor of the club's 40th anniversary.  

“We had four different times we picked up trees and branches and put them in our trucks and hauled them away (after the December wind storm),” Heacock said.

The 40-turned-57 acts of kindness ran the gamut from assisting Feeding South Dakota with food distribution in Rapid City, to presenting Student of Distinction Awards to Rapid City Alternative Academy students, to Rotary Club members traveling to India earlier this year to vaccinate children against polio. Worldwide, Rotary has nearly eradicated polio by vaccinating three billion children.

Rushmore Rotary Club members gathered on March 24 for an anniversary celebration that highlighted their four decades of uplifting accomplishments.

“We love being together and serving our community,” Heacock said.

Among the club’s biggest contributions is its support of Children’s Home Society. That began in 1989 with a golf tournament, Heacock said.

 Rushmore Rotary projects board
A photo board documenting many of Rushmore Rotary Club's projects was displayed when club members gathered on March 24 at Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City to celebrate the club's 40th anniversary. Rushmore Rotary Club members participate in service projects locally and internationally. 

“We have raised over $2.6 million for the Children’s Home Society, and that’s through an annual golf tournament we have and also a gala,” Heacock said. “We make quite an impact there. Rushmore Rotary has been involved in every building expansion that the Children’s Home Society has done in the Black Hills.”

Rushmore Rotary Club’s influence can be seen and experienced throughout the community. One of its ongoing projects was starting the Sponsor of the Day program at Storybook Island. This helps the park continue to provide free admission. Additionally, Sponsor of the Day helps fund the Carousel House for the park’s refurbished, rare vintage Allan Herschell carousel. Sponsor of the Day also funds the 100 Acre Woods expansion and the Storybook Symphony.

Club members regularly clean up highways, assist with Feeding South Dakota food distributions, serve lunch monthly at Cornerstone Rescue Mission, support students at Rapid City Alternative Academy, and provide in-person and financial disaster relief for events such as a recent apartment fire in Kyle.

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

In 2024, Rushmore Rotary Club partnered with Rapid City Rotary Club to give Rapid City a permanent symbol of peace and goodwill: the clubs installed a Peace Pole in Memorial Park.

Peace Poles are upright posts with the message “May Peace Prevail on Earth” written in a different language on each side. The Peace Pole in Memorial Park proclaims “May Peace Prevail on Earth” in English, Lakota, German and Japanese. Lakota was chosen to honor the Indigenous population, while German and Japanese were chosen in recognition of Rapid City’s Sister Cities: Apolda, Germany, and Nikko City, Japan.

 Peace Pole 2 (copy)
Representatives from the local Lakota community and from Rapid City’s Sister City groups attended the Oct. 5, 2024, Peace Pole dedication along with local members of Rushmore Rotary Club and Rapid City Rotary Club. The Peace Pole is a permanent gift to Rapid City to encourage people to  

Peace Poles are meant to remind everyone to think, speak and act in the spirit of peace and harmony.

“I’m grateful for the Rushmore Rotary Club and all the great work its members do in our community and the world,” Heacock said. “The club has been such a positive influence in my life, and I’ve met so many wonderful people that truly live our motto of ‘Service Above Self’.”

title: Kathleen "Kathy" Denholm
description: November 11, 1941 - March 31, 2026
_full_text:
November 11, 1941 - March 31, 2026

Kathleen E. Denholm (née Samson), 84, passed unexpectedly but peacefully on March 31, 2026 at Aspen Grove Assisted Living Center near her home in Rapid City, SD.

Kathy was the quintessential homemaker, wife, mom and grandmother. Her home was very important to her. It was a place with the prettiest flowers, the most delicious foods, and a record number of ways to decorate with peacocks--her favorite bird. She was a very hard-working woman with high intelligence, an outgoing personality, a quick wit, and a distinctively elegant style. She was striking, yet tastefully refined.

Throughout her adult life, Kathy participated in ladies' bridge clubs where she developed genuine and enduring friendships. Kathy also loved playing Texas Hold 'Em and Omaha Poker in Deadwood, SD. She was a very good player which earned her nicknames like "Poker Kate" and "Diamond Lil".

She loved the people she met over the years and they loved her brilliant smile and classy charm.

Kathy was born November 11, 1941 in Sisseton, SD and was raised in Eden, SD where she attended elementary school at the Sacred Heart Catholic Parish. She worked to support herself while going to Webster High School and graduated in 1959. She married her husband of 64 years, James Denholm, June 17, 1961. Jim and Kathy completed Barber and Cosmetology Schools, respectively, in Sioux City, IA. They then moved to Denver, CO where Jim became a barber and Kathy soon became the mom of 2 daughters only 11 months apart. They then moved to Albuquerque, NM where Jim pursued a career in sales with the Gillette Safety Razor Company. In 1967 they moved to Aberdeen, SD where they added another daughter to their family and finally had a son! In 1974 they moved to Rapid City, SD where Jim worked full-time for the National Rural Electrification Cooperative Association and they concurrently opened a retail auto detailing business called Ming. Kathy kept the books and exercised an astute control over expenses. Her efforts were instrumental in their successful business exit.

Kathy is survived by her loving husband, Jim Denholm, and her four children Kara Rae Linn (Larry) of Casper, WY, Becki Denholm (John) of Las Vegas, NV, Cindy Pfingston (Eddie) of Rapid City, SD, and Jamie Denholm (Jessica) of Rapid City, SD; seven grandchildren, Calvin and Matthew Knauer, Alysa, Katie, and Jace Denholm, and Jamison and Lindsey Pfingston. She is survived by sisters Claudia Gottsleben (Watertown, SD), Mavis Hanten (Watertown, SD), Gloria Engebretson (Burnsville, MN), Judy Meier (Winnebago, IL) and brother Leon Samson (Elko, MN) and over 20 beloved nieces and nephews.

Kathy was preceded in death by her parents, Steve and Hilda Samson; brothers Duane Cordie and Alan Samson; sister Rosemarie Brewer; nephews Danny Hanten and Mike Brewer; and nieces Kristie Hanten and Leslie Brewer.

A Celebration of Life for Kathy will take place on Friday, May 22, 2026 at 1:00 pm at the Kirk Funeral Home, 1051 E. Minnesota St., Rapid City, SD. A reception will follow at 3:00 pm at the Eagles Club, 1410 Centre St., Rapid City, SD.

The family gratefully acknowledges the staff at Dolan Creek and Aspen Grove Assisted Living Centers, and St. Martin's Rehab Hospital, with special thanks to the Aides who took care of Kathy in the final months of her life. She truly loved each one of you!

title: Eldene K. Henderson
description: December 5, 1939 - January 3, 2026
_full_text:
December 5, 1939 - January 3, 2026

Eldene K. Henderson, age 86, of Rapid City, died on Saturday, January 3, 2026 at The Village at Skyline Pines in Rapid City.

Eldene was born on December 5, 1939, in Ipswich, South Dakota, to Walter and Mildred (Wietgrefe) Hieb. She grew up in Selby, where she developed a lifelong love of learning and a deep commitment to her community. While attending South Dakota State University, she met and married Larry Henderson. Together, they raised three children: Dean (Kim) of Rapid City; David (Bonnie) of Rapid City; and Gayle Bunn of Orlando, FL.

After graduating from college, Eldene began her career in the Rapid City school system. She earned her Master's Degree at Black Hills State University and became a school counselor at South Junior High School. There, she touched the lives of countless students and formed many enduring friendships. Her compassion and dedication left a lasting mark on a generation of children.

In retirement, Eldene continued to serve her community, working tirelessly to improve North Rapid and serving on the Board of Directors for Lifeways and on the Sustainability Committee for the City of Rapid City. She was a long-time member of Atonement Lutheran Church, where she sang in the choir and played the piano and organ. She generously donated the land where the church now stands.

Eldene will be remembered for her passion for education and her devotion to family and community. Her legacy lives on in the many lives she enriched. She is survived by her sister, Verla Shumacher of Sioux Falls; her brother, Robert Hieb (Beverly) of Kadoka; her children; ten grandchildren; and many great-grandchildren.

A Celebration of Life Service will be held at 10 a.m. on Friday, April 17, 2026 at Atonement Lutheran Church, Rapid City. Burial will follow in Mountain View Cemetery, Rapid City.
