title: Crews respond to wildfire covering about 20 acres near Hill City, off Highway 87
description: Crews are on the scene of the 87 wildfire off Highway 87.
_full_text:
Crews are on the scene of the 87 wildfire off Highway 87.

The U.S. Forest Service said firefighters responded to the fire, located about 4 miles south of Hill City in Pennington County off Highway 87 (Needles Highway), on Friday afternoon, April 10. The fire is currently estimated at 15-20 acres.

 041126-nws-wildfire-p2.jpg

It's near the KOA campground and people are advised to the avoid the area, the Pennington County Sheriff's Office said.

"Please avoid the area so fire units can work safely," the sheriff's office said in a social media alert.

 041126-nws-wildfire-p1.jpg

Aircraft, including a helicopter, an air attack platform, and two single engine air tankers (SEATs) are currently working the fire along with federal, state, and local resources, the forest service said.

A fire investigator has been ordered to the scene. Please avoid the area so that firefighters can work safely.

As of 4:30 p.m., officials had not issued evacuation orders.

 041126-nws-wildfire-p3.jpg

The weather isn't helping, with the area still in a red flag warning. The warning continues through Saturday, which features potential record highs in the 80s in the forecast.

"Warm, dry, and breezy weather will support critical fire weather conditions over northeast Wyoming and southwest SD Saturday. In addition, there is a slight chance of dry thunderstorms in the late afternoon and evening, mainly over northeast WY," the weather service said in its warning.

title: Man arrested after confrontation with employees at Fleet Farm in Rapid City
description: The Rapid City Police Department said a man was arrested after he was in a confrontation with employees at Fleet Farm before leaving with a number of items he didn’t pay for, including ammunition.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department said a man was arrested after he was in a confrontation with employees at Fleet Farm before leaving with a number of items he didn’t pay for, including ammunition.

Officers were called to the store at 1001 E. Mall Drive around noon on Thursday, April 9, on a report of a theft that had just occurred.

On arrival, police located a man matching the suspect description walking along a nearby access road. When the man saw officers arrive in the area, he allegedly attempted to discard several items from his waistband, police said. Officers detained the man, Michael Gear, 66, of Rapid City, and staff positively identified the suspect.

It was determined he had taken nearly $1,000 worth of merchandise, including handgun ammunition. He was arrested on suspicion of theft and on a parole hold before being transported to the Pennington County Jail. Jail records also show a drug possession charge.

Gear was no longer listed on the Pennington County Jail roster as of Friday afternoon, April 10. Court records only show one charge, first-degree petty theft.

title: Box Elder man arrested for vandalism, starting personal-use fire at Rapid City park
description: A Box Elder man faces charges after allegedly kicking a police cruiser door while being detained for setting a fire at a Rapid City park.
_full_text:
A Box Elder man faces charges after allegedly kicking a police cruiser door while being detained for setting a fire at a Rapid City park.

The Rapid City Police Department said it received a request for a welfare check on Zane Emery, 31, around 6:50 p.m. on Thursday, April 9. Police said Emery had allegedly made recent threatening messages on social media.

Police said officers located Emery in Memorial Park in Rapid City, where he had started a fire on the ground, for personal use. Officers found out Emery was on parole. In the process of being taken into custody, Emery allegedly kicked the door of a patrol car, causing "significant damage," police said.

Officers arrested Emery on suspicion of setting a fire in a city park, vandalism and on a parole hold. Pennington County Jail records also show a resisting arrest charge. He's being held on no bond and is due in court at 10 a.m. on Monday, April 13.

title: Fire burns down Porcupine's One Spirit Okini Market, which aided with food and supplies for dialysis and diabetes patients
description: In the early morning hours on Easter Sunday, April 5 One Spirit founder Jeri Baker received a call from responding officials notifying her a fire had been reported at the One Spirit Okini Market in Porcupine.
_full_text:
In the early morning hours on Easter Sunday, April 5 One Spirit founder Jeri Baker received a call from responding officials notifying her a fire had been reported at the One Spirit Okini Market in Porcupine.

"By the time we got there, the building was pretty much covered with smoke and some flames inside that we could see and then it wasn't long before the fire was shooting through the roof," Baker said.

 Okini Market Fire
On Sunday, April 5, a fire burned down the One Spirit Okini Market in Porcupine. 

No injuries were reported and no other nearby structures were impacted by the fire, she said. Attempts to reach the Martin Volunteer Fire Department for additional details weren't immediately successful.

Baker said an investigation is ongoing into what started the fire, but she believes it began inside the store.

One Spirit is a nonprofit that provides programs for youth and families on Pine Ridge Reservation. The organization is now seeking community donations to rebuild the marketplace.

 Fire
One Spirit Found Jeri Baker stands near the rubble of the Okini Market in Porcupine. 

The market and attached food pantry helped community members afford basic necessities like clothing, household items, blankets and food. The food pantry aimed at providing food items to dialysis and diabetes patients was set to fully open before the fire destroyed the new services.  

The word "Okini" in Lakota means "to share material goods," encompassing the mission of the market, Baker said

Baker said a dialysis clinic was just five miles away, and another clinic was in the town of Pine Ridge.

"People in dialysis, when they're receiving dialysis, they're very cold, because they're cleansing the blood. So, they wanted blankets and so we'd had a blanket drive to get blankets for them. We had things like lotions and other supplies that are necessary for their hygiene and their well-being," Baker said.

She said the building had been used by One Spirit for nearly four years, with the goal of helping out the community not just by their store but through other services, including the Allen Youth Center, creating laundry mats and hosting youth camps.

"I want to thank the people of Rapid City and surrounding areas for reaching out, for saying and in many ways, how much they care and for helping out when something was needed," Baker said.

The nonprofit is asking for monetary donations with the goal of $40,000 to replace some of the items that the store lost in the fire but also for other items for various services.

Baker said they have reached $17,000 in donations so far. Go to facebook.com/OneSpiritPineRidge to donate.

"We will rebuild it, but we will rebuild it in a better way, as much as we can, and as much as the money allows. We will make it so that it can be built in a way that meets the needs of the people," Baker said.

title: Police: Suspect assaulted man at Walmart, took his purchases
description: The Rapid City Police Department arrested a man accused of stealing items from a Walmart customer.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department arrested a man accused of stealing items from a Walmart customer.

Police said officers were called to the Walmart at 100 Stumer Road around 2:50 p.m. on Thursday, April 9, on a report of an assault in the parking lot.

Trev Jacobs, 30, of Lead, was arrested after an investigation.

Police said officers arrived on scene and spoke to several witnesses and determined the assault victim had just exited the store with the items he purchased when another man in a vehicle, Jacobs, began yelling at him.

Jacobs then exited the vehicle and assaulted the alleged victim, police said, and took his recently purchased items. Jacobs then got back in his vehicle and left.

Officers located the suspect in his vehicle a short time later and found the stolen items on him, police said. Police arrested Jacobs on suspicion of simple assault, theft and on a parole hold before taking him to the Pennington County Jail.

He remains at the jail, with no bond set, as of Friday afternoon, April 10, according to jail records. He's due in court at 10 a.m. on Monday, April 13.

title: Jury finds Kyle man not guilty in connection to Rapid City apartment complex stabbing
description: A Pennington County jury found a Kyle man not guilty of attempted murder late Thursday, April 9.
_full_text:
A Pennington County jury found a Kyle man not guilty of attempted murder late Thursday, April 9.

Adam L. Brown's attempted murder trial started Wednesday, April 8. He'd been charged with two counts of first-degree attempted murder and a count of being a habitual offender, according to South Dakota court records. He was found not guilty on all counts.

Brown is 27, according to South Dakota Department of Corrections records.

Brown was arrested in connection to an Oct. 1, 2025, tabbing at an apartment complex at 20 Surfwood Drive.

title: Judge rejects plea agreement in case of missing Navajo elder Ella Mae Begay
byline: By SAVANNAH PETERS and JACQUES BILLEAUD - Associated Press
description: PHOENIX (AP) — A federal judge in Phoenix has rejected a plea agreement that would have allowed a man who admitted to beating a Navajo elder and leaving her for dead to avoid more prison time.
_full_text:
PHOENIX (AP) — A federal judge in Phoenix has rejected a plea agreement that would have allowed a man who admitted to beating a Navajo elder and leaving her for dead to avoid more prison time.

Preston Henry Tolth, 26, now will face trial on charges of carjacking and assault in relation to the 2021 disappearance of Ella Mae Begay. A trial date hasn't been set.

Under the agreement, Tolth would have been released on a sentence of three years of time served in exchange for acknowledging his role in the crime and pleading guilty to a single count of robbery.

Known as a gifted weaver of pictorial rugs, Ella Mae Begay was 62 years-old when she vanished from Sweetwater, Arizona, the small community on the northern part of the Navajo Nation where she was raised and later brought up her own three children.

Begay’s disappearance received national media attention and helped highlight the broader crisis of Indigenous people who go missing or are killed at disproportionate rates. Nearly five years after she disappeared, Begay has not been found.

The rare decision to reject a plea agreement followed anguished testimony from Begay's family members who told the court Tolth should not walk free without revealing Begay's location.

Seraphine Warren described her aunt as a warm and sweet person who opted for “hugs instead of handshakes," and implored the judge not to "give up on her" by accepting a plea agreement that Warren said offered no justice to the grieving family.

“Accountability is not time served,” Warren told the judge tearfully. “It’s about truth, and we still don’t have the truth.”

Gerald Begay, Ella Mae’s son, said, “I feel like the justice system has failed me.”

Tolth, whose father was dating Begay's sister, was identified as a person of interest within days of Begay's disappearance. He initially denied involvement but in a later interrogation, confessed to stealing Begay's truck with her in it, punching her repeatedly and leaving her on the side of the road.

Tolth was set to face trial in 2024, but a federal judge dealt prosecutors a major blow by ruling his confession inadmissible, saying Tolth had been unlawfully coerced by an FBI agent who lied about evidence that law enforcement had against him after Tolth had invoked his right to remain silent.

The U.S. Attorney's office for Arizona and Tolth's public defenders declined to comment on the judge's rejection of the plea agreement.

Tolth did not speak at Thursday's hearing. His attorney asked the judge to consider his unstable childhood and history of homelessness and substance abuse, calling his three years in federal custody a reasonable sentence.

A federal prosecutor said the suppression of Tolth's confession weakened the government's case and that the plea agreement would provide Begay's family with more certainty and finality than a trial with sparse evidence. Begay's family members disagree.

“We want to see this go to trial because we have nothing to lose,” Warren said. “If we lose, at least we fought.”

title: Ticket lottery for Mount Rushmore July 3 fireworks now open
description: The ticket lottery to attend the patriotic festivities and fireworks display at Mount Rushmore National Memorial on July 3 is open, the memorial announced.
_full_text:
The ticket lottery to attend the patriotic festivities and fireworks display at Mount Rushmore National Memorial on July 3 is open, the memorial announced.

The ticket lottery closes Sunday, April 12.

Tickets are required to join the celebration and will be distributed through an online lottery, the memorial said. Each applicant may request up to four tickets and choice of parking at Mount Rushmore or shuttle service from Rapid City. Lottery results will be shared on Tuesday, April 14.

For more details and to submit a lottery application, go to https://www.recreation.gov/ticket/facility/300010.

Per the memorial's website, the park’s iconic setting will host a spectacular Independence Day celebration in partnership with the State of South Dakota.

 Rushmore Fireworks 2020
Fireworks go off at Mount Rushmore in 2020 for the first time in 11 years. The ticket lottery for the July 3, 2026 fireworks is now open. 

"(The) fireworks display will be the highlight of a full evening of festivities, including educational programs, patriotic tributes, and musical entertainment commemorating the 250th anniversary of the birth of the United States," the website says.

In an announcement about the ticket lottery, the memorial said protection of the Black Hills and Mount Rushmore National Memorial remains central to event planning for July 3. South Dakota's wildfire season is already underway.

"The National Park Service is coordinating closely with partner agencies to support a safe and memorable celebration for all attendees while continuing its commitment to caring for the landscape and cultural resources of the Memorial," the memorial said.

Go to https://go.nps.gov/moru250 for more information.

title: Commission delays action on green energy zoning ordinance to May 19
description: The Pennington County Commission voted 5-0 to delay amendments to the county's zoning ordinance that ban utility-scale wind and solar on agricultural land. The proposed changes also provide restrictions for personal-use wind and solar.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Commission voted 5-0 to delay amendments to the county's zoning ordinance that ban utility-scale wind and solar on agricultural land. The proposed changes also provide restrictions for personal-use wind and solar. 

The proposed changes are to Section 317 of the county's ordinance, which governs wind and solar, include regulations to pitched-roof mounted systems.

"For all roof-mounted systems, the elevation must show the highest finished slope of the solar collector and the slope of the finished roof surface on which it is mounted ... The panels will not extend beyond the roof ridge line and must meet Pennington County height restrictions for the zoning district," the proposed ordinance change states.

 Ron Rosskencht
Commissioners Ron Rossknecht (District 1), pictured, and Gary Drewes (District 5) have been opposed to the changes barring utility wind and scale projects from agriculturally zoned land since the beginning. 

Additionally, ground-mounted systems for solar panels must not exceed 15 feet.

Commissioner Deb Hadcock (District 3) noted at the Tuesday, April 7, that a delay was necessary to add additional restrictions to the zoning ordinance.

On March 3, the commission laid the groundwork to ban wind and solar on agricultural land after residents on the east side of the county expressed concerns about projects near New Underwood.

The first step was an amendment to Comprehensive Plan-View to 2040, which passed with a 3-2 vote. Commissioners Ron Rossknecht (District 1) and Gary Drewes (District 5) voted no. Utility-scale wind and solar systems are set to be permitted in industrial districts. 

The county's comprehensive plan is defined as a guide for government officials, residents and developers in regard to "decision-making" for current and potential future development within the county. The plan specifically provides a framework for land-use decisions, public service expansions, economic development, resource management and the general growth of the county.

 Jerry Derr
Commissioners Jerry Derr (District 4), pictured, Deb Hadcock (District 3), and Ron Weifenbach (District 2) have supported changes to the county's comprehensive plan and zoning ordinance for utility-scale wind and solar projects. 

The step currently in the works is changes to the zoning ordinance.

The Pennington County zoning ordinance serves as a regulatory framework indicating land use and development within the county, ensuring that land is utilized in a manner that aligns with community standards and environmental considerations.

It establishes specific zoning districts and outlines permitted uses, building height restrictions and setbacks, thereby promoting orderly development and mitigating potential conflicts between different land uses.

title: 3 seats on Pennington County Commission up for grabs, 2 incumbents not running
description: The Pennington County Commission race is on.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Commission race is on.

Three of the five seats on the board are up for election on June 2. The current seats up for election are held by Commissioners Ron Rossknecht (District 1), Deb Hadcock (District 3) and Gary Drewes (District 5).

Hadcock is the only commissioner seeking re-election.

Rossknecht and Drewes shared with the Journal their reason for stepping away.

"I live near Sheridan Lake, which is in District 1. I had no background in county government back in 2018, but I interfaced with county departments on a weekly basis as I appraised real estate ... I thought with my 30 years of appraisal experience and familiarity with the Black Hills National Forest, I could bring something valuable to the table," Rossknecht said.

 County building outside
The Pennington County Board of Commissioners will meet at 9 a.m. on Tuesday, Feb. 17, at the new Pennington County Administration Building at 900 Concourse Drive in Rapid City. 

He said some of his goals in the position were to complete the Sheridan Lake Road reconstruction, South Rochford Road paving, Rochford Road (chipped sealed), and Mystic Road (mag watered). 

He said these county projects are now complete.

"I want to be a snowbird and go where it's warm in the winter, and you can’t do that and serve as a commissioner," Rossknecht said.

He noted parts of district one include the Black Hills National Forest, which has its own ongoing challenges with mining, recreation, population growth, road systems, infrastructure and forestry.

Drewes told the Journal he did not seek a seat on the county commission to make it a career.

"When I see new voices and interest in performing public service, I am more than willing to step aside," Drewes said.

On Monday, April 6, the Pennington County Auditor's office drew names to determine how names will be listed on the June 2 primary ballot.

 Man casts vote
A man casts his vote on Monday, Jan. 19 at the Pennington County Administration building during early voting in the special election to determine the fate of the Destination District TIF. The official election is scheduled for Tuesday, Jan. 20.  

The official ballot goes:

District one: Zachary Bush, Mike Mueller (both Republicans)

District three: Randy Decker, Deb Hadcock (both Republicans)

District 5: Jefferey Heines (running uncontested as a Republican) and Tim Williams (running uncontested as a Democrat).

Absentee voting begins on April 17 and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  

Voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

 South Dakota 2026 election calendar
Click to read the full document.

title: Updated: Rapid City Council passes ordinance legalizing backyard chickens
description: The Rapid City Council voted 7-2 Monday, April 6, to approve an ordinance legalizing backyard chickens. Council members John Roberts and Lance Lehmann, both of Ward 4, voted no.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Council voted 7-2 Monday, April 6, to approve an ordinance legalizing backyard chickens. Council members John Roberts and Lance Lehmann, both of Ward 4, voted no.

The ordinance would legalize up to six hens on residential property, excluding any roosters. A second reading is required.
Council President Rod Pettigrew was absent from the meeting.

 Stephen Tamang
Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1), right, a supporter for the legalization of backyard chickens, continued his efforts at the April 1 Legal and Finance Committee meeting. Councilman Lance Lehmann (Ward 4), left, voted against. Both voted the same way at the Monday, April 6, Rapid City Council meeting. 

The item has been proposed to the council many times over the last decade, but with additional regulations and stronger enforcement, the pro-hen community and Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) hope to get the ordinance approved.
At the meeting, Councilwoman Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2) said, "I just wanted to say thank you for all of our speakers this evening here in support of this ordinance, and many of you are also here on Wednesday (at the Legal and Finance) meeting. I just really admire the dedication that you have (for) this. I myself am not planning to get chickens or hens in my property, but I do plan on supporting this."
Roberts, who voted no on the ordinance, said, "I've been here for a very long time, and it takes a lot to make me change my mind and change my vote, and I voted against it every single time, and I vote against it again. But that being said, it doesn't really matter, because it's going to pass, because there's not enough votes to stop at this time."
Mayor Jason Salmaun said, "I will support this ordinance so long as we mitigate the impacts on neighbors. And it's much easier to start with a more restrictive ordinance. And over time ... (the city can) always relax certain parts of it later."
Currently, the Rapid City Municipal Code prohibits the keeping of backyard hens except on land that is zoned for agricultural uses. 
The proposed ordinance requires containment for chickens through an enclosed coop and chicken run, an enclosed outdoor space, to keep predators out. The coops must be ventilated and provide a minimum area of at least 2 square feet per chicken. Runs must be attached to the coop and have a minimum area of at least 10 square feet per chicken.
Coops, runs and exercise yards must be 25 feet from any residential dwelling, church, school, or place of business, excluding the residence on the subject property, and 15 feet from any lot line.

The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines. 
"It is unlawful to keep hens where the provisions of § 6.08.021 are not met. Each day a violation occurs is a separate offense. In addition to any other remedies or causes of action the city may possess, the keeping or maintaining of a hen in a manner inconsistent with § 6.08.021 is declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated by the city as provided by state law and City ordinance," the ordinance reads.

Violations include owning roosters, failing to provide feed and water at all times and/or keeping poultry for commercial use.

title: Update: Rapid City police report missing Box Elder woman has been located
description: The Rapid City Police Department said 21-year-old Lacey Nickel of Box Elder has been located.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department said 21-year-old Lacey Nickel of Box Elder has been located.

Nickel was reported missing on April 8, having last been seen in the downtown Rapid City area at around 4 p.m. Nickel has a diminished capacity to care for herself, which is the reason for concern for her safety, police said.

 040926-nws-missing-boxelder-p1.jpg
Lacey Nickel

title: Rapid City Area Schools officials excited as district puts forward $107 million bond vote
description: A $107 million Rapid City Area Schools bond question set to appear on the Nov. 3, ballot reflects strong fiscal responsibility, long-term planning and thoughtful use of taxpayer dollars, district officials said at a press conference the morning after the Rapid City Area Schools Board voted unanimously in favor of the bond.
_full_text:
A $107 million Rapid City Area Schools bond question set to appear on the Nov. 3, ballot reflects strong fiscal responsibility, long-term planning and thoughtful use of taxpayer dollars, district officials said at a press conference the morning after the Rapid City Area Schools Board voted unanimously in favor of the bond.

The Tuesday, April 7, vote was technically the first reading of the resolution, but the overwhelming board support indicates it will pass a second reading and head to voters in just a matter of months. 

"I want to stress the idea that this proposal was really built around a very simple principle, and that principle was, 'How do we best invest our resources wisely now to avoid paying more later?'" RCAS Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse said. "With buildings averaging over 50 years old, we are at a point where major systems must be replaced, not patched."

 Sasse
RCAS Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse, said the district worked to mitigate the impact of the bond to property taxpayers.  

The tax levy will be dedicated to school improvements and construction. The district's most recent bond attempt came in 2020 — with a price tag of $189.5 million. It accrued a vote of 56%, short of the required 60%. Voters in the district have not passed a bond since the 1976. 

What will the bond fund?
The bond, if approved, will finance school improvements, including constructing, renovating and improving school facilities, including but not limited to:
 HVAC energy and efficiency upgrades at Horace Mann Elementary, Meadowbrook Elementary, and North Middle School ($32 million)
Safety and security upgrades at Black Hawk, Grandview, Knollwood, Southwest Middle, and West Middle schools ($3 million)
Renovation and an addition to South Park Elementary ($12 million)
Construction of a new elementary school on the Parkview property ($40 million)
Safety and security improvements at Stevens High School ($20 million)
Furnishing and equipping the improvements
Paying the costs of issuance for the bonds

"This has been a real long, windy road to get to this spot since 2020 and the failed bond referendum," Sasse said.

The cost of the bond and the impact to taxpayers was a major concern throughout the last bond process, Sasse said, adding the district did "everything we could to try to pull that tax burden down."

The bond is estimated to cost .49 cents per $1,000 of assessed property value. If a property is assessed at $400,000, the bond would cost the property owner $196 a year, or $16.33 a month. That's a reduction from $260 a year, or $21.57 a month, if the district had gone forward with the $125 million package.

The District has a tax calculator available on its bond page www.rcas.org/page/bond-information. It allows residents to calculate how much the bond would cost them depending on the assessed value of their property. 

"In most cases, I think it will be much less than they are anticipating. But this way, they can be very informed about what the cost will be to them as an individual taxpayer," Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson said.

 Thompson
Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson smiles as she checks her notes and speaks to the media about the bond. 

Thompson said the bond is about one thing — "giving our students and staff environments that they need to succeed every day."

"We know that students learn best in environments that are safe, comfortable and consistent. This plan addresses all three of those things," Thompson said. 

 Jami Jo Thompson, RCAS
RCAS Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson speaks at the press conference on Tuesday, April 8, about the recently board-approved bond. 

Thompson said the improvements will improve air quality and temperature to support student health, attendance and concentration.

"It reduces overcrowding, which allows our teachers to support our students with more individualized attention and better classroom management, and it eliminates portable classrooms, which ensures that students have access to fully equipped, safe and permanent learning spaces," she continued.

Jade Clement, construction planning manager for RCAS, said the plan focuses on the most critical needs, which are safety systems and space. 

"It really starts with the condition of our current facilities. Many of our building systems, especially when it comes to HVAC and controls, are at or beyond their expected lifespan. That leads to more breakdowns, higher cost and really just inefficient operations throughout the day," Clement said.

 Clement
Jade Clement, construction planning manager for RCAS, said the plan focuses on the most critical needs, which are safety systems and space.  

The plan, if the bond passes, includes the closure of Robbinsdale Elementary. Sasse said it would be "very costly" to bring the building up to adequate educational standards. In some cases, updates can't be made because of the structure of the building. 

 Jade Clement, RCAS
Jade Clement, RCAS construction planning manager, speaks at a press conference on Wednesday, April 8, about facilities improvements needed in the district. 

Sasse said the idea currently is to demolish the building and sell the property, but, "there are so many domino pieces that have to fall into place for that to be a reality."

The timeline of projects slated underneath the potential bond is about three years into the future, Sasse said. He added the district has put in a significant amount of time and effort into factoring in the cost of inflation and materials increasing as time goes on. 

"We've done our very best to guard against something like that happening and having a situation where we thought the project was going to be $5 million, and it comes back at $10 million," he said. "...There's still the element of the unknown, but we think we've done a very good job of trying to protect against something like that happening."

title: What to know about a Pennington County TIF district proposal that would include land within Rapid City limits
description: "I had a conversation with Mr. Tamang, told him straight exactly what was happening," Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach said at the council meeting. "He chose not to listen to me."
_full_text:
Pennington County is looking to create a Tax Increment Financing District on an estimated 172-acre lot located generally south of Catron Boulevard, west of Fifth Street and east of Black Hills Boulevard. Part of the TIF area is within Rapid City limits, and the county has requested permission for that area to be used in the TIF district.
According to Rapid City documents, Black Hills Marketplace would be used for major retail and restaurant developments, as well as single- and multi-family residential developments. No detailed site plans were provided by the city in the committee agenda.
The city limits section is on Catron Boulevard. Ainslie could not give an exact figure of the city's property in the project site, but estimated it at less than 5% of the total 172-acre site. Ainslie also said it's too early in the TIF process for a project cost valuation.
The Rapid City Council voted 9-0 on Monday, April, 6, to delay action on the TIF proposal to its April 20 meeting. Council President Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) was absent from the meeting. The Rapid City Legal and Finance Committee voted 4-0 against the TIF, with Councilman and Committee Chair Greg Strommen (Ward 3) abstaining.
Ainslie said if the TIF resolution is denied by the city, the county could still pass the TIF, excluding the area that is in city limits.
The city took the TIF to the county when the city's work with the developer wasn't able to move forward. "I called up Commissioner (Ron) Weifenbach and asked would the county be willing to (take on the project). He said that they would be willing to consider, that's not a guarantee that the county would agree, but they would be willing to look at a TIF application, and see if they would approve it," Ainslie told the Journal.
Ainslie said at issue is a request for the city to join in the TIF boundary with the county. There is no approval of a TIF project plan at this time. Ainslie also said the city would have some oversight of the project. He said if the city does approve of the TIF, the city still controls the planning and zoning aspects of the project.
Council members Kevin Maher (Ward 3) and John Roberts (Ward 4) both said they support the TIF at the April 6 meeting.

 Tensions rise over Pennington County TIF at Rapid City Council meeting
"I had a conversation with Mr. Tamang, told him straight exactly what was happening," Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach said at the council meeting. "He chose not to listen to me."

title: Mimi Shewey
_full_text:
May 15, 1936 - April 4, 2026

Mimi Charlotte Shewey (known only to telemarketers by her given name Miriam) died April 4, 2026 at the age of 89 after a long and characteristically stubborn fight against Alzheimer's. She was surrounded by her family who lovingly provided dedicated care throughout her lengthy illness.

She was born May 15, 1936 to Judge Lynn and Lenore Milne. Following graduation from Sturgis High School, she enrolled at the University of Wyoming. The next year she decided her parents had been right all along and moved to the University of South Dakota where she graduated in 1958 with a major in business education. Life as a Coyote was a great fit for her between Chi Omega sorority, dance troupes, and the student newspaper.

Mimi's heart was always in the classroom and impacting the lives of children. One of her most cherished times was teaching in Saline, Michigan from 1960-61. She was honored to be invited back for their 50th class reunion.

Returning to Sturgis, she was a frequent presence in classrooms as a substitute teacher and delighted in sharing her knowledge to various grades as a guest speaker and performing as "Danny the Dragon" during elementary library puppet shows. She was a coach for the high school's Soul 20 dance team, a host home for Junior Miss contestants, and was nominated for the South Dakota Education Association Friend of Education award. She was especially honored to receive a "Friend of School Nurses" award from the association of Meade District School Nurses.

The greatest impact to education Mimi made was serving 13 years on the Meade 46-1 School Board. The motivation for her service was to ensure a quality education was accessible to all children. Always a passionate proponent for supporting teachers, she fought for fair teacher pay and their control over curriculum. During the building design of the Bear Butte Elementary School, she insisted teachers serve on the planning committee to ensure they had a voice in ensuring the environment would support successful learning for decades to come.

Known as mom, mother, and Lady, she raised Becky, Amy, and Brett with love and clear expectations. She instilled the principles of kindness, generosity, thoughtfulness, independence, and passionately taking principled stands. She said her grandchildren Hannah, Harrison, and Lucy were "the loves of my life." Just as a grandma should do, she was always up for sharing a "sweet treat." Her home cooked meals were often planned around what to make for dessert, especially a pie. The height and fluffiness of her pie meringue had no culinary rival.

As long as she could get to a vacation destination by rail or sail (never an airplane), she was ready for travel. Sharing the love of travel with family and friends was an annual tradition. On a number of Alaskan cruises, she always packed elastic-waisted pants so she could go to every midnight buffet. What started as a trip to see Phantom of the Opera in Denver, turned into years of hosting bus trips to theater productions with her friend Kris Hubbard.

At home, almost as important as her kids, was the "Spruce Street Coffee Gang" which has been chatting and laughing weekly for over 60 years. Many stray cats on Spruce Street wandered to her house and into her heart. She was happiest with a golden retriever at her feet and a purring cat on her lap while she ate Reese's Peanut Butter Cups.

The church door key for the 1st Presbyterian Church was on the ring right next to the one for her own home. In 1975 she established the Chapel Bell Choir and directed it for 41 years. She adored the women in her bell choir who she lovingly referred to as "The Ding-A-Lings." Mimi was a fixture in the Chancel Choir's soprano row, served on Session, various leadership roles in the church and Circle. Those decades of service to the church were recognized with the Outstanding Senior Presbyterian and Treasured Vessel honors. Mimi always told people she met while on her vacations to come to the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally primarily so they could eat at the Presbyterian rally breakfast. She started as the breakfast cashier and was promoted to her favorite role as the "biscuits and gravy lady."

Often planting flowers well into the dark, her green thumb found peace working in her garden, but her kids weren't always as peaceful about how much weeding they had to do. As a child of the Great Depression, Mimi was frugal in many purchases unless it was an antique auction. Then her bidding went wild for furniture and glassware. At Christmas, her home sparkled with decorations. Anyone who didn't have a place to go were always invited to her overflowing Christmas dinners.

Civic life in Sturgis was where she invested countless hours. The monthly Chapter AY PEO meetings were a mainstay on her schedule over 70 years of membership. Some of the varied organizations she served was as a founding member of the Sturgis Rally Charities Foundation, chairwoman of the first Sturgis United Way campaign, the committee to pass a bond initiative for building the Sturgis Community Center, Sturgis hospital foundation, the Sturgis Area Arts Council, Meals on Wheels, and numerous other charities and associations. The generous financial support she provided for many causes was always done anonymously.

She is survived by her daughters and their spouses Becky and Scott Peterson, Sturgis, SD and Amy and Mark Bradley, Lebanon, OR, her son and his partner Brett Shewey and Melissa Burkland, Fargo, ND, grandchildren Hannah and Jake Lewis, Naples, FL, Harrison Shewey and Lucy Shewey, Fargo, ND, and her across-the-street neighbor and dear friend Carol Walker.

Visitation will be held 4-6 p.m. on Tuesday, April 7 at Kinkade Funeral Chapel. The funeral service will be held at the Sturgis 1st Presbyterian Church at 10 a.m. on Wednesday, April 8 and interment immediately following at the Bear Butte Cemetery. A luncheon will be served afterward at the church's Eriskine Clubroom. In lieu of flowers, memorials may be given to the Sturgis Animal Shelter, 1140 Otter Road Sturgis, SD 57785.

You may leave condolences for the family and watch the service at www.kinkadefunerals.com.

Kinkade Funeral Chapel

1235 Junction Avenue

Sturgis, SD 57785

title: Lynna Faye Engebretsen
description: December 21, 1943 - March 17, 2026
_full_text:
December 21, 1943 - March 17, 2026

Lynna Faye Engebretsen, age 82, of Chadron, Nebraska, and Douglas, Wyoming, passed away on Tuesday, March 17, 2026. A celebration of her life will be held later this spring when the flowers she loved so much are in bloom. Online condolences may be expressed by visiting Lynna's obituary page viewable at www.reverencefuneralparlor.com.

Born in Harrison, NE, to Ted and Rachel (Dunkin) Eberspecher, Lynna Faye was known for her infectious laughter, a touch of sass, and a heart that made everyone feel at home.

Throughout her life, Lynna Faye found joy in service to others, working for many years as a cosmetologist and later as a dedicated nurse's aide in nursing homes. When she wasn't caring for people, she could be found tending to her beloved flower gardens. Her family always came first, and she loved them fiercely. Her spirit will be deeply missed by all who knew her.

Survivors include her daughter, Cherokee Rotherham (Matt); grandchildren, Austin and Adrianne Kennedy; and sister, Gail Haas.

Lynna Faye was preceded in death by her parents; her husband, Nels; her son, Richard Almazon; and sisters, Teddi Lou and Meredith.

title: Trial begins for Kyle man arrested for stabbing at Rapid City apartment complex
description: A Kyle man's attempted murder trial started Wednesday, April 8.
_full_text:
A Kyle man's attempted murder trial started Wednesday, April 8.

Adam L. Brown is charged with two counts of first-degree attempted murder and a count of being a habitual offender, according to South Dakota court records. Brown is 27, according to South Dakota Department of Corrections records.

The Rapid City Police Department said officers were dispatched to the apartment complex at 20 Surfwood Drive around 4:40 p.m. on Wednesday, Oct. 1, 2025, on a report of a stabbing.

Police said as officers were arriving in the area, they spotted a man matching the suspect description fleeing the building on foot. In short order, police found Brown and arrested him on suspicion of attempted murder.

Police said they found the injured person, a man, inside the building with several stab wounds. Medics took the man the hospital for treatment of serious injuries.

title: Pennington County Sheriff’s Office issues alert on missing Hill City man
description: The Pennington County Sheriff’s Office is asking for the public’s assistance in locating a missing Hill City man, Ronald Deloss Blachard, 86. Blachard was last seen in Hill City at 5 p.m. on Monday.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Sheriff’s Office is asking for the public’s assistance in locating a missing Hill City man, Ronald Deloss Blachard, 86. Blachard was last seen in Hill City at 5 p.m. on Monday.

Ronald is a white male, 6-foot-1, 200 pounds, the sheriff's office said.

 image001 (2).png
Ronald Deloss Blachard 

Blachard is very active and is known to frequently walk with a walking stick, the sheriff's office said. His regular walks are typically along Deerfield Road and the Mickelson Trail, but he could be walking anywhere in the area. It is unknown what he might be wearing. There is a concern for Ronald’s health as he suffers from high blood pressure.

Blachard recently arrived in South Dakota from Arizona on Saturday and typically spends the summer in the Black Hills, the sheriff's office said.

If you have seen this individual or have any information on his whereabouts, please contact Pennington County Sheriff’s Office Investigator Marc Davies at 605-394-6115.

title: What to know about the ordinance legalizing backyard chickens in Rapid City
description: The Rapid City Council is one meeting away from approving backyard chickens.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Council is one meeting away from approving backyard chickens.

 Chicken Coop
A chicken coop for sale outside Ace Hardware on West Main Street in Rapid City on April 7, 2026.  

Here's what to know about the proposed ordinance:

Currently, the Rapid City Municipal Code prohibits the keeping of backyard hens except on land that is zoned for agricultural uses.
The ordinance would legalize up to six hens on residential property, with roosters prohibited. The proposed ordinance requires containment for chickens through an enclosed coop and chicken run, an enclosed outdoor space, to keep predators out. The coops must be ventilated and provide a minimum area of at least 2 square feet per chicken. Runs must be attached to the coop and have a minimum area of at least 10 square feet per chicken. The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines.
Setbacks: Coops, runs and exercise yards must be 25 feet from any residential dwelling, church, school, or place of business, excluding the residence on the subject property, and 15 feet from any lot line.
"It is unlawful to keep hens where the provisions of § 6.08.021 are not met. Each day a violation occurs is a separate offense. In addition to any other remedies or causes of action the city may possess, the keeping or maintaining of a hen in a manner inconsistent with § 6.08.021 is declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated by the city as provided by state law and City ordinance," the ordinance reads.
Violations include owning roosters, failing to provide feed and water at all times and/or keeping poultry for commercial use. The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines.
"Any area where hens are kept shall be maintained in a clean condition to avoid dust, odor, waste, and unsanitary conditions. Detectable odors on a neighboring property emanating from an area where hens are kept is a violation of this Section," the ordinance reads.

 Backyard chickens ordinance
Click to read the full ordinance document.

 Updated: Rapid City Council passes ordinance legalizing backyard chickens
Currently, the Rapid City Municipal Code prohibits the keeping of backyard hens except on land that is zoned for agricultural uses.

title: What to know about the NDN Collective lawsuit against the U.S. Forest Service
description: Click to read the full lawsuit document.
_full_text:
NDN Collective, Black Hills Clean Water Alliance and Earthworks have filed a lawsuit in federal court against the U.S. Forest Service over an exploratory mining permit the plaintiffs argue will encroach on a protected Indigenous site, Pe’ Sla.
On Feb. 27, the U.S. Forest Service issued a permit to Pete Lien & Sons for the Rochford Exploration Drilling Project, a large mineral exploration drilling project on USFS-managed public lands near Rochford in west Pennington County. The permit allows the company to use its federal mining claims to look for graphite in the area. The Forest Service granted the permit. A decision memo from the Forest Service allows for 18 boreholes at a maximum depth of 1,000 feet.
The lawsuit names the U.S. Forest Service, U.S. Department of Agriculture and USFS Mystic District Ranger James Gubbels. According to court records, the defendants have been summoned in the case.
Pe' Sla (Bald Spot) is an area in the Black Hills marked by a rocky peak overlooking a large meadow, according to the National Park Service. It sits in the Black Hills west of Rapid City. According to a release from the plaintiffs: Pe’ Sla has been a place of ceremony for the Lakota and other Indigenous peoples for thousands of years. It is a site encompassing several thousand acres, with part of the site owned by sovereign tribal nations of the Oceti Sakowin and part of the site within U.S. Forest Service lands.
A 2014 Memorandum of Understanding between the U.S. Forest Service and representative tribal nations of the Oceti Sakowin acknowledged and agreed that Pe’ Sla was of great cultural and religious importance to the Oceti Sakowin, the plaintiffs said in the release. The 2014 MOU established a 2-mile buffer zone around Pe’ Sla, and the U.S. Forest Service committed to protecting that area. The closest borehole in the exploratory mining area is less than ½ mile from the boundary of Pe’ Sla, within the 2-mile buffer, according to the release.
In a court filing, the plaintiffs said the federal government violated the National Environmental Protection Act by incorrectly using a "categorical exclusion" to avoid factoring in concerns from tribal communities, clean water organizations and others.
According to the Department of Energy's NEPA website, "a categorical exclusion (CE) is a class of actions that a federal agency has determined, after review by (the Council on Environmental Quality), do not individually or cumulatively have a significant effect on the human environment and for which, therefore, neither an environmental assessment nor an environmental impact statement is normally required. The use of categorical exclusions can reduce paperwork and save time and resources." NDN and partners argue the move is a violation of the act.
Gubbels said he was unable to comment on the case, as did the U.S. Forest Service Office of Communication, which directed inquiries to the U.S. Department of Justice. A message to the DOJ on Friday, April 3, hadn't been returned as of Tuesday, April 7.

 NDN Collective vs U.S. Forest Service
Click to read the full lawsuit document.

 USFS Decision Memo
Click to read the document.

 NDN Collective, partners file lawsuit against U.S. Forest Service over Black Hills mining project
NDN Collective, Black Hills Clean Water Alliance and Earthworks argue a mining project would disrupt Pe’ Sla, a sacred site in the hills.

title: Locals weigh in on addiction, incarceration and recidivism at Dusty Johnson roundtable
description: The importance of family, accountability and treatment emerged as the central themes in a discussion among local law enforcement, health providers, lawmakers and Legislature members at a roundtable hosted by South Dakota governor candidate Dusty Johnson.
_full_text:
The importance of family, accountability and treatment emerged as the central themes in a discussion among local law enforcement, health providers, lawmakers and Legislature members at a roundtable hosted by South Dakota governor candidate Dusty Johnson.

The public safety roundtable at the Rapid City Public Library on Tuesday, April 7, focused on how to combat South Dakota’s battle with addiction and high rates of incarceration and recidivism. It also served as a vehicle for Johnson to launch his public safety plan as the congressman vies for the highest office in his home state. 

 Tamang, Johnson and Salamun
Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, center, speaks at a roundtable at the Rapid City Public Library on Tuesday, April 7, as Rapid City Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1), left, and Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun, right, listen.  

In the Republican primary for the governor's race on June 2, Johnson is up against current Gov. Larry Rhoden, state Speaker of the House Jon Hansen and businessman Toby Doeden. South Dakota Democratic Party Executive Director Dan Ahlers is the lone Democratic candidate. 

On Tuesday, Johnson launched his “Safer South Dakota” initiative, which calls for more offender accountability and consequences for drug dealers, rooted in a lower threshold for the legal definition of a drug dealer. Currently, South Dakota law requires proof of intent to distribute. Johnson said he would advocate for legislation making anyone who possesses more than three grams of fentanyl or methamphetamine be charged with intent to distribute. 

“This would make it clear. Listen, if you got a lot of meth and a lot of fentanyl, you are clearly planning to distribute that, we're going to charge you with that crime, we're going to convict you and you're going to prison,” Johnson said.

He also advocated for parental notification when minors overdose and real-time tracking for overdose data, and said he’s in favor of parole board oversight on clemency and sentence commutations.

On the same day, through a South Dakota Department of Corrections press release, Rhoden announced rehabilitation and parole supervision reform plans through the expansion of work with the Council of State Governments Justice Center to improve rehabilitation and parole supervision.

According to a DOC press release, the department signed a contract for $160,000 with the Council of State Governments Justice Center to continue providing support to the Governor’s Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force through March 31, 2027. The contract will fund the completion of a comprehensive programming assessment, reentry assessment, final recommendation development with the Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force (CRTF) and help translate those recommendations into action, according to the release.

Rhoden announced the membership of his task force in October 2025. The task force was established through aexecutive order 2025-07, following the passage and signing of Senate Bill 2, which provides for a new prison in Sioux Falls. Both SB 2 and EO 2025-07 were signed at the conclusion of the special session on Sept. 23, 2025.

In his Tuesday announcement, Johnson said he'd create a task force on the first day in office, and it would be co-chaired by former long-time Pennington County Sheriff Kevin Thom and Minnehaha County State's Attorney Daniel Haggar. 

 Kevin Thom
Rep. Dusty Johnson announced former Pennington County Sheriff Kevin Thom, pictured here, will co-chair a Supervision and Release Task Force alongside Minnehaha County State’s Attorney Daniel Haggar 

"I mean, accountability is a serious problem with state custody, and I hear that every single week, multiple times," Johnson said. "We need to, I think, take the top off the system, look into it, understand what we need to do better."

In response to Rhoden’s announcement, Johnson told a press gaggle at the library, “If our policy leadership causes the state to get its act together, everybody wins. I mean, this is not about credit. This is about saving lives and making our communities safer.” 

A look at South Dakota’s addiction, incarceration and recidivism rates

Addiction and the prison system touches the lives of South Dakotans across the state. 

From 2020 to 2024, there were 357 unintentional and undetermined overdose deaths in South Dakota. There were 73 deaths in 2024, which was a 12% increase from 65 deaths in 2023, but a decrease from the 82 deaths in 2021, according to the state Department of Health. An overwhelming majority of those overdoses over the five year span, 98%, were ruled accidental. 

Also per the DOH, opioids and stimulants account for the largest proportion of overdose deaths in South Dakota. During all five years, methamphetamine was the most common stimulant, and fentanyl was the most common opioid involved in unintentional and undetermined overdose deaths.

 Johnson and Salamun
Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, left, takes notes as Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun, right, speaks about public safety.  

According to the Sentencing Project, South Dakota's imprisonment rate is 370 per 100,000 people, 15th in the country and higher than the national rate of 355 per 100,000.

South Dakota was one of 14 states where recidivism increased between 2011 and 2021 when the state went from 42% to 50%. The Justice Center estimates that the state's recidivism costs $44 per person — the ninth highest rate in the country, South Dakota Newswatch reported, based on data from the Justice Center.

Panelists emphasize importance of family, accountability, treatment

At the meeting, Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun said, “Public safety starts in the heart and in the home. That's the greatest areas of impact.”

“I want the state, and I believe they are, to be as committed to not only enforcing the law, but also the three aspects you need: prevention, treatment and accountability. All three of those things have to be true at the same time,” he said.

Salamun expressed concern the state is going to “nickel and dime” opioid settlement dollars rather than investing in existing programs. 

 Johnson, Salamun and Harlow
Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun, center, speaks at the roundtable as Rep. Dusty Johnson, left, and Dr. Mark Harlow, right, listens.  

The South Dakota Department of Social Services opioid settlement fund dashboard shows the state has received nearly $33 million since disbursement began in December 2022. Of that, $9.6 million has gone to local governments. Rapid City and Pennington County have received a combined $1.5 million and reported about $300,000 in spending. The majority of the funds, 70%, are under the state’s purview. 

Rhoden and Department of Social Services Secretary Matt Althoff announced Thursday, April 2, that $7.8 million in opioid settlement funding would be distributed to 10 organizations across the state, including West River Mental Health and Rapid City Triage 360/Pennington County Sheriff’s Office at the Care Campus, for projects targeting substance abuse.

“Instead of throwing pebbles at the issue, we have an opportunity right now to throw boulders,” Salamun said.

Panelist Lt. Tim Doyle with the Rapid City Police Department has worked in patrol, the city’s schools and the department’s quality of life unit, which interacts with the city’s homeless population.

 Tim Doyle
Lt. Tim Doyle with the Rapid City Police Department listens during a round table on Public Safety hosted by Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, at the Rapid City Public Library on Tuesday, April 7. 

“I think first we have to know what the issue is, and that's the family,” Doyle told the Journal. 

The other piece, Doyle said during the roundtable, is, “We need these kids in school.”

“There's only so much we can do with the family. But the biggest protective factor for those kids is going to be, are they in school?” he said.

Nicole Mitzel, a Republican candidate for South Dakota Senate District 35, said addiction is personal to her as a recovering alcoholic who’s been sober nearly 10 years.

“I have seen firsthand the importance of this in my life. It’s a family disease. My dad’s sober. My sister is sober. I’m sober,” Mitzel said, also offering her perspective as a former correctional officer at the juvenile service center. 

“I think that these kids don't have a purpose. They don't have something that they can look forward to, something that they want to be better for, and I'm just really excited to see what we can do to change that,” she said.

Rapid City Councilman Stephen Tamang is the director of addiction medicine and community health services at Monument Health Rapid City Hospital.

Seated to Johnson’s right, Tamang credited South Dakota’s overdose rates not being higher to, “a pretty amazing network of folks and organizations that care about people with opioid use disorder and other(s) throughout the state.”

 Tamang and Salamun
Rapid City Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1), left, speaks at the roundtable as Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, takes notes.  

“We don't necessarily need a lot of shiny, flashy new things. We need data to look at what's working, and we need to talk to the experts at what is actually working with good data and make those better and supported,” Tamang said.

Seated next to Salamun was Dr. Mark Harlow, physician at Oyate Health Center and volunteer at Cornerstone Rescue Mission and the Cornerstone Respite Center. He emphasized the need for culturally adaptive programs that fit into the Native culture more effectively. 

“There are things we can do to enhance the cultural integration of traditional Lakota values and make the rehab program relevant to that. That would be probably the biggest point of emphasis,” Harlow said.

Harlow highlighted Oaye Luta Okolakiciye (Moving Forward in a Sacred Way), known as OLO, as a program that provides that cultural integration into addiction recovery. OLO, located on the northside of Rapid City, is a Lakota-based nonprofit that provides a “cultural community-based approach to substance and alcohol abuse addiction and behavior health management program.”

He also pointed to the Great Plains Tribal Health Board and Oyate Health as organizations that are working to prevent overdoses. 

“We've distributed over 3,000 doses of Narcan into the Native community and instructed people on how to use them properly,” Harlow said.

Johnson commended the group’s compassion in their discussion. 

“This is not hopeless. And there are good things happening here. Those good things I think are so often happening quietly and frankly, without coordination across the state. I think we've got an opportunity to do better with that,” he said.

 Johnson, Lt. Doyle talk accountability
Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, who is running for governor, and Rapid City Police Lt. Tim Doyle, talk about offender accountability. 

 PHOTOS: Local leaders meet with gubernatorial candidate to discuss addiction, treatment

title: Rapid City Area Schools moves bond referendum forward, set to appear on November ballot
description: The Rapid City Area Schools Board voted unanimously on Tuesday, April 7, to place a $107 million bond referendum on the ballot.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Area Schools Board voted unanimously on Tuesday, April 7, to place a $107 million bond referendum on the ballot.

As long as the item passes a second reading, voters on Nov. 3, 2026, will be asked to decide whether to add a tax levy dedicated to school improvements and construction. The district's most recent bond proposal attempt came in 2020 — with a price tag of $189.5 million. It accrued a vote of 56%, short of the required 60%.

The bond, if approved, will finance school improvements, including constructing, renovating and improving school facilities, including but not limited to:

HVAC energy and efficiency upgrades at Horace Mann Elementary, Meadowbrook Elementary, and North Middle School ($32 million)
Safety and security upgrades at Black Hawk, Grandview, Knollwood, Southwest Middle, and West Middle schools ($3 million)
Renovation and an addition to South Park Elementary ($12 million)
Construction of a new elementary school on the Parkview property ($40 million)
Safety and security improvements at Stevens High School ($20 million)
Furnishing and equipping the improvements
Paying the costs of issuance for the bonds

RCAS Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse said the closure of Robbinsdale Elementary is part of the plan.

For the bond to pass, 60% of voters would need to approve it. The referendum is a reduction from a $125 million bond sent out in a survey last year. The original set of critical projects were identified at a ticket price of $214 million. 

Weighted support from the survey came in at 58%. Two major concerns that emerge was the tax impact and the overall cost, Sasse said.

"The district heard this message and we acted on it," Sasse said. 

The adjustment down to $107 million reduced the costs to property owners by 33%, Sasse said.

The bond is estimated to cost .49 cents per $1,000 of assessed property value. If a property is assessed at $400,000, the bond would cost the property owner $196 a year, or $16.33 a month. That's a reduction from $260 a year, or $21.57 a month, if the district had gone forward with the $125 million package.

"We are making informed and educated decisions to prevent being in a crisis," Jamie Clapham, board vice president and Area 6 representative, said at the meeting, noting she worries about what the district will do if facilities fail and children have nowhere to go. 

RCAS Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson said the plan is fiscally responsible, based on research and responsive to community feedback.

"It is carefully planned to work in conjunction with our existing capital outlay funds, and most importantly, it is necessary to meet our students' safety and learning needs. Every student within RCAS deserves a safe, comfortable place to go, and this plan, it supports the important work that our teachers are doing every day, and it invests in our students," Thompson said.

Christine Stephenson, Area 5, said the board's vote is, "the beginning of a long and exciting campaign to get the community fired up about this." 

Angela Rafferty, Area 3, asked the community to invest in staff and students, who deserve to be comfortable in an environment where they can learn.

Katy Urban, Area 7, said one of the reasons she ran is because she wanted to see facilities addressed. 

"It's almost a little surreal that it's finally going on a ballot," she said.

Board President Troy Carr, Area 2, said the referendum is putting kids first.

"The last comment I want to make to the community is your support for this referendum is an investment in the future of our district," Carr said.

Walt Swan, Area 4, and Donna Moore, Area 1, did not make comments on the bond, but voted in favor of it.

title: Rapid City man enters plea in rape case, to be sentenced in June
description: A Rapid City man entered an Alford plea on Tuesday, April 7, for fourth-degree rape.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man entered an Alford plea on Tuesday, April 7, for fourth-degree rape.

David Sprague appeared for his change of plea hearing before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

An Alford plea lets the defendant maintain their innocence while admitting the prosecution has enough evidence to convict at trial. The plea is still legally treated as a guilty plea leading to a conviction, sentencing and potential prison time. 

Sprague was initially indicted for first-degree rape for an incident that occurred on Aug. 17, 2023 involving an 11-year-old. However, through a plea agreement with the state, Sprague entered an Alford plea to fourth-degree rape and in exchange, the state requested local jail time or probation at sentencing.

In South Dakota, fourth-degree rape is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. Whereas first-degree rape is a Class C felony with a maximum sentence of life in prison and a $50,000 fine, it also has a mandatory minimum of 15 years in prison. 

Sprague's sentencing is set for 3:30 p.m. on June 9.

title: Military thriller authors host workshops April 10, 11 for veterans, readers, writers
description: David Bruns and J.R. Olson , best-selling authors of military thrillers, will host three in-person events in Rapid City on April 10 and 11.
_full_text:
David Bruns and J.R. Olson, best-selling authors of military thrillers, will host three in-person events in Rapid City on April 10 and 11.

Bruns and Olson are United States Navy veterans from Minnesota whose novels are often described as delivering “tomorrow’s headlines today.” Bruns and Olson have collaborated on novels that have collectively sold more than a quarter-million copies. The authors will engage with local readers, writers, and the military veteran community while in Rapid City.

‘From Military Service to Military Thrillers’

Bruns and Olson will address the Order of Daedalians, a fraternal organization of military aviators, at 5:30 p.m. Friday, April 10, at VFW Post 1273. Seating is limited; to check seating availability, contact Mike Beason at f4epilot@rap.midco.net. This exclusive presentation, “From Military Service to Military Thrillers,” will focus on the intersection of fact and fiction in Bruns and Olson’s national security thrillers, and the authors will discuss their creative partnership that has lasted more than a decade.

Bruns, a former submarine officer, and Olson, a retired naval intelligence officer, co-write novels that focus on security threats of our modern world.

The Writer’s Life and Fiction Writing Workshop

Bruns and Olson will team up with Rapid City educator and writing instructor Molly Barari of Heirlooms Creative Writing for a two-hour intensive workshop from 9 to 11 a.m. Saturday, April 11, at Minneluzahan Senior Center. This workshop is designed for writers of all ages and skill levels.

The workshop is free to the public; RSVPs are encouraged. To RSVP or for more information, contact Barari at mollybarari@gmail.com.

Bruns and Olson describe the first hour as “a deep dive into the nuts and bolts of a professional author’s career.” Drawing on their backgrounds as Navy veterans and best-selling thriller authors, they will discuss storytelling, publishing, and how real-world military experience informs their fiction.

Throughout their more than decade-long partnership, Bruns and Olson have published novels with a traditional major publishing house, released a series of books through a mid-sized press, have crowdfunded some novels, and have independently published their work. Their most recent novel, “Weapons Free,” is a modern-day submarine thriller in the style of “The Hunt for Red October.”

During the second hour, Barari will guide participants through a live fiction writing exercise. Participants can share their work with Barari, Bruns and Olson in a supportive environment.

‘Real-World Threats to Page-Turning Fiction’

Bruns and Olson will present a lively discussion, “Real-World Threats to Page-Turning Fiction,” from 3 to 4 p.m. Saturday, April 11, at Rapid City Public Library. The presentation is free and open to the public. The authors will discuss their military thrillers and offer a behind-the-scenes look at their creative partnership that has spanned more than a decade.

Their latest release is “Weapons Free,” a modern-day submarine thriller in the style of Tom Clancy's “The Hunt for Red October.”

For more information about the authors, visit bruns-olson.com.
