title: Don Phillips
description: August 17, 1940 - March 15, 2026
_full_text:
August 17, 1940 - March 15, 2026

Don Lee Phillips, 85. Don passed away in his home in Mission, Texas. Don was born in Buffalo, S.D. The second son of five to Chester (Chet) and Leah Vroman Phillips He grew up on the family ranch in the Slim Buttes of South Dakota. It was an idealic life and he often shared many wonderful memories of life on the ranch. Roping, gathering cattle, branding and helping neighbors do the same. All five boys learned to ride at an early age and learned the cowboy way from both their parents. Donnie graduated from Belle Fourche High School in 1958. He was the State high school tie down calf roper of the year. That earned him a trip to the National High Rodeo Finals in Slidell, La. He went on to continue his education at SDSU. Don married Sandra Vieths in 1961. They had two children, Colette and Ned. Don was involved in the country life of ranching and raising a family. He began his second career in law enforcement. He was on the police force in Scottsdale, Ariz. and a Deputy for Pennington County. It was a perfect fit for his personality. He was never quick to anger and always had the ability to see another person's perspective. In 1996 Don married his love of 14 years, Pat Uhrig. He gained a bonus family of a daughter, Natalie, and two sons, Rodney and Jason. Don retired 2003 from Pennington County as the Director of the 911 Dispatch center. Surviving this wonderful man are his wife of 30 years, Pat; daughter, Colette (John) Adams of Hampton, Tenn; son, Ned, Natalie (Kevin) Baumberger of Cleveland, Ohio; Rodney Uhrig Rapid City and Jason (Heidi) Uhrig of Gillette, Wyo.; six grandchildren, Carmen and Alisha Adams, Lyndea and Austin Baumberger and Lane and Colt Uhrig; four great grands, Randi, Rylee Reagan and Fynlee; brothers, Todd (Jerilee) Phillips Sheridan, Wyo. and Noel (Dawn) Phillips Benson, Ariz. Don was as kind and patient of a man that you could ever meet. He and Pat loved to go dancing (country music of course) and loved golfing four or five times a week. And to tell life stories. He will be missed by many. His love for family and friends was absolutely unconditional. A celebration of his life will be held at the cabin in Hermosa later this summer.

title: Nolan D. Pottorff
description: April 2, 2026
_full_text:
April 2, 2026

Nolan D. Pottorff, of Rapid City, S.D., passed away on April 2, 2026. Funeral arrangements are being made with Kirk Funeral Home.

title: Rapid City Council delays Pennington County TIF vote to April 20 meeting
description: Pennington County is looking to create a Tax Increment Financing District on an estimated 172-acre lot located generally south of Catron Boulevard, west of Fifth Street and east of Black Hills Boulevard.
_full_text:
Pennington County is looking to create a Tax Increment Financing District on an estimated 172-acre lot located generally south of Catron Boulevard, west of Fifth Street and east of Black Hills Boulevard.

Part of the TIF area is within Rapid City limits and the county has requested permission for that area to be used in the district.

The Rapid City Council on Monday, April 6, voted 9-0 to delay action on the resolution until the next council meeting on April 20. Council President Rod Pettigrew was absent from the meeting.

According to city documents, the lot would be utilized for major retail shopping and restaurant developments, as well as single and multi-family residential developments. The city limits section is on Catron Boulevard. 

Rapid City Finance Director Daniel Ainslie could not give an exact figure of the city's property in the project site, but estimated it at less than 5% of the total 172-acre site. Ainslie also said it's too early in the TIF process for a project cost valuation.

The project details additional improvements to Black Hills Boulevard and Fifth Street. The Legal and Finance Committee voted 4-0 against the TIF, with Councilman and Committee Chair Greg Strommen (Ward 3) abstaining.

Stay with RapidCityJournal.com for more coverage on the vote.

title: Reversing downtown decline in Centerville, South Dakota
byline: By BART PFANKUCH/South Dakota News Watch - South Dakota News Watch
description: This small eastern South Dakota town is well known for its deep agricultural roots, its popular Fourth of July celebration and its regionally famous bakery specializing in bi-colored “zebra donuts.”
_full_text:
This small eastern South Dakota town is well known for its deep agricultural roots, its popular Fourth of July celebration and its regionally famous bakery specializing in bi-colored “zebra donuts.”

But now, Centerville has a new claim to fame: The town of about 900 people located 40 miles southwest of Sioux Falls has become known as a statewide leader in downtown redevelopment.

The successful effort to buck the trend of declining Main Street districts in small South Dakota towns has been fueled in part by a progressive approach to development by local leaders and a sense of entrepreneurship by local business owners.

But the redevelopment of Centerville has largely been driven by implementation of a strong vacant building ordinance that has become a model for other municipalities across the state.

“Vacancies in the central business district are a detriment to your city,” said Jared Hybertson, economic development coordinator in Centerville. “I hear from a lot of other communities looking for guidance because this is a prevalent problem across the state.”

Not a good look for downtowns

A decade ago, Centerville had 14 vacant storefronts in a downtown that encompasses only a few square blocks.

Some properties were occasionally used as rentals, a few served as storage for junk or remnants of former business and others were simply withering toward condemnation. The look was one of decline that was not enticing to potential new visitors, residents or business owners.

“I was frustrated at the number of vacant buildings we had,” Hybertson told News Watch. “Perception-wise, as a small town, you’re either growing or you’re dying, and there isn’t much in between.”

In 2017, the Centerville City Council passed the vacant buildings ordinance that uses a registration system, building inspections, warning letters, threats of fines and eventual fines to prompt property owners to shape up or sell their buildings to make way for new opportunities.

With the power of the ordinance in hand, Hybertson and a team of committed local officials and business people have ushered a major turnaround in the downtown.

Over roughly the past decade, the Centerville Development Corporation has undertaken efforts to either buy and rehabilitate or buy and tear down nearly a dozen dilapidated or deserted buildings, many of which are now occupied by new businesses.

Centerville has new additions downtown that include a women’s clothing boutique, a salon, a tattoo parlor, a discount retail store, two restaurant locations and a historic building converted into a combination museum/art gallery/visitors center. Plans are in place for further redevelopment projects that include loft housing and an event center.

The new businesses have made Centerville more of a destination for visitors, strengthened the overall economy and led to increases in sales tax revenues.

“It’s really been a joint citywide effort,” Hybertson said. “The ordinance definitely allows us to give property owners a little push to get something done with those properties.”

Building a model for downtown rebirth

Many rural communities in South Dakota have suffered population and economic downturns over the past few decades.

Cities with numerous vacant downtown buildings can suffer from a sense of general decline that can turn away visitors, new residents and potential businesses, said Paula Jensen, a vice president of the community development group Dakota Resources.

“We see what the end game is if we don’t pay attention to this,” she said.

Jensen said it is unlikely that downtown districts in small towns will return to the days decades ago when people gathered frequently to sell agricultural goods, buy all the things they need and make personal connections.

But attracting new commercial, retail and service businesses to a downtown can spur overall community growth, Jensen said.

“This isn’t going to take us back to the 1950s when everybody came to town on Wednesday and Saturdays to sell their eggs and their cream,” she said. “But the very purpose of maintaining Main Streets is getting business owners into those buildings to rejuvenate downtowns and make them lively places again.”

Jensen said downtown revitalization is a long-range project that requires a big and constant commitment and that replacing vacant storefronts with new businesses is just one part of the effort.

“This isn’t just putting flowers on Main Street to beautify it,” she said. “It’s making a decision to incrementally improve their Main Street and show that they’re in it for the long haul, so people will want to establish a business there.”

Jensen said Dakota Resources works to share and promote successful ideas that are replicable in other communities across the state. To that end, the Dakota Resources welcomed Hybertson to a community meeting it hosted in Murdo last fall where he gave a slide presentation on the vacant building ordinance to community leaders from around the state.

Building relationships to revive buildings

While the ordinance gives Centerville a mechanism to address vacancies, it still takes a great deal of patience and negotiation to get properties sold or redeveloped. Hybertson said.

He and city officials use the ordinance as a cudgel tempered by genuine kindness and a willingness to communicate with and compromise with owners of vacant properties. The approach is somewhat of an iron fist in a velvet glove.

“It’s really about fostering a relationship and building trust because a lot of times these owners just don’t know what to do with their buildings,” Hybertson said.

The effort to convert an aging historic building into the museum/community center provides a good example. The Sioux Falls owner of the building had tried but failed to start businesses there and was hesitant to give up the property.

After numerous discussions with Hybertson, she agreed to sell for $6,000. With about $100,000 in grants and another $100,000 from the development corporation, the renovated building that is now a central gathering place and tourist destination.

“It creates a good vibe because a vibrant downtown makes everyone feel better about their community,” Hybertson said.

A good idea, but tough to implement

Other towns are following Centerville’s lead, but implementing policies that impose on private property owners can be a tough go in South Dakota.

“It can get tricky and controversial when it comes to property rights,” Hybertson said.

He acknowledged that it takes patience, resilience and sometimes a bit of luck and good timing to make the ordinance effective. Even after improvements are made, maintaining a profitable business is a challenge in small towns, as evidenced by the recent closure of a Mexican restaurant and a coffee shop in Centerville.

“Sometimes it feels like one step forward and two steps back,” he said.

Officials from the city of Hurley contacted Hybertson and ultimately enacted a vacant building ordinance in October 2024, but the policy has not been implemented yet, said city finance officer Marcy Hillman.

Hurley is a town of 385 people located 30 miles southeast of Sioux Falls, and it has struggled to maintain a vibrant downtown, Hillman said. The city has no grocery store and lost a salon that operated downtown but celebrated the recent reopening of the Hurley Bar & Grill, she said.

The town has a handful of vacant buildings downtown, but it has been difficult to enforce its ordinance because Hurley has only four city employees and relies solely on Hillman to perform many of the municipal tasks.

“There’s a lot of towns in South Dakota in the same position as us,” Hillman said. “Our council wants to get something going downtown, but it’s challenging when you don’t have someone who can focus just on that.”

A passion for clothes and community

Christen Cunningham is a benefactor of the effort to find the best use of existing buildings in downtown Centerville.

A few years ago, Cunningham and her husband left Colorado to move back to the town where her parents and grandparents lived, and she bought a building she has converted into a thriving women’s clothing store.

Christen & Company boutique is located in a building formerly used by the late beloved town historian Sherree Dee Schmiedt to store artifacts and collectibles from Centerville’s history. Many of those remnants of local history were moved into the museum/community center a few doors down the block after the renovation was complete.

While the boutique is not operating in a storefront directly influenced by the city’s vacant building policy, Cunningham said her business is an example of the benefits of a community-wide effort to inject energy into its downtown.

“It’s creating room for opportunity,” she said. “To this day, people tell me they can’t believe this boutique is operating in Centerville.”

The spirit of re-use may continue, as Cunningham has plans to renovate the second floor of the clothing store into short-term rental units, and she has purchased the former bowling alley next door and hopes to convert it into additional space for the boutique or possibly an event hall. Meanwhile, her daughter has moved to Centerville and is operating a beauty salon down the block.

“I like that you can actually talk to people here and realize how connected you are to one another,” she said.

By focusing on downtown redevelopment, and finding new uses for existing structures, Centerville has become a place that is embracing its roots and celebrating its past while also fostering a bright future, Cunningham said.

“The biggest blessing is the relationships we’re making here, because we’re not just selling clothes,” she said. “My mission is to make women feel important and valued and that they’re seen so they feel better when they leave than they did when they came in.”

This story was originally published by South Dakota News Watch and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

title: Rapid City man arrested on reckless burning allegation
description: A Rapid City man was arrested on Thursday, April 2, after police found a pile of branches on fire in a residential area. The incident occurred the same week Black Hills agencies sent out their social media campaign for residents to be more mindful about fire safety.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was arrested on Thursday, April 2, after police found a pile of branches on fire in a residential area. The incident occurred the same week Black Hills agencies sent out their social media campaign for residents to be more mindful about fire safety.  

Angelo Shiraldi, 50, was arrested on suspicion of reckless burning and obstruction. 

At around 2:30 p.m. on Thursday, police responded to 220 E. Denver Street for a report of a man who set a pile of branches on fire and left the scene in a car. Police noted the fire was smoldering near a residence, which firefighters began working to extinguish.

As the firefighters worked, the man, identified as Shiraldi, returned. Police said he began to yell at the firefighters and obstruct them from putting the fire out. He was later detained. 

As of Monday, April 6, Shiraldi was not listed on the Pennington County Jail's roster and no recent cases are listed in online court records. The courthouse was closed for the Easter weekend, including Monday.

title: Rapid City woman arrested after allegedly striking officer in the face
description: A Rapid City woman was arrested Saturday, April 4, after she allegedly struck a police officer in the face and continued to kick him as he attempted to detain her.
_full_text:
A Rapid City woman was arrested Saturday, April 4, after she allegedly struck a police officer in the face and continued to kick him as he attempted to detain her. 

Victoria Craig, 24, was arrested on suspicion of simple assault, simple assault on law enforcement and a parole hold. 

At around 2:35 p.m. Saturday, police responded to an apartment at 120 Surfwood Drive for a report of a woman assaulting another woman. 

Upon arrival, police found Craig leaving the apartment with blood on her hands, the department said. She was told to stop and refused. When an officer grabbed her arm, she struck him in the face. 

Craig was taken to the ground, where police said she continued to kick the officer as other officers came to assist. 

Witness statements said Craig was the one responsible for the initial assault and police discovered she was on parole. According to court records, Craig was convicted in 2021 of aggravated assault against law enforcement for a 2020 incident. 

As of Monday, April 6, Craig is being held at the Pennington County Courthouse with no bond.

title: Rapid City police investigate reports of gunshots on Easter Sunday, one home struck
description: The Rapid City Police Department is investigating a shooting that occurred on Sunday, April 5.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department is investigating a shooting that occurred on Sunday, April 5. 

At around 1:50 p.m. police responded to the 1100 block of Northeast Drive for a report of gunshots heard in the area. 

An owner of a home in the 1100 block of Dilger Avenue showed police that there was a bullet hole through one of their windows. Several shell casings were retrieved from the area. No other damages or injuries were reported.

The case is being investigated by the RCPD's Criminal Investigations Division for further follow up.

title: Chapel Lane Bridge project delayed due to grant denial; town hall meeting scheduled
description: On Monday, April 6, Rapid City Officials announced a delay on the Chapel Lane Bridge replacement project due to grant funding denial. The city is still working on other funding sources to continue the infrastructure improvements.
_full_text:
On Monday, April 6, Rapid City Officials announced a delay on the Chapel Lane Bridge replacement project due to grant funding denial. The city is still working on other funding sources to continue the infrastructure improvements.

Rapid City Public Works Director Mike Theis said, "The status right now is we're going to postpone the bridge project. We were hoping to be able to go out for bids at this point, and unfortunately, since we did not get the bridge grant, we'll have to delay that process until we find and secure funding for the bridge replacement program going forward."

Theis said the city was hoping to receive funding from the 2026 South Dakota Bridge Improvement Grant Program for the renovations that would cover 80% of the $8 million renovations cost.

 Chapel Lane Bridge
Chapel Lane Bridge, built in 1974, has ongoing infrastructure damages that warrant replacement, city officials said.  

"There were a total of 57 bridge projects that applied for the grants in the (Department of Transportation). They selected 10 projects across the state," Theis said on why the grant was denied.

Due to the delay, the city will be hosting a townhall meeting on Tuesday, April 14, at 5:30 p.m. inside council chambers at City Hall.

According to Theis, the bridge was constructed in 1974 as a replacement for the bridge destroyed in the 1972 flood. The existing bridge has served the residents of the Chapel Valley neighborhood as the only entry and exit point.

In April 2025, a hole was discovered in the concrete deck of the bridge, where repairs were made. In August 2025, weight restrictions were placed until a new bridge can be constructed. 

The weight limits are 18 tons for a single-axle vehicles, 30 tons for double-axle vehicles.

"Those will stay in place until we are able to replace the bridge. But as of right now, we just continue to monitor the bridge to look for any additional damages. But at this point, we'll just keep the same restrictions in place that we've had in place for the last year," Theis said.

 Mike Theis
Rapid City Public Works Director Mike Theis spoke to the press on Monday, April 6, announcing a delay on Chapel Lane Bridge replacement project due to denial of grant. 

He added the city is looking into the plausibility of adding another access point to the community, but he couldn't give further information at this time.

"Again, we were hopeful that we would have the grant dollars available with that. As you know, not an option for this year. We're looking at potentially applying for the grant to get next year, as well as finding other funding sources, but it is one of the high priorities for us in the city," Theis concluded.

title: Flags fly at half staff in Sturgis, Meade County to honor former mayor Forrester
description: All flags on city-owned property in Sturgis will fly at half staff in honor of former Sturgis mayor Kevin Forrester, who died Easter Sunday, April 5.
_full_text:
All flags on city-owned property in Sturgis will fly at half staff in honor of former Sturgis mayor Kevin Forrester, who died Easter Sunday, April 5.

In a formal proclamation issued Monday, April 6, by the City of Sturgis, Mayor Pro Tempore Tony Dargatz recognized Forrester’s years of dedicated service to the community, noting his leadership, integrity, and lasting impact on the City of Sturgis.

“Forrester faithfully served the City of Sturgis with honor, integrity, and dedication, providing steadfast leadership and a deep commitment to the citizens of this community,” the proclamation states.

City officials also highlighted Forrester’s strong sense of responsibility and vision, as well as the meaningful contributions he made to local government and the betterment of the community.

“It is fitting and proper that the City of Sturgis recognizes his life, legacy, and service with dignity and respect,” the proclamation states.

The proclamation from the City of Sturgis directs that flags be lowered immediately and remain at half-staff through the day following Forrester’s memorial service, at which time they will be returned to full staff. The date and other details about Forrester’s memorial service are expected to be announced in the coming days, according to a news release from the City of Sturgis.

Forrester, 58, resigned as Sturgis mayor on March 23 due to health reasons. Forrester was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in the fall of 2025.

Forrester served 28 years with Meade County as facility manager and IT director. He was appointed to Sturgis City Council (Ward 4) to fill the seat vacated by Steve Keszler in 2021 after Keszler resigned.

Forrester became mayor in October 2024, after then-mayor Angela Wilkinson resigned. Forrester was unopposed when he ran for mayor in the April election in 2025.

According to biographical information provided by the City of Sturgis, Forrester led major infrastructure and technology improvements, including a remodel of the historic Erskine School building, and improvements to the county election system. Forrester also served as Meade County Auditor after being appointed to the position in 2021 after the resignation of Lisa Schieffer. Forrester retired from Meade County service in June 2023.

Forrester was a lifelong advocate for outdoor recreation and trails, and he had more than 30 years of experience building and maintaining trail systems. Forrester was a founding member of local trails initiatives and he was the founder of K4 Trails in 2021.

 Harley great-grandson and Sturgis Mayor Kevin Forrester
Sturgis Mayor Kevin Forrester, right, spoke with Bill Davidson, great-grandson of one of the four founders of Harley-Davidson, during the 85th Sturgis Motorcycle Rally in August 2025. Forrester resigned as mayor of Sturgis on March 23, 2026, citing health reasons, saying he wanted to focus on his family and his health. Forrester was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in the fall of 2025. He died April 5, 2026. 

Forrester served two terms as chairman of the Bureau of Land Management Resource Advisory Council.

In recognition of Forrester’s service, the Meade County Board of Commissioners also issued a proclamation on Monday, April 6, that all flags on Meade County property be flown at half-staff, effective immediately until the day after Forrester’s memorial service.

“Kevin Forrester’s commitment, integrity and contributions have left a lasting impact on colleagues, the organization and the community,” the Board of Commissioners said in the proclamation. “His years of service reflect not only professional excellence but also a deep sense of responsibility and pride in his work. He will be remembered with respect, gratitude and honor.”

When he announced his resignation last month, Forrester said he was grateful for the professionalism, compassion, and support he received from city staff and many others in the community.

“The strength of the city does not rest with one person, it rests with the team,” Forrester said. “I have been proud to serve alongside the amazing city of Sturgis staff and council.”

A lifelong Sturgis resident, Forrester graduated from Sturgis Brown High School in 1985. He is survived by his wife, Nicole Forrester, three sons, Ty, Chad and Brooks, and his sisters, Debra Hughes of Rapid City and Darla Burge of Sturgis.

Kevin Forrester Day

The City of Sturgis and Meade County jointly honored Forrester’s life and legacy with the official proclamation of “Kevin Forrester Day.” Dargatz and Meade County Commission Chair Gary Deering read the proclamation on Thursday, April 2, and the proclamation was posted to social media.

 Forrester
Kevin Forrester 

The Sturgis City Council will read the proclamation during its regular meeting on Monday, April 6, followed by a reading by the Meade County Commission on Tuesday, April 14.

Kevin Forrester Day will be observed on June 10, which coincides with the first Sturgis Music on Main event of the summer. Kevin Forrester Day will be an opportunity for the community celebrate Forrester’s legacy and the impact he had on Sturgis and Meade County.

The proclamation said throughout his career, Forrester was known for his steady leadership, commitment to community, and a deep belief in the importance of public service. The proclamation recognizes not only his professional accomplishments, but also his dedication to family, his love of the outdoors, and his lasting impact on the people and community he served.

title: Authorities release name of Rapid City man killed in Highway 44 crash
description: Authorities have released the name of a Rapid City man who died in a single-vehicle crash near Scenic in Pennington County.
_full_text:
Authorities have released the name of a Rapid City man who died in a single-vehicle crash near Scenic in Pennington County.

The South Dakota Highway Patrol said John Waylon Miller, 46, died from injuries suffered in the April 2 crash.

The patrol said the crash occurred around 6:57 a.m. on South Dakota Highway 44 near the 92-mile marker, about 5 miles east of Scenic.

The patrol said Miller was driving a 2007 Toyota 4Runner eastbound on Highway 44 near mile marker 92 when he failed to negotiate a curve and went off the roadway into the north ditch, over-corrected across the highway and entered the south ditch, where the vehicle rolled before coming to rest. He was not wearing a seat belt, the patrol said.

The driver sustained fatal injuries in the crash, the patrol said. An investigation into the crash continues.

title: Focusing on the positive: Ukrainian photographer captures Rapid City's happiness
description: When Viktoriia Vasylysyn and her sister stepped onto the pavement of Rapid City with the weight of their entire world compressed into a few bags and a mere $500, she never pictured her life would change so much in a matter of weeks.
_full_text:
When Viktoriia Vasylysyn and her sister stepped onto the pavement of Rapid City with the weight of their entire world compressed into a few bags and a mere $500, she never pictured her life would change so much in a matter of weeks.

Back home in Ivano-Frankivsk, Ukraine, Vasylysyn didn't just live, she thrived, driven by a passion to help others.

 Viktoriia Vasylysyn
Back home in Ivano-Frankivsk, Ukraine, Vasylysyn didn't just live, she thrived driven by a passion to help others. She's finding that again in Rapid City. 

It was there she celebrated graduating in the top five of her university and became a doctor. Her career then blossomed into education, where she not only taught students medical practice, but the English language as well. Each passion and dream was supported by her parents and family. 

That life, however, was shattered on Feb. 24, 2022, when Russian airstrikes replaced the melody of her ambitions with the roar of war.

For Vasylysyn, the full-scale invasion didn't just stop her clocks; it severed the thread of a future she spent her whole life making, leaving her to build a new one from nothing in a land she never expected to call home. 

To find family in the U.S.

Though the road ahead for Vasylysyn and her sister was guaranteed to be a long one, she said they were fortunate to have met a man named David Sheehy several years prior to the war, during a vacation to the United States. 

Their friendship, maintained mainly through emails throughout the years, suddenly changed when Sheehy made his first phone call to the sisters shortly after Russia invaded Ukraine. 

Vasylysyn said he called to make sure they were safe and asked if they needed anything. But through some careful consideration after Vasylysyn's school got bombed and several conversations, together they made a plan for the pair to move to America, with Sheehy as their sponsor. 

Over the year that followed, Sheehy helped the sisters start their life anew, by giving them a place to stay and by driving them to and from the jobs they found in Rapid City. Bit by bit, Sheehy was more than a friend. He was family.

 Photo two of Viktoriia Vasylysyn
Viktoriia Vasylysyn said her and her sister were able to start over thanks to their sponsor, David Sheehy.  

"He's an awesome person. I have never met someone who's gave so much, without getting anything back," Vasylysyn said.

Vasylysyn said she and her sister often refer to Sheehy as their American dad, as he's been there for each monumental moment for them, such as their weddings and the birth of Vasylysyn's child.

Sheehy said he met the girl's about nine years ago, when they came to visit when several students from all over the world would come to Hot Springs for a summer internship. He said he admired how much the sisters loved the Black Hills and sharing about their life in Ukraine. The friendship would later transition strictly to email after the summer.

But when Sheehy heard about the first bombing in Ukraine in 2022, he said his heart sank and he had to make sure the sisters were OK. For Sheehy, there was no doubt in his mind that he would help Vasylysyn and her sister in any way he could; to him, their friendship progressively blossomed into a genuine family. 

"It is wonderful," Sheehy said. "From a lone bachelor to discover friends like this late in life... It feels very much like family."

 Photo three of Viktoriia Vasylysyn
After her life turned unexpecting around since Russia invaded Ukraine, Viktoriia Vasylysyn has dedicated her life to capturing Rapid City's happiness with one picture at a time.  

Sheehy said he doesn't have kids of his own and is estranged with his family, so when Vasylysyn and her sister stumbled into his life, he said it was the best thing that ever happened to him. 

Though Sheehy chuckles at the idea of being called their American dad, he said he has an immense amount of respect for their Ukrainian parents, who raised them to be kind women. 

"I think the world of them," Sheehy said. "... I just admire their parents so much for raising such wonderful, wonderful girls. These are two of the nicest people I've ever met and we have so much fun together. They have a great sense of humor, a sense of adventure and it's such a joy to know them. They are one of the best things that ever happened to me."  

Vasylysyn said she appreciated everything Sheehy did to ensure she and her sister had the best head start possible. However, the road was a long and difficult one.

"It was very difficult," Vasylysyn said. "... For a life being completely independent, for a little bit I became completely dependent and it was very, very difficult."

Vasylysyn worked day in and day out to begin her life again. Though she was a doctor in Ukraine, Vasylysyn didn't have the money or time to attempt to reclaim that title in the United States.

She said it was hard knowing who she was and what she accomplished before the war, to then be someone who was sharing a mattress on the floor in a single room trying to start their life over.

 Photo four of Viktoriia Vasylysyn
Viktoriia Vasylysyn wants to unite Rapid City by taking a photo of the people and asking what makes them happy.  

Bit by bit, Vasylysyn was able to start over, even beginning her own family with her husband and child.

Now three years since moving to Rapid City, Vasylysyn has dedicated her life helping refugees and immigrants as they begin their lives in South Dakota through her job as a humanitarian parolee supervisor at Lutheran Social Services of South Dakota. 

Despite achieving such great feats and finding happiness with her family and work, Vasylysyn said a piece of her was still missing since leaving Ukraine. 

"My social light disappeared," Vasylysyn said. "And I'm a very active, social person and so that was the moment I realized that I need to do something." 

Find yourself, one picture at a time

Even as a child, Vasylysyn was rarely seen without a camera, darting through her family's home to capture the world in motion. No moment was too small or too grand for her lens; she found beauty in the mundane and the monumental alike. 

"I was taking photos of everything I saw," Vasylysyn said. 

Eventually, the turbulence of life forced her passion into a long hiatus. The shutter went silent for years until a pivotal moment of revival. Her then-boyfriend, now husband, Edward Valdez, gifted her with her first proper camera since she moved to the United States. 

The gift came with a single, heartfelt condition that she use it only to capture what makes her happy. 

Vasylysyn took that request to heart and immediately began looking at the world through her camera lens. From pictures of her family and friends to her favorite places in Rapid City, Vasylysyn began capturing everything that made her smile. 

In the wake of major life transitions and the challenges of starting over, that simple gesture from Valdez transformed a camera into emotional recovery.

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

"I was so inspired inside of me, like maybe that's something I need to work on," Vasylysyn said. "Because I love it."

Despite finding meaning once again, Vasylysyn still craved more — capturing her own happiness wasn't enough, she wanted to know what made others happy and to record every single moment.

At the beginning of January 2026, Vasylysyn decided to hunt for happiness on the streets of Rapid City. She began talking to strangers walking downtown, asking about their lives and what makes them happy. Capturing their smiles as they described joyful moments and people in their lives. 

"I wanted to make something to reunite people," Vasylysyn said. "That's how I came up with the idea to meet people randomly on the street and take photos in the moment while they are happy." 

Sheehy said he was proud of Vasylysyn following her dreams and having the same love for the Black Hills as he does. 

Vasylysyn's mission to unite Rapid City with one happy memory to another didn't stop there. She dedicated a social media page, titled Viktoriia Vasylyshyn Street Photographer, for the public to see and find others that find happiness in the same things. 

"Everyone should be accepted and find their own happiness in life," Vasylysyn said. 

Through the seismic shifts in her life, Vasylysyn found herself falling in love with Rapid City one frame at a time. Through her camera lens, the once unfamiliar landscape and its people began to feel like home, and she soon realized she couldn't imagine doing anything else.

title: Roads, Libertyland TIF, housing among topics covered at Rapid City Council candidate forum
description: Around 180 people packed into the Canyon Lake Senior Center to hear from 10 candidates running for five seats on the Rapid City Council. This year every ward is contested for the first time since 2002.
_full_text:
Around 180 people packed into the Canyon Lake Senior Center to hear from 10 candidates running for five seats on the Rapid City Council. This year every ward is contested for the first time since 2002.

The current seats up for election on June 2 are held by Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2), Kevin Maher (Ward 3), John Roberts (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5).

Pettigrew is the only council member not seeking reelection.

 Rapid City Council candidates
Ten Rapid City Council candidates participated in a Citizens for Liberty forum on Thursday, April 16, at Canyon Lake Senior Center. 

Those throwing their hat in the race who are not currently on the council are Murray Lee (Ward 1), Christopher Vanderhoof (Ward 2), Andrea Schaefer (Ward 3), Valeriah Big Eagle (Ward 4), Pat Roseland (Ward 5) and Laura Armstrong (Ward 5).

Ward 4 candidate Ardin Cychosz announced on April 15 that he would be dropping out due to work-related schedule conflicts.

 Crowd
About 180 people attended Thursday's forum. 

The nearly two-hour forum on Thursday, April, 16, was hosted by South Dakota Citizens for Liberty.

Introductions

Every candidate was allotted a two-minute introduction to start the forum.

Ward 1 candidates:

Alderman Biberdorf said, "My slogan is be part of the solution or be part of the problem." He said he's a Christian conservative committed to faith, family and service.

"For me, in truth, I'm really passionate about, number one, making this a great place to have business. Rapid City should be open for business. I believe small businesses and large businesses drive us and give us a great place to raise a family."

 Josh Biberdorf
Ward 1 candidate and current Councilman Josh Biberdorf. 

Lee said he is from Rapid City, but traveled and lived in Arizona, California and Texas for work. In 2005, he moved back to the area to stay closer to his and his wife's family.

"Family is central to who I am. It's central to who my wife is. We've always been deeply involved in our family's lives.... So I understand how important a strong support system is, whether that's family, whether that's the people you call your neighbors, whether that's strangers," Lee said.

He said he is running on issues related to the city's growth, community safety and fiscal spending.

Ward 2 candidates:

Vanderhoof said, "You're all my neighbors. That's how I look at this. We all need to do this together."

He said he comes from New Jersey and has been living in Rapid City for 16 years. He is running on helping out the community and treating residents like neighbors.

 Christopher Vanderhoof
Ward 2 candidate Christopher Vanderhoof. 

"That's what I believe in. We need to help each other out. Knock on a neighbor's door. 'Is everything OK?' 'How are you doing?' I think that's where the city needs to be again. I'm here to help. I'm here to be here for you and listen," Vanderhoof said.

Alderman for Ward 2, Seachris said, "One of the biggest challenges we face today is getting out ahead of growth. Priority isn't just growth, it's coordinated economic development, where institutions work together, resources are aligned and we are able to diversify our local economy."

She said through being a parent, volunteer, coach and council member, she is involved in the community.

Ward 3 candidates:

Schaefer said she is a business owner and former dance studio owner, which makes her connected to the needs of the community.

"Being a small business owner, I think, is a great strength for somebody that wants (this) job. Small business owners, as many of you know, are connected to the community in a real, authentic way," Schaefer.

 Andrea Schaefer
Ward 3 candidate Andrea Schaefer. 

Alderman Maher described himself as fiscally conservative, with a background in business.

He said on seeking re-election, "There's a lot of talk about taxpayers should not support certain things. So I really believe that growth should pay for growth, and that is something that I will work on. I also think we need to strengthen our relationship with the (South Dakota) School of Mines."

 Kevin Maher
Ward 3 candidate and current Councilman Kevin Maher. 

Ward 4 candidates:

Big Eagle said she comes from Lake Andes, South Dakota and received her doctorate in education leadership to serve the community.

She said growing up fishing, farming and in a single-parent household made her the person she is today.

"My motto is our community knows what our community needs, and that's each and every one of you, your perspectives, your work experiences and the working class. That's where I came from, and so just wanting to be able to share a little bit of that with you," she said.

Alderman Roberts said he's been on the council for over a decade, originally running after seeing how the county was spending money. He worked for the county, so he couldn't run for a county office. He said the council works best when people work together.

One area he said he had worked on is safety in the northside of the city. He said city staff and Rapid City Chief of Police Don Hedrick have aided in creating police and fire precincts in the ward to address crime and safety.

"I think everybody knows that we have a crime problem in Rapid City. We have for quite a while. I think it's getting better," Roberts said.

Ward 5 candidates:

Roseland said he is a U.S. Navy veteran and worked as a registered nurse in anesthesiology for 30 years before retiring in 2016. He previously served on the council for Ward 5 from 2022 to 2025.

"I want Rapid City to be the best Rapid City it could be. I want safe neighborhoods. I want responsible growth. I want Rapid City to be open for all businesses and new investments," Roseland said.

 Laura Armstong
Ward 5 candidate Laura Armstrong. 

Armstrong said she has served on the council for Ward 5 for two terms, and twice served as council president.

She is currently the executive director for the Minneluzahan Senior Center.

"In recent years, I feel City Hall has shifted (to) less transparency, less collaboration and far too many decisions have been made without public input," Armstrong said.

She noted issues of nonprofits in the city closing, bus stop removals, Vision Fund projects and an increase in costs of city services as concerning.

Q&A

Citizens for Liberty President Tonchi Weaver opened the public questions portion of the event by asking the candidates what the main issue they've heard about in their ward.

Ward 1:

Lee said roadways, aging infrastructure, safety, housing and affordability in general are key issues in the community.

Biberdorf discussed crime, homelessness and roads.

Ward 2:

Seachris said she's heard of issues on aging infrastructure, roadway conditions and aging park systems and neighborhood amenities.

Vanderhoof said roads and housing upkeep for residents are the main two issues he's heard.

Ward 3:

Maher said roadway improvements have been an issue, stating the city has three projects underway to address issues on Wonderland Road, Carriage Hill Drive and Skyline Drive.

 Forum
From left, Rapid City Council candidates Andrea Schaefer, Kevin Maher, John Roberts, Pat Roseland and Laura Armstrong. 

Schaefer noted safety concerns about the Chapel Lane Bridge reconstruction being delayed and the impacts on residents. She also noted concerns about wildfires and roadways.

Ward 4:

Roberts said roadways, aging infrastructure, property taxes and crime are main issues.

Big Eagle said the biggest issue is housing and affordability in the area. She added public safety and livable wages are common issues.

Ward 5:

Roseland said roadway potholes, population growth and homelessness are key issues.

Armstrong named infrastructure, development, education, accessibility and social spaces as areas she wants to focus on.

Libertyland TIF

One resident asked the candidates whether they were in favor of the Libertyland USA Tax Increment Financing District, which was rejected by voters in January.

The Destination District TIF, which included the Libertyland project, was a proposed $125 million TIF in infrastructure development for the future erection of above-ground construction.

The project site was located north of Rushmore Crossing off East Mall Drive.

 Forum
From left, Rapid City Council candidates Josh Biberdorf, Murray Lee, Christopher Vanderhoof, Lindsey Seachris, Andrea Schaefer and Kevin Maher. 

The TIF for the Destination District was first approved in July 2025, then, after an administrative error, the council voted and reapproved the TIF and project plan again in September 2026.

Thereafter, a bipartisan group, made up of state Sen. Taffy Howard, R-Rapid City, who represents District 34, Weaver — of South Dakota Citizens for Liberty — Democracy in Action and other volunteers, collected signatures to take the TIF to a vote.

On Tuesday, Jan. 20, the "no" side received 7,965 votes (compared to 3,415, yes votes), shooting down the TIF.

Ward 1:

Lee said he was a "no" vote, saying, "In my development work (I) learned about TIFs and how they're supposed to be used, what parameters are supposed to follow, all the qualifications that they're supposed to meet to be considered for a TIF. ... But I don't think that it qualified to the letter for a TIF."

 Murray Lee
Ward 1 candidate Murray Lee. 

Biberdorf said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I would just say that for me, the lens and the optics always is, is this going to be something that's going to help low-income workforce housing when it comes to a TIF, or is it going to be an economic driver for this town. Everybody wants our roads fixed. The fact is, none of us up here going to do that because there's not enough money in the coffers."

Ward 2:

Seachris said she voted "yes," saying, "We knew this was going to be a hot topic tonight. I'm in support of Libertyland. I'm in support of TIFs in general as an economic development tool. So long as there is a community benefit tied into what we would see as a return after the period of time has elapsed," Seachris said.

 Lindsey Seachris
Ward 2 candidate and current Councilwoman Lindsey Seachris. 

Vanderhoof said he was a "no" vote.

"I believe that the TIF was a little too big. They made all these claims where they could make all this money for us. (My) question is, 'why can't that money go back into what they were doing?' If they were going to make that money, they can have the infrastructure and everything done on themselves," Vanderhoof said.

Ward 3:

Maher said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I voted for the Libertyland TIF, but I really, really respect the voters' decision. It was two to one to kill the TIF, and so I support that."

 Trio
From left, Councilman Josh Biberdorf, Councilman John Roberts and council candidate Pat Roseland. 

Schaefer said she was a "no" vote, saying, "I voted no, sort of as an uninformed voter. I think for me, the way it was marketed just felt a little off the mark. It felt a little dishonest to me, and I felt like the public was a little underestimated on what we knew about what a TIF was."

Ward 4:

Roberts said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I voted yes on this TIF. I voted yes on quite a few TIFs, and I voted no on quite a few TIF and I've stopped quite a few TIFs before they even got to that process."

 John Roberts
Ward 4 candidate and current Councilwoman John Roberts. 

Big Eagle said she was a "no" vote saying, "It was seeing our community struggle not having a place to live, the high crime rate, all of these things going on in Ward 4. Then seeing Libertyland right there, I feel that we could have used a TIF in a different way. If it was for responsive, affordable community development, then yes, I would. I would definitely be interested in supporting a TIF like Libertyland."

Ward 5:

Armstrong said she was a "no" vote

"I felt like it was rushed and pushed, and that's why I voted no. I will say I'm grateful that we did have the Libertyland vote because it unified people that usually work against one another, and brought people together."

 Line to ask questions
Attendees formed a long line to ask questions of Rapid City Council candidates during a forum on Thursday, April 16. 

Roseland said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "North Rapid needed some development out there. It seemed like that was the last place that people wanted to go and wanted to develop. And with this came up, I thought, why not? The (Pete Lien) family wanted to put in lots of money to help develop in that area ... We the People, had their choice to say yes or no. They said no, and I voted yes for it, and I'm not ashamed of that."

 Pat Roseland
Ward 5 candidate Pat Roseland. 

Voting information

Absentee voting is delayed because of ballot printing issued but was set to begin on April 17 and continue until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.

The county's auditor's office released a statement notifying voters absentee ballots will not be available on the first day of the absentee voting period.

"Under South Dakota law, absentee voting is scheduled to begin 46 days prior to an election pursuant to SDCL 12-19-1.2. However, the candidate petition filing deadline was March 31st, as established in SDCL 12-6-4, which determines the final list of candidates to appear on the ballot. This statutory timing results in a brief delay between the start of the absentee voting period and the availability of ballots," the release said.

This delay is a result of state law timelines and is not caused by the Pennington County Auditor’s Office or the County Auditor.

The voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

 PHOTOS and VIDEO: Citizens for Liberty Rapid City Council candidate forum
Citizens for Liberty hosted a Rapid City Council candidate forum on Thursday, April 16, at the Canyon Lake Senior Center. All 10 candidates participated.

title: Trio of St. Thomas More students win Rapid City-hosted western South Dakota Civics Bee
description: A trio of St. Thomas More students won the local National Civics Bee competition, advancing to the state finals in June.
_full_text:
A trio of St. Thomas More students won the local National Civics Bee competition, advancing to the state finals in June.

They were among 18 West River eighth graders who competed in the Civics Bee on Saturday, April 18, at the Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City.

 Kaylin Menkhaus
"I was really surprised and excited," Kaylin Menkhaus said after taking first place in the local National Civics Bee competition in Rapid City. 

Kaylin Menkhaus earned first-place accolades. Ellie Koenig was second and Regan Morganfield took third place.

"I was really surprised and excited," Menkhaus said after the event.

Participants advanced to Saturday's regional finals via an essay contest. Laura Jones with local sponsor Elevate Rapid City explained students across western South Dakota submitted essays "about a problem they see in their community, and then how they would address that problem, who they would ask for assistance and what kind of civic virtues their problem might involve or address."

 Civics Bee
Eighteen middle schoolers, chosen for their essays, competed in two rounds of multiple-choice questions during the local National Civics Bee competition on Saturday, April 18, at the Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City. 

Those essays were scored by judges across the country, choosing 20 finalists for the Rapid City competition, Jones explained. Two were unable to attend on Saturday.

The regional finals featured two rounds of multiple-choice civics questions for the 18 participants. The top five advanced to the final round, where they explained their essays and fielded questions from a trio of judges who'd examined all 18 essays. The judges' input, plus the multiple-choice question scores, determined the top three.

 Civics Bee
The regional finals featured two rounds of multiple-choice civics questions for the 18 participants. 

"Really strong essays submitted, really great out there in the community," Jones said. "Lot of different problems, really varied, and pretty exciting to see kids starting to think about how they might address medical needs for homeless people or community gardening or advocacy for children with disabilities. A lot of great ideas. I really hope they keep this passion as they age."

Menkhaus focused on food deserts in Rapid City, proposing a community garden program for residents in those areas.

 Question
One of the questions posed in the Civics Bee in Rapid City. 

"So that they could grow their own food and they'd also be learning life skills," the student said.

"I'm excited," she said when asked about moving on to the state finals. Jones said they'd be held sometime in June.

The National Civics Bee is an initiative to encourage more young Americans to engage in civics and contribute to their communities, Elevate said in a release. Organized by the U.S. Chamber of Commerce Foundation, the National Civics Bee is an annual nonpartisan civics competition for middle schoolers that "increases civic knowledge, skills, and disposition among young Americans, their families and communities."

 Judges
From left, Civics Bee judges Crystal Ortbahn of the John T. Vucurevich Foundation, Kaelixte Wagner of Black Hills Federal Credit Union and Shalom Baer Gee of the Rapid City Journal look over their notes after the question-and-answer round with the top five finalists. 

“Each year, the National Civics Bee reminds us how much promise and talent exists in communities across the country. Students step up with thoughtful solutions, bold ideas, and a willingness to participate—exactly the qualities our democracy and workforce need. We’re proud to support a program that strengthens civic knowledge and skills for all young people and helps build the foundation for a stronger future.” Hilary Crow, vice president for civics with the U.S. Chamber of Commerce Foundation, said in the release.

Kaelixte Wagner of Black Hills Federal Credit Union, Crystal Ortbahn of the John T. Vucurevich Foundation and Shalom Baer Gee of the Rapid City Journal served as volunteer judges in Rapid City.

 Do you know the answer to these National Civics Bee questions?
Videos of a quintet of questions posed to competitors (and audience members playing along on their phone) at the regional National Civics Bee on Saturday, April 18, in Rapid City. 

 Kaylin Menkhaus
Kaylin Menkhaus earned first place at the local National Civics Bee competition in Rapid City. 

 Rapid City Civics Bee top 5
The top five at the Civics Bee on Saturday, April 18, at the Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City.

title: Ladner announces re-election campaign for South Dakota House District 30
description: State Rep. Trish Ladner announced that she is running for re-election to the South Dakota House of Representatives in District 30.
_full_text:
State Rep. Trish Ladner announced that she is running for re-election to the South Dakota House of Representatives in District 30.

In a release, Ladner said running reaffirms her commitment to serving her constituents by working on property tax reform, improving education, keeping our neighborhoods safe and opening workplace opportunities that help to ensure South Dakota remains a place where we can live, work, and raise a family.

 ladner.jpg

Ladner is joined in the race by Republicans Rep. Tim Goodwin and Stephen Saint. The top two in the June primary will advance to the November general election. Bret Swanson is unopposed in the Democratic primary.

“South Dakota is on the rise, and this moment calls for strong, principled leadership that puts our state first. We need leaders who will stand firm in our values, cut through the noise in Pierre, and make thoughtful, forward-looking decisions,” Ladner said in the release.

During her time in office, Ladner said she has championed legislation to reform the property tax system, grow workforce through expanding technical education addressing workforce shortages and advocating for veterans and VA services.

In the Legislature, Ladner noted serves on two influential committees: Local Government and Agriculture and Natural Resources, saying she's consistently defended local decision-making.

Her key priorities include:

• Continuing to work to lower and stabilize property taxes for families, seniors, veterans, disabled citizens and business owners

• Work to keep government small, local, and accountable to the people

• Support veterans and strengthen VA services

• Cutting unnecessary regulations and wasteful spending

• Protecting local control of education

• Standing-up for the rural way of life we all cherish

“It is an honor to serve District 30.” Ladner said. "I am grateful for your trust, and I will continue to work for you – not special interest groups.”

District 30 includes Fall River, Custer, and portions of Pennington Counties. Absentee voting began April 17, though many counties don't have ballots ready yet because of the time crunch between the filing deadline and start of early voting, and the Republican primary election will be held on June 2.

title: Michael James Long
description: September 15, 1938 - March 29, 2026
_full_text:
September 15, 1938 - March 29, 2026

Michael James Long, born September 15, 1938, in Rapid City, South Dakota, passed away. He was the son of Loretta and James Long.

Mike grew up in South Dakota and graduated from Fairburn High School before proudly serving his country in the United States Navy.

Mike married Phyllis Balaberda, and together they had two children, Mitchell and Stephanie.

In 1987, Mike married Ann Goff, expanding his family with her two children, Stacie and Scott.

Mike is survived by his four children, Mitchell, Stephanie, Stacie, Scott, along with eight grandchildren: McKella, Jordan, Billie, Aimee, Adam, and Alex. He also leaves behind three great grandchildren: Mia, JJ, and Novella.

title: Ramona Dale Strandell
description: March 6, 1930 - April 9, 2026
_full_text:
March 6, 1930 - April 9, 2026

Ramona Dale Strandell, 96, passed away April 9, 2026, at Brookdale Marianna Butte in Loveland, Colorado. She was born on March 6, 1930, to William and Jessie Burgess in Kansas City, Missouri.

At the age of 16 she and her family moved to Sturgis, SD where she attended Sturgis High School, class of 1947. After graduation, Ramona attended Black Hills Teachers College in Spearfish, SD, which is now known as Black Hills State University. During her college years, Ramona met the love of her life, Donald Strandell, the two married on August 6, 1950, in Sturgis.

Ramona and Don moved to Don's hometown of Wall, SD where she worked at Wall Drug Store for some time before leaving that role to raise her 3 daughters. They eventually settled in Spearfish, SD in 2004. Ramona took an active role in the United Methodist Church in both Wall and Spearfish, singing in the choir and also helping produce the monthly bulletin.

After Don's passing in 2008 Ramona remained in Spearfish until eventually relocating to Colorado in 2014, living in both Estes Park and then Loveland where she remained until her passing.

Ramona is survived by her daughters Julie Strandell of Loveland, Colorado and Joie (Kerry) Willuweit of Estes Park, Colorado, grandchildren Jessica Willuweit and great-grandchildren Edison and Quinton Shoemaker all of Estes Park.

She was preceded by her parents William and Jessie Burgess, her beloved husband Don Strandell, her sister Carol and brothers Gene and Bill, a daughter Bonnie and a son-in-law Tony.

The family wishes to express their heartfelt gratitude to the caregivers and staff at Brookdale Marianna Butte. Your dedicated care and the kindness you showed our mother will always be remembered with deep appreciation.

title: Leatrice "Lea" Catherine Mills
description: August 26, 1930 - March 31, 2026
_full_text:
August 26, 1930 - March 31, 2026

Leatrice Catherine Mills "Lea," 95, of Rapid City, passed away on March 31, 2026, at the Monument Health Hospice House with family present. Lea, an Oglala Lakota tribal member of the Pine Ridge Reservation of South Dakota was born on August 26, 1930, to parents, Raymond Big Crow and Theresa Garcia Patton Big Crow. When family asked about her birthplace, she would proudly convey that she was born in a tent at the Wolf Creek Community, east of Pine Ridge, S.D., and was delivered and cared for by her grandmother, Suzie Big Crow.

She married Chester Mills on August 4, 1948. After many years of ranching and country life in Porcupine, S.D., the family relocated to the San Francisco Bay area in California under the U.S Government's 1950's American Indian Relocation Act. In 1957, the Mills family moved to Rapid City. In 1968, her eldest son, Arthur Lee Mills was killed in action in Vietnam and she became a Gold Star Mother -- something she never wanted yet was proud of the title which to her was one of honor reflecting Arthur's courage, resilience and service to his country.

Her adventurous spirit was unmistakable as she traveled numerous times to the west coast, and east coast. She journeyed to the Alaskan bush several times where she fished, camped and traveled in small bush airplanes.

Lea was a fluent Lakota speaker, a homemaker, an expert quilter, an accomplished seamstress, and craftsperson. She was a longtime collector of turtles, angels and cardinal figurines which provided her with spiritual and symbolic connections. Lea was active in the St. Isaac Jogues Parish and was a member of the St. Isaac Jogues sewing ladies' group for 48 years, where she formed cherished, lifelong friendships.

It was through her grace, guidance, encouragement and positive attitude that she inspired her family to live their best life which included furthering their education; hence all five children have college degrees. She was there to help family members when times were difficult and the advice of, "sometimes you have to step back, be patient and let things be, but that's ok," or her encouragement of "just saying – you are strong."

Lea is now reunited with her husband, Chester; son, Arthur Lee Mills; infant grandson, Kevin Livermont; grandson, Eli X Livermont; infant great-grandson, David Mills; and great-grandson, Reese Livermont. Remaining to honor and cherish her memory are daughters, Joann Livermont, Rapid City, Brenda Mills, Rapid City, and Geri Schlanger, husband, Patrick, Denver, Colo.; sons, Mark Mills, wife, Chris, Cleveland, Ohio, and John Mills, wife, Cherri, Fredericksburg, Va.; ten grandchildren, Arthur Livermont, Gabrielle Griswold, Alix Anderson, Kelly Mills, Adam Schlanger, Desirae Dodd, Mercedes Mills, Aubrey Housley, Samantha Mills, Dominic Mills; and eight great-grandchildren.

She was the youngest of ten children and the last surviving member of her family. Siblings preceding her in death were Louise (Big Crow) Ruff, Alvina (Big Crow) Eagle Bull, James Big Crow, Lloyd Big Crow, Thelma (Big Crow) Bettelyoun, Francis X. Big Crow, Ava Marie (Big Crow) Ruff, and her half siblings, Grace Allman Mills and George Patton.

The family wishes to express their deepest gratitude to our sister, Brenda, for her unwavering dedication and compassionate care for our mother.

Thank you to the amazing staff at Monument Health Hospice House for their loving, exceptional, and respectful care during our mother's time there. Also, a special thank you to the 10th floor staff at Monument Hospital for care provided to her.

Upon her request cremation has taken place; interment will be next to our father at the Black Hills National Cemetery, in Sturgis, S.D. Services to take place at a later date.
