title: Rapid City Fire Department Capt. Eric O'Connor receives Smokey Bear Award
description: The Rapid City Fire Department's very own Captain Eric O'Connor received the 2026 Bronze Smokey Bear Award for his state-level efforts in wildfire prevention.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Fire Department's very own Captain Eric O'Connor received the 2026 Bronze Smokey Bear Award for his state-level efforts in wildfire prevention.

USDA Forest Service Chief Thomas M. Schultz, Jr. recently announced the 2026 Smokey Bear Award winners, recognizing outstanding service in wildfire prevention. O’Connor was honored for his leadership, dedication, and commitment to making the community safer and stronger through wildfire prevention and mitigation.

 Eric O'Connor
Eric O'Connor 

O'Connor is one of six people to receive the bronze award. He worked with the Bureau of Land Management district to create a workforce program that provides recent military veterans with training and hands-on experience in wildfire prevention, fuels mitigation, and operational activities.

The program continues to grow beyond Rapid City, sustaining wildfire prevention education and actively reducing wildfire risk. O’Connor used prescribed burns and other mitigation activities as an opportunity to educate his community and strengthen partnerships. 

O’Connor told the Journal last year the program began in June 2013 in a cooperative agreement with the Bureau of Land Management. 

 Eric O'Connor (copy)
Rapid City Fire Department Wildland Lt. Eric O'Connor walks into a ditch among flames during a controlled burn in Rapid City in April 2023.

title: State asks for help after goat decapitated in Spearfish Canyon
description: Warning: This story features a graphic photo at the conclusion of the text.
_full_text:
Warning: This story features a graphic photo at the conclusion of the text.

The South Dakota Turn in Poachers (TIP) program is asking the public for help identifying the suspect who cut off a mountain goat's head.

TIP, a South Dakota Game Fish and Parks program, said on the morning of April 8, 2026, a dead mountain goat was reported at the bottom of Bridal Veil Falls in Spearfish Canyon. Before a GFP biologist could reach the carcass, someone had removed and taken the animal’s head.

 041826-nws-goat-p1.jpg
"Although this mountain goat was not poached, it is illegal to take or possess any part of the animal without proper authorization," TIP said in an alert. 

"Although this mountain goat was not poached, it is illegal to take or possess any part of the animal without proper authorization," TIP said in an alert, citing South Dakota law SDCL 41-14-32, which prohibits the unlawful taking, possession, sale, or transportation of birds, animals, or fish — including any parts of those animals. A mountain goat is classified as big game, making this case a Class 1 misdemeanor.

 041826-nws-goat-p3.jpg
Point your smartphone camera at the QR code, then tap the link to access the Turn in Poachers tip form. 

Anyone with information is asked to contact the Turn In Poachers (TIPs) hotline at 1-888-OVERBAG (1-888-683-7224) or the local Wildlife Conservation Officer at (605) 381-4704, or submit a secure online report via gfp.sd.gov.

Callers may remain confidential or completely anonymous. Individuals who provide information leading to a successful prosecution are eligible for a cash reward.

 041826-nws-goat-p2.jpg
The South Dakota Turn in Poachers (TIP) program is asking the public for help identifying who cut off a mountain goat's head. 

 PHOTOS: Mountain goats at Spearfish Canyon
Mountain goats graze in Spearfish Canyon. According to South Dakota Game, Fish and Parks, an isolated population of Rocky Mountain Goats has existed within the Black Hills since the 1920s.

title: A brotherhood forged in fire: RCFD vet program rapidly grows
description: After serving their country, about 200,000 veterans transition into the civilian world every year , according to the Government Accountability Office. What is considered an exciting next chapter for most veterans’ lives can be a difficult challenge for others.
_full_text:
After serving their country, about 200,000 veterans transition into the civilian world every year, according to the Government Accountability Office. What is considered an exciting next chapter for most veterans’ lives can be a difficult challenge for others.

According to Pew Research Center, about half of post-9/11 veterans say it was somewhat (32%) or very (16%) difficult for them to readjust to civilian life after the military. Some of the challenges veterans face are emotional strains, financial and health struggles.

 112225-nws-rcfdvet-p1
Rapid City Fire Department’s Wildland Fuels Module is an effort to help give veterans a chance to continue serving in a new way. The program focuses on career development, physical and mental wellness and reducing wildfire risks across the community. 

Rapid City Fire Department’s Wildland Fuels Module is an effort to help combat this and help give veterans a chance to continue serving in a new way. The program focuses on career development, physical and mental wellness and reducing wildfire risks across the community.

“It’s designed to get military veterans as they exit the military and get them into the civilian-style workforce,” RCFD Wildland Fuels Module Capt. Eric O’Connor said. “While still providing a service to their community, but also benefitting them with career pathway development and other opportunities.”

O’Connor said the program began in June 2013 in a cooperative agreement with the Bureau of Land Management. And for the first time over 12 years, the group finally has a place to call their own — the Rapid City Fire Station 2 located at 1205 North Maple Avenue.

 RCFD Wildland Fuels Module Picture
The Rapid City Fire Department’s Wildland Fuels Module program began in June 2013 in a cooperative agreement with the Bureau of Land Management. 

The location was granted to their group, after Rapid City Fire Station 1 finished construction, giving the program the opportunity to grow.

The group currently consist of eight members, a mix of veterans supporters, which is the largest they’ve had since forming. O’Connor said they had to expand their candidate pool to meet the needs of the community, but will retain at least 75% veteran participation.

From implementing prescribed fires across the black hills to providing cords of fire wood to households in Pine Ridge to stay warm the upcoming winter, O’Connor said each member is actively serving their community one step at a time.

 RCFD Wildland Fuels Module Picture
The group currently consist of eight members, a mix of veterans supporters, which is the largest they’ve had since forming in 2013. 

Jeremy Atwell, who served in the Army for six years at the 82nd Airborne Infantry, joined the program in 2022 looking for a sense of purpose.

“You’re still a soldier, but without a mission,” Atwell said. “... So when I found this crew it gave me a sense of brotherhood, that camaraderie, a sense of purpose and another mission to serve and continue to do something worthwhile.”

Though Atwell wanted to take on a full-time career with the crew in 2022, there were no openings at the time. But his dreams finally became a reality this year, as Atwell completed his first week leading new members as crew lieutenant.

Todd Scott he learned about the program while he was still serving in the Army in the 10th Mountain Division and went through the Department of Defense Skillbridge Program to begin his training in the Black Hills.

Scott said a position in the program is something “that is earned” and gives each member, including himself, a reason to protect the public once more. He said it's important for each candidate to have a “hunger” to serve their community.

 RCFD Wildland Fuels Module Picture
For the first time over 12 years, the Rapid City Fire Department’s Wildland Fuels Module finally has a place to call their own — the Rapid City Fire Station 2 at 1205 North Maple Avenue. 

“We come in here everyday and we try to earn it,” Scott said. “We want to be a part of the community, make a difference and make sure that we protect the people and prevent things that could be disasters.”

O’Connor said they will begin recruitment on Jan. 1, 2026 and are enthusiastic about what the new year will bring. He said it’ll be the first year they will have such “a robust educational team” to lead any new recruits.

Though veteran applicants are highly sought after, O’Connor said with the increasing case load they are seeking anybody who is able to help them in their efforts.

title: Rapid City area law enforcement partners with Ring on video evidence
description: The Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office said Friday, April 17, they've partnered with Ring, a popular manufacturer of home security video systems, in order to facilitate the sharing of video evidence with law enforcement.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office said Friday, April 17, they've partnered with Ring, a popular manufacturer of home security video systems, in order to facilitate the sharing of video evidence with law enforcement.

The partnership comes within Ring’s "Neighbors" app, a mobile application that allows Ring users to share information about public safety topics and incidents among other users in their community.

The RCPD and PCSO now have an official presence within the "Neighbors" app, the agencies said in a joint press release.

The presence "will allow local law enforcement to send neighborhood-specific requests for video/information from Ring users in specific neighborhoods as it relates to ongoing investigations into criminal incidents."

For example, they noted, if law enforcement is investigating a series of vehicle burglaries impacting a specific neighborhood, detectives/investigators can send out a request that will alert Ring users in that specific neighborhood to check their security systems for any video of suspicious activity as it relates to the burglary investigation.

 Amazon New Devices
New Ring doorbells and cameras are displayed Tuesday, Sept. 30, 2025, at an Amazon event in New York. 

Participation is at the discretion of the Ring customer, and law enforcement has no way to obtain video without a customer voluntarily providing it.

“Officers of the Rapid City Police Department value our ongoing partnership with the residents of our city,” Capt. Andy Becker, commander of RCPD’s Criminal Investigation Division, said in the release. “Video evidence is a regular component of most modern-day criminal investigations, and this partnership through the Ring Neighbors app is one more way we can let the community know how they can assist officers in working to hold criminal offenders who choose to victimize our neighborhoods accountable for their actions.”

Sheriff's Office Investigations Capt. Taylor Sperle called it, "More eyes help solve crime."

"As more homeowners realize the importance of a security system with easy sharing options to local law enforcement, there will be broader neighborhood benefits," Sperle said in the release. "This is another tool to help law enforcement deter and solve crime.”

The agencies explained the Community Request function is specific to Ring video systems and the associated "Neighbors" app. Should a member of the public wish to report suspicious activity in their neighborhood, they are still encouraged to contact the RCPD or PCSO directly at 911 if it’s an emergency, or at 605-394-4131 if it’s an incident requiring timely law enforcement attention.

title: Faith man found guilty after trial of first-degree burglary
description: A Faith man faces up to 25 years in prison on first-degree burglary charges.
_full_text:
A Faith man faces up to 25 years in prison on first-degree burglary charges.

The U.S. Attorney's Office for South Dakoa announced that a jury has convicted Dalton Hump, 34, of Faith, of one count of first-degree burglary following a two-day jury trial in federal district court in Pierre. The verdict was returned on April 2.

The charge carries a maximum penalty of 25 years in custody and/or a $250,000 fine, according to the U.S. Attorney's Office.

Hump was indicted by a federal grand jury in October 2025.

According to a release from the U.S. Attorney's Office:

At trial, the evidence established that on the evening of July 10, 2024, Hump unlawfully entered a residence in Ziebach County, in the Cheyenne River Sioux Indian Reservation. Hump assaulted two individuals inside the residence before being forced outside.

This matter was prosecuted by the U.S. Attorney’s Office because the Major Crimes Act, a federal statute, mandates that certain violent crimes alleged to have occurred in Indian country be prosecuted in federal court as opposed to state court.

This case was investigated by the FBI and the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe Law Enforcement Services. Assistant U.S. Attorney Wayne Venhuizen prosecuted the case.

A presentence investigation was ordered. Sentencing has not yet been scheduled. Hump was placed in the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service pending sentencing.

title: Demonstrators gather outside Ellsworth for Sahela Sangrait-Dubray; Box Elder police chief references changes
description: BOX ELDER — The sun rose on the Black Hills on Friday, April 17, and slowly began to melt the spring snow as a steady flow of traffic made its way towards the Ellsworth Air Force Base main gate.
_full_text:
BOX ELDER — The sun rose on the Black Hills on Friday, April 17, and slowly began to melt the spring snow as a steady flow of traffic made its way towards the Ellsworth Air Force Base main gate.

Along the road, about a dozen people gathered holding up signs and flags in the bitter wind. Several bore the face of a young woman and wording such as, "Sahela should have been protected. Instead, she became another name in a growing list that should never exist." Some drivers simply continued on their way. Others honked in support.

Sahela (Different Cheyenne Woman) Sangrait-Dubray, a Cheyenne River Lakota woman, was allegedly murdered on Aug. 11, 2024 on Ellsworth Air Force Base. She was 21 years old. 

Sangrait-Dubray's body was found on March 4, 2025, outside of Hill City, 40 miles from Box Elder. Slightly over a year since she was found, her remains were returned to her family, who will hold a wake service in the evening on Friday, April 17, and a funeral on Saturday, April 18, according to her obituary published in Native Sun News Today. 

 Lorna Cuny
Medicine Wheel Ride Executive Director Lorna Cuny holds a sign in honor of Sangrait-Dubray.  

The obituary reads, in part, "Sahela will be remembered by her vibrant soul and how she loved meeting new people and making friends with you. She loved music, anime, cooking and baking, playing basketball, and going on adventures. Helping the community was a passion of hers. Sahela’s bright spirit touched many lives. Her time here was far too short but her memory will live on in the hearts of those who knew and loved her."

Quinterius Charles Chappelle, a 24-year-old airman, is charged with first-degree murder in federal court. His jury trial has not been scheduled at this time. According to federal court documents, Drew Durand of Rapid City is charged with accessory after the fact and misprision of a felony in connection to the case.

The Medicine Wheel Ride organized the rally on Friday. Medicine Wheel Ride Executive Director and Co-founder Lorna Cuny, Oglala Lakota, told the Journal, "This family has been through a lot, and I think that being out here and calling out some of the systemic failures in this case is really important." 

"How can we protect our women? What can we do better to call out these issues that affect our women to keep our women safe?" Cuny continued. 

Native American are more likely to experience violence
According to the National Criminal Justice Training Center, Native American women are 10 times more likely to be murdered than all other ethnicities, with murder being the third leading cause of death for Native women. More than 84% have experienced violence, more than 56% have experienced sexual violence, more than 55% have been physically abused by an intimate partner, and 40% of all sex trafficking victims are Native women.

Before the demonstrators headed to the roadside with their signs, they spoke with Box Elder Chief of Police Robert Connell and Assistant Chief of Police Gregory Jones.

 Police Chief
Box Elder Police Chief Robert Connell speaks with Darlene Gomez during the rally. 

"I'm glad you guys are here ... Hopefully nothing goes sideways, but we're here to help accommodate," Connell said. "If anybody looks like they're trying to interfere, or whatever, please call us."

Cuny said the police were "really receptive," kept up communication the day before the rally, and met with her at the location Friday morning to show them where they could stand. 

"I really do appreciate that," Cuny said, adding she hopes the door can stay open for meaningful conversations.

Sangrait was last seen on Aug. 10, 2024, dancing with friends at Press Start in Rapid City. Friends and family of Sangrait-Dubray said they last interacted with her on Aug. 10 and had been attempting to report her missing to law enforcement since then. 

She wasn't entered into the state missing person's database until Feb. 10, 2025, when Box Elder Police took a missing person report from her sister, according to court documents. Sangrait-Dubray's sister last spoke with her on the phone in July 2024 and last received a text from her on Aug. 11, 2024, according to a search warrant affidavit. The text read, "Good night, I love you." 

Darlene Gomez, an attorney who works causes related to promoting Missing and Murdered Indigenous People cases and serves as general counsel for the Medicine Wheel Ride, is representing Sangrait-Dubray's family. She engaged in a conversation with the police before the rally about procedure when it comes to missing persons cases.

"I didn't become chief here until September of 2024. This case was the first thing on my radar, besides trying to find a place to live," Connell — the police chief — said. "So what we've done since then has created a stronger communication policy and directives with the base."

 Justice Rally
A woman holds a sign advocating for justice for Sahela Sangrait-Dubray. 

Connell said that includes sharing radio encryption codes with the base, which wasn't done before, as well as establishing a direct phone line with security forces on the base, and embedding one of his investigators into the Air Force Office of Special Investigations (OSI) as a liaison.

"Anything on base that involves a civilian, my investigator now is brought in on that with OSI. Anything off base involving an airman, he is basically working alongside OSI off base pertaining to airman incidents," he said. "So we have really tried to shore up all the loose ends in communication for any type of case involving airmen on and off base and civilians on base."

 Darlene Gomez
Darlene Gomez is an attorney representing the family of Sahela Sangrait-Dubray. 

Gomez said Sangrait-Dubray was involved in a domestic violence call with Chappelle before she was killed. Court documents show there was a domestic disturbance at Chappelle's on-base residence on Aug. 10, 2024, that the Air Force knew the two had been seen together on base, and Chappelle had snuck Sangrait-Dubray on base without property authority. 

When security forces arrived for the Aug. 10 call, no one was home, per court documents. Another woman who was at Chappelle's residence on that day said she saw a woman there, who she later identified as Sangrait-Dubray in a photo lineup. The woman said Chappelle told her two or three days later that Sangrait-Dubray moved to Florida. 

When a Pennington County Sheriff's Office investigator spoke with an OSI lieutenant, he learned Chappelle was in the process of being discharged from the military due to several allegations of domestic violence. 

Gomez questioned Connell about why a correlation wasn't made between Chappelle and Sangrait-Dubray. 

"All I can tell you is the changes that I have made for the department," Connnell said.

The Journal has requested an interview with the Box Elder Police Department for more information on the timeline of the missing person report and when they began to receive calls about Sangrait-Dubray.

 PHOTOS: Rally held outside Ellsworth Air Force Base for Sahela Sangrait-Dubray
On Friday April 17, family members and members of the Medicine Wheel Riders held a rally for justice for the killing of Sahela Toka Win (Different Cheyenne Woman) Sangrait-Dubray. Sangrait-Dubray (Cheyenne River Lakota) was allegedly murdered in August 2025 on Ellsworth Air Force Base. Quinterius Charles Chappelle, a 24-year-old airman, is charged with first-degree murder in federal court.

title: 'We sleep safe in our beds because rough people stand ready in the night:' A tour of former nuclear missile site
description: PHILIP — Nearly 80 miles outside Rapid City, the town of Philip is home to decommissioned missile sites, facilities once tasked with deterring and, if needed, starting and ending World War III.
_full_text:
PHILIP — Nearly 80 miles outside Rapid City, the town of Philip is home to decommissioned missile sites, facilities once tasked with deterring and, if needed, starting and ending World War III.

"We sleep safe in our beds because rough people stand ready in the night," Minutemen Missile National Historic Site Park Guide Jim Boensch said about the site's mission.

 Teaser
A dummy missile at the Delat-09 missile launching site in South Dakota. 

Boensch is not only a park guide but also worked at the sites when they were in operation, serving from 1971 to 1975 in the U.S. Air Force, working as one of a two-man team tasked with turning the nuclear keys.

"The number one rule in the intercontinental ballistic missile business, the pointy end of the missile must go up," Boensch said wryly.

Start of the arms race

According to the National Park Service, after the development of the atomic bomb in 1942 by the U.S and usage over Japan in 1945 (killing and wounding at least 120,000 people in Hiroshima and Nagasaki), the nuclear arms race began.

After working with the U.S. in World War II as a member of the Allies, the Soviet Union began testing its own nuclear bombs, during the Cold War (1945-1991).

 Last crew
The last group to serve at the Delta-01 launch facility near Philip. 

According to Boensch, starting in the 1960s, an estimated 1,000 nuclear missiles were once scattered around the Great Plains area, with 150 alone in South Dakota.

The reason the plains were selected, Boensch said, was because of strategic advantages the missiles would have. The distance from the Soviet Union, ample time to respond if adversaries used submarines to launch off the U.S. coasts, as well as low population numbers in the region, were seen as ideal for states like South Dakota, North Dakota, Montana, Wyoming, Nebraska and Missouri as missile site placements during the Cold War.

 Jim Boensch
Jim Boensch shows off the entrance to the Delta-01 Launch Facility, once able to fire nuclear rockets around the world. 

The South Dakota Minuteman Missile National Historic Site housed Minuteman I and II nuclear missiles, operating from 1963 to 1991.

He said the Minutemen Missile II had a range of up to 7,000 miles, with the ability to reach targets such as Moscow in under 30 minutes (or less).

Each nuclear warhead attached to the missiles was capable of delivering a 1.2 megaton nuclear detonation.

Boensch was one of many people to serve under the U.S. Airforce to operate in the Delat-01 Launch Facility, a control room buried under a small military post.

Minutemen Missile 

"My role here was missile combat crew commander ...  I ended up serving in all 15 launch control centers over a period of four years," Boensch said.

 Delta-09 site map
A map shows the Delta-09 missile site was once capable of firing toward Moscow, Russia. 

During this time in global conflicts, he said the missile launch control and missile sites were well-known and publicly visible to actively deter the Soviet Union from starting a nuclear conflict.

The Delta-01 Launch Facility was one of 15 facilities in South Dakota capable of launching the warheads.

He said the facility was a two-man operation, with 24 hours of duty service before being switched out with another duo. The facility was operated 24/7, 356 days of the year. Once inside, the crew was sealed by a blast-proof vault door. Their responsibilities included active radio communication with various military officials and to stand by for necessary launch commands.

Boensch said he could not share what launch codes looked like (as in, if they were a combination of letters and numbers).

 Launch room
The launch room for Delta-01, which required a two-man crew at all times, 365 days a year. 

"Your word was your bond. But I never worried about it too much. My theory was that all I could do at this level was to be the very best missile crew member I could be, and then worrying about it would do no good whatsoever," Boensch said. "Believing in a higher power also helps."

 Sign
A warning sign leftover from Delta-09's operation. 

It would take a code and two keys turned at the same time by the two crew members to fire the missiles. He noted, had the call come in, the crew would not have been given information on the target.

If the keys were turned, they'd launch 50 missiles, including at the Delta-09 missile silo, which still stands at the Philip site.

Above the underground launch facility stand a couple of buildings housing a guard's room, sleeping quarters and amenities like a kitchen for the military personnel to use during their occupation.

START Treaty

Boensch said starting in 1982, negotiations on the Strategic Arms Reduction Talks between the U.S. and the Soviet Union began, with the aim of reducing the stockpile of nuclear missiles both countries had.

 Nuclear keys
A key slot that would launch nuclear missiles if armed by the two-man crew. 

In 1991, the START I Treaty was signed, effectively reducing both countries' stockpile to no more than 6,000 nuclear warheads.

 Blast door
A mural spoofing a famous pizza slogan from the 1990s painted on the Delta-01 blast door. 

Boensch said thereafter the Delta-01 and Delta-09 sites were decommissioned and tasked to be preserved as a historical marker under the purview of the National Park Service.

"It actually took us longer to tear the place down than it did to build it ... (they) buried it all and then left them open for 90 days so the satellites could observe it on both sides," Boensch said about the other sites.

 Sculpture
A sculpture representing both superpowers of the Cold War, outside of the visitor center for the Minuteman Missile site. 

He said Russian officials could watch the destruction of the various facilities, as well as traveling out to the Delta-01 and Delta-09 sites to make sure they were fully decommissioned. It ended South Dakota's nuclear launching capabilities. 

Now the sites sit as a reminder for guests of the destruction the Cold War both adversaries could have unleashed.

He said an estimated 400 Minuteman III missiles remain active and ready in North Dakota, Wyoming and Montana.

Guests can learn more at the Visitor Center for the site, located north of Exit 131 on Interstate 90, and visitors can schedule tours of the sites.

 PHOTOS: The Minutemen Missile National Historic Site near Philip
The Minutemen Missile National Historic Site is located near Philip. The Visitor Center is located north of Exit 131 on Interstate 90, and visitors can schedule tours of the sites.

title: Stuck in Neutral: Nebraska used to be a leader in creating jobs and spurring growth. Not anymore.
byline: By HENRY J. CORDES/Flatwater Free Press - Flatwater Free Press
description: Not long ago, Nebraska’s growth was quite literally a national cover story.
_full_text:
Not long ago, Nebraska’s growth was quite literally a national cover story.

Nebraska Gov. Pete Ricketts soared through the air wearing skis and goggles on the Olympic-themed March 2018 cover of Site Selection, an economic development industry magazine.

The cartoon image illustrated Nebraska’s high-flying, three-year run as winner of the “Governor’s Cup,” awarded to the state landing the most economic development projects per capita in the nation.

But when Site Selection last month named the top development states for 2025, Nebraska was nowhere to be found. It didn’t crack the magazine’s top 10 at all, the second straight year it missed the list.

It’s the latest indicator that formerly front-running Nebraska has lost its edge when it comes to competing for jobs and economic growth, creating what state business leaders see as an urgent challenge and cloud darkening the state’s future.

A Flatwater Free Press analysis comparing Nebraska job growth to that of six neighboring states illustrates the state’s diminished economic competitiveness — and suggests it has cost Nebraska 70,000 jobs in recent years.

Those vanishing jobs mean fewer families, less prosperity and lower tax collections to support education and other vital services, said Dana Bradford, an Omaha businessman who has worked on the state’s employment challenges as past chairman of the Greater Omaha Chamber and Aksarben Foundation.

“It’s a silent killer,” Bradford told the Flatwater Free Press. “Somewhere along the line, companies just decided, ‘We are going somewhere else.’”

Economists, business leaders, economic developers and others cite a variety of complex challenges behind Nebraska’s slip in growing jobs, including the state’s worker shortage, a lack of affordable housing and child care, a lack of growth in high-paying jobs and the longtime “brain drain” of young college graduates to other states.

But some also cite a far simpler reason: State leaders have not made growth a priority.

A statewide economic development strategy produced in 2019 by a blue-ribbon panel of Nebraskans was largely shelved. State policymakers did enact some of Blueprint Nebraska’s tax recommendations — in a way that burdened state finances with a deepening structural deficit.

Tens of millions of dollars have been sliced from economic development programs as Gov. Jim Pillen and the Legislature work to balance the budget.

The Pillen administration, through cuts and attrition, has slashed staffing inside the Nebraska Department of Economic Development by 27% in the past nine months alone.

State lawmakers have capped tax incentive payments, creating uncertainty in the programs. Last year, they passed a Pillen-championed anti-China bill that made many of the nation’s biggest corporations ineligible to receive tax incentives, a move they’re now seeking to fix.

Nebraska has “stepped back” from its focus on economic development at the same time that competing states are stepping up efforts, the Greater Omaha Chamber said in a recent report.

“I just don’t think we’ve really had a commitment to growth in the state,” said Eric Thompson, a University of Nebraska-Lincoln economist. “I just think we’ve been focused on other priorities — and that’s OK. But this is one of the things that happens.”

There are recent signs the state has begun to turn new attention to growing jobs.

A Pillen-sponsored tax bill enhancing state tax incentives passed the Legislature this year, as did measures to expand child care support and spur housing construction.

The Nebraska Chamber of Commerce and Industry is preparing to dust off Blueprint Nebraska and give it new life. The chamber earlier this year quietly launched a growth-focused initiative it’s now preparing to roll out statewide.

Matt Williams, the state chamber’s interim president, is hopeful numbers like those in the Flatwater analysis spotlight the urgent need to respond now.

“We have to use new thoughts and new energies and new directions to step up and solve these problems,” he said. “The trajectory we are on right now is not going to be healthy for us in the long term.”

But it won’t be easy. He and other business leaders say it will take a concerted statewide effort — over a number of years — to alter the state’s current course.

(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)

In 1987, the economic anxiety across Nebraska was palpable.

Omaha was reeling from the departure of an energy company — the not-yet-infamous Enron — to Houston. Food giant ConAgra was threatening to leave, too. The deepest agricultural depression in a half-century threatened the entire state. There was a sinking feeling Nebraska was being left behind.

“The sky could be falling,” recalled John Cederberg, a business accountant long active in state policy. “I don’t think it was psychological. It was real.”

In response, the Legislature for the first time enacted major business incentives, providing tax cuts for businesses that invest and create jobs in the state.

The program known by its LB 775 bill number was blasted as costly “corporate welfare.” But the results were hard to argue.

In the decade prior to LB 775, Nebraska’s private sector job growth trailed the average for the other states in the north-central Great Plains — Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, South Dakota, North Dakota and Minnesota.

After, it began easily topping the region average, typically leading all the states but the Dakotas in average annual growth. A Flatwater analysis shows that by 2000, Nebraska had 90,000 more jobs than it would have had it continued to trail the region’s growth as before.

Nebraska remained a regional job growth leader into this century. But sometime around 2010, the narrative flipped.

Nebraska’s job growth had exceeded the regional average in 14 of 19 years prior to 2010. It has trailed the region in 11 of the 16 years since.

Nebraska’s annual average growth rate was 42% higher than the region’s in the two decades before 2010. If it had kept that pace from 2010 on, the state would boast 70,000 more jobs than it currently does.

Instead, it has trailed the region’s growth by 6% in that time frame.

Nebraska has lost ground to every regional competitor except Iowa, which has recently posted even more dismal job growth.

While Nebraska’s private sector employment was flat over the past two years, Iowa lost 28,000 such jobs. The other five states in the region added a combined 50,000 jobs in that time.

The new analysis follows studies released last fall by the Omaha chamber and Aksarben Foundation that found job growth in Omaha and Lincoln is trailing regional peer cities.

But the Flatwater analysis offers a longer view and reveals the starkly different fortunes before and after 2010. That appears to suggest something fundamental has changed.

Josie Schafer, director of the University of Nebraska at Omaha’s Center for Public Affairs Research, said 2010 is meaningful to her, because that’s right around the time Nebraska and the nation hit a “demographic cliff.”

The oldest Baby Boomers were beginning to enter their retirement years, while the smaller millennial generation was moving into the workforce.

“The Baby Boom generation retiring is such a big deal because … we don’t have a replacement for it,” she said.

The resulting worker shortage changed the game, as states’ attention turned from chasing companies to chasing people. Nebraska, it appears, has not fared well in that competition.

That notion is also supported by data on the “brain drain” — the loss through migration of college-educated people — that Schafer tracks.

In 2010, Nebraska was annually losing a net of about 1,000 people with bachelor’s degrees or higher. By 2020: 4,000 per year. The figure is still over 3,000 annually.

Bryan Slone, former CEO of the state chamber, said 2010 also signaled the dawn of a technology boom marked by the explosion of smartphones and apps.

In 2010, Apple sold fewer than 40 million iPhones. In 2015, it sold 230 million.

Nebraska has a growing tech sector, Slone said, but it’s not growing like other states and has not been viewed as a technology state. That’s making it harder to attract tech jobs and young tech workers.

“It’s very easy in this era to go in a downward spiral … because there’s a chicken and egg,” he said. “You can’t grow your economy without young people, but you can’t attract young people without having a growing economy.“

In 2010, Nebraska and the nation were also emerging from the Great Recession brought on by a housing market collapse. Housing construction in Nebraska dipped sharply amid the recession. It has never recovered.

That has led to a shortage of affordable housing and caused housing prices to spike, putting a single-family home out of the reach of many families. That, too, makes it harder to attract workers and families.

Nebraska could long boast cheap housing as a big selling point, and compared to the coasts, that’s still true. But in the seven-state region, a Flatwater analysis of federal cost-of-living data suggests Nebraska’s housing costs since 2008 have risen from fourth highest to the second highest.

“I do believe housing is a big part of this,” Slone said.

The need to build more housing. Attract young workers. Grow tech. All of those have a familiar ring. In fact, the state nearly seven years ago came out with a “blueprint” for tackling such issues.

(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)

During nearly a decade as CEO of Omaha-based Union Pacific railroad, Lance Fritz saw firsthand how ferociously Nebraska’s neighbors fight for jobs.

He remembers Arkansas’ then-governor had a war room next to his office, where maps on the wall with pins and markers denoted new business targets. Iowa, Kansas and Utah aggressively pursued UP jobs — some even making bids to wrest the entire headquarters out of the city it’s been based in since 1862.

“I would get calls all the time with different packages, seeing if they could make it attractive enough to locate there or relocate there — exit Nebraska entirely,” he said.

Some of the offers were tempting, he said, though never quite enough to justify the pain the company and its employees would endure by picking up and leaving.

Nebraska, Fritz said, needs to worry about that equation all the time.

“Whether we recognize it or not, Nebraska is in a fight for its economic survival, and the fact that we are not growing is very troublesome,” he said. “In my personal opinion, we’re not doing enough to make us look attractive to the vast population that could potentially be here.”

Around 2017, Fritz and other Omaha business leaders expressed concerns to then-Gov. Ricketts about the state’s economic trajectory and their difficulty finding workers.

That helped prompt Ricketts, then-University of Nebraska President Hank Bounds and others to launch Blueprint Nebraska, a statewide effort to produce an economic development strategy.

Fritz co-chaired the effort with Owen Palm, a major implement dealer in Nebraska’s Panhandle. Its steering committee featured business and community leaders from across the state, and it received input from thousands of Nebraskans.

In 2019, the panel’s report offered myriad ways to grow. They included tax reform, aligning the state education system from pre-K to career, stepped up housing construction and growing key industries, including tech.

But full-scale efforts to make this plan a reality never got off the ground, Slone and others said.

COVID-19 hit, making it harder to move forward and posing its own economic challenges. Slone and Fritz noted progress was made in some areas, including taxes.

A Blueprint follow-up tax study called for cutting income taxes and unpopular property taxes to make the state more competitive while also broadening the state sales tax to assure stable state finances.

The Legislature under governors Ricketts and Pillen moved to significantly cut both income and property taxes, but hasn’t broadened the sales tax.

And after federal COVID relief money vanished, the pain of simply cutting taxes, but not replacing the lost revenue, has become obvious.

The income tax cuts are estimated by the Legislature to have reduced state revenues by more than $800 million in the current fiscal year. And dollars devoted to reducing property taxes have become an ever-growing share of the budget, now amounting to one-sixth of all state spending.

Those are big reasons the state over the last two years has been making hundreds of millions of dollars in cuts to state spending — among them tens of millions in cuts to economic development programs intended to help grow the state.

Such cuts have fallen on an internship program aimed at addressing the worker shortage, a national marketing campaign to attract workers, new housing support, business site development funds and a tax credit helping businesses pay for the cost of hiring and relocating out-of-state workers.

“If you look at the facts, you can easily see (economic development) is not a priority,” said Bradford, the former Omaha chamber chair. “Some people are OK with just focusing on property taxes.”

Without a focus on growing the state, he said, Nebraskans’ tax burdens are only going to increase.

An Omaha chamber report in December likewise raised questions about the state’s “posture toward economic development.”

“Economic development efforts statewide have difficulty succeeding,” it read, “when the state is not an integral partner.”

In addition to the tax changes, Blueprint may have contributed to original decisions by the Legislature to boost funding for affordable housing construction.

Fritz also noted that to attract workers, the Blueprint report had recommended Nebraska focus on becoming a more welcoming state. He said that has been undercut by controversial social issues lawmakers have prioritized in recent years.

While the Legislature’s default position for decades was to support tax incentives, Cederberg and others said, in the past decade such programs have faced more pushback. The Legislature at one point struggled to get the votes to reauthorize the state’s main incentive program, now called ImagiNE Nebraska.

Pillen has also been seen by some within the business community as not friendly to incentives. Last year, Pillen said all of Nebraska’s tax credit and relief programs “should be focused on working-class Nebraskans, not Fortune 500 companies.” And the Department of Revenue under his anti-China bill sent letters seeking to retroactively deny businesses incentives they had earned for growing in the state.

But Pillen also introduced and pushed an incentives bill passed by lawmakers in April, one whose main purpose is to lure high-paying jobs in Union Pacific’s proposed merger with Norfolk Southern. It includes other enhancements to the state’s incentives programs and received praise from the Omaha chamber.

The Pillen administration in a statement defended the governor’s record on economic development, saying he has made it a “central priority.”

The statement mentioned numerous Pillen initiatives, including tax cuts, scholarships for high ACT scorers and a new regional economic development strategy launched with the state chamber in 2024.

The administration has acknowledged recent reductions in the Department of Economic Development, defending it as right-sizing the agency after it swelled due to pandemic-era programs.

“Nebraskans are amazing innovators who can outcompete anyone, anywhere,” Pillen said in the statement. “We’re creating a culture that’s primed for long-term economic prosperity by investing in our kids, cutting taxes, boosting value-added agriculture, and giving businesses the freedom to grow.”

Business leaders, though, say there remains much more to be done.

(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)(asterisk)

During a future-focused meeting in La Vista in late February, the Nebraska chamber brought Blueprint leaders Fritz and Palm back to the stage. Fritz’s message to the hundreds gathered was simple.

“Pick up the ball,” he said.

The gathering kicked off what the Nebraska chamber is calling “Go Big Future,” a plan meant to refocus the state on the growth-centered goals and competitive strategies spinning out of the Blueprint report.

“Some of those things, honestly, if they had been done, we would not see the change we’re seeing in the (job growth) data,” said the chamber’s Williams.

Go Big Future, like Blueprint, will focus on workforce and technology. A third prong: Making sure Nebraska has enough energy to grow — an issue that has risen to prominence since Blueprint.

One of the themes at the Go Big Future kickoff was the need to recognize the challenges the state faces and the effort required to address them.

Nebraska starts with a higher quality of life than most states, Slone said, making him optimistic it can reverse recent job growth trends. But it will take a sustained and broad-based effort.

“This has to be tackled by government, business, philanthropy and community leaders,” Slone said. “Everybody.”

This story was originally published by Flatwater Free Press and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

title: Pennington County Auditor provides clarification on absentee voting guidance
description: The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is providing clarification to voters regarding absentee voting procedures for the upcoming June 2 Primary Election.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is providing clarification to voters regarding absentee voting procedures for the upcoming June 2 Primary Election.

Recent statewide guidance has referenced the potential use of sample ballots for absentee voting in situations where official ballots are not yet available. After careful review, the Pennington County Auditor’s Office has evaluated this guidance in the context of current South Dakota law.

State statute SDCL 12-16-17 outlines the use of sample ballots in specific circumstances when official ballots have been fully exhausted. Based on consultation with legal counsel and discussions with election officials across the state, the Pennington County Auditor’s Office does not interpret this statute as applying prior to the availability of official ballots.

As a result, Pennington County will proceed with absentee voting once official ballots are finalized and received.

“Our responsibility is to ensure that every vote is handled with the highest level of integrity,” said Sabrina Green, Pennington County Auditor. “We have taken the time to seek legal guidance and collaborate with fellow auditors to ensure we are following the law as it is written. It is important to us that every ballot issued is accurate, secure, and beyond question.”

The Auditor’s Office remains committed to keeping voters informed and will continue to provide updates as ballot preparation is completed.

Voters with questions are encouraged to contact the Pennington County Auditor’s Office at 605-394-2153.

title: Sales tax may increase in Rapid City due to recent Legislature bills
description: Passed during the 2026 South Dakota Legislature session, two bills aimed at reducing property taxes in South Dakota signed by Gov. Larry Rhoden could bring sales tax increases across the state. Another bill would give municipalities a temporary 1% sales tax increase to fund special projects.
_full_text:
Passed during the 2026 South Dakota Legislature session, two bills aimed at reducing property taxes in South Dakota signed by Gov. Larry Rhoden could bring sales tax increases across the state. Another bill would give municipalities a temporary 1% sales tax increase to fund special projects.

 City Administration Center
The Rapid City administration building on 300 Sixth Street. 

The Journal spoke with Finance Director Daniel Ainslie on how this could affect residents. 

Senate Bill 96

Senate Bill 96 provides counties the ability to increase sales tax by up to 0.5% (including on cities within the counties), with the proceeds dedicated to reducing property taxes on owner-occupied properties. 

"(In) Pennington County, where we have so many tourists and activities, that would essentially mean that a lot of people from outside the county come here anyway on vacation. That new sales tax generation would be collected by the state, and the state would pass all that on to the county ... (That) means starting in 2027, property taxes for owner-occupied housing would see a historic reduction," Ainslie said.

 Daniel Ainslie
Daniel Ainslie 

According to Ainslie, the current sales tax rate in Rapid City is 6.2%, a combination of the state's 4.2% rate and the city's 2% rate.

As part of the rollout of the property tax relief bills, the Governor's Office released an interactive map showing the savings from the bills.
As an example of the property tax relief provided by the bill, if a home in Pennington County were assessed at $325,000, its property taxes would decrease by 22%.

The 2026 levy rate for Pennington County is 2.488 and a home valued at $325,000 would have a property tax of $808.60. The estimated 22% decrease in property tax brought on by SB 96 would then decrease the cost to roughly $603.70.

A mill levy is a property tax based on the assessed value of a property. One mill is equal to one dollar per $1,000 of assessed value. The levy is calculated by determining the amount of revenue each jurisdiction needs to fund its budget for public services, such as schools, parks and libraries.
Pennington County Commissioners could vote on the local sales tax increase starting July 1, 2026. The commissioners discussed the topic on Tuesday, March 17, but did not indicate their plans.
Senate Bill 245

According to reporting by South Dakota Searchlight, Senate Bill 245 would capture $114 million in ongoing annual revenue from next year’s scheduled increase of the statewide sales tax rate from 4.2% to 4.5%.

This funding would then be used to reduce local school district property taxes on owner-occupied homes.

Additionally, $56 million would be taken from state reserves to get the effort started before the sales tax rate increase.
In the same interactive map from the Governor's Office, a home in Pennington County valued at $325,000 would pay $547 in property taxes that would go to the school, saving an estimated 13%.
Ainslie said this will go into effect on July 1, 2027.
Senate Bill 1245
Senate Bill 1245 gives municipalities the option to raise sales tax by 1% to fund capital projects. 
"Essentially, what it does is authorize a different funding mechanism for projects in a community. And so specifically, the legislation says that it can be a project the city owns, or a project the city would turn over to the county, or project the city would turn over to the schools," Ainslie said. "It needs to be something kind of unique and kind of solitary identifiable."
As defined by the bill, a municipality will have to create a five-member capital improvement board to review and vote on project proposals. A project can be selected and will be taken to a public vote (requiring a minimum of 60% voter approval).
"Essentially, the mayor can appoint a committee. The committee can study the issues, then come forward with a project plan. And if the project is, say $40 million bucks to do X, they can present it to the council, and then the council would look and say, if it's $40 million that's equal to a 1% sales tax (collected) in a year. So they could say yes, we would vote to increase sales tax by 1% for a year," Ainslie explained.
He said then it would need to be referred to the electorate for an election.
The money raised by the tax would go into a special capital fund, for a maximum duration of five years, or until the collections exceed the needed amount.
Municipalities cannot impose another such tax within 24 months after the collection of funds for the special project.
"This would be an opportunity to say here's another funding stream, that way sales tax could generate money not just from the residents, but also from tourists and people coming into your community and have them offset some of the costs," Ainslie said.
"In my example, it's a $40 million project. So once $40 million is collected, then the tax goes away, and then it can't be brought up again for at least two years. So it can't just be an ongoing 1% extra tax," Ainslie explained.
Concerns over increasing sales tax
The Journal asked Ainslie whether raising sales tax on residents, when combined with seasonal tourism tax, could hurt wallets.
Altogether, these sales tax increases could take it to nearly 8% sales tax in Rapid City.
The tourism tax of 1.5% across the state runs from June through September on services and purchases on hotels and lodging, campgrounds, motor vehicle rentals, recreational equipment rentals, recreational services, spectator events, attractions and visitor-intensive businesses.
"No, absolutely, it definitely could start end up being fairly high. But I think the idea is that you're offsetting a substantial amount of people's property taxes," Ainslie said.
Ainslie said property tax from 2019 to 2024 increased dramatically across the state, and a relief strategy was needed. Searchlight reported since 2017, property tax payments have gone up 47% for owner-occupied homes and 36% for commercial property, while rising 3% for agricultural property.
"In 2019, owner-occupied homes paid 39% of the property tax across the state; in 2024, it was 44%, so there's been a large shift in the cost of government to owner-occupied housing. Yeah, that's why there's so much concern, and with that, that hasn't even been equally shared across the entire state," Ainslie said.
He continued, saying the majority of property tax increases are in the Black Hills and Sioux Falls regions.
"That's why there was so much of a push to say we got to find other offsetting revenue sources to reduce that number, because that is not sustainable. You can't have in five years owner-occupied housing increased by 47%."

title: Rapid City man sentenced to 10 years in federal prison for conspiring to distribute fentanyl, meth
description: A Rapid City man faces 10 years in federal prison on drug charges.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man faces 10 years in federal prison on drug charges.

United States Attorney Ron Parsons said in a release that District Judge Camela C. Theeler sentenced Ezra Bald Eagle, 27, to prison after he was convicted of conspiracy to distribute a controlled substance.

According to a release from the U.S. Attorney's Office:

Bald Eagle was indicted for Conspiracy to Distribute a Controlled Substance by a federal grand jury in June 2025. He pleaded guilty on December 22, 2025.

Between December 2024 and May 2025, Bald Eagle cultivated a network in the Rapid City area to facilitate the sale of fentanyl and methamphetamine. Bald Eagle routinely traveled to Colorado to purchase large quantities of methamphetamine and fentanyl and then transported the narcotics into Rapid City. Once back in South Dakota, Bald Eagle and his network of sub-distributors sold large quantities of methamphetamine and fentanyl within the Rapid City area. Bald Eagle’s operation ended on May 20, 2025, when law enforcement conducted a targeted traffic stop as Bald Eagle returned to South Dakota from purchasing narcotics in Colorado. During the stop, officers seized over 3,300 counterfeit M/30 fentanyl pills, 96 grams of fentanyl powder, and three quarters of a pound of methamphetamine.

This case is part of Operation Take Back America, a nationwide initiative that marshals the full resources of the Department of Justice to "repel the invasion of illegal immigration, achieve the total elimination of cartels and transnational criminal organizations (TCOs), and protect our communities from the perpetrators of violent crime." Operation Take Back America streamlines efforts and resources from the Department’s Organized Crime Drug Enforcement Task Forces (OCDETFs) and Project Safe Neighborhood (PSN).

This case was investigated by the Unified Narcotics Enforcement Team (UNET), Drug Enforcement Administration, and the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms, and Explosives. UNET is comprised of law enforcement from the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, the Rapid City Police Department, South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation, the South Dakota Highway Patrol and the South Dakota National Guard. Assistant U.S. Attorney Edward C. Tarbay prosecuted the case.

The sentencing took place on March 30. Bald Eagle was immediately remanded to the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service.

title: Suspect enters new guilty plea in the case of missing Navajo grandmother Ella Mae Begay
byline: By SAVANNAH PETERS and JACQUES BILLEAUD - Associated Press
description: PHOENIX (AP) — A man charged in the disappearance of a Navajo grandmother whose case has highlighted the crisis of violence against Native Americans pleaded guilty Thursday to robbery as part of a second agreement reached with prosecutors.
_full_text:
PHOENIX (AP) — A man charged in the disappearance of a Navajo grandmother whose case has highlighted the crisis of violence against Native Americans pleaded guilty Thursday to robbery as part of a second agreement reached with prosecutors.

Preston Henry Tolth, 26, could be sentenced to a maximum of five years in federal prison, with credit for three years already served, under the conditions of the proposed agreement.

U.S. District Judge Douglas Rayes asked Tolth during a hearing in Phoenix if he was entering the plea because he was, in fact, guilty.

“Yes, your honor,” Tolth said.

Ella Mae Begay was 62 when she vanished in 2021 from Sweetwater, Arizona, a community in the northern part of the Navajo Nation where she spent her days visiting with relatives and weaving rugs that she sold to nearby trading posts.

In 2022, Begay's niece Seraphine Warren walked from the Navajo Nation to Washington, D.C., to raise awareness about her aunt's disappearance and the high rate of homicides and missing persons cases in Indian Country. Begay's case has drawn national media attention and provided fuel for tribal leaders and victim advocates as they push for more law enforcement resources and cooperation between tribal and federal investigators.

Navajo Nation police identified Tolth, whose father was dating Begay's sister, as a suspect within days of her disappearance.

Under the conditions of the agreement, Tolth acknowledged using force to take Ella Mae Begay’s pickup truck, striking her in the face several times and leaving her on the side of the road before driving away and eventually selling the truck for money and methamphetamine.

Rayes is scheduled in May to review the latest plea agreement, which also protects Tolth from future murder or manslaughter charges in relation to Begay's case.

Members of Begay’s family say prosecutors negotiated the agreement against their wishes and without their input.

Begay’s son, Gerald Begay, took a break from his construction job in Denver to listen to Thursday’s hearing by phone. He told The Associated Press that he plans to attend the next court hearing and ask the judge to reject the plea agreement in hopes that Tolth can be put on trial.

“The prosecutors aren’t thinking about our rights or what we need as a family,” he said.

In an emailed statement, Lennea Montandon, a spokesperson with the U.S. Attorney’s Office for the district of Arizona, said the office has complied with federal victims' rights law while prosecuting Tolth and will continue to do so. Tolth's public defender declined to comment on the new agreement.

In a 2021 FBI interrogation, Tolth confessed to attacking Begay in a fit of anger and leaving her for dead. But Rayes dealt prosecutors a major blow by ruling that confession inadmissible in court, saying the FBI agent had unlawfully coerced Tolth into waiving his right to remain silent. In court filings, prosecutors acknowledged that weakened the government’s case significantly.

At a hearing earlier this month, Rayes rejected an earlier agreement that would have released Tolth from federal custody on a sentence of three years of time served in exchange for a guilty plea. The judge's rare move came after Begay's grieving family members testified that Tolth should not walk free without leading investigators to her remains.

“Accountability is not time served,” Begay's niece Seraphine Warren had told the judge. “It’s about truth, and we still don’t have the truth.”

Peters reported from Edgewood, New Mexico.

title: Authorities release name of man killed, suspect in Sturgis vehicle chase
description: Authorities have released the name of a man killed during a vehicle pursuit in Sturgis.
_full_text:
Authorities have released the name of a man killed during a vehicle pursuit in Sturgis.

The Sturgis Police Department said 26-year-old Egon Lake of Whitewood died from injuries suffered in a crash on Tuesday, April 14.

The driver of the suspect vehicle in the chase was Don Crisafulli, 50, of Sturgis. Police said Crisafulli's injuries are no longer critical and his condition is improving. Charges against the driver are pending.

The police department said it received a report of a stolen vehicle at a residence in Sturgis around 10:39 a.m. on Tuesday. Officers responded to the location and quickly located the vehicle.

As officers approached the vehicle to make contact with the driver, Crisafulli allegedly fled the scene at a high rate of speed, the department said. Officers initiated a traffic stop, but Crisafulli, driving a newer Ford F-250, failed to yield and continued westbound on Lazelle Street, "driving recklessly" at speeds estimated to exceed 70 miles per hour, police said.

The suspect vehicle passed other westbound traffic before colliding with a white Honda Accord, driven by Lake, also traveling westbound on Lazelle Street, police said.

Both drivers were trapped in their vehicles, unresponsive, and required extrication. A medical helicopter took Crisafulli to an area hospital, while Lake died from his injuries.

"Due to the severity of the accident," Sturgis police said it has asked South Dakota Highway Patrol for assistance in the investigation. The Sturgis Police Department is conducting a separate criminal investigation involving the driver of the stolen vehicle. Criminal charges are pending, and the incident remains under active investigation.

In separate cases, Crisafulli is charged with possession of a controlled substance (meth) in connection to March 4 and Oct. 26, 2025 arrests in Meade County, according to South Dakota court records. He's pleaded not guilty in both cases. Court records do not show a file for the crash yet.

title: Johnson pushes for bill that would bring Missouri River water to western South Dakota
description: U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson is pushing for a study to examine bringing Missouri River water to the Western Dakota Regional Water System.
_full_text:
U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson is pushing for a study to examine bringing Missouri River water to the Western Dakota Regional Water System.

Johnson testified before the U.S. House Committee on Natural Resources during a hearing Thursday, April 16, on his Western South Dakota Water Supply Project Feasibility Study Act bill, which allocates $10 million to the Bureau of Reclamation to carry out a feasibility study for a proposed water supply project that would connect the Missouri River to western South Dakota, his office said in a release.

“It’s hard to overstate the importance of water,” Johnson said in the release. “If we want a vibrant and healthy West River, we have to have the Western Dakota Regional Water System project, and I’m proud to support this important step forward.”

Johnson is in a four-way race to be South Dakota's Republican nominee for governor. Joining him in the June 2 primary are Gov. Larry Rhoden, businessman Toby Doeden and state Speaker of the House Jon Hansen.

 Johnson and Salamun
Rep. Dusty Johnson, R-South Dakota, left, takes notes as Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun, right, speaks about public safety during an event on Tuesday, April 7. 

The bill would direct the U.S. Department of the Interior to conduct the study, evaluating a proposed project that would provide municipal, rural and industrial water to the system. The bill also calls for consultation with federal, state, tribal and local stakeholders.

The bill directs the study to examine the feasibility of the project, while also proposing how costs would be split between federal and non-federal partners. The final report would be submitted to Congress and made publicly available.

The authority provided by the bill would expire 10 years after enactment.

 Missouri River
U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson is pushing for a study to examine bringing Missouri River water to the Western Dakota Regional Water System. 

The purpose of a feasibility study is to ensure that a water resources project is technically, environmentally and economically sound, Johnson's office said in the release.

Estimates put the cost of implementing a system to bring Missouri River water to western South Dakota at $2 billion. Supporters see it as similar to the Lewis and Clark Regional Water System. That system, which is 97% complete, provides Missouri River water to southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, according to the system's website. The system reached Madison in August 2024, marking the last city in South Dakota to connect.

 Water study chart.png
This chart shows that the estimated water supply in western Pennington County is already insufficient to cover demand in a dry period and will eventually be unable to meet the rising need for water in an average year. 

In July 2025, the U.S. Geological Survey published a groundwater report on Black Hills aquifers in South Dakota and Wyoming, which brought attention to findings that the water usage in the Rapid City area exceeded recharge for the Madison, Minnelusa and Inyan Kara aquifers. Bart Pfankuch of South Dakota News Watch first reported on the study.

A 2019 study done by the South Dakota School of Mines found that the water availability in the Black Hills region would not meet the demand during a drought.

Johnson introduced his bill in January.

“Western Dakota Regional Water System has completed five years of foundational work, including demand projections, intake evaluations and regional system planning. The State of South Dakota has invested more than $12 million, alongside strong local support from our 49 members. The project is ready for the next step: a Bureau of Reclamation feasibility study,” Kristin Conzet, executive director of the Western Dakota Regional Water System, said in the release. “We are grateful to Rep. Johnson and his team for their professionalism and assistance throughout the federal process.”

 PHOTOS: The Missouri River near Chamberlain and Oacoma
A view from the banks of the Missouri River in Chamberlain and Oacoma.

title: Rapid City plans to take 'aggressive' actions to remove graffiti
description: It’s a scene all too common in Rapid City: park bathrooms, businesses, utility boxes, vacant buildings adorned with graffiti markings. The markings deface public and private property, may contain obscene language or gestures, and are often part of gang-related activity.
_full_text:
It’s a scene all too common in Rapid City: park bathrooms, businesses, utility boxes, vacant buildings adorned with graffiti markings. The markings deface public and private property, may contain obscene language or gestures, and are often part of gang-related activity.

Effective this spring, the City of Rapid City is taking an aggressive approach to reducing and removing graffiti in the community and they are seeking the public’s assistance in the reporting of graffiti on city-owned property, private property and public utilities.

The public is encouraged to report incidences of graffiti including locations. The City’s goal is to have the graffiti removed within 24 hours of the report.

Currently, the public can use the “Report A Problem” feature on the Public Works page of the City website. The City is revamping its website and the new site will include options to provide additional details in the reporting of graffiti issues. The public can access the “Report A Problem” feature at https://bit.ly/RapidCityLightsAndPotholes , which can be utilized to report other public infrastructure issues such as potholes and non-working street or traffic lights.

“Graffiti is a concern in Rapid City. It doesn’t matter where you look, it can be in our parks, on the side of businesses or vacant buildings, on utility boxes or garages,” said Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun. “The markings take away from the beauty of our community, are often offensive, and negatively impact on the personality of our community and our residents.”

Graffiti removal would be done in many instances by the IGNITE team through the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office or Rapid City Public Works staff. The Rapid City Parks Department will address graffiti issues involving parks-owned assets such as restrooms, playgrounds and picnic areas. Graffiti on private property can be reported to the City’s Code Enforcement Division, which will address incidences with property owners to have the graffiti removed, abated or removed by vendors hired by the city. Graffiti on utility-owned infrastructure, such as utility boxes and railroad areas, will be referred to the appropriate utility. Utilities will be given 10 days to remove the graffiti.

Graffiti is defined as any unauthorized marking, painting, drawing, etching or inscription. Graffiti markings often include gang-related messaging and obscene or vulgar language.

title: Rapid City man pleads not guilty to attempted murder
description: A Rapid City man pleaded not guilty to several aggravated assault charges and attempted first-degree murder for an alleged knife incident that occurred on Sept. 20, 2025.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man pleaded not guilty to several aggravated assault charges and attempted first-degree murder for an alleged knife incident that occurred on Sept. 20, 2025. 

Greeley White Face, 56, appeared for his arraignment before Presiding Circuit Court Judge Heidi Linngren at the Pennington County Courthouse on Thursday, April 16.

White Face is indicted for attempted first-degree murder and two counts of aggravated assault, each charge is in the alternative. White Face can only be convicted of one of the charges, not two or more. 

In South Dakota, attempted first-degree murder is a Class 2 felony with a maximum sentence of 25 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. Aggravated assault is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. 

The state also filed Part II information, labeling White Face as a habitual offender. White Face was convicted on July 25, 2022, for reckless burning or exploding. 

White Face's next court appearance is set for 2:15 p.m. on April 30.

title: Absentee ballots will not be available at start of early voting period for June primary
description: The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is notifying voters that absentee ballots for the upcoming June 2 Primary Election will not be available on the first day of the absentee voting period.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Auditor’s Office is notifying voters that absentee ballots for the upcoming June 2 Primary Election will not be available on the first day of the absentee voting period.

Under South Dakota law, absentee voting is scheduled to begin 46 days prior to an election pursuant to SDCL 12-19-1.2. However, the candidate petition filing deadline was March 31st, as established in SDCL 12-6-4, which determines the final list of candidates to appear on the ballot. This statutory timing results in a brief delay between the start of the absentee voting period and the availability of ballots.

Ballot preparation includes certification, layout, programming, proofing, and printing. Because ballots cannot be finalized until after the petition filing deadline has passed and candidate eligibility is confirmed, additional time is required.

This delay is a result of state law timelines and is not caused by the Pennington County Auditor’s Office or the County Auditor.

The Auditor’s Office is working diligently to complete this process as quickly as possible. Once ballots are available:

Absentee ballots will be mailed immediately to voters who have submitted applications
In-person absentee voting will begin at the Auditor’s Office
Updates will be shared through the county website, local media, and official communication channels

“Our office is committed to ensuring ballots are accurate and available to voters as quickly as possible,” said Sabrina Green, Pennington County Auditor. “In the meantime, we encourage everyone to take a moment to check that they are registered to vote and that their information is up to date on the state’s website. If you’re not registered, our office is here to help.”

Voters are encouraged to:

Submit absentee ballot applications early
Verify their voter registration status by visiting the South Dakota Secretary of State’s website
Contact or visit the Auditor’s Office for assistance with voter registration if needed
Monitor official county communications or call our office for updates on ballot availability

The deadline to request an absentee ballot remains 5:00 p.m. the day before the election, pursuant to SDCL 12-19-2.

For more information, please contact the Pennington County Auditor’s Office at 605-394-2153 or visit PennCo.org.

title: Rapid City crews preparing to sweep every street in city in May
description: Rapid City: Get ready for Sweeps Month!
_full_text:
Rapid City: Get ready for Sweeps Month!

City crews at Rapid City’s Streets Division are again preparing for "Sweeps Month" this May — no, not the old-style TV Sweeps Month when networks jockeyed for ratings with blockbuster movies and season-ending suspense-filled shows.

 PHOTO Sweeper.jpg
Next month, crews from the City’s Streets Division are planning on sweeping every street on Rapid City’s 400-plus mile transportation system. 

Next month, crews from the City’s Streets Division are planning on sweeping every street on Rapid City’s 400-plus mile transportation system, putting the fleet of 10 sweepers to the test to collect debris left over on streets from the winter season. Most of the City’s street system contains multiple lanes of traffic.

Last May, crews traveled 4,122 miles, logged just shy of 265 hours of driving time and collected 385.5 tons of material off City streets.

And like last year, City officials are inviting the public to monitor the progress and work of the sweeper crews during May. The public can monitor the location of the sweepers by visiting https://www.rcgov.org/departments/public-works/streets-division.html . Sweeper crews will get started May 1 with plans to complete work on all 21 routes by May 27.

Drivers are advised to use caution when approaching or passing the slow-moving street sweepers and to be mindful of oncoming traffic in the work area.

 PHOTO City Sweeper.jpg
Last May, crews traveled 4,122 miles, logged just shy of 265 hours of driving time and collected 385.5 tons of material off City streets. 

On average, City sweepers collect more than 2,000 tons of material from city streets each year. That’s a lot of gravel, dirt, rock and other debris, which includes cups, bottles, cigarette butts and paper items.

“Our city sweepers may move along at slow speeds but they move tons of debris off city streets each year,” said City Streets Superintendent Jesse Rieb. “Like last year, the crews are up for the challenge. They are looking forward to attacking every street in Rapid City and collecting debris left over from the winter season.”

The sweepers and crews collect tons of material left behind from the thousands of cars traveling city streets on a daily basis, as well as picking up debris residing on streets and gutters produced from various weather conditions, and material deposited from washouts produced from heavy precipitation.

Sweeper crews work in residential areas during the day and main line areas at night for safety reasons to minimize interaction with traffic. The collected material is dropped off at the Street Shop detention area on Steele Avenue, dried and then taken to the Rapid City Landfill, which uses the material for cover.

 PHOTOS: Dinosaur Park in Rapid City
Dinosaur Park is a Rapid City institution. Opened in 1937, the park features a variety of dinosaur sculptures and a panoramic view of Rapid City and the Black Hills.
