title: Ardin Cychosz drops out of Rapid City Council Ward 4 race
description: On Wednesday morning, April 15, Rapid City Council Ward 4 candidate Ardin Cychosz announced on Facebook he is dropping out of the race.
_full_text:
On Wednesday morning, April 15, Rapid City Council Ward 4 candidate Ardin Cychosz announced on Facebook he is dropping out of the race.

 Ardin Cychosz
Ardin Cychosz  

"This was not an easy decision. It is one of the more difficult and deeply considered decisions I have had to make. Over the past several months, I have had the opportunity to meet and speak with hundreds of residents and many great community leaders, and I am incredibly grateful for the conversations, support, and trust that so many of you extended to me," Cychosz said in the post.

One seat on each of the five wards is currently contested. Ward 4 still has Valeriah Big Eagle and incumbent John Roberts are still in the running.

According to Cychosz, his current job at Creative Iron Works & Black Hills Powder Coating has him committed to further responsibilities, making balancing both roles less viable.

He told the Journal that one conflicting issue would be traveling, as he will be stepping into a new role within the company.

Cychosz's name will still be on the ballot in the June 2 election, and he wants to make it clear he is dropping out.

 Sign at the library
A sign in the Rapid City Public Library directs voters to the booths on Tuesday, Jan. 20. 

"I have chosen to step back rather than overextend and risk underdelivering. Serving on our City Council is a privilege that deserves full attention and a high standard of execution, " Cychosz wrote. "While I am withdrawing from active candidacy, I understand that my name may still appear on the ballot due to timing, and I want to be transparent about that to avoid confusion."
 
The current seats up for election on June 2 are held by Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2), Kevin Maher (Ward 3), John Roberts (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5).
Biberdorf, Seachris, Maher and Roberts have turned in their required 50 valid signatures to qualify for the ballot on the June 2 election. Petitions were due on Tuesday, March 24.

Pettigrew is not seeking reelection.
The official order the ballot goes:

Ward 1: Josh Biberdorf, Murray Lee

Ward 2: Christopher Vanderhoof, Lindsey Seachris

Ward 3: Andrea Schaefer, Kevin Maher

Ward 4: Valeriah Big Eagle, John Roberts, Ardin Jay Cychosz

Ward 5: Pat Roseland, Laura Armstrong
Absentee voting begins on April 17 and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  

Voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.
For more information or questions, contact the City Finance Department at 605-394-4143.

title: Rapid City Legal and Finance Committee approves 2nd reading of backyard chickens ordinance
description: The Rapid Legal and Finance Committee approved the second reading of a backyard chickens ordinance during its Wednesday, April 15, meeting. Final approval will be needed by City Council at the April 20 meeting.
_full_text:
The Rapid Legal and Finance Committee approved the second reading of a backyard chickens ordinance during its Wednesday, April 15, meeting. Final approval will be needed by City Council at the April 20 meeting.

The committee voted 3-1, with Councilman Lance Lehmann (Ward 4) voting no.

The ordinance would legalize up to six hens on residential property, excluding any roosters.

The item has been proposed to the council many times over the last decade, but with additional regulations and stronger enforcement, the pro-hen community and Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) hope to get the ordinance approved.

Currently, Rapid City Municipal Code prohibits the keeping of backyard hens except on land that is zoned for agricultural uses. 

The proposed ordinance requires containment for chickens through an enclosed coop and chicken run, an enclosed outdoor space, to keep predators out. The coops must be ventilated and provide a minimum area of at least 2 square feet per chicken. Runs must be attached to the coop and have a minimum area of at least 10 square feet per chicken.
Coops, runs and exercise yards must be 25 feet from any residential dwelling, church, school, or place of business, excluding the residence on the subject property, and 15 feet from any lot line.

The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines. 
"It is unlawful to keep hens where the provisions of § 6.08.021 are not met. Each day a violation occurs is a separate offense. In addition to any other remedies or causes of action the city may possess, the keeping or maintaining of a hen in a manner inconsistent with § 6.08.021 is declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated by the city as provided by state law and City ordinance," the ordinance reads.

Violations include owning roosters, failing to provide feed and water at all times and/or keeping poultry for commercial use.

title: 'The most difficult:' Final defendant sent to prison for Surfwood double murders in Rapid City
description: Louis Perea was 19 years old when he and several others entered an apartment in Surfwood Drive left unlocked by an underage accomplice. Two of the occupants, members of rival gang, ended up dead.
_full_text:
Louis Perea was 19 years old when he and several others entered an apartment in Surfwood Drive left unlocked by an underage accomplice. Two of the occupants, members of rival gang, ended up dead. 

On Tuesday, April 14, the now 23-year-old Perea sat in a Pennington County courtroom wearing crisp red and white stripes for his sentencing on aiding and abetting first-degree manslaughter. He pleaded guilty on April 13, 2023, almost exactly three years before his sentencing. 

"This is the culmination of this entire case," Presiding Circuit Court Judge Heidi Linngren said.

 Louis Perea
Louis Perea 

The killings of 29-year-old Joseph Standing Bear, of Norris, and 37-year-old Petan Milk, of Rapid City, led to a series of arrests spanning jurisdictions, charges, plea deals and a triple-defendant trial. All told, eight people faced charges for either involvement in the deaths or the aftermath. Two of those were minors at the time, so their cases are not public. Half of the adults took plea deals and half were convicted at trial. 

John Murphy, Perea's defense attorney, said his client has worked with law enforcement and sat in jail for three years, "and has suffered for it." 

The red and white stripes Perea wore indicate he is being held in administrative segregation.

"He's terrified of what's going to happen once he's sentenced," Murphy said. 

Perea cooperated with law enforcement and testified at the trial of three others, including the shooter. The jury found the three guilty on all charges. 

"I tried my best to do everything in this case," Perea told the judge. 

Kevin Krull, a prosecutor on the case from the attorney general's office, told the judge that out of everyone who testified in the case, "He (Perea) probably did the best."

In Perea's plea agreement, the state agreed to ask for no more than 30 years in prison. Generally, the state does not recommend a number lower than what is in the plea agreement, but Krull told the judge the state supported a sentence from 25 to 30 years. 

Linngren said out of all the sentencings in the case, "this one is probably the most difficult." She said she sees a "glimmer of hope" in Perea before sentencing him to 25 years in prison because of "the work you did and the honesty you portrayed."

"Mr. Perea is the only one who has expressed any type of remorse," Linngren said. 

She also noted his cooperation and the fact that he came forward with the truth even though he knew it was dangerous.

Even with the lowest IQ of the group, he knew it was the right thing to do, Linngren said. Murphy said Perea's IQ is 75, per a psychological evaluation conducted ahead of sentencing. The evaluator, Dr. Kari Scovel in Rapid City, also found Perea suffers from alcohol-related neurological development disorder.

"My client was a young 19," Murphy said, adding Scovel noted he's impressionable. 

Those factors, which were considered mitigating and impacted Linngren's sentencing decision, but Linngren said Perea's impressionability, "in a way, makes him dangerous." 

The judge also emphasized the fact that two people were "executed" without a chance to defend themselves. 

"Two people were violently killed, and nobody did anything to help them," Linngren told Perea.

Linngren urged Perea to take advantage of the resources available to him in prison and noted he'll be eligible for parole because the crime was committed just a year before the state passed the truth-in-sentencing law.

The law made 18 charges ineligible for parole, including first-degree manslaughter. Since Perea falls under the old system, he'll be eligible for parole after serving 50% of his sentence.

Other defendants sentences and charges

Erin Provancial, 45, was painted as the mother figure of the group. She stayed in the vehicle during the killings. A Pennington County jury convicted her on Feb. 24 of two counts of aiding and abetting first-degree murder and one count of conspiracy to commit first-degree murder. She was sentenced to life in prison on all charges on March 9. 
Dillon Wilson, 22, was one of the people who went into the apartment, per trial testimony. A jury found him guilty on Feb. 24 of two counts of aiding and abetting first-degree murder and one count of conspiracy to commit first-degree murder. He was sentenced to life in prison on all charges on March 9, on the same day as Provancial. 
Montell Olivera, 29, was the triggerman per trial testimony. A Pennington County Jury found Olivera guilty on Tuesday, Feb. 24, on two counts of first-degree murder and one count of conspiracy to commit first-degree murder. He was sentenced to life on all charges on March 13.
Chase Quick Bear, 23, was also in the apartment. He pleaded guilty in April 2023 to aiding and abetting aggravated assault. On April 13, he was sentenced to 15 years in prison, the maximum sentence for the charge. 
Benita Cisneros, 43, the driver, pleaded guilty to aiding and abetting first degree manslaughter on Nov. 16, 2023. She was sentenced on April 1 to 35 years in prison.

title: Kentucky man pleads not guilty to shooting death at Oxford Apartments in Rapid City
description: A Kentucky man pleaded not guilty to first-degree murder and other charges related to the death of a man at a north Rapid City apartment building.
_full_text:
A Kentucky man pleaded not guilty to first-degree murder and other charges related to the death of a man at a north Rapid City apartment building.

Kane Youngman, 30, appeared for his arraignment before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse on Tuesday, April 14.

Police arrested Youngman on Friday, March 13, for his alleged connection to the Aug. 27, 2025, shooting death of 29-year-old Monte Gakin at Oxford Apartments at 225 E. Watertown St. The arrest was nearly seven months after the killing. Youngman was located at an apartment on East Anamosa Street.

Youngman is charged with first-degree murder and, in the alternative, second-degree murder, meaning he can only be found guilty of one or the other, not both. He was also indicted for possession of a firearm by a felon. 

In South Dakota, first-degree murder is a Class A felony with a mandatory minimum of life in prison, and a maximum punishment of the death penalty. Second-degree murder is a Class B felony which has a mandatory minimum of life in prison and may include a $50,000 fine. Possession of a firearm by a felon is a Class 6 felony with a maximum sentence of two years in prison and a $4,000 fine. 

Youngman is labeled as a habitual offender, as the state has filed a Part II information. The document highlights that Youngman was convicted in August 2018 of possession of a firearm by a prohibited person, in February 2017 he was convicted of escape from custody, and in March 2016 he was convicted of assault with a dangerous weapon.

The Part II Information can increase the possession of a firearm as a felon from a Class 6 felony to a Class C felony, with a maximum punishment of life in prison. 

Officers were called to the scene where Gakin died around 7:15 p.m. on Aug. 27, 2025, on a report of gunshots heard in the building, police said. Officers found Gakin suffering from gunshot wounds inside an apartment. Police rendered emergency first aid to Gakin but he ultimately died at the building.

Over the course of the investigation, investigators established Youngman’s involvement in the shooting through physical evidence recovered from the scene and ongoing forensic testing and analysis, police said.

Police told the Journal in January 2026 investigators had conducted dozens of interviews, reviewed hours of video footage and analyzed and examined numerous pieces of evidence. The primary persons of interest in the case had been identified, police said at the time, noting there wasn't an ongoing threat to the public.

Youngman's next hearing is set for 1:30 p.m. on May 5.

title: The data center boom meets resistance in Maine as lawmakers pass a yearlong freeze
byline: By PATRICK WHITTLE and MARC LEVY - Associated Press
description: PORTLAND, Maine (AP) — Maine could impose the nation’s first statewide moratorium on energy-hungry data centers in a sign of growing political opposition to tech giants’ massive structures that have stoked fears about blackouts, rising electricity bills and voracious water needs.
_full_text:
PORTLAND, Maine (AP) — Maine could impose the nation’s first statewide moratorium on energy-hungry data centers in a sign of growing political opposition to tech giants’ massive structures that have stoked fears about blackouts, rising electricity bills and voracious water needs.

The legislation arose in a state that isn't necessarily a destination for the computer-stocked warehouses that power artificial intelligence, but a couple of proposals there generated intense community backlash and helped propel a measure quickly through the state's Democratic-controlled Legislature. Lawmakers on Tuesday approved sending the bill to Gov. Janet Mills, a Democrat who is running for U.S. Senate.

It's the latest sign that increasingly stiff opposition at the local level is gaining a foothold higher up the political ladder. Tech giants and the data centers they are building have high-level support from President Donald Trump's administration and many governors, who see them as economic engines and essential for winning the artificial intelligence race with China, even as voters raise concerns about the enormous amount of power data centers use. Analysts also warn of the possibility of blackouts in the mid-Atlantic grid in the coming years.

Proposals to slap a moratorium on data centers have been introduced in at least a dozen states, but other than Maine’s, none have even passed a legislative chamber. That's as some states struggle to meet clean energy goals and the centers emerge as a barrier.

Maine's legislation would institute a moratorium for more than a year on data centers above a certain size, and create a special council to help towns vet potential projects. Mills’ office did not respond to a request for comment Wednesday on whether she plans to sign the bill.

“It's not that there's no place for data centers in Maine,” said Democratic Rep. Melanie Sachs, who sponsored the measure. “Frankly, the tradeoffs have not been shown to be of benefit to our ratepayers, water usage or community benefit in terms of economic activity.”

Why Maine?

Despite Maine's relatively low profile among developers of massive data centers, called hyperscalers, supporters of the projects said the moratorium will still matter long into the future to all sorts of industries.

“It says that the state is willing to essentially put a blanket ban on you if it decides that you may be politically unfavorable,” said Dan Diorio of the Data Center Coalition, a trade association that includes tech companies and developers.

They said it could deter data center developers from going to Maine and deprive the state of a long-term economic development anchor that attracts other industries. It also means local builders and labor unions won't develop the skills necessary to build the facilities and might leave them lagging behind other states, they said.

“We think that these data centers could bring good jobs, good opportunities to these regions,” said Montana Towers, a policy analyst with the free market Maine Policy Institute. “And a lot of these concerns about them are luddite in nature.”

Nonetheless, several communities in Maine have raised concerns about a lack of transparency in potential data center projects. The Maine moratorium is largely about getting those communities to have input in the development process, said Joe Oliva, a spokesperson for the Maine Broadband Coalition and GrowSmart Maine, which both supported the moratorium.

“If this is going to come, we want to be in early and often on the conversation," Oliva said.

Growing opposition

Since last summer, community opposition has become a serious concern for data center developers, with numerous municipalities defeating their proposals in planning and zoning board votes before packed rooms of angry residents.

A handful of counties and municipalities in the U.S. have imposed a moratorium, and some bills emerged in states where development is brisk such as Virginia, Michigan and Wisconsin. Voters in Festus, Missouri, a suburb of St. Louis, replaced half of their city's eight-member city council this month amid a backlash over a data center project.

Some bills died without action, like one in Georgia, a data center hotspot. Other states have explored other ways to rein in data centers, such as by imposing tougher standards around water and energy use, transparency and protection of ratepayers and communities.

In Ohio, residents are trying to bypass the Legislature and get a measure on the ballot in November that is designed to permanently ban hyperscale data centers. They'll need to gather more than 400,000 voter signatures by July 1 in what is perhaps the strictest measure of any under consideration.

Public officials, developers and other interests could otherwise “make this state a virtually unbroken field of data centers,” said Austin Baurichter, a Cincinnati-area lawyer who is helping organize the effort.

In South Dakota, a one-year moratorium bill failed in a state Senate committee amid opposition from power plant owners and data center developers. The governor also opposed it, telling senators that such planning is best done at the local level and that a statewide moratorium holds back municipalities that want a data center.

The sponsor, Republican Sen. Taffy Howard, told senators that “citizens are asking for this” and that the opponents are all lobbyists, "billion-dollar corporations" and government officials.

"Are you going to listen to the people or the paid lobbyists?” she asked.

Levy reported from Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.

Follow Patrick Whittle on X at https://x.com/pxwhittle and Marc Levy at https://x.com/timelywriter

title: National American University: Where students come to learn the architecture of strategic security
byline: Sponsored content by Mary McHugh, Brand Ave. Studios contributing writer
description: From a small-town sheriff trying to maintain public safety to a national security adviser identifying vulnerabilities in a potential cyberattack for the president of the United States, strategic security helps prevent and deter threats to community leaders, corporate executives and organizations in today’s complex world.
_full_text:
nau getty

From a small-town sheriff trying to maintain public safety to a national security adviser identifying vulnerabilities in a potential cyberattack for the president of the United States, strategic security helps prevent and deter threats to community leaders, corporate executives and organizations in today’s complex world.

At National American University’s Henley-Putnam School of Strategic Security, students learn from the associate to the doctoral levels of study about intelligence and threat management designed to prepare them for careers in the security field, including government intelligence, private security and federal agencies. The program integrates intelligence management, counterterrorism and protection management into a cohesive strategic framework. Courses are taught by faculty with field experience in organizations such as the FBI, CIA and military intelligence.

Unlike traditional programs that often focus on political or military history, NAU’s curriculum emphasizes proactive deterrence and the application of objective information to prevent threats before they occur.

“We don’t teach the study of war,” said Barbara Burke, Ph.D., dean of Henley-Putnam School of Strategic Security. “We teach the architecture of safety, with the goal of protecting people, places, things and ideas. The most successful things our graduates have done, you’ve never seen. Why? Because they were successful in preventing a threat. Whether the goal is to protect a CEO, a power grid or a trade secret, our job is to make those entities untouchable, surrounded by a 360-degree shield.”

Burke said that “shield” can be thought of as guns, gates and guards. The presence of a gun says, “not today.” A gate, be it a physical border crossing or an IT firewall, serves as a point of denial. And, guards, who provide the human element of security, are trained to spot a lie or a threat long before it reaches the gate.

“We transform our students into safety architects,” Burke said. “They learn to handle the ‘what-ifs’ so the world doesn’t have to, ensuring that the ‘what-ifs’ never happened and never become what happened. We don’t just teach how to catch a thief; we teach how to make the thief realize he’s already lost before he even touches the gates.”

Graduates are trained to assume leadership roles in public and private sectors. These can include careers as an intelligence analyst, counterterrorism specialist, federal marshal, secret service agent, chief security officer or strategic security adviser, to name a few. Experts in the field are increasingly sought by corporations and nonprofits who protect critical infrastructure, data and personnel.

These jobs can include identifying competitors attempting to steal corporate trade secrets, protecting personnel globally in high-threat environments, planning security during the construction or renovation of a facility, identifying and neutralizing foreign intelligence activities, and limiting access to sensitive data and critical systems.

With the integration of AI into most facets of life today, Burke said graduates learn to become the “human in the loop” in terms of security technology.

“They learn to interpret and guide the subtle differences AI might produce during an exercise and learn how to question the feedback,” she said. “We teach them critical thinking skills, predictive analysis skills, communication skills so you can recognize what info needs to be gathered, how and where to get that info, to look at it critically and then have the courage to present it or speak against the group thought, and communicate to the decision-maker or become part of the decision cycle themselves. These are basic skills taught and wrapped around our courses from the associate through the doctorate level.”

For more information on NAU’s Henley-Putnam School of Strategic Security programs, please visit national.edu/schools/henley-putnam.

This content was produced by Brand Ave. Studios. The news and editorial departments had no role in its creation or display. Brand Ave. Studios connects advertisers with a targeted audience through compelling content programs, from concept to production and distribution. For more information contact sales@brandavestudios.com.
