title: Federal jury convicts Porcupine man of assault and witness tampering
description: A federal jury convicted a Porcupine man on a number of counts related to an abusive relationship.
_full_text:
A federal jury convicted a Porcupine man on a number of counts related to an abusive relationship.

The U.S. Attorney's Office for South Dakota said Jimmy Pilcher, 49, was found guilty of assault by strangulation and suffocation, three counts of assault resulting in substantial bodily injury to a spouse, intimate partner or dating partner, assault with a dangerous weapon and witness tampering, following a three-day jury trial in federal district court in Rapid City. The jury returned the verdict on April 9, 2026. 

The charges carry a maximum penalty of a sentence of 20 years in prison and/or a $250,000 fine, and a $600 special assessment to the Federal Crime Victims Fund.

Pilcher was indicted by a federal grand jury in January 2026.

According to a release from the U.S. Attorney's Office:

During the wintertime of 2024, Pilcher began a romantic relationship with the victim. Shortly after their relationship started, Pilcher began controlling the victim by monitoring her communications with others. When Pilcher started to lose control of his victim, he began assaulting her and using other forms of mental and emotional manipulation on her.

In July 2025, Pilcher saw a picture of the victim with another man and accused her of cheating on him. Pilcher became enraged and punched the victim in the face and stomach and pulled her hair. He kneed her thigh, then strangled the victim over a kitchen sink. This strangulation caused the victim to see flashes of light and impeded her ability to breathe.

The next day she received medical attention at Indian Health Services, and the staff observed injuries consistent with abuse, including bruising around her neck.

In October 2025, Pilcher assaulted the same victim in the head and face inside a truck in rural Pine Ridge. Witnesses intervened and were able to get the victim away from Pilcher. The Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety responded to the scene and arrested Pilcher. Following his arrest, Pilcher began contacting the victim from within the jail. Pilcher was able to convince the victim not to show up for court and was eventually released from jail. A tribal protection order was, however, still in effect.

On December 5, 2025, in violation of the protection order, Pilcher took the victim inside his truck and drove to a field away from his residence. While inside the truck, Pilcher accused her of cheating on him with another male. Pilcher dragged the victim across the seat, threw her out of the truck, and pulled her up by her hair. During this beating, Pilcher struck the victim in the face, hit her in the back of the head, punched and kicked her with shod feet, and strangled her. During the beating, Pilcher threatened to kill her and her family. The victim was able to get away from Pilcher and got a ride to her house. Pilcher was eventually arrested by law enforcement.

After his arrest, Pilcher contacted the victim from the jail in Pine Ridge. Pilcher attempted to persuade the victim into not pursuing charges and pleaded with her not to report the full extent of the abuse. Pilcher told the victim and his sister that he had spoken with law enforcement and was aware that his case was under federal investigation.

This matter was prosecuted by the U.S. Attorney’s Office because the Major Crimes Act, a federal statute, mandates that certain violent crimes alleged to have occurred in Indian country be prosecuted in federal court as opposed to state court. This case was investigated by the Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety. Assistant U.S. Attorney Megan Poppen prosecuted the case.

A presentence investigation was ordered and sentencing will be scheduled at a later date. The defendant was remanded to the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service.

title: Police: Stolen vehicle suspect flees, hits and kills driver in Sturgis
description: One person is dead after a vehicle pursuit in Sturgis.
_full_text:
One person is dead after a vehicle pursuit in Sturgis.

The Sturgis Police Department said around 10:39 a.m. on Tuesday, April 14, it received a report of a stolen vehicle at a residence in Sturgis. Officers responded to the location and quickly located the vehicle.

As officers approached the vehicle to make contact with the driver, the suspect fled the scene at a high rate of speed, the department said. Officers initiated a traffic stop, but the suspect, driving a newer Ford F-250, failed to yield and continued westbound on Lazelle Street, "driving recklessly" at speeds estimated to exceed 70 miles per hour, police said.

The suspect vehicle passed other westbound traffic before colliding with a white Honda Accord also traveling westbound on Lazelle Street, police said.

Both drivers were trapped in their vehicles, unresponsive, and required extrication. The driver of the stolen truck was transported by medical helicopter to an area hospital in critical condition. The driver of the Honda Accord sustained fatal injuries in the crash.

"Due to the severity of the accident," Sturgis police said it has asked South Dakota Highway Patrol for assistance in the investigation. The Sturgis Police Department is conducting a separate criminal investigation involving the driver of the stolen vehicle. Criminal charges are pending, and the incident remains under active investigation.

The police department did not release the suspect's name.

Agencies assisting at the scene include the South Dakota Highway Patrol, Meade County Sheriff’s Office, Meade County Dispatch, Sturgis Fire and Ambulance, and the Sturgis Public Works Department.

title: Rhoden announces $4 million investment for gunsmith program at Western Dakota Tech in Rapid City
description: A new gunsmithing curriculum is coming to the Western Dakota Technical College in Rapid City.
_full_text:
A new gunsmithing curriculum is coming to the Western Dakota Technical College in Rapid City.

 Rhoden
Gov. Larry Rhoden announced $4 millions in Future Fund dollars will go to the Western Dakota Technical College for a gunsmithing program. 

"As other states have taken away Second Amendment rights, South Dakota said, 'Not here'" Gov. Larry Rhoden said during an announcement event at the Rapid City college on Tuesday, April 14. "Here we welcome firearm businesses with open arms."

Rhoden said he'll award $4 million from the Future Fund to the college to purchase equipment and the accredited Colorado School of Trades, which focuses on gunsmithing.

The governor is running for re-election and faces three opponents in the Republican primary, which will be held on June 2: U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson of Mitchell, state Speaker of the House Jon Hansen of Dell Rapids and businessman Toby Doeden of Aberdeen.

 Kendra Ericson
Western Dakota Technical College President Kendra Ericson speaks during an announcement event about the schools impending gunsmithing program on Tuesday, April 14, at Western Dakota Tech. 

"I want to extend a sincere thank you to the governor. Thank you for joining us today, for your dedication and commitment to our South Dakota workforce, national security industries and everything that you're doing right here in Rapid City and in-house at Western Dakota Tech," Ericson said. "We pride ourselves in the education that we deliver."

Ericson said the new program will expand on the college's hands-on training, now including defense and firearms manufacturing.

 Gunsmithing
Colorado School of Trades President Ryan Lishner, left, speaks with Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun. 

The technical college provides degree programs in business and computers, construction and manufacturing, energy and environmental technologies, health sciences, legal and public services, transportation technologies and more.

Ericson said the college expects to start the gunsmithing program in the fall of 2027, with 30 to 36 students enrolled. Specifics on what kind of gun parts they'll use is still being determined. 

 Rhoden Welding
Gov. Larry Rhoden showcases his welding abilities at Western Dakota Technical College on Tuesday, April 14. 

Rhoden said this announcement, along with the recent announcement of the new South Dakota Defense Institute in Rapid City, will further expand the national security and defense industry in the state.

"This initiative allows us to once again expand in an industry area with both defense and firearms, to expand what we're doing here at Western Dakota Tech. I also want to recognize President Ryan Lishner of the Colorado School of Trades. Thank you for your partnership and certainly your expertise and firearms," Ericson said.

 Western Dakota Tech
Western Dakota Technical College is located at 600 Mickelson Drive in Rapid City. 

Lishner said, "At the end of the day we're looking for the growth in the industry and the success of students. (We) found that through Colorado legislation, South Dakota was willing to support our program in the firearms industry at a much higher level than Colorado. Within the last couple years, we've seen our legislature enact laws that are starting to impede what firearms dealers and firearm manufacturers can do, and that's getting to where it's impacting our education process."

Lishner said their program currently has 140 students with 14 to 20 faculty and staff. Ericson said the college, based in Lakewood, Colorado, will move all its material to Rapid City, citing that stricter gun laws in Colorado have made the industry concerned.

On job markets for graduates of the two-year program, Ericson said, "They do enter the workforce in multiple ways, whether that is in the firearms manufacturing, being their own business owner or working for a high-end armory (business)," in addition to military service.

"With our national defense being the next big industry, the businesses we already have here in the Black Hills and western South Dakota and South Dakota in general, and our vision for the future of the defense industry, everything fits. This is, I think, the perfect location for a gunsmithing school," Rhoden said.

Ericson said Rhoden will be the school's commencement keynote for the graduating class on May 16.

title: 'Lack of accountability': Rapid City area agencies launch parole task force
byline: MADISON WILLIS, MIKE BROWNLEE and SHALOM BAER GEE Journal staff
description: The Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County State’s Attorney are highlighting the arrests of parolees and building a task force as gubernatorial candidates also push for reform.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County State’s Attorney are highlighting the arrests of parolees and building a task force as gubernatorial candidates also push for reform.

The two West River law enforcement agencies sent out a joint release, emphasizing the arrests of seven parolees, on Monday, April 6, and announced about a week later — on Tuesday, April 14 — they’ll be forming a parole task force and meeting with the South Dakota Department of Corrections to discuss the topic.

“It’s time for a concerted effort,” Pennington County State’s Attorney Lara Roetzel said during a Tuesday press conference.

 South Dakota State Penitentiary
An exterior view of the South Dakota State Penitentiary in Sioux Falls. 

In the joint Monday, April 6, release, the local law enforcement agencies said they “feel it is important for our citizens to have an understanding of the circumstances under which these individuals are causing issues in our community. These individuals are, by definition, still in the custody of the South Dakota Department of Corrections after being convicted of previous felonies and sentenced to serve time in prison for those crimes."

“For years, local law enforcement has had a front row seat to the lack of accountability for parolees who continue to reoffend while they are allowed to serve out their prison sentences outside of the penitentiary setting,” Rapid City Police Chief Don Hedrick told the Journal. “Our community deserves systems that protect our law-abiding citizens from victimization and prioritize public safety.”

The Journal took the press release as a jumping-off point to have a look into the parole system and some of the statistics currently available. 

How does parole work, and what are prison populations? 

A person on parole, also known as a parolee, is an individual who has been released from a penitentiary before the completion of their sentence on a felony conviction. While on parole, a parolee is not fully discharged from prison and remains under the supervision of the state and a parole agent, where they serve the remainder of their sentence in the community.

“It's important to note what being on parole means, right? It means you went to prison after being convicted of committing a felony, and then you serve part of your sentence and you're released early with the expectation of serving the remainder of your sentence in the community while being law-abiding,” Rapid City Assistant Chief of Police Scott Sitts said at the press conference. “In order to have to commit a violent felony to be sent back, I don't think that's community expectations, to be honest with you.”

 Rapid City Police Department talks about parole arrests
Rapid City Police Department Assistant Chief Scott Sitts talks about parole arrests in the past year during a press conference on Tuesday, April 14. 

In the state, there are eight state correctional facilities, 10 parole offices, seven juvenile field offices and contracts with two local facilities. There were 720 parolees and 12 parole agents in the DOC’s West Region in 2025, according to the South Dakota Department of Corrections 2025 annual report. The region includes the nine western-most counties in the state, along with Jackson and Bennett counties.

According to the report, there is an average daily population of 3,899 adult incarcerated offenders and 165 juvenile offenders, stretching a system built for 2,775 to 135% of its intended capacity. The average daily population of parolees was 3,232 in 2025. 

As of Monday, April 13, there are 115 absconders, according to the Department of Corrections. An absconder is an offender on parole or suspended sentence under the supervision of parole services whose location is unknown.

The average daily cost to house an adult offender in a correctional facility in fiscal year 2025 was $93.26, costing $34,029 annually, while the daily cost of a parolee is $14.55, $5,311 annually.

 Rapid City women's prison
Construction crews work on the Rapid City Correctional Facility, which is scheduled to open this summer. 

The Department of Corrections noted to the Journal that parole is determined in three ways, with each method set by state law and not the DOC:

Violent offenses fall under the “Truth in Sentencing” law passed by the South Dakota Legislature in 2023, with parole based on the sentence by a judge. For instance, if a judge sentences a defendant to 10 years in prison with five years suspended, the five years suspended would be served on parole.
Discretionary parole granted by the state Parole Board for offenders who are not compliant with their parole requirements and appear before the board.
For non-violent offenders, presumptive parole is used, with offenders serving a percentage of their sentence depending on how many times they’ve offended.

According to the South Dakota Department of Corrections website, parole services consist of parole agents, supervisors and support staff, conduct investigations, assist with parole revocation hearings and assist in finding necessary community services.

Parole services also need to conduct a community risk/needs assessment and community risk re-assessment, while considering the offender's criminal history and behavior to predict the success of supervision for each case. Offenders are assigned an assessed risk level while incarcerated and are then reassessed in the community, DOC's website said. Parole agents repeat these assessments at least every three months.

South Dakota administrative rules state that before an inmate can be released on parole or serve a suspended sentence, the individual has to enter a supervision agreement that details general and special limitations that are set forth by the court or parole services. Additional conditions may be imposed by the Parole Board or parole staff based on the parolee's individual needs, the South Dakota DOC's website said.

"If a parolee violates these conditions, they face sanctions determined by a graduated response matrix. Serious violations may lead to a technical parole violation (TPV), prompting a revocation hearing with the Parole Board. If revocation occurs, the parolee will be returned to a state correctional facility," the Department of Corrections said.

Despite this, a parolee is not automatically returned to a state correctional facility for every violation of their supervision conditions. The parole agent follows a graduated response matrix to determine an appropriate response, while considering the nature of the violation and level of supervision.

"This approach aims to balance accountability with the opportunity for rehabilitation," the Department of Corrections said.

The Journal requested local parolee arrest statistics in 2025 from the Rapid City Police Department and Pennington County Sheriff’s Office on Thursday, April 9. The agencies had not provided the stats as of Tuesday afternoon, but a spokesperson for the police department said they are working on gathering them.

Most parolees don't go back to prison with a new felony conviction

The RCPD and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office said in their joint release parolees create “ongoing issues in our communities,” and the agencies are working to engage with South Dakota Department of Corrections officials “to find out why these individuals are allowed to carry out their sentences outside the physical confines of the penitentiary setting.”

Most of the seven parolees highlighted in the April 6 release were out on non-violent convictions and got picked up on a mix of theft, burglary, drug and DUI charges. However, local officials highlighted violent crimes on Tuesday, April 14, such as the case of Joshua Peacock, who was convicted for several kidnapping charges and a burglary charge in 2014. He was released from prison on parole on Feb. 1, 2025, and committed a similar crime no more than 12 hours after being released.

The 2025 report from the Department of Corrections showed there were a total of 124 people who came back to prison for a parole violation with a new conviction. There were 979 that were sent back for parole violations, but no new convictions. Roetzel said she was “surprised” by the statistics.

 Admission types
Page 28 of the FY 2025 South Dakota Department of Corrections Statistical Report shows a breakdown of admissions.  

“Statistics are often hard because they're hard to source, and a lot of it depends on the semantics of words and how things are counted and assessed,” she said. “But over the last year, my approach has been to work really hard and fast to get that conviction so that we have the hope of a subsequent parole violation.”

A spokesperson for Roetzel told the Journal, “We believe we are prosecuting a number of parolees for new crimes, and they are not counting them as violations.”’

Roetzel said part of the local task force will be to get strong, local statistics about what's happening in the community, including tracking parole holds. The task force will begin work on Wednesday, April 15.

 Sitts, Roetzel and Hughes
From left, Rapid City Assistant Police Chief Scott Sitts, Pennington County State's Attorney Lara Roetzel, and Pennington County Sheriff's Office Chief Deputy Mark Hughes, at a press conference at the Public Safety Building in Rapid City on Tuesday, April 14. The agencies announced they’re be forming a parole task force and meeting with the South Dakota Department of Corrections to discuss the issue. 

DOC says it's committed to working with law enforcement

Asked about the police department and sheriff’s office statements regarding parolees, the Department of Corrections told the Journal it is committed to working with local law enforcement to strengthen parole enforcement.

“Initial parole is determined by their sentence and factors in state law. The Department of Corrections is required to release qualifying individuals to parole,” the department said in a statement, noting the agency has worked with local law enforcement to reduce the number of parole absconders from the 400s to around 100. “And Gov. (Larry) Rhoden signed tougher penalties for parolees into law last session that will take effect July 1. We will be listening to local law enforcement on additional ways to deliver results.”

In March, Rhoden signed SB 42, which increases the offense level for parolees who ingest a controlled substance from a Class 6 to a Class 5 felony, increasing the maximum penalty from two years to five years.

 South Dakota State Penitentiary
Cells in the South Dakota State Penitentiary in Sioux Falls. 

South Dakota has high incarceration and recidivism rates

According to the Sentencing Project, South Dakota's imprisonment rate is 370 per 100,000 people, 15th in the country and higher than the national rate of 355 per 100,000. The Sentencing Project is a nonprofit that “advocates for effective and humane responses to crime that minimize imprisonment and criminalization of youth and adults,” according to its website.

South Dakota was one of 14 states where recidivism increased between 2011 and 2021, when the state went from 42% to 50%. The Justice Center estimates that the state's recidivism costs $44 per person — the ninth highest rate in the country, South Dakota Newswatch reported, based on data from the Justice Center.

In September 2025, South Dakota lawmakers approved a 1,500 bed, $650 million prison construction project in Sioux Falls.

South Dakota Searchlight reported the prison will be built in northeast Sioux Falls, near the Sioux Falls Area Humane Society and three miles from the current penitentiary. Site clearing began in December 2025 and is estimated to be completed by 2030.

Additionally, in 2023 the South Dakota Department of Corrections began construction of a $60 million women’s prison in Rapid City. The 20-acre site will be a new medium-security women’s prison and have space for 300 beds to help ease overcrowding at the state’s only women’s prison in Pierre, which as of 2025 is 150% over capacity. The DOC announced in late March Eric Aldridge, who has a 30-year corrections career, will serve as warden of the facility. The women’s prison is predicted to be completed in late spring or early summer of 2026, according to the Department of Corrections.

‘Lack of consequences’: Officials question effectiveness of current system

Both the Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office said seven parole arrests in one day is high, but is not uncommon for both entities to get two or three parolee arrests a day, highlighting weekends where they have made up to nine parolee arrests in the past. 

“In Pennington County, I have a continuing and growing concern about our current parole system and lack of consequences undermining public safety and straining law enforcement resources,” Sheriff Brian Mueller told the Journal. “We see a troubling pattern of individuals being released from prison, with new criminal charges often within a short period of time. We are asking that high-risk offenders who have demonstrated ongoing violence while incarcerated, to serve more, if not the entirety, of their sentence. And when parolees do commit new crimes, they need to be addressed promptly and firmly.”

 Hughes
Pennington County Sheriff's Office Chief Deputy Mark Hughes speaks at a press conference about a local parole task force at the Public Safety Building in Rapid City on Tuesday, April 14.  

Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun also expressed his concerns about parole affecting the community, saying "something clearly needs to change."

"Folks, parole is a problem and it's directly impacting our community," Salamun posted on Facebook. "...A parolee is someone released from prison before finishing their full sentence for felony crimes. They’re supposed to serve the rest of their time in the community under strict supervision by the state and parole agent. But when they reoffend, it raises serious questions about whether the system is working or just creating a dangerous revolving door."

The Pennington County Sheriff’s Office and Rapid City Police Department said they are discussing long-term solutions and problem-solving with the South Dakota Department of Corrections to address violent repeat offenders, “but we need immediate action to improve this safety concern.”

Governor candidates compete on parole reforms

On Tuesday, April 7, South Dakota governor candidate and U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson launched his “Safer South Dakota” initiative, which calls for more offender accountability and consequences for drug dealers, rooted in a lower threshold for the legal definition of a drug dealer. Currently, South Dakota law requires proof of intent to distribute. Johnson said he would advocate for legislation making anyone who possesses more than three grams of fentanyl or methamphetamine be charged with intent to distribute.

“This would make it clear. Listen, if you got a lot of meth and a lot of fentanyl, you are clearly planning to distribute that, we're going to charge you with that crime, we're going to convict you and you're going to prison,” Johnson said.

 Pennington County Jail - wide angle north view
The Pennington County Jail in Rapid City. 

He also advocated for parental notification when minors overdose and real-time tracking for overdose data, and said he’s in favor of parole board oversight on clemency and sentence commutations.

On the same day, through a South Dakota Department of Corrections press release, Gov. Larry Rhoden announced rehabilitation and parole supervision reform plans through the expansion of work with the Council of State Governments Justice Center to improve rehabilitation and parole supervision.

According to a DOC release, the department signed a contract for $160,000 with the Council of State Governments Justice Center to continue providing support to the Governor’s Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force through March 31, 2027. The contract will fund the completion of a comprehensive programming assessment, a re-entry assessment, and the development of final recommendations with the Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force (CRTF), and will help translate those recommendations into action, according to the release.

 Prison
The old warden's house at the south entrance of the South Dakota State Penitentiary in northern Sioux Falls is pictured on Feb. 13, 2025. The prison was originally built before statehood in 1881. 

Rhoden announced the membership of his task force in October 2025. The task force was established through executive order 2025-07, following the passage and signing of Senate Bill 2, which provides for a new prison in Sioux Falls. Both SB 2 and EO 2025-07 were signed at the conclusion of the special session on Sept. 23, 2025.

In his announcement, Johnson said he'd create a task force on the first day in office, and it would be co-chaired by former long-time Pennington County Sheriff Kevin Thom and Minnehaha County State's Attorney Daniel Haggar.

 Recent parolee arrests highlighted by Rapid City Police Department, Pennington County Sheriff's Office
A collection of stories on recent parolee arrests highlighted by Rapid City Police Department, Pennington County Sheriff's Office.

title: Spearfish businessman arrested on felony drug charges in Drug Free Zone
description: South Dakota’s Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North announced the arrest of a Spearfish Businessman for two felony drug charges, both occurring in a Drug Free Zone.
_full_text:
South Dakota’s Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North announced the arrest of a Spearfish Businessman for two felony drug charges, both occurring in a Drug Free Zone.

Christopher Lee Miller, 41, is charged with one felony count of distribution of a Schedule I substance in a Drug Free Zone and one felony count of possession with the intent to distribute in a Drug Free Zone, Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North said in a release. The substance has been identified as THCA + HXY 9-THC + Delta 8-THC.

Miller owns HotHead Vapor in Spearfish.

Spearfish Police received a tip about the suspect, and arrested him on Tuesday, March 31, following an investigation by state and local authorities, the narcotics team said. Recovered in the investigation were approximately 97 THCA + HXY 9-THC + Delta 8-THC live Resin vape pins, Delta 8 infused gummies and more than $1,600 in U.S. currency.

Miller was released on a $5,000 bond and his arraignment is scheduled for April 16, the release said.

The Unified Narcotic Enforcement Team-North consists of the South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation, Spearfish Police Department, Belle Fourche Police Department, Sturgis Police Department and the Meade County Sheriff’s Office.

title: 2 arrested after theft at Target in Rapid City
description: Two men were arrested after police said they stole items from a Rapid City Target.
_full_text:
Two men were arrested after police said they stole items from a Rapid City Target.

A 38-year-old was arrested on suspicion of theft and a parole hold, while a 29-year-old was arrested on suspicion of two counts of theft, possession of a controlled substance and a parole hold.

The 38-year-old was on probation for a 2022 grand theft conviction, where he was sentenced to two years in prison followed by four years of probation. The other man was convicted for a 2023 third-degree burglary charge and was sentenced to five years in prison.

At around 5:30 p.m. on Monday, April 6, Rapid City police responded to a report of a theft that taken place at Target at 1415 Eglin Street. It was reported a man attempted to leave the store without paying for merchandise and eventually left in an SUV.

Police were able to find the SUV and initiate a traffic stop, where they found the 38-year-old as the driver. A search of the vehicle turned up a blue backpack, allegedly owned by the 29-year-old, containing fentanyl.

Police learned the 29-year-old was also stealing items from the store and was last seen walking away from the store. He was later found by law enforcement near the 1300 block of Luna Avenue with several new-in-box items. Police identified him as the suspect responsible for two recent thefts during the course of the day.

The Journal does not name the suspect or suspects in some crime stories because we won't follow the case through the court system.

title: Theft at Rapid City Target leads to K9 pursuit
description: A Rapid City man was arrested after theft at a local Target ended with a K9 pursuit.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was arrested after theft at a local Target ended with a K9 pursuit.

The 34-year-old was arrested on suspicion of theft, third-degree vandalism, obstruction, concealed weapons violation, four warrants and a parole hold.

The man has pending several theft, grand theft and traffic charges in Meade, Lawrence and Butte Counties, each labeled as failure to comply or appear according to the Pennington County Jail records.

The records also show he has several parole violations for second-degree burglary, two forgery counts, two identity theft counts, grand theft, and two counts of unauthorized ingestion of a controlled drug-third offense.

At around 8 p.m. on Monday, April 6, police responded to a report of a man inside Target at 1415 Eglin Street using a knife to open items and conceal them.

Upon arrival, police found the man attempting to leave the store. An officer grabbed the man in an attempt to stop him, however, he pulled away and took off running.

The suspected jumped a fence north of 1510 Eglin Street and ran across Interstate 90. A police K9 unit was used to track him, where he was later found at a nearby construction site.

The Journal does not name the suspect or suspects in some crime stories because we won't follow the case through the court system.

title: Two arrested after traffic stop at MacArthur Street and Haines Avenue in Rapid City
description: Two Rapid City people were arrested Monday, April 6, after a traffic stop led to a drug arrest.
_full_text:
Two Rapid City people were arrested Monday, April 6, after a traffic stop led to a drug arrest.

A 56-year-old was arrested on suspicion of a parole hold, driving under revocation and a windshield violation. A 31-year-old woman was arrested on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance and possession of drug paraphernalia.

The 56-year-old was convicted in December 2024 for possession of a controlled substance and was sentenced to two years suspended that would run concurrently to a previous charge in a 2021 Meade County conviction.

Additionally, the 56-year-old was convicted in Pennington County in 2022 for possession of a controlled substance and was sentenced to four years in prison. This sentence ran concurrent to his Meade County conviction in 2021 for unauthorized ingestions of a controlled substance, where he was sentenced to five years probation.

At around 10:40 p.m. on Monday, April 6, police saw a pickup truck operating with a windshield violation. A traffic stop was initiated at the corner of MacArthur Street and Haines Avenue.

Police determined the 56-year-old, who was driving, had their license revoked and was on parole. The 31-year-old woman was found to be in possession of methamphetamine and paraphernalia.

The Journal does not name the suspect or suspects in some crime stories because we won't follow the case through the court system.

title: Three arrested after alleged drunk driving incident
description: Three people were arrested after a traffic stop turned into several drug arrests on Monday, April 6.
_full_text:
Three people were arrested after a traffic stop turned into several drug arrests on Monday, April 6.

A 34-year-old Pierre woman was arrested on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance, no proof of insurance and failure to yield. A 37-year-old from Rapid City was arrested on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance.

A 28-year-old woman was arrested on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance and a parole hold.

The 28-year-old woman was convicted in 2025 for similar charges and was sentenced to five years in prison, all suspended. Which would run concurrent to a 2023 conviction of another possession of a controlled substance charge where she was given the same sentence.

At around 11:10 p.m. on Monday, April 6, Pennington County deputies saw a car with a windshield violation on 4602 East Highway 44. The car also exited a parking lot and failed to stop when entering the roadway.

A traffic stop was initiated in a parking lot at 3216 E. Highway 44, where a deputy made contact with the driver, the Pierre woman. The deputy noted the strong smell of alcohol coming from the car and asked her to exit the vehicle.

Law enforcement noted several open alcohol containers and drug items in the car. The two passengers were asked to also exit the vehicle. Law enforcement was able to find a baggie that field-tested positive for methamphetamine.

The Journal does not name the suspect or suspects in some crime stories because we won't follow the case through the court system.

title: 4 arrested for alleged burglary in Rapid City
description: Four people, two of whom were on parole at the time, were arrested on Monday, April 6, after being found with a truck filled with stolen items from a home.
_full_text:
Four people, two of whom were on parole at the time, were arrested on Monday, April 6, after being found with a truck filled with stolen items from a home.

A 53-year-old Rapid City man was arrested on suspicion of aiding and abetting, receiving stolen property, third-degree burglary, third-degree vandalism and a parole hold. A 44-year-old Hill City man was arrested on suspicion of aiding and abetting, receiving stolen property, third-degree burglary and a parole hold.

Additionally, a 41-year-old was arrested on suspicion of aiding and abetting, receiving stolen property, third-degree burglary and possession of burglary tools, and a 38-year-old Rapid City woman was arrested on suspicion of receiving stolen property, third-degree burglary and aiding and abetting.

The 53-year-old was previously convicted in Codington County in 2023 for grand theft, sentenced to 10 years in prison with five suspended. Later that year, he violated probation with an unauthorized ingestion of a controlled drug or substance in schedule one or two, however, and was sentenced to a five-year suspended sentence.

The Hill City man had numerous drug convictions between 2021 and 2024, being convicted as a habitual offender with three or more prior felony convictions of possession of a controlled substance in schedules one or two. On Sept. 23, 2025, he was sentenced to a four-year suspended sentence.

At around 4 a.m. on Monday, a deputy was conducting an extra patrol in the 25500 block of Bradsky Road when they saw a suspicious vehicle driving through a nearby field.

The deputy made contact with the driver and determined the car had no business being in the area after contacting the property owner. The four in the vehicle were asked to exit the car.

In the bed of the truck, the deputy noted there were several stolen items from a nearby property. The sheriff's office said it was determined that the four had just burglarized a nearby residence and stolen several items.

The Journal does not name the suspect or suspects in some crime stories because we won't follow the case through the court system.

title: Man sentenced for 2022 double homicide in Rapid City apartment
description: A Rapid City man was sentenced to 15 years in prison for aiding and abetting aggravated assault during a 2022 double murder at an apartment in Rapid City.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was sentenced to 15 years in prison for aiding and abetting aggravated assault during a 2022 double murder at an apartment in Rapid City. 

Chase Quick Bear, 23, appeared for his sentencing hearing on Monday, April 13, before Presiding Circuit Court Judge Heidi Linngren at the Pennington County Courthouse.

 Chase Quick Bear
Chase Quick Bear 

Quick Bear's hearing took place on Monday afternoon, with Assistant Attorney General Kevin Krull providing victim impact letters to the court — he said the victim's family couldn't attend.

Krull said Quick Bear was the first of the eight defendants to speak truthfully to law enforcement and was offered a plea agreement for his testimony against his co-defendants Dillion Wilson, 22, Erin Provancial, 45, and Montell Olivera, 29.

Quick Bear was initially indicted for aiding and abetting first-degree murder and conspiracy to commit first-degree murder. Through a plea agreement with the state, Quick Bear pleaded guilty in April 2023 to aiding and abetting aggravated assault, and in exchange, the state dismissed all other charges. 

In South Dakota, aggravated assault is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. 

Quick Bear's attorney, Matthew Stevens, said it was clear Quick Bear was not "the ringleader" of the incident and was driven by fear. 

"He feared the consequences to himself if he didn't go up in that apartment," Stevens said. 

Stevens asked the court consider a suspended sentence, point out Quick Bear was the first to speak to law enforcement. He also questioned Quick Bear's safety in prison after he testified. 

Before sentencing, Linngren said she considered a suspended sentence; however, due to Quick Bear collecting several other violent charges since his arrest, she said it is "concerning." Linngren highlighted an incident in February at the Pennington County Jail, where he punched a person 22 times and threatened them. 

As Linngren sentenced Quick Bear to 15 years in prison, Quick Bear's mother and loved ones held onto each other as they cried. 

"My son, no!" Quick Bear's mother cried out. "I love you my son." 

Background of the case 

The Surfwood murder trial concluded on Tuesday, Feb. 24, bringing a close to the state's case against three people for an allegedly gang-related shooting of two men in a Rapid City apartment.

Joseph Standing Bear, 29, of Norris, and Petan Milk, 37, of Rapid City, were found dead on Aug. 20, 2022, in an apartment at 100 Surfwood Drive.

The state has painted a picture of a revenge killing in retaliation for the death of 32-year-old Cory Old Horse, who was shot and killed across the street 11 days before the double homicide. Old Horse was a member of Gangster Disciples (GD) and was killed by The Boyz (TBZ), Deputy Pennington County State's Olivia Siglin said. Several witnesses testified the three defendants were part of the Gangster Disciples. Standing Bear and Milk's social media and trial testimony indicate they were involved in TBZ.

However, testimony from one of the lead investigators on the Old Horse case, Jodi Randazzo, confirmed Standing Bear and Milk were never marked as suspects in Old Horse's death.

Rapid City Police Department spokesman Brendyn Medina said on Thursday, Feb. 26, the department deemed Old Horse's as a "justifiable homicide" in the months after Old Horse's death.

Medina said the investigation revealed Old Horse broke into an apartment at Knollwood Townhomes armed with a gun. Old Horse then got into a physical altercation with someone in the apartment, which resulted in the resident shooting Old Horse with his own gun.

Medina confirmed neither Standing Bear nor Milk was the shooter.

The number of people charged with either involvement in the killings or the aftermath has swelled to eight. According to testimony at trial, five of them were in the apartment at the time of the killings, two were in the vehicle and one wasn't present but hid with the murder weapon afterwards. A ninth person was also in the vehicle, but he does not face any charges. The state said he was just in the wrong place at the wrong time.

Three put their fates in the hands of a Pennington County jury in February — Wilson, Provancial and 29-year-old Montell Olivera, all from Rapid City. Olivera was found guilty of two counts of first-degree murder, while Wilson and Provancial were found guilty of two counts of aiding and abetting first-degree murder. All three were convicted of one count of conspiracy to commit first-degree murder.

Two people were minors at the time and face charges in juvenile court. Both testified during the trial. One said she was the first to enter the apartment and left the door unlocked, but she didn't think anyone would get killed. The other teen testified he was with the group the night before the killings and he hid from police with two guns afterwards, but he wasn't present or involved with the shooting itself. He was arrested with the guns. One gun, a .45 caliber 1911, matched bullet casings at the scene, a forensic firearms expert testified.

The three other adults — Louis Perea, 20, of Kyle; Chase Quick Bear, 20, of Rapid City, and Benita Cisneros, 43, of Rapid City — took plea deals and also testified in the trial. They all said they didn't know going to the apartment would end in the deaths. The teen girl, Perea and Quick Baer testified Olivera shot the two men. Cisneros testified she was the driver and did not go into the apartment. Provancial also stayed in the vehicle, per trial testimony.

title: Aldridge named warden of soon-to-open Rapid City women's prison
description: The South Dakota Department of Corrections announced Eric Aldridge will serve as the first warden of the Rapid City Correctional Facility, which is scheduled to open this summer.
_full_text:
The South Dakota Department of Corrections announced Eric Aldridge will serve as the first warden of the Rapid City Correctional Facility, which is scheduled to open this summer.

 Eric Aldridge.jpg

“Warden Aldridge has 30 years of experience in corrections and brings an innovative approach to our system,” Secretary of Corrections Nick Lamb said in a late March release. “We’re fortunate to have someone with his experience join our team.”

Aldridge has served as a rehabilitation counselor, security assessment analyst, and in facility leadership positions, according to the release. He most recently served as the warden of the Fluvanna Correctional Center for Women, a Level III security facility operated by the Virginia Department of Corrections in Troy, Virginia.

 Rapid City women's prison
Construction crews work on the Rapid City Correctional Facility, which is scheduled to open this summer. 

Aldridge holds a B.S. in Criminal Justice from Weber State University and an M.P.A. from Brigham Young University. He will begin his work in South Dakota on April 1, 2026. A photo is attached.

“I’m excited for the opportunity to serve as warden of a new facility and to implement industry best practices,” Aldridge said in the release. “My focus is on positive change and improving the lives of offenders so that they will re-enter society as productive citizens.”

According to the Department of Corrections, the Rapid City Correctional Facility will be a Level III security facility with 300 beds to house medium and minimum custody offenders, along with a Mother-Infant program. 

The facility will utilize a therapeutic community model for substance use disorder treatment with offenders receiving 4-5 hours of therapeutic interventions per day from a comprehensive team including medical, behavioral health, and vocational professionals.

 041526-nws-warden-p2.jpg
Officials break ground on the Rapid City Correctional Facility in October 2023. 

“This will be a first-of-its-kind program in South Dakota, with a structured residential treatment program designed to specialize in the treatment of substance use disorders and the rehabilitation of offenders through peer and staff led, community-based therapy,” Lamb said. “We have not used this model in our other facilities because of a lack of available programming space. This modern facility allows us to do so.”

The facility will also feature a Business–Entrepreneurship program, a partnership between the Department of Corrections (DOC), the state Department of Labor and Regulation (DLR), and Western Dakota Technical College. As part of the Business-Entrepreneurship program, offenders will earn a certificate for the Hospitality Training Program from the Department of Tourism.

Efforts to staff the facility are underway. Click here to find out about job opportunities.

title: The Average Tax Refund for South Dakota Residents Exceeds $3,400
description: As the April tax filing deadline approaches, millions of Americans are preparing their returns and anticipating potential refunds. For many, the federal tax refund represents an important financial boost — one that can help cover expenses, reduce debt, or increase savings.
_full_text:
As the April tax filing deadline approaches, millions of Americans are preparing their returns and anticipating potential refunds. For many, the federal tax refund represents an important financial boost — one that can help cover expenses, reduce debt, or increase savings.

Using the latest data from the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), Upgraded Points, a company that provides advice on credit card rewards programs and other financial products, explores how refund amounts have changed in recent years and how they vary across income levels and geographic areas. The report looks at national trends in average refund size, the share of taxpayers receiving refunds, and detailed breakdowns by income group, state, and county — offering a closer look at where refunds are highest and for whom.

Here are the key takeaways from the analysis:

Most Americans receive refunds: Roughly three-quarters of all tax filers receive refunds, averaging nearly $3,600 annually.
Refunds vary by income: Average refunds increase with household income; however, high-income taxpayers are the least likely to receive refunds.
Geographic variation in refund size: Average refund amounts are highest in parts of the South and Mountain West, especially Florida, Texas, Wyoming, and Nevada.
Paper check vs. direct deposit: About 10% of taxpayers don’t use direct deposit, with paper check use highest in rural areas and in pockets of the northern U.S.

How Much Do Americans Receive in Tax Refunds?

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

Over the past 15 years, the average federal tax refund has generally ranged from $2,800 to $3,000, but the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted that pattern. During the 2020 filing season, the average refund dropped to $2,549, reflecting widespread job losses and reduced earnings early in the crisis.

In the years that followed, refunds rebounded sharply. A combination of rising wages, expanded tax credits, and other stimulus measures pushed the average refund to a record $3,252 by 2022. Yet as pandemic-era tax credits expired, fewer taxpayers received refunds. The proportion of returns resulting in a refund dropped from 77.1% in 2021 to just 62.8% by 2025. However, preliminary data for the 2026 tax season currently shows a higher average refund of $3,571 and a 72.9% refund rate, though early filing trends typically skew these figures upward.

Average Tax Refund by Income Group

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

Tax refunds vary significantly by income level, with higher-income households receiving larger refunds on average, and a smaller share of these households receiving any refund at all. According to the latest IRS data, filers earning less than $50,000 received an average refund of $2,766 after adjusting for inflation, with over 77% of total returns in this group receiving refunds. Refund participation was nearly universal among the lowest-income group, with 99.3% of those eligible choosing to receive a refund rather than applying their overpayment to next year’s taxes.

For middle-income filers earning $50,000 to $100,000, the average refund rose to $3,211, though just 68.3% received one. Refunds increased further to $4,820 for those earning $100,000 to $200,000, but only 55.4% of these returns resulted in a refund.

At incomes above $200,000, the average refund surged to $17,668. Yet just 35% of returns received a refund, and nearly one-third of filers with overpayments opted to apply their credit to the following year’s taxes instead.

States With the Largest Tax Refunds

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

Average federal tax refunds vary widely across states, with a clear concentration of higher refunds in parts of the South and Mountain West. Below are the 5 states with the highest average refunds, based on the latest IRS data:

1. Florida

Florida reported the highest average refund in the country at $4,433 after adjusting for inflation. Out of more than 11.1 million federal tax returns filed, about 7.5 million (67.1%) resulted in a refund.

2. Texas

In Texas, the average refund was $4,344, slightly below the average in Florida. The state had the second-largest overall filing volume, with 13.6 million returns filed and over 9.7 million (71.3%) receiving a refund.

3. Wyoming

Despite its smaller population, Wyoming recorded an average refund of $4,282. Of the 280,750 returns filed, about 190,000 (67.8%) received a refund.

4. Nevada

Nevada had an average refund of $4,193, with 1.08 million refunds issued out of 1.6 million total returns — an overall refund rate of roughly 69.6%.

5. Louisiana

Rounding out the top 5, Louisiana reported an average refund of $4,117, issuing 1.4 million refunds across nearly 2 million returns, reflecting a 73.0% refund rate — the third-highest among states.

Counties With the Largest Tax Refunds

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

At the local level, some of the highest average tax refunds in the country are concentrated in counties with especially high incomes. Each of the 5 counties listed below falls within the top 5% of the U.S. income distribution, and all are known for their concentrations of wealth and high-value property. These counties also tend to have lower overall refund issuance rates, reflecting more complex tax situations and a greater likelihood that filers apply overpayments to future tax years.

1. Teton County, Wyoming

Located in northwestern Wyoming and home to the town of Jackson, Teton County had the highest average refund in the nation at $15,156. Of the 15,210 federal tax returns filed, only about 7,900 (51.9%) resulted in a refund — well below the national average.

2. Pitkin County, Colorado

Home to the resort town of Aspen, Pitkin County recorded an average refund of $8,756. Roughly 5,470 of the county’s 10,520 filers received a refund, for a 52.0% refund rate, similar to Teton County's.

3. Summit County, Utah

Nestled in the Wasatch Range and encompassing Park City, Summit County had an average refund of $8,481. Out of nearly 25,000 returns filed, 13,940 (55.8%) received a refund.

4. Collier County, Florida

Located on Florida’s Gulf Coast and including the city of Naples, Collier County reported an average refund of $7,674. Of the 214,600 tax returns filed, roughly 121,400 (56.6%) resulted in a refund.

5. Shackelford County, Texas

Though much smaller in size, Shackelford County — a rural area north of Abilene — had an average refund of $7,375. Just 940 of its 1,490 returns (63.1%) received a refund, suggesting a smaller but relatively affluent taxpayer base.

Where Americans Still Rely on Paper Checks for Refunds

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

While just over 90% of Americans received their tax refunds via direct deposit in the most recent full year of IRS data, around 10% of taxpayers still opted to receive paper checks. This nationwide figure, however, masks substantial regional variation.

Across most states, direct deposit is the clear norm, with particularly high adoption rates in the South and West. However, in several New England and Midwestern states, the use of traditional paper checks remains comparatively higher. County-level data show even more pronounced disparities, especially in rural areas. In parts of Montana and the Dakotas, for example, fewer than 70% of refunds are issued via direct deposit, suggesting continued reliance on paper checks in remote and low-population areas. These counties also tend to have older average populations, a demographic more likely to use brick-and-mortar banks and less likely to adopt newer technologies.

How Do Americans Prefer To File Their Tax Returns?

Source: Upgraded Points analysis of IRS data

Just as direct deposit has largely replaced paper checks, the way Americans submit their tax returns has undergone a dramatic transformation. In 2009, 66.1% of all tax returns were submitted using e-file. As consumer tax preparation software became more accessible and the IRS expanded its digital infrastructure, that share steadily climbed, surpassing 80% by 2012 and continuing a gradual upward trajectory throughout the decade.

Much like its impact on refund sizes, the COVID-19 pandemic served as a major catalyst for further digital adoption, pushing the share of e-filed returns past the 90% threshold for the first time during the 2020 filing season. Today, electronic filing is nearly universal. For the 2026 filing season, preliminary data indicates that an unprecedented 98.3% of tax returns will be submitted via e-file, aligning with the overwhelming preference for direct deposit to process returns quickly and securely.

Here is a summary of the data for South Dakota:

Average refund amount (overall): $3,458
Average refund amount (<$50k): $2,413
Average refund amount ($50$–$100k): $3,216
Average refund amount ($100k–$200k): $4,690
Average refund amount ($200k+): $20,839
Share of returns with refunds: 67.1%
Share of refunds issued via direct deposit: 90.1%

For more information, a detailed methodology, and complete results, see How Large of a Tax Refund Will You Get? Average Refunds by State and County on Upgraded Points.

title: Eagle Butte man sentenced to federal prison for possessing stolen ammunition
description: An Eagle Butt man faces 14 months in prison for possession of stolen ammunition.
_full_text:
An Eagle Butt man faces 14 months in prison for possession of stolen ammunition.

United States Attorney Ron Parsons announced today that U.S. District Judge Eric C. Schulte Nathanal Benois, 26, on March 30, according to a release from the U.S. Attorney's Office for South Dakota.

According to a release:

Benoist was indicted by a federal grand jury in March 2025. He pleaded guilty on July 28, 2025.

The conviction stems from Benoist breaking into a store in Eagle Butte, South Dakota, in the Cheyenne River Sioux Indian Reservation on Nov. 1, 2024, while the store was closed. Benoist shot out the front window of the store, entered the store through the broken window, and stole ammunition.

This case is part of Project Safe Neighborhoods (PSN), a program bringing together all levels of law enforcement and the communities they serve to reduce violent crime and gun violence, and to make our neighborhoods safer for everyone.

On May 26, 2021, the Department launched a violent crime reduction strategy strengthening PSN based on these core principles: fostering trust and legitimacy in our communities, supporting community-based organizations that help prevent violence from occurring in the first place, setting focused and strategic enforcement priorities, and measuring the results.

This case was investigated by the FBI and the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe Law Enforcement Service. Assistant U.S. Attorney Wayne Venhuizen prosecuted the case.

Benoist was immediately remanded to the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service.

title: Pine Ridge man sentenced to 15 years in prison for killing his nephew
description: U.S. District Judge Camela C. Theeler sentenced a Pine Ridge man convicted of voluntary manslaughter to 15 years in federal prison.
_full_text:
U.S. District Judge Camela C. Theeler sentenced a Pine Ridge man convicted of voluntary manslaughter to 15 years in federal prison.

Theeler sentenced Eric Anduja, 53, on Monday, April 13, according to a release from the U.S. Attorney's Office for South Dakota.

Anduja was charged by a superseding information with voluntary manslaughter, the release said. He pleaded guilty on January 6, 2026.

According to the U.S. Attorney's Office:

In May 2025, Anduja became intoxicated and got into a disagreement with Alban “Boots” Vitales. During the argument, Anduja ultimately stabbed Vitales in the neck with a kitchen knife. Vitales ran from the residence where the argument had taken place, sought help and died on the front door step of a neighbor’s home.

This matter was prosecuted by the U.S. Attorney’s Office because the Major Crimes Act, a federal statute, mandates that certain violent crimes alleged to have occurred in Indian Country be prosecuted in Federal court as opposed to State court.

This case was investigated by the FBI and the Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety Criminal Investigations Division. Assistant U.S. Attorney Paige Petersen prosecuted the case.

Anduja was immediately remanded to the custody of the U.S. Marshals Service.

title: Guatemalan man sentenced in Rapid City for illegal reentry after deportation
description: A Guatemalan man convicted of illegally re-entering the country was sentenced to time served and deported in South Dakota federal court.
_full_text:
A Guatemalan man convicted of illegally re-entering the country was sentenced to time served and deported in South Dakota federal court.

Wilber Zetino-Alvarez, 29, was convicted of illegal reentry after deportation, according to a release for the U.S. Attorney's Office for South Dakota. On March 30 at the federal courthouse in Rapid City, U.S. District Judge Camela C. Theeler sentenced Zetino-Alvarez to time served. He was then immediately deported and has arrived back in Guatemala, the U.S. Attorney's Office said.

Zetino-Alvarez was indicted in November 2025. He pleaded guilty on Dec. 19, 2025.

According to the U.S. Attorney's Office:

On Oct. 21, 2025, Zetino-Alvarez was found in the United States in South Dakota by law enforcement. Law enforcement learned he is an alien unlawfully present in the United States. He had been deported from the United States twice before. Zetino-Alvarez did not receive permission to reenter the country. Reentering the United States after deportation is a federal felony.

This case was investigated by the U.S. Department of Homeland Security Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Assistant U.S. Attorney Benjamin Schroeder prosecuted the case.
