title: Rapid City airman stationed in Montana killed in vehicle crash
description: A Rapid City man serving in the Air Force suffered fatal injuries in a vehicle crash in Montana.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man serving in the Air Force suffered fatal injuries in a vehicle crash in Montana.

Malmstrom Air Force Base in Montana said Airman 1st Class Daris Benally, 24, of Rapid City, died in an off-base crash on Thursday, April 9.

Benally was a missile handler assigned to the 341st Missile Maintenance Squadron at the base.

"There are no words that can fill the void left by this loss," Col. Luke Stover, 341st Maintenance Group commander, said in a release. "Beyond the uniform, Daris was a friend, a teammate, and a loved one. Our support and deepest sympathies go out to all who knew and loved him."

“The loss of Daris Benally weighs heavily on many of us. Moments like this affect everyone differently, and it’s important we take care of ourselves and each other," Col. Daniel Voorhies, 341st Missile Wing commander, said in the release. "If you’re struggling, don’t carry it alone, lean on your teammates and reach out for support.”

The accident is currently under investigation by the Great Falls (Montana) Police Department, the base said.

title: Takeaways from AP and Lee's report on how soybean farmers were impacted by tariffs, Iran war
description: Midwest soybean farmers have faced persistent financial headwinds in recent years, which were compounded by tariffs and the war in the Middle East, reporting from Lee Enterprises and The Associated Press found.
_full_text:
Midwest soybean farmers have faced persistent financial headwinds in recent years, which were compounded by tariffs and the war in the Middle East, reporting from Lee Enterprises and The Associated Press found.

Farmers' costs, such as equipment, have crept up over time while soybean prices have stayed low. Tariffs levied by the Trump administration last year and the monthslong trade war with China only made things worse, soybean producers say. Then the Iran war bottled up shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, restricting global fertilizer supplies and sending fuel and fertilizer prices soaring. A ceasefire deal announced April 7 raised hope that bottlenecks in the strait would abate, but the future of the agreement was uncertain and experts say it will take time for global supply chains to recover.

The AP and Lee Enterprises interviewed experts and soybean farmers across multiple Midwest states.

Here are key takeaways from the report:

Rising costs, low soybean prices have hurt farmers' margins

Soybeans, which are used for livestock feed, food and biofuels, are among the top U.S. agricultural exports. But soybean prices have been persistently low in recent years. The global market has been awash in soybeans, driven in part by Brazil, which surpassed the U.S. as the world’s largest soybean producer years ago.

“If we look at global soybean production over the past several years, it continues to set record, after record, after record,” said Chad Hart, an agricultural economist at Iowa State University. “There’s been just large supplies globally, and that has led to depressed prices.”

Meanwhile, Midwest soybean farmers’ costs have risen. Overall farm production expenses, including seed and pesticide, have increased over time, according to the U.S. Department of Agriculture. Operating costs for soybean production have stayed elevated since 2020 and are projected to increase again in 2026, according to the agency.

The cost of land also is a major issue for farmers, experts say. Midwest crop land values have increased. And most regional farmers rent some of their land, according to Joana Colussi, research assistant professor in the department of agricultural economics at Purdue University.

The U.S.-China trade war in 2025 has lasting impacts

Sweeping tariffs levied by President Donald Trump in April 2025 exacerbated a trade war with China, the top buyer of U.S. soybeans. China responded with retaliatory tariffs and effectively boycotted U.S. soybeans, cutting off a major export market for Midwest farmers and driving the price of soybeans even lower. The U.S. and China eventually reached a deal in late 2025. Beijing committed to buying 12 million metric tons of soybeans by January and at least 25 million metric tons annually for the next three years. China has since met its initial soybean purchase goal and the Trump administration also rolled out a $12 billion temporary aid package in December to boost farmers affected by the trade war.

But the damage is already done, experts and farmers say. While China’s renewed purchases and the federal payments are helping, it’s not enough to recover farmers’ losses. Even after federal assistance, farmers still lost almost $75 per harvested acre of soybeans in the 2025 crop, according to the American Soybean Association. And the trade war further pushed China toward competing soybean exporters, such as Brazil — accelerating a trend of declining U.S. soybean exports to China.

Joseph Glauber, former chief economist at the Department of Agriculture between 2008 and 2014, said global competitors to U.S. soybean farmers gained from the trade war. The U.S. is not as dominant in the global soybean export market as it used to be, Glauber added.

The Iran war further drove up costs for farmers

After the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on Feb. 28, a severe slowdown in shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz sent the price of oil soaring. The shipping disruption also largely stopped the export of nitrogen fertilizers manufactured in the Persian Gulf and limited access to key fertilizer ingredients. The price of urea, the most widely traded nitrogen fertilizer, skyrocketed.

Soybeans don’t require nitrogen fertilizer, but it’s vital for corn and most soybean farmers also grow corn. About half the global supply of urea comes from the Middle East, and Qatar and Saudi Arabia are two of the top sources of U.S. fertilizer imports, according to the American Farm Bureau Federation.

The U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire last week that included reopening the strait of Hormuz, but traffic remained slowed amid disagreements over Israeli attacks in Lebanon, and the price of urea remains elevated.

Many Midwest farmers bought their fertilizer well in advance of the spring planting season. But some farmers who didn’t buy early face elevated prices.

The war also caused gasoline and diesel prices to surge, causing further headaches for farmers. Oil prices dropped following the ceasefire announcement, but the war and the closure of the strait will have lasting impacts on farmers, said Seth Goldstein, a senior equity analyst at Morningstar, an investment research company. Facilities in the Middle East that are critical for exporting chemicals, oil and other commodities were damaged or destroyed during the war and it will take time for supply chains to recover, he said.

title: Authorities suspend full ground and air search for missing Hill City man
description: A week after an 86-year-old Hill City man went missing, the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office announced it is suspending the full ground and air search.
_full_text:
A week after an 86-year-old Hill City man went missing, the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office announced it is suspending the full ground and air search. 

Ronald Deloss Blanchard, 86, was last seen in Hill City at 5 p.m. on Monday, April 6. The Pennington County Sheriff's Office announced the suspension of the search on Monday, April 13. 

Ronald is a white male, 6-foot-1, 200 pounds, the sheriff's office said.

 image001 (2).png
Ronald Deloss Blachard 

Blanchard is very active and is known to frequently walk with a walking stick, the sheriff's office said. His regular walks are typically along Deerfield Road and the Mickelson Trail, but he could be walking anywhere in the area. It is unknown what he might be wearing. There is a concern for Ronald’s health as he suffers from high blood pressure.

Blanchard recently arrived in South Dakota from Arizona on Saturday and typically spends the summer in the Black Hills, the sheriff's office said.

If you have seen this individual or have any information on his whereabouts, please contact Pennington County Sheriff’s Office Investigator Marc Davies at 605-394-6115.

In the April 13 update, the sheriff's office thanked the multiple agencies that have assisted in the extensive search led by Pennington County Search & Rescue:

Hill City Fire
Hill City Ambulance
Custer County Search and Rescue
South Dakota Wildland Fire
Colorado Four Mile District
Mystic Ranger Forest Service
Lawrence County Search and Rescue
Rockerville Volunteer Fire Department
US Forest Service
Pennington County Emergency Management
Civil Air Patrol

title: Time to vote on youth artwork from 150th anniversary contest in Rapid City
description: The submissions are in and it’s time for the public to pick the winning art works submitted by local youth as part of Rapid City’s 150th anniversary youth art contest.
_full_text:
The submissions are in and it’s time for the public to pick the winning art works submitted by local youth as part of Rapid City’s 150th anniversary youth art contest.

The winning artwork pieces in each age division will be included in the 2076 time capsule to be buried in Founders Park in late May.

Approximately 200 entries were submitted by local youth ages 6-14. The theme for the contest is “My Favorite Thing About the Community I Live In’. Finalists have been determined and all entries are on display in the lobby of Rapid City’s City Hall. The public is asked to select one from each of the finalists displayed in the three age categories of 6-8, 9-12 and 13+. The public can visit City Hall and make their selections or make selections on City Facebook posts. Deadline for voting is May 1.

“We had an excellent response to the art contest,” said event organizer Michelle Buchanan. “Especially among the younger grades. The creative talents of the young artists really shined and many captured the theme in their works. We look forward to turning it over to the public for selection of the winners.”

The contest was open to Rapid City public, private and home school students. The City partnered with the Rapid City School District, encouraging students to submit entries to their art teachers. Private and home school students were encouraged to contact the Rapid City Mayor’s Office for contest materials.

 PHOTOS: The colors, art and messages of Art Alley in downtown Rapid City
Photos from Art Alley in downtown Rapid City.

title: Lead community rallies together to host first ever LeadCON
description: LEAD — After a year of planning and a joint dream to bring more people to Lead, the Lead Chamber of Commerce, along with community members and sponsors, hosted their first-ever LeadCON at the Historic Homestake Opera House.
_full_text:
LEAD — After a year of planning and a joint dream to bring more people to Lead, the Lead Chamber of Commerce, along with community members and sponsors, hosted their first-ever LeadCON at the Historic Homestake Opera House. 

Attendees of the event were able to partake in cosplay competitions, Smash Bros and Warhammer 40k tournaments, play DND games and listen to several panels from writers, cosplayers and dungeon masters. 

 DND Dungeon Master panel
Attendees at the LeadCON event got to hear from cosplayers, DND dungeon masters and local writers at discussion panels on Saturday, April 11. 

Board President of Lead Chamber of Commerce Levi Wilson said the idea to put on a convention was "a collaborative grassroots effort," led by what he called the Lead Council of Nerds. As a member of that council, Wilson said they wanted to make an event where they can bring in sci-fi and fantasy fans to Lead.

"This event is running on a lot of charitable donation and goodwill from the people who've stepped up to do this. Everyone is volunteering an extensive amount of their time. The chamber of commerce facilitates a lot of events, but it's running on a lot of donated manpower and womanpower," Wilson said. "It's running on a lot of donated nerd power." 

Executive Director of Lead Chamber of Commerce Jami Grangaard said on Saturday, April 11, they were able to sell 140 presale tickets for the two-day event and were having a steady flow of people buying tickets at the door. She said she was surprised by the turnout for their first ever attempt in hosting this event. 

 Talking to vendors
More than 100 people, dressed in costumes running the gamut from their favorite characters to their own homebrew, gathered at the first LeadCON at the Historic Homestake Opera House on Saturday.  

"I am blown away by the amount of people who bought presale tickets, simply because we live in the Black Hills and it's a 45-minute drive to so many different events and activities, so typically we notice people don't buy advance sale tickets," Grangaard said. "... We're excited to bring people to Lead, to be shopping on our Main Street, visiting this building and just enjoying this beautiful day." 

Between the tournaments and the panels, guests were invited to explore the opera house throughout the day to see the many vendors and artists.  

On Saturday, there was a special event that featured a showing of the 2004 indie hit movie, "Napoleon Dynamite," followed by a Q&A with the film's producer, Sean Covel of Deadwood. 

With several pages of hand-drawn Pokémon to choose from, one particular vendor, Journee Todhunter, was completely booked up on Saturday as she offered to do flash tattoos. Todhunter said she was excited to combine her nerdy side with her art when she was invited to be a part of this event. 

 Homestuck cosplay
More than 100 people attended the first LeadCON in Lead on Saturday, April 11. "This event is running on a lot of charitable donation and goodwill from the people who've stepped up to do this. Everyone is volunteering an extensive amount of their time," organizer Levi Wilson said. "It's running on a lot of donated nerd power."  

"Though I prefer large-scale stuff, I want everyone to have the opportunity to get kind of smaller pieces at events like this," Todhunter said. "But this event I wanted to come to, because I was able to nerd out on all my designs." 

 DND Figures
Several different artists and vendors took up the top floor of the Historic Homestake Opera House for LeadCON, selling DND figures, swords, playing cards and more. 

After years as a dungeon master in his DND games and wanting to change his community, Wilson couldn't help but smile as he wandered the halls at the convention. He said he never imagined he would be able to combine his two passions into one building, but was overjoyed to see so many like-minded individuals. 

With the Black Hills Con overlapping with Black Hills Pride in Rapid City's Memorial Park, Wilson said he was never able to go to a local convention, so LeadCON is his very first. 

Grangaard and Wilson both agreed that they would like to see this event grow and plan on making it an annual event. Grangaard said if they continue to get enough traction, she hopes she can get more businesses from Main Street to be a part of the festivities so they can grow the event from one building to an entire street. 

Wilson said they have a lot of opportunity to grow after their first year and when everyone put their heads together, the options are limitless.

 PHOTOS: Over a hundred people gather at first LeadCON
Over a hundred people gathered for the first ever LeadCon at Historic Homestake Opera House on Saturday, April 11. From panels to videogame competitions, fans got to enjoy the two-day event to the fullest.

title: New signs show the way to Dinosaur Park in Rapid City
description: New signs show the way to Dinosaur Park in Rapid City.
_full_text:
New signs show the way to Dinosaur Park in Rapid City.

Public Works staff installed the signs on Friday, April 10, replacing signs that had been up since 1991.

 Sign
New signage is in place to direct people to Dinosaur Park in Rapid City. 

“You can’t have enough ways to promote Dinosaur Park to both our residents and our visitors,” Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun said in a release. “In recent years, Dinosaur Park went through major renovations and it makes sense that the signage promoting the park gets a makeover as well.”

 Brontosaurus
The sun provides a halo to the brontosaurus at Dinosaur Park on Saturday, April 11, in Rapid City. 

The city noted the new brown-colored signs feature a white outline of a dinosaur symbol with directional arrows and the words "Dinosaur Park." The current green-colored signs feature a solid-white dinosaur.

The new signage directs people to the park that was dedicated in June 1937. According to a placard at the park, Dinosaur Park was commissioned to capitalize on the popularity of Mount Rushmore. The nonprofit Living New Deal organization's history notes, "R. L. Bronson, secretary of the Rapid City Chamber of Commerce, first propositioned the idea of a Dinosaur Park to federal agencies after visiting the Chicago Century of Progress Exposition and viewed a mechanically operated reproduction of a brontosaurus."

 Brontosaurus
The brontosaurus at Dinosaur Park towers over Rapid City on Saturday, April 11. 

Emmet Sullivan was commissioned to construct dinosaur sculptures along the gravel route, now Skyline Drive, to Mount Rushmore, according to the placard.

Sculptor Emmet Sullivan "was given 15 months and $25,000 of WPA funding to complete the project," the placard reads. "He invented his own system to build the dinosaurs where he embedded two-inch black pipe in concrete to anchor the framework and then created a structure for the bodies from rebar, mesh, and more concrete."

 Stegosaurus
Children play on the stegosaurus at Dinosaur Park on Saturday, April 11, in Rapid City. 

It was a Works Progress Administration project during the Great Depression.

"There is a project record with the names of the WPA workers, supporters, Rapid City Chamber of Commerce members, and other City officials sealed in a packet and placed inside the thigh of the Apatosaurus," the placard notes, adding, "Or so the legend goes."

 Sign
A new Dinosaur Park directional sign on West Boulevard in Rapid City. 

Back to the signs: Rapid City history buff and City Public Information Officer Darrell Shoemaker noted in the city's release Traffic Operations Supervisor Greg Buskerud was a seasonal employee in 1991 who helped hand-draw the original dinosaur logo to create a stencil for the signs.

When the City transitioned to digital design in the 2000s, Buskerud converted that drawing into the computer file used for many years, the release noted. "Now, 35 years later, he has designed the new signage, incorporating the updated logo celebrating Rapid City’s 150th Anniversary."

“Over the course of my career in Traffic Operations, I’ve seen traffic sign design evolve into a more safety-focused system," Buskerud said in the release. “Today’s signs are more reflective, more durable, and easier to read at a glance, even in challenging conditions. The update to the signs leading to Dinosaur Park is a great example of blending creative elements with modern safety updates, helping residents and visitors navigate more easily while improving overall safety. It’s rewarding to see how these improvements make a real difference in our community.”

A total of 14 signs will be installed during the next week at various locations on Mount Rushmore Road, West Boulevard, St. Joseph Street, Main Street, Quincy Street and Tower Road.

 Skyline
Looking out from Dinosaur Park. 

 PHOTOS: Dinosaur Park in Rapid City
Dinosaur Park is a Rapid City institution. Opened in 1937, the park features a variety of dinosaur sculptures and a panoramic view of Rapid City and the Black Hills.

title: Updated: One person injured in two-vehicle crash near Founders Park Drive in Rapid City
description: One man received a traffic ticket and was taken to the hospital after two-vehicle crash on Omaha Street at the Founders Park Drive intersection.
_full_text:
One man received a traffic ticket and was taken to the hospital after two-vehicle crash on Omaha Street at the Founders Park Drive intersection.

The crash was spotted by the Journal around 9:20 a.m. on Monday, April 13. A Rapid City Police Department spokesperson said the crash was reported around 8:45 a.m. 

The eastbound vehicle attempted to make a left turn to head north on Founders Park Drive when it struck the westbound vehicle. The driver of the eastbound vehicle received a failure to yield citation. The charge is a Class 2 misdemeanor with a maximum penalty of 30 days in jail and up to a $500 fine.

 IMG_9437.jpeg
Crews responded to a two-vehicle crash on Omaha Street on Monday, April 13.

title: Rhoden announces Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force members
description: In October 2025, Gov. Larry Rhoden announced the membership of the Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force, which will determine the best path forward for expanding rehabilitation services as the state plans for the new prison.
_full_text:
In October 2025, Gov. Larry Rhoden announced the membership of the Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force, which will determine the best path forward for expanding rehabilitation services as the state plans for the new prison.

“Securing a new prison was a major win for our state. Pairing our new facility with strong programming will lead to the best outcomes for inmates and result in safer communities for generations to come. This task force will prioritize reducing recidivism and getting offenders back to work,” Rhoden said in a release.

The task force was established through Executive Order 2025-07. Following the passage and signing of SB 2, which provides for a new prison in Sioux Falls. Both SB 2 and EO 2025-07 were signed at the conclusion of the special session on Sept. 23, 2025.

The Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force will assess the programming needs of the inmate population, understand options for faith-based and Native American-focused programs, study best practices for re-entry models, and make recommendations on how to best use the expanded space of the new facility.

“I am a strong believer in bringing voices from broad perspectives to the table. The task force’s diverse membership will ensure sound programming for our inmate population,” continued Gov. Rhoden.

The members of the Correctional Rehabilitation Task Force are:

Lt. Governor Tony Venhuizen, Chair

Mayor of Sioux Falls Paul TenHaken

Judge Patrick T. Pardy, Third Judicial Circuit

Senate President Pro Tempore Chris Karr, R-District 11

Sen. Tamara Grove, R-District 26

Sen. Joy Hohn, R-District 9

Sen. Paul Miskimins, R-District 20

Sen. Ernie Otten, R-District 6

Sen. Sue Peterson, R-District 13

Sen. Jamie Smith, D-District 15

Rep. John Hughes, R-District 13

Rep. Greg Jamison, R-District 12

Rep. Chris Kassin, R-District 17

Rep. Brian Mulder, R-District 11

Rep. Tim Reisch, R-District 8

Rep. Tesa Schwans, R-District 9

Rep. Eric Emery, D-District 26A

Rep. Kadyn Wittman, D-District 15

Minnehaha County State’s Attorney Danial Haggar

Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller

Southeast Tech President Cory Clasemann

Minnehaha County Jail Warden Mike Mattson

State Court Administrator Greg Sattizahn, Unified Judicial System

Sioux Falls City Council Member Rich Merkouris

Founder of I.Am.Legacy Erik Brings White

CEO of Great Plains Tribal Leader’s Health Board Jerilyn Church

Executive Director of SD Council of Mental Health Centers Terry Dosch

Sioux Falls School Board Member Dawn Marie Johnson

Secretary of Labor and Regulation Marcia Hultman

Department of Corrections Deputy Secretary Brent Fluke

The task force will begin its work later this month.

title: Officials say 79 wildfire was human caused
description: The 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 5,408 acres, an interagency team responding to the fire said in a Monday, April 13, update.
_full_text:
The 79 wildfire near Buffalo Gap affects about 5,408 acres, an interagency team responding to the fire said in a Monday, April 13, update.

That total's slightly down from the estimate of 6,000 issued Sunday morning. Firefighters now have the blaze 60% contained.

The fire's located about 3 miles north of Buffalo Gap, the update said. According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, it is located just east of Highway 79 along East French Creek Road in eastern Custer County.

In its Monday alert, the team said the fire was human-caused. Officials did not immediately respond to a request from the Journal for additional information.

In the update, the team said, "Firefighters have made excellent progress in strengthening and securing the fire perimeter. We expect to see significant gains in containment by end of (Monday)."

The fire was reported around 11:35 a.m. on Saturday, April 11, according to Watch Duty, a nonprofit wildfire tracking site run by active and retired firefighters, dispatchers, and first responders, according to its website.

Gov. Larry Rhoden has declared a state of emergency for portions of southwest South Dakota affected by the 79 wildfire. The emergency declaration allows fire suppression costs to be covered by the state fire suppression fund in accordance with SDCL 41-20A-8, DPS said in a release.

"In the Lame Johnny Creek drainage, there are still Cottonwood trees that are putting up smoke within the interior of the burn site. While this fire consumed primarily grass, trees within multiple drainages are among the only heavy fuels in the 79's seven-mile path," officials said in the Monday update.

title: Meet Flash Wild, South Dakota Wildland Fire's mascot
description: Make room, Smokey The Bear, there's a new mascot in the woods.
_full_text:
Make room, Smokey The Bear, there's a new mascot in the woods. 

South Dakota Wildland Fire unveiled Flash Wild, a firefighting fox, to media on Friday, April 24, with an embargo on the announcement until Sunday, April 26.

 Morris and Flash
Amanda Morris, fire prevention specialist of South Dakota Wildland Fire, first sketched Flash Wild years ago. 

"There's a lot of other mascots within the nation, and so it's exciting that we as a state agency now have our own mascot that we can be proud of," said Amada Morris, fire prevention specialist of South Dakota Wildland Fire.

Flash will be in wildfire prevention messaging and in schools, parades and community events.

He's a creation of Morris, who did a pencil sketch three years ago of a real fox that once roamed the South Dakota Wildand Fire Rapid City campus. She shared it with Public Information Officer Steve Long, who hung it on his bulletin board in his office. Flash is named after a dog Morris had as a child who was very loyal, and Wild is short for wildfire.

"When I became the fire prevention specialist, with the support of management, I started working toward making him a reality. We applied for and received a grant specifically to create Flash Wild. It's been a lot of work, but three years later, here he is," she said.

The grant was roughly $69,000 from the Forest Action Plan with the U.S. Forest Service. Morris said $10,000 will cover his costume, and the rest will go towards swag like banners, frisbees and other items. 

 Flash Wild
Flash Wild, based on a fox that used to roam the grounds of the South Dakota Wildland Fire Rapid City campus, is the agency's new mascot.  

"I plan to have a live celebration when his costume comes, and so I would like to invite Smokey and a couple other mascots for the big reveal of his costume," Morris said.

His physical mascot costume is in line for production. Morris expects he'll be ready within the next year for a meet and greet. At the time of the announcement, Morris said Flash was number 50 on the list for creation with the Mascot Company out of Canada and the United Kingdom. In December, he was number 75. Fun fact: The company also made Nugget, of the Rapid City Rush.

title: Respite center in Rapid City provides place to recover and find hope for the future
description: Michael Milk is 25 years old, and he's been hospitalized a few times for issues related to alcohol.
_full_text:
Michael Milk is 25 years old, and he's been hospitalized a few times for issues related to alcohol.

"It was my pancreas," Milk said. "And the hangovers were the worst, because you can die from a hangover. Heart attacks. Cardiac arrest. It goes on and on. And the last time I was hanging over really bad, I felt like I was gonna die, so that's why I went to the hospital."

 Michael Milk
Michael Milk, 25, sits on his bed at the Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center. The Spider-Man pillow was a gift from his mother.  

After being discharged from Monument Health Rapid City Hospital, Milk continued his recovery at Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center at 4080 Tower Road in Rapid City. The former Big Sky Motel has been transformed into a place of physical healing, but for Milk, it's been more than that.

"Being up here, it's in a sober environment, and they really help you not to think about drinking," Milk said. "They got a lot of great people here, great smiles, great faces. And I met new people, met new friends, got new numbers. You know, everybody's going to treatment, and it's, it's actually a really good thing for a lot of people."

When Milk sat down with the Journal on Thursday, April 2, it was just a few days before he left the state to attend rehab for six months in Oregon. 

"(In the past), I would stay sober for a little bit, but then it was just the temptation to drink. It just wasn't a good thing for me to go home," Milk said. "Most people I know like to drink, and just being around sober people ... It's been a great opportunity just to talk to sober people and get a real insight into being sober."

 Michael Milk
Michael Milk, 25, stayed at the Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center before heading for treatment out of state.  

Milk left for rehab the next Monday. He's one of roughly 135 people for whom Cornerstone has provided respite care since Feb. 4, 2025. At first, Cornerstone provided care at the mission at 30 Main Street with four to six beds. The respite center on Tower Road opened on Nov. 4, 2025, with 20 beds.

First of its kind in South Dakota

The respite center, the first of its kind in the state, is a collaboration between the South Dakota Department of Health, Monument Health, Rapid City Fire Department, and Cornerstone. It houses homeless people who are well enough to leave the hospital, but not well enough to go back on the streets. 

Lysa Allison, executive director of Cornerstone Rescue Mission, said people at the respite center are there to recover from surgery, leg injuries, wounds, or to gain clarity after being discharged for alcohol-related medical issues, like Milk did.

"That's been a real, real nice thing to see," Allison said.

 Coffee and cereal
Coffee and cereal sit in the common room at the Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center.  

The state provided about $1.2 million in seed money for the facility through a grant, Monument Health provides medical care, and the fire department's community health workers provide transportation and other services.

From Feb. 4, 2025, to March 27, 2026, the Cornerstone has provided respite care to 135 people who've stayed a combined 2,017 nights. Allison said that means the center has saved more than 2,000 hospital beds. The average stay at the respite center is 15 days. Mobile medics have transported guests to 476 appointments in that same time frame.

"The experience has been great," said Matthew Hower, operations manager at the respite center. "The overall consensus of the guests that come here is 'we never knew something like this existed.' It breaks the cycle of hospital, street, detox, jail, hospital."

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

Hower said residents have used words like "hope" to describe their experiences.

The center has six staff members, and two people are there 24/7. Visitors can come to the center as long as there's a 24-hour notice and they stay in the common areas.

"The numbers speak for themselves. That's taking a toll off of emergency services, the police department calls for service, but more importantly, it's safer," Hower said. "We could say that we're literally saving lives because they're not on the street. The streets are dangerous."

 Matthew Hower
Matthew Hower, operations manager at the Cornerstone Rescue Mission respite center, splits his time between the respite center and Cornerstone Rescue Mission on Main Street in Rapid City.  

Calls for service to the area 

Although operations at the center have gone well, per Hower, Allison and Milk, the center's opening was hard fought. Over two dozen Rapid Citians filed a lawsuit to block the center, arguing the Rapid City Council did not have the authority to approve a permit for the center and that it would cause them injury. A Pennington County judge ruled in favor of the center in September 2025, and the center celebrated a ribbon-cutting the next month. 

 Calls for service map
The Rapid City Police Department provided calls for service statistics to the Journal for the highlighted area around the Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center.  

"I would say that they should get to know the people first before they judge, because it seems like they were judging before they even gave it a chance," Milk said of the naysayers.

The Journal requested calls for service statistics from the Rapid City Police Department for the area around the respite center to compare calls for service in the timeframe the center has been open to the same time frame a year ago.

 Rolls
Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center provides guests with a warm place to recover and three meals a day. 

From Nov. 4, 2024, to April 4, 2025, there were 161 calls for service in the area. In that same timeframe since the center opened, there were 93 calls for service. On Skyline Drive and Tower Road, the calls for service in that Nov. 4, 2024, to April 4, 2025 timeframe were at 129. That number decreased in both the reactive and proactive categories for a total number of 84 since the center opened.

Since the respite center opened, there have been two calls for service that directly involved its address, police said. One was a welfare check and the other was a disturbance.

 Kitchen
Cornerstone Rescue Mission's respite center includes a kitchen. 

Milk's goal when he gets done with rehab is to help other people get sober or go into the medical field.

"I would like to come back here and work because it's such a great place," he said.

title: Rapid City police investigate after man found dead at outdoor encampment
description: The Rapid City Police Department is investigating after a man was found dead at an improvised outdoor encampment.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department is investigating after a man was found dead at an improvised outdoor encampment. 

At around 7:55 a.m. on Friday, April 24, police responded to the area south of East Philadelphia Street and North Cherry Avenue for a report of an unresponsive man. 

Upon arrival police were directed to nearby drainage ditch and discovered an improvised outdoor encampment. Police found the man and determined he was dead. 

Police noted no preliminary signs of trauma or other foul play on the man's body. An autopsy will be scheduled and the investigation into the man's death remains active and ongoing.

title: 'I stabbed him in his chest': Man pleads guilty to manslaughter for downtown Rapid City stabbing
description: "I stabbed him in his chest," a Rapid City man said at a hearing during which he pleaded guilty to first-degree manslaughter.
_full_text:
"I stabbed him in his chest," a Rapid City man said at a hearing during which he pleaded guilty to first-degree manslaughter.

Diontae Eagleman, 23, appeared for his change of plea hearing before Circuit Court Judge Matthew Brown at the Pennington County Courthouse Friday, April 24. 

Francis Tobacco, 55, died at the hospital after a stabbing that occurred around 7 p.m. in the 400 block of Sixth Street in downtown Rapid City on April 1, 2025, just over a year ago. Eagleman was arrested on suspicion of first-degree murder, according to the Rapid City Police Department.

According to police, the two unhoused men knew each other, and the stabbing was not random in nature.

"This is a tragic incident for our community," RCPD Criminal Investigations Division Commander Capt. Christian Sigel told the Journal in April 2025.

 Downtown photo
The Rapid City Police investigate the scene downtown after a fatal stabbing occurred on Tuesday, April 1, 2025. Diontae Eagleman, 22, was arrested overnight for first-degree murder. He's since pleaded guilty to a manslaughter charge. 

In the wake of the stabbing death, the police department increased its presence downtown, announcing it would assign officers to the downtown area to ensure "a consistent presence" with foot patrols.

Eagleman was initially charged with first-degree murder, which is a Class A felony with a mandatory minimum of life in prison and the possibility of the death penalty. He was scheduled to begin his trial on May 18, however, that was canceled due to the change of plea. 

Per a plea agreement, Eagleman pleaded guilty to first-degree manslaughter, and the state has agreed to request 40 years in prison at sentencing. 

In South Dakota, first-degree manslaughter is a Class C felony with a maximum sentence of life in prison and a $50,000 fine. 

Eagleman's sentencing is set for June 15 at 9 a.m.

title: South Dakota Wildland Fire on how to protect your home from fire
description: In the past decade, more than 35,000 structures were destroyed by wildfires — an average of 3,500 a year, according to the U.S. Forest Service.
_full_text:
In the past decade, more than 35,000 structures were destroyed by wildfires — an average of 3,500 a year, according to the U.S. Forest Service. 

Logan Brown, fuels program manager with South Dakota Wildland Fire, said 90% of homes lost to wildfires are destroyed by embers and provided some tips for how to protect homes.

"These embers are just burning material that are blown in front of the fire, sometimes upwards a couple miles in front of that flame front," Brown said at a media availability in Rapid City on Friday, April 24. "...Most of what we need to do at the home level and the property level is preventing those embers from essentially causing fires on the home and around it."

 Brown
Logan Brown, fuels program manager with South Dakota Wildland Fire, speaks at a media availability about fire safety on Friday, April 24. South Dakota Wildland Fire held the event at its main offices in west Rapid City. 

1. Protect the immediate area around the house

Brown explained the concept of the home ignition zone, which includes the home itself and surrounding 100 feet. The most critical zone to protect is the immediate zone, the home itself and the surrounding 5 feet.

One of the most common issues, Brown said, is pine needles and leaves built up against the house, especially under decks.  

"That's a real big concern because those embers will come up and they'll land on those needles, and all of a sudden, they'll ignite those and then you got needles or leaf litter or grasses that are burning directly against the house," Brown said.

To avoid that, Brown said a screen can be installed along the deck to keep pine needles from gathering. He also recommended surrounding the perimeter of the house with 3 to 5 feet of gravel to keep fire off the house. Mulch, when it dries, will burn.

Using ignition-resistant plants and building materials is also a way to ward off fire.

Brown said to focus on using ignition-resistant plants and spacing them out, "where it's not going to be directly contacting your home, or you have a whole sea of different plants that are going to burn."

Deciduous trees tend to be more fire resistant and when they are dormant, as there is less “fuel” to carry fire through their canopies, according to an article by SDSU Extension Master Gardner Field Specialist Prairey Walkling.

The Western Fire Chiefs Association recommends looking for the following characteristics in fire-resistant plants: supple leaves that are moist or have high water content, watery sap with little odor, plants with an open-growth structure (space between branches), no dead wood, and thick bark that does not peel away from the trunk. Some examples include sedum, stonecrop, creeping phlox, lilac, sumac, vine maple, hackberry, crabapple and western catalpa.

Junipers, despite their popularity, are a no-go.

"They call them the green gasoline, because they do burn even when they're green," Brown said. "And within the juniper plant or bush, there's actually a lot of dead material in there as well, so when the embers get in there, it can ignite very readily, even though it looks green."

 Surrounded house (copy)
Scorched earth, caused by the Coyote Flats wildfire, surrounds a home near Rockerville on Thursday, March 19. 

2. The intermediate zone

The intermediate zone if from 5 feet to 30 feet from the house. It's in this zone that the focus is reducing fire intensity, Brown said. 

"This is where we're looking at your yard," he said.

Keep grass mowed down to 4 to 6 inches within that 30-foot zone around the house, and keep firewood out of the zone.

"Mowing that lawn is very critical," Brown said.

3. The extended zone

The area 30 to 200 feet from the home is the extended zone, and the focus is on fire severity, not necessarily preventing fire completely.

"If that flame fronts coming in, we're just trying to make sure that it reduces its severity so it's not going to burn with intensity where it's going to cause damage to the home," Brown said.

Lower limbs of trees in this zone should be cut to prevent fire from climbing up the trees and into the canopy.

title: Living legacy: Garden club, city raising awareness about Rapid City's ‘hidden gem’ arboretum
description: A peaceful arboretum, tucked between Sioux Park and Mountain View Road, is one of Rapid City’s overlooked pleasures. With new signage to mark the arboretum’s entrance, the Rapid City Garden Club encourages the community to explore this outdoor retreat.
_full_text:
A peaceful arboretum, tucked between Sioux Park and Mountain View Road, is one of Rapid City’s overlooked pleasures. With new signage to mark the arboretum’s entrance, the Rapid City Garden Club encourages the community to explore this outdoor retreat.

On Saturday, April 25, the Rapid City Garden Club and the City of Rapid City are hosting a 9 a.m. ribbon cutting and rededication ceremony in honor of a new sign at Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane. The new sign marks the entrance to this public arboretum, which was established in 1981. It’s located at 1111 Mountain View Road behind Monument Health Behavioral Health Center.

 Arboretum view
The Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane in Rapid City, pictured here on Wednesday, April 22, includes about 100 mature and newly planted evergreen and deciduous trees. Posts seen near the trees have plaques that identify the species of each tree.  

The new sign was installed April 22, in time for Arbor Day on April 24 and the ribbon cutting on April 25. Still to come is a new metal sign in the arboretum that will have a QR code. Visitors can scan the code to learn about the history of the arboretum, said Greta Chapman, a member of the Rapid City Garden Club.

Chapman said the city also plans to install a symbol sign on Mountain View Road. The sign, with a tree and arrow, will point passersby to the arboretum.

 Arboretum new sign
A new sign installed April 22, 2026, marks the entrance to the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane so the arboretum is easier for the public to find and enjoy. 

The arboretum and various education gardens the Rapid City Garden Club maintains are “wonderful resources that are there for everyone,” said club member Rachel Lindvall. “If we can share that with people, it’s good for all of us. It gives people a greater appreciation of outdoors and natural resources.”

A man with a dream

The arboretum is named for Ralph Coe, who envisioned an arboretum for the city in 1961. Coe was a longtime resident of western South Dakota and a retired range conservationist for the Soil Conservation Service. Coe collaborated with city staff to start the arboretum.

 Arboretum stairs
Stone stairs at one end of the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane lead to a bridge that crosses Rapid Creek and connects to Sioux Park near the Master Gardeners' herb garden and a sundial that was a gift from the Rapid City Garden Club. Garden club member Greta Chapman said the stairs seem to be made from the same stone used for the sundial. "It really is a great connector," Chapman said of the stairs and bridge that links the arboretum to Sioux Park. 

In 1981, the Rapid City Garden Club held a dedication ceremony and the first seven trees were planted to start the arboretum, which was initially called Memory Lane. Developing the arboretum has been a joint effort between the Rapid City Garden Club and the City of Rapid City. The arboretum is intended as a place for education, beauty and reflection.

A mix of more than 100 young and established evergreen and deciduous trees grow in the arboretum, accompanied by posts and plaques that identify the type of tree. Each tree is assigned a number that corresponds to a map that lists the GPS location and type of each tree.

 Arboretum Garden Club members and granite wall
From left, Rapid City Garden Club members Greta Chapman, Hannah Hayes and Rachel Lindvall pose with the granite wall that lists names of deceased garden club members. The granite wall is in the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane. It was installed about 1981 when the arboretum was first dedicated. Throughout the arboretum, plaques, benches and the granite wall pay tribute to the memory of loved ones. 

Memory Lane remains an important aspect of the arboretum. Many of the trees, benches, and a granite monument honor loved ones and Rapid City Garden Club’s honorary and deceased members.

The public is encouraged to sponsor trees in memory of loved ones. Go to rapidcitygardenclub.org/arboretum-memory-lane for more information about sponsoring a memorial tree.

Garden club member Hannah Hayes calls the arboretum and education gardens throughout the city a “living collection” and a legacy for locals and visitors.

 Arboretum tree plaque
Plaques can be found near many trees in the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane in Rapid City. Numbers on each plaque refer to a tree map that gives GPS locations for each tree and the type of tree.  

“One of my favorite things is there’s so much to learn (from other club members) every time I go to the education gardens. Being able to reach out to people who’ve gardened in this area for years and years and years and who … want to spread that information is the place to be,” Hayes said. “We’re all passionate about it.”

Even if plant lovers don’t have their own green space at home, the arboretum and education gardens and the Rapid City Garden Club provide opportunities to enjoy the beauty of plants and flowers throughout Rapid City, she said.

 Arboretum Memory Lane sign
A QR code attached to this sign in Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane can be scanned so visitors can learn more about the history of the arboretum. 

This arboretum specifically also has contributed to Rapid City’s recognition as a Tree City USA by the Arbor Day Foundation for 44 consecutive years, according to a release from the Rapid City Garden Club.

Tree City USA recognition reflects the city's dedication to urban forest management and fulfilling the four criteria of the Tree City USA program: maintaining a tree board or department; implementing a tree care ordinance; allocating an annual community forestry budget; and holding an Arbor Day Observance.

 Arboretum connector to Sioux Park
Stairs up a hillside in the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane connect the arboretum to this bridge across Rapid Creek that leads to Sioux Park in Rapid City.

title: Arboretum part of Rapid City Garden Club's gift of beauty to the community
description: Much of the beauty found throughout Rapid City was created or gifted by the Rapid City Garden Club . Founded in 1947, the club has taken on numerous projects to benefit the community.
_full_text:
Much of the beauty found throughout Rapid City was created or gifted by the Rapid City Garden Club. Founded in 1947, the club has taken on numerous projects to benefit the community.

In addition to collaborating with the City of Rapid City on the Ralph Cole Arboretum and Memory Lane, for the club’s 50th anniversary it established and maintains a butterfly garden at Canyon Lake Park. The club marked its 60th anniversary by developing an education garden at Canyon Lake Activity Center, and a bee pollinator garden for its 75th anniversary.

The Noordermeer Gardens Sundial in Sioux Park is a gift from the Rapid City Garden Club. The club has donated money for new doors for the horticulture building at Central States Fairgrounds. The club has given trees, bushes, shrubs, flowers and bulbs to many Rapid City buildings. The club also awards scholarships.

 Arboretum view from top of stairs
A view of the Ralph Coe Arboretum and Memory Lane in Rapid City is seen on April 22, 2026. The arboretum is one of many public gardens the Rapid City Garden Club has helped establish and maintain throughout the community.  

Grant funds from the club provide flowers at elderly housing units, landscaping at area schools, and sprinkler systems at Central States Fairgrounds and Storybook Island. They’ve donated roses for Roosevelt Park, provided park benches throughout the city, and much more.

Rapid City Garden Club has about 100 members and always welcomes more. All ages of gardeners are invited. The multi-generational aspect of the club is something members like Hannah Hayes, Rachel Lindvall and Greta Chapman cherish.

The club is welcome to those who have their own gardens and those who don’t. Public gardens offer club members plenty of opportunities to care for beautiful spaces throughout the city.

“You don’t need any previous gardening experience,” Hayes said, noting the club is open to experienced and novice gardeners and those who want to learn.

Encouraging people of all ages to get involved is what will keep the club as vibrant as the gardens it cares for, Chapman said.

Especially as the club prepares for its 80th anniversary in 2027, “we want to honor our past but we’re seeing a future,” Chapman said. “We’re a multi-generational club.”

Rapid City Garden Club meetings are held on the fourth Thursday of each month at Canyon Lake Activity Center. June, July and August are spent in the club’s gardens, and November and December are dedicated to making and selling wreaths and other décor for the club’s annual fundraiser. Follow Rapid City Garden Club on Facebook or go to rapidcitygardenclub.org for more information.

title: South Dakota state fire meteorologist on wildfire season: 'Average precipitation is not going to cut it'
description: Darren Clabo, the South Dakota state fire meteorologist, is looking forward to May and June.
_full_text:
Darren Clabo, the South Dakota state fire meteorologist, is looking forward to May and June. 

"May and June are the two wettest months of the year. That precipitation that we get in May and June dictates our fire season," Clabo said on Friday, April 24, at a media availability held at the state's wildland fire Rapid City offices.

 Darren Clabo
Darren Clabo, South Dakota state fire meteorologist, speaks about the wildfire season outlook at a media availability in Rapid City on Friday, April 24. 

The Qury fire burned over 9,000 acres near Custer in March, and the area has seen other smaller fires before the start of the height of fire season, including Coyote Flats Fire near Rockerville that burned 385 acres in March. The 79 fire burned over 5,400 acres north of Buffalo Gap before reaching 100% containment on April 14. The Murphy Fire near Hermosa was at 429 acres as of the afternoon of Friday, April 24.

Peak fire season in South Dakota — which Clabo said is the end of June, July and August — hasn't even started. How it'll go depends first on how much moisture the sky will loan to the land and plants this spring, and second on how much moisture the summer heat will take back. 

"The signals that we're seeing, there's no real strong signal to above or below average precipitation, but even average precipitation is not going to cut it, especially considering that we already see those warmer than average temperatures," Clabo said.

If precipitation stays below average over the next couple months, "We're going to have a tough fire season," Clabo said. "And it's not just South Dakota, it's all of the western United States. It's going to be a tough situation if we don't get any springtime moisture."

 Surrounded house (copy)
Scorched earth, caused by the Coyote Flats wildfire, surrounds a home near Rockerville on Thursday, March 19. 

Since the start of the year, the Black Hills and southern South Dakota has received 30-40% of the average precipitation, making for an exceptionally dry year. Clabo said the southwestern quarter of the state has seen the hottest three-month period on record. 

"Our temperatures have been exceeding 10 degrees above average over the course of three months, which is really unprecedented," he said.

Those higher-than-average temperatures are expected to continue into the summer.

The low moisture, high heat, and lack of snow throughout the winter, "really has set the state for burning the fuels, and that's what we've been seeing."

In addition to those factors, the lack of blizzards in recent years has added to fire risk. Clabo explained snow drifts lay down grasses, which made them less susceptible to fire than grasses that are standing straight up.

"And if you go anywhere across South Dakota this year, you see vertically oriented dead grass, and it burns exceptionally well," he said. "This year has been kind of the perfect year in terms of setting the stage for a lot of wildfires, and we've seen a lot of wildfires because of that."

Clabo attributed the dry year to the "natural variability" of the state's climate. Using Rapid City as the example, Clabo said the city gets an average of 17 inches of precipitation each year, which includes rain and snow. Rarely, however, does Rapid City get exactly 17 inches of precipitation. 

"We either don't get rain, or we do get rain," he said. "We're kind of like one thunderstorm away from a desert out here, and so that natural variability comes into play. This year, we just happen to have that variability tilt us towards dry conditions."
