title: Linda Plett
description: September 13, 1957 - April 16, 2026
_full_text:
September 13, 1957 - April 16, 2026

RAPID CITY - Linda Kaye Plett, age 68, of Rapid City, S.D., passed away on April 16th, 2026.

She was born on September 13, 1957, in Sioux Lookout, Ontario, Canada, to Clyde Hurtig and Rose Kmiecik. She grew up in Rapid Valley, S.D., and graduated from Central High School. She went on to have a fulfilling career in IT, including serving the State of South Dakota as a database administrator.

Linda was passionate about arts and crafts and mentored others in organizing craft shows. She was a cheese connoisseur, film buff, and proud grandmother. Later in life, she enjoyed a fulfilling retirement, which included traveling with friends and visiting family throughout the country.

She is survived by her four siblings, Steve Oliver, Michael Oliver, Tina Shafer, and Terry Oliver; her three sons, Jeremy, Denver, and Trevor Plett; and her two grandchildren, Katia and Noah Plett.

She was preceded in death by her parents; her husband, Ivan Plett; and her brother, Dale Hurtig.

A memorial service will be held on May 2nd at 10 a.m. at Osheim & Schmidt Funeral Home located at 2700 Jackson Blvd, Rapid City, S.D.

title: Jerry Walker
description: August 26, 1946 - April 12, 2026
_full_text:
August 26, 1946 - April 12, 2026

Jerry Lee Walker, 79, passed away on April 12, 2026, at his home in Gillette, WY, surrounded by his family, following a lengthy battle with PSP. Jerry was born on August 26, 1946, in Spearfish, SD to Austin and Edith (Fletcher) Walker.

Jerry was raised on the family ranch near Alzada, MT, where he attended country school as a child and graduated from Belle Fourche High School in 1964. After graduation, he served in the U.S. Army in Germany until 1967. Following his time in the service, he moved to Denver, CO, where he married Cheryl Steinbach on April 6, 1968. Once married, they relocated to Anchorage, AK, where Jerry worked on the North Slope. Their first daughter, Tracy, was born there in 1968. After two years in Alaska, they moved back to Belle Fourche, SD, where their second daughter, Tami, was born in 1970.

In 1976, Jerry and Cheryl moved to Newcastle, WY, where they would raise their family in the house that Jerry built with the help of many friends and family. Jerry was hired at Jacobs Ranch Coal Mine, where he worked as a shovel mechanic until his retirement in 2004. In 2013, Jerry and Cheryl moved to Gillette, WY to be closer to family.

Jerry enjoyed fishing, camping, and spending time with family at his cabin in the Black Hills that he built in the early 2000's. He spent his time there riding four-wheelers and catching trout from

the pond he stocked yearly. Jerry loved his bass fishing tournaments and was a member of the Bass Bandits of Rapid City, SD. He loved waterskiing and taught his daughters and friends to ski. One of his favorite things was his annual fishing trip to the Missouri River with his brother. Jerry was a NASCAR fan and spent every Sunday watching the races even until the end.

Jerry loved spending time with his grandkids and going to all their events. His passion for wrestling took him to many of his grandson's tournaments, where he earned the "Greatest Fan" award at WAWA state wrestling. Jerry lit up every time one of his grandkids or great grandkids came to visit him. His love for them was unconditional.

Surviving family members include his loving wife of 58 years, Cheryl; daughters, Tracy (Bill) Olson and Tami (Joe) Baird; grandchildren, Tyler Olson, Tanner (Taylor) Olson, Brooklyn (Nick) Eggert, Trent (Benyapa) Olson, and Branson Cowles; great grandchildren, Kinsey, Tatum, Taegan, Hunter, Evelynne, Macy and Milana; brothers, Marvin (Rose) Walker and Robert (Marina) Walker; numerous nieces and nephews.

Jerry was preceded in death by his parents; his sister, Sheila; and his in-laws, Ruben and Evelyn Steinbach.

A Celebration of Life will be held Saturday, May 2, 2026, in the community room of Mt. Pisgah Cemetery, 804 S. Emerson Ave., Gillette, WY at 2:00 p.m.

In lieu of flowers, the family requests donations to be made in Jerry's name to Camel Kids Wrestling, PO Box 1826, Gillette, WY 82717 or Venmo to @CamelKids-Wrestling.

You may sign his online guestbook at www.kinkadefunerals.com.

Kinkade Funeral Home

1235 Junction Avenue

Sturgis, SD 57785

6053473336

title: Man who broke into Rapid City home, assaulted woman sentenced to prison
description: A Rapid City man who was previously convicted of a 2013 burglary and kidnapping was sentenced to 25 years for similar crimes he committed 14 hours after being let out of parole in 2025.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man who was previously convicted of a 2013 burglary and kidnapping was sentenced to 25 years for similar crimes he committed 14 hours after being let out of parole in 2025.

Joshua Peacock, 40, appeared for his sentencing before Circuit Court Judge Jeffery Connolly at the Pennington County Courthouse on Friday, April 24. 

 Joshua Peacock
Joshua Peacock 

Peacock pleaded guilty to first-degree burglary through a plea agreement with the state. In exchange for his guilty plea, the state dismissed all other charges and agreed to recommend a 25-year sentence.

Peacock was initially charged with first-degree burglary, second-degree kidnapping, aggravated assault and simple assault. Peacock was also labeled as a habitual offender for a 2013 incident with similar charges, meaning information from the case can be used to increase the maximum penalty for the felony charges.

'Innocence lost:' February 2025 incident  

A mother of three fought back tears as she took the stand on Friday, retelling the court the day, Feb. 1, 2025, that changed her life forever. 

"This experience has changed me in ways I'm still figuring out," the woman said. 

She said she was sleeping in her bedroom wh

en Peacock woke her up, and she saw her 3-year-old daughter in his arms. He gave the child back to her after she woke up.

He threatened to kill and rape them both if she screamed and woke up her husband and son, who was sleeping on the couch in the living room. 

Peacock told the woman he had a partner who was holding her husband at gunpoint. She said he demanded her car keys and began asking her to take off her clothes.

 'Lack of accountability': Rapid City area agencies launch parole task force
Local law enforcement is launching a task force and governor candidates are presenting plans. What does the South Dakota Department of Corrections and the numbers say?

The woman screamed and Peacock began punching her, eventually strangulating her by covering her nose and mouth. She said she fought back and eventually broke free, screaming for help.

"Peacock did not just break into a house, he invaded my life, my mind and my sense of safety," she said. "He brought terror into the most sacred space in my life while I held my child in my arms. That is something no mother should ever have to endure."

Her 12-year-old son also testified at court on Friday, recalling the night he was woken up by his mother's screams. He said he immediately ran to the kitchen and grabbed a knife before running to his mother's aid. However, Peacock had already fled the home before he could get there. 

Peacock left a backpack and brass knuckles behind. During the incident, Peacock stole a phone from the home, which was later used by law enforcement to help locate him. 

The boy said he had "lost his innocence" that night, upset with himself that he felt compelled to harm another person to save his mom. He said the incident made him question his faith and himself.

The boy said he forgave Peacock, but there should be no leniency in the sentencing. 

'Live or die:' 2013 kidnapping and burglary

Eleven years and eight months has passed since a mother of two testified in the courtroom against Peacock. She took the stand once more to speak about the November 2013 incident on Friday, in what she hoped to be for the last time.

"This isn't about what happened in the past," the woman said. "It's about preventing future victims."

On Nov. 9, 2013, Peacock awakened his 17-year-old stepdaughter and forced her to go with him to look for methamphetamine. She managed to get away and seek help.

Peacock's second victim was a man who arrived at his Van Buren Street home to find his door kicked in. Peacock, hiding inside, grabbed the man's laptop and fled.

A few doors down on Van Buren Street, Peacock lay in wait for his third victim, a 26-year-old woman and mother of two.

The woman said she was returning home after dropping off her daughter and was putting away her children's toys when she turned to find Peacock coming out of her bedroom. 

Her children were not home at the time of the incident.

Peacock told the mother he had a gun and ordered her to take off some of her clothes and search for pornography videos while he continuously threatened her life. She said he eventually forced her to drive him around Rapid City. 

"I didn't know if I would live or die," the woman said. 

Recognizing signs of drug use in Peacock, the woman took control and convinced him she could get money at a Walmart where she worked.

After parking, she took the keys, left Peacock in the car and, resisting the urge to run, walked toward the store. She could hear Peacock talking, pretending to tell her mother he would kill the woman if she didn't return with the cash. Her mother was not in the car or on the phone with him. 

Once inside, the woman was shaking so hard that all she could say was "Hide me," when she found an employee she knew.

Managers called 911. By the time police arrived, Peacock had fled.

The woman testified during Peacock's sentencing in 2014 that there was a kitchen knife in the backseat when she got back to the car in the Walmart parking lot. 

He was sentenced in August 2014 to 17 years for the attempted kidnapping of the 17-year-old and concurrent sentences of 12 years for second-degree kidnapping for actions with the woman he held captive and 10 years for the burglary charge.

The three sentences were set to put Peacock behind bars until December 2030. Peacock was released on parole in 2023 and was later put back in prison for parole violation. He was released again on parole on Feb. 1, 2025 and 14 hours later broke into another family's home.

Sentencing

In the middle of the courtroom on Friday, State's Attorney Lara Roetzel held out a size 2T nightgown with pink and blue flowers — the exact same nightgown the 3-year-old girl was wearing when Peacock took her from her room and was "weaponized against her own mother."

Roetzel said Peacock not only broke into the home, but surveyed the area, keeping track of everyone in the house before he acted. 

Roetzel criticized how Peacock has spent the majority of his life behind bars, highlighting a prior drug conviction and the 2013 burglary and kidnapping incident. Roetzel said he was released on parole twice for the 2013 conviction, the first time was in 2023 and the second in 2025, where he repeated the same crime. 

"There's something very wrong with a man so driven by perversion that he cannot wait even one day to commit the same crime," Roetzel said. 

Roetzel requested the court consider a 25-year sentence and give "the community what it deserves to be protected from him (Peacock)."

Peacock's attorney Paul Pietz said Peacock has a history of addiction and mental health issues rooted from PTSD. Pietz expressed concern about what treatments will be available for Peacock if the court followed the 25-year request. He would be 70 years old by the time the sentence ended.

Pietz highlighted how the rate of recidivism significantly drops at the age of 50 and requested the court consider a suspended sentence so that Peacock has the opportunity to be guided through parole. 

 Man who broke into two Rapid City homes, held a woman captive in 2013 does it again
After being released on parole for the 2013 kidnapping and burglary charges, Joshua Peacock was arrested again for similar crimes. This time, he took a child from its mother. 

Peacock apologized to each of the victims who testified and their families. He told the 12-year-old boy that he shouldn't feel bad and that he "did the right thing by protecting your mom."

"I hope today you will get some closure," Peacock said before asking for forgiveness.

Before imposing a sentence, Connolly noted the severity of the offense and Peacock's criminal history, emphasizing the need to protect the public, before following the state's request.

Before Peacock was taken out of the courtroom, the victims and their family asked the court if they could pray with Peacock. Together with their heads lowered, they all joined in prayer.

Peacock will not be eligible for parole due to the South Dakota's truth in sentencing law passed by the South Dakota Legislature in 2023. First-degree burglary is one of the crimes with no parole eligibility under the law.

title: Authorities arrest man wanted for Pennington County armed robbery near Piedmont
description: Authorities have arrested a parole absconder wanted in connection to an armed robbery.
_full_text:
Authorities have arrested a parole absconder wanted in connection to an armed robbery.

The Meade County Sheriff's Office said law enforcement arrested Deja Guthmiller, 22, of Rapid City, on Friday, April 24. He'd been wanted for burglary and armed robbery in Pennington County and was a wanted parole violator, the office said.

“This was a dangerous and rapidly evolving situation involving an individual with a demonstrated history of violence," Meade County Sheriff Pat West said in a release from the office. "The coordinated response by our partner agencies ensured that this threat was addressed quickly and safely, minimizing risk to our community. We are fortunate to have strong working relationships across jurisdictions that allow us to respond effectively in situations like this.”

Authorities had searched for Guthmiller in the area of Meadow Retreat Road and Erickson Ranch Road in rural Meade County east of Piedmont and Summerset on Thursday night, April 23.

Early on Friday, the Meade County Sheriff’s Office said it received information indicating Guthmiller may have returned to a family residence located in the 5500 block of Meadow Retreat Road in Piedmont.

 042526-nws-search-p1.jpg
The Meade County Sheriff's Office said law enforcement arrested Deja Guthmiller, 22, of Rapid City, on Friday, April 24, in the Piedmont area. 

Law enforcement was able to confirm Guthmiller’s presence inside the residence. Based on this information, deputies obtained a search warrant, and South Dakota Parole Services issued an absconder warrant for his arrest. Due to credible threats previously made by Guthmiller, combined with a recent history of violence, he was considered a significant risk to public safety, the sheriff's office said.

The sheriff's office requested the assistance of the Special Response Team, comprised of members from the Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office.

Through coordinated efforts among multiple law enforcement agencies, Guthmiller was safely taken into custody without further incident, the Meade County Sheriff's Office said.

 guthmiller.jpg

“While this incident required significant resources, other agencies stepped in to assist with unrelated calls for service throughout Meade County," West said in the release. "This level of cooperation is critical in rural areas and reflects a shared commitment to public safety across western South Dakota.”

Authorities took Guthmiller to the Pennington County Jail, the Meade County Sheriff's Office said. He was not yet listed on the jail roster as of around 4:15 p.m. on Friday.

According to South Dakota court records, he's charged with first-degree robbery in connection to an April 5 incident and with aggravated criminal entry of a motor vehicle, intentional damage to property and second-degree petty theft in connection to his alleged actions on April 23.

South Dakota Department of Corrections records show Guthmiller was out on parole after being sentenced for discharging a firearm at an occupied structure in June 2023 in Pennington County and for intentional damage to property (more than $2,500 in damage, less than $5,000) in August 2023 in Lawrence County.

His parole period had been set to end in August 2028.

Agencies involved in the effort that led to the arrest include the South Dakota Highway Patrol, Rapid City Police Department, Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, Sturgis Police Department, South Dakota Game, Fish and Parks, South Dakota Parole Services, United States Marshals Service, Meade County 911 Communications and Pennington County 911 Communications.

title: After ballot delay, Pennington County absentee voting begins April 23
description: The ballots will be ready.
_full_text:
The ballots will be ready.

The Pennington County Auditor’s Office said absentee voting will begin at 7 a.m. on Thursday, April 23. Early voting had been delayed from the legal start date, April 17, because of a delay in printing ballots caused by the March 31 candidate filing deadline.

Following the start date, voters may cast absentee ballots at the Auditor’s Office located at 900 Concourse Drive, from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. Monday-Friday.

title: Officials break ground Rapid Valley Elementary expansion
description: The children chanted, "Dig! Dig! Dig!" as dust flew in air outside Rapid Valley Elementary School on Monday, April 20.
_full_text:
The children chanted, "Dig! Dig! Dig!" as dust flew in air outside Rapid Valley Elementary School on Monday, April 20.

Officials, students and teachers celebrated the groundbreaking and marked the start of work on the renovation and expansion of Rapid Valley Elementary. The building, constructed in 1985, is 46,000 square feet with a maximum capacity of 524 students, according to the district's master plan. The addition will add 25,000 square feet, according to a presentation by Chamberlain Architects at the Feb. 17 Rapid City Area Schools Board meeting. 

 Children watch
Rapid Valley Elementary School students sat and watched the groundbreaking. 

The board on April 7 approved a roughly $20.48 million contract with Scull Construction for the addition and renovations in the existing building. The renovations include the addition of air conditioning, relocation of the entrance and security improvements, reconfiguration of the playgrounds, new classrooms, a new gym, a new office area and other improvements, according to Chamberlain Architects and an RCAS announcement. The project is expected to take 18 months, according to the district. 

Rapid Valley Elementary Principal Rhoda Bryan said students and families will need to have patience and grace as construction is underway. "You might be in the middle of your math lesson and they may tear down the tree outside your window," she said to the students gathered at the groundbreaking.

 RCAS superintendent talks about Rapid Valley expansion
Rapid City Area Schools Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson talks about the Rapid Valley Elementary expansion at the groundbreaking on Monday, April 20. 

"Today represents more than just the start of construction. It reflects our shared commitment to growth, opportunity and the future of our students and community. This project is a testament to what can be accomplished when a community comes together to support education," said Jade Clement, RCAS construction and planning manager.

RCAS Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson said the project strengthens the future for students and the community and creates a modern and inspiring space. 

"Schools are the heart of a community. They are places where children discover who they are, where educators dedicate themselves to shaping lives, and where families place their trust and hope for their children's futures. This project represents our commitment to honor that trust," Thompson said.

 RCAS superintendent
RCAS Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson said to the students gathered at the groundbreaking. "This expansion is a commitment to our students, to provide you with a safe, modern and inspiring space where you can learn, grow and dream without limits." 

Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun also attended. He urged the students sitting on the ground to thank their teachers and support staff. They replied in unison, "Thank you!"

"We love our kids. We believe in our future. And what I love about projects like this is it proves it. It proves that we are investing in the next generation," Salamun said. "It's also about you, teachers. I want to thank you specifically, again, because this is about you having a great place to work, a great environment in which to help kids learn and grow and be who they're made to be, and that's what you do each and every day."

Rapid Valley fifth-graders Molly Ude, Marchesa Garneaux and Noah Sahli spoke with media after the groundbreaking.

 Students
Rapid Valley fifth-graders Molly Ude, Marchesa Garneaux and Noah Sahli pose for a photo by a piece of construction equipment at the groundbreaking. Molly said, "They're starting groundbreaking because they're starting to build the whole new school that's going to be bigger, more spacious for more kids to come and learn." Marchesa said, "Today is a celebration for Rapid Valley because this school has been here for a while." Noah said, "What I'm excited about for the Rapid Valley expansion is for the air conditioning because all the new kids that are coming in here, the kindergartners and any other new kids that are coming here, I'm really excited for them to experience air conditioning. What I'm really nervous about is for the construction workers to get severely hurt."  

Molly said, "They're starting groundbreaking because they're starting to build the whole new school that's going to be bigger, more spacious for more kids to come and learn."
Marchesa said, "Today is a celebration for Rapid Valley because this school has been here for a while."
Noah said, "What I'm excited about for the Rapid Valley expansion is for the air conditioning because all the new kids that are coming in here, the kindergartners and any other new kids that are coming here, I'm really excited for them to experience air conditioning. What I'm really nervous about is for the construction workers to get severely hurt."

The groundbreaking took place a couple weeks after the school board voted unanimously to place a $107 million bond referendum on the Nov. 3 ballot for school facilities. 

 Photos from the Rapid Valley Elementary expansion groundbreaking
Officials, students and teachers celebrated the Rapid Valley Elementary expansion and renovation groundbreaking on Monday, April 20. The addition will add 25,000 square feet and add classrooms and other facilities. The building, constructed in 1985, is 46,000 square-foot with a maximum capacity of 524 students, according to the district's master plan.

title: Murphy wildfire grew slightly, crews continue effort
description: The Murphy wildfire near Hermosa grew slightly, up to 429 acres as of Friday morning, April 24.
_full_text:
The Murphy wildfire near Hermosa grew slightly, up to 429 acres as of Friday morning, April 24.

That's according to WildCAD tracking from South Dakota fire agencies. The first is around 10% contained, according to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety. The fire is 3 miles northwest of Hermosa.

The Murphy wildfire began around midnight on Thursday, April 23.

In a Thursday evening update, DPS said, "Some firefighters will work overnight to mop-up and patrol. Others will return in the a.m."

Snow moving into the area undoubtedly helped. Precipitation continues more is possible this afternoon and this evening, according to the National Weather Service's Rapid City office.

title: Update: Local law enforcement searches for man wanted for robbery in Rapid City, Box Elder
description: The Meade County Sheriff's Office said law enforcement is looking for a wanted parolee suspected in a burglary and armed robbery.
_full_text:
The Meade County Sheriff's Office said law enforcement is looking for a wanted parolee suspected in a burglary and armed robbery. 

The office said in a 7:30 p.m. alert on Thursday, April 24, there was a large law enforcement presence in the area of Meadow Retreat Road/Erickson Ranch Road. A 9:30 p.m. update said the search has been extended.

Deja Guthmiller, 22, of Rapid City, is suspected in a burglary and robbery in the Rapid City and Box Elder area, the Meade County Sheriff's Office said.

Guthmiller is considered armed and dangerous. "We are asking residents in the area of Erickson Ranch Road and Meadow Retreat to maintain vigilance and lock your homes and outbuildings," the sheriff's office said.

In the update, the sheriff's office said additional resources have moved into the area, including surrounding neighborhoods. "We continue to look for Guthmiller."

The South Dakota Highway Patrol is searching the area with drones. Agencies involved include the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, Rapid City Police Department, SD Highway Patrol, US Marshals, and SD Parole Services.

"We are encouraging everyone to call 911 if they see anything suspicious," the Meade County Sheriff's Office said.

title: They returned to the scene of the crime: 2 arrested for Rapid City burglaries
description: Two suspects face charges after they returned to take photos at their crime scene, according to the Rapid City Police Department.
_full_text:
Two suspects face charges after they returned to take photos at their crime scene, according to the Rapid City Police Department.

Icamna Blue Legs, 22, of Rapid City, and Payten Lakota, 18, of Rapid City, face charges after a pair of incidents, the department said.

Police said around 12:25 a.m. on Thursday, April 23, an officer on patrol noted an audible alarm coming from 49 E. Omaha St. The location is a Big D gas station.

As the officer pulled into the parking lot, he observed that someone had smashed one of the windows at the business. Police contacted store management, who arrived in order to view video footage from the location.

A review of footage showed two men approaching the outside of the business, police said. One then allegedly used an object to break the window before both men ran away from the scene.

Later, around 1:15 a.m., police were dispatched to 1507 E. Saint Patrick St. for a report of a burglary, the department said. That location is another Big D gas station. Witnesses reported seeing two male subjects fleeing the location, police said.

Police arrived and observed a broken window at the business. Video from the business showed two men breaking the front window and entering the store.

They allegedly grabbed a number of items before fleeing the store to the south.

While on scene, officers saw two people approach the store. One of them began taking photos of the damage.

Police made contact with them and identified them as Blue Legs and Lakota. Officers determined they were the men seen in the video from both gas stations.

Officers arrested Blue Legs on suspicion of third-degree burglary and felony vandalism and Lakota on suspicion of third-degree burglary, minor in possession of alcohol, and underage consumption of alcoholic beverages.

Police said officers took both suspects to the Pennington County Jail. Jail records didn't list either as of Thursday afternoon. 

 Crime and courts coverage from the Rapid City Journal
A look at the latest crime and courts coverage by the Rapid City Journal staff.

title: Rapid City man arrested after rollover crash
description: A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes.
_full_text:
A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes.

Robin Masters, 67, was arrested on suspicion of reckless driving.

At around 1:05 p.m. on Wednesday, March 25, police responded to the 2400 block of Canyon Lake Drive for a report of a crash involving several vehicles.

 032826-nws-masters-p1.jpg
A Rapid City man was arrested Wednesday, March 25, after his alleged speeding through a neighborhood resulted in two rollover crashes. 

After speaking with witnesses at the scene, police determined a Fiat 500, driven by Masters, was driving eastbound on Canyon Lake Drive at a high rate of speed. Masters entered the lane of oncoming traffic, then swerved back into the eastbound lane, striking a motorcycle and causing a Buick passenger vehicle to swerve.

The Fiat and the Buick both left the roadway and rolled. The driver of the Buick was transported to the hospital for minor injuries. The motorcyclist was uninjured.

As of Thursday, March 26, Masters was not found on the Pennington County Jail roster. He appeared in court on Thursday, March 26, and is set to appear for a dispositional conference on May 4. Prosecutors and defense discuss a potential resolution of a case, sometimes through a plea deal, at such conferences.

Court records show Masters was charged in November 2025 with failure to stop and provide information after a property damage accident, reckless driving and following too closely.

Missing Pine Ridge man found dead

Nearly a month after last being seen, the Oglala Sioux Tribe Department of Public Safety said it found a missing Pine Ridge man dead.

Dale Glenn Warrior Sr., 64, was last seen on Feb. 27 driving a silver 2006 Pontiac Grand Prix GT2 in the Wounded Knee area. The department said on Thursday, March 26, that Warrior and his vehicle had been found.

Warrior's family has been notified.

"We would like to express our gratitude to the community for your assistance and support throughout this search," the department said on Facebook. "...We extend our sincerest condolences to his family and loved ones during this difficult time."

The department said the case remains under investigation and investigators are currently waiting on autopsy reports to determine an official cause of death.

The department didn't say when or where Warrior was found. Attempts to reach the department for additional information weren't immediately successful on Thursday.

10-year-old dies from injuries suffered in accidental shooting

Law enforcement reported a 10-year-old died on Sunday, March 22, due to an accidental shooting.

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said deputies responded to an area near Owanka on Sunday for a report of a juvenile who had been shot.

During the initial investigation, it was determined that the 10-year-old juvenile had sustained a gunshot wound during what is believed to be "an accidental family-related incident."

Despite response efforts, the juvenile died from injuries suffered in the shooting, the sheriff's office said.

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said it is actively investigating the fatal shooting.

Man arrested for starting fire near Roosevelt Park

A Rapid City man was arrested on Wednesday, March 25, after he allegedly started a fire under the East Omaha Bridge near Roosevelt Park.

Tony Hessler, 38, was placed under arrest on suspicion of city park fire violation and a bond revocation.

At around 12:20 p.m. on Wednesday, police were contacted by a city park ranger about a man who had started a fire on a pile of leaves under the East Omaha Bridge near Roosevelt Park. Police contacted Hessler shortly after.

The park ranger ensured the fire was extinguished.

Hessler is being held at the Pennington County Jail with no bond set.

Sheriff's office investigates shooting in Rapid Valley

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said it is investigating a shooting in Rapid Valley that left one man injured.

Deputies were called to a residence on Teewinot Drive at around 11:56 p.m. on Thursday, March 26, on a report of a disturbance, the sheriff's office said in a Friday, March 27, release.

Deputies made contact with two men and a woman in the residence. One of the men suffered from a gunshot wound that is not considered life-threatening, the sheriff's office said. Medics took the man to the hospital for treatment and he remains there in stable condition. The other two individuals at the residence exhibited injuries consistent with a physical altercation.

An investigation into the incident continues as the sheriff's office works with the Rapid City Police Department. All individuals believed to be involved in this incident have been identified and accounted for, the sheriff's office said. Investigators have determined that the parties involved are known to one another.

The sheriff's office did not release the names of those involved or who fired the weapon. The Journal has reached out for additional information.

title: Pennington County to receive $615,000 from opioid settlement funds, plus more on opioid efforts
description: Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller updated the Board of Commissioners on a $615,000 grant the state's Opioid Advisory Committee awarded the county. The committee is part of the South Dakota Department of Social Services.
_full_text:
Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller updated the Board of Commissioners on a $615,000 grant the state's Opioid Advisory Committee awarded the county. The committee is part of the South Dakota Department of Social Services.

At the Tuesday, April 21, commission meeting, Mueller said the $615,000 will soon go into the county's restricted opioid budget in increments of around $205,000.

The board didn't take action at the meeting, as the update was a presentation only.

 Sheriff Brian Mueller
Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller speaks to the Board of Commissioners on opioid settlement funding coming to the county on Tuesday, April 21. 

"I sit on a local opioid group that's run through the combination of city, county and several other partners evaluating our response to substance abuse strategies in our community and as part of that process, we went through a process to apply for state opioid funding. The state department of social services is sitting on several million dollars' worth of opioid funding," Mueller said to the commission. "We're trying to get that released into our communities to have the biggest impact."

The local opioid committee is made up of Rapid City Council members Stephen Tamang, Callie Meyer and Lance Lehmann, Mueller, Rapid City Police Chief Don Hedrick, Rapid City Fire Chief Jason Culberson, Chief of Staff of the City of Rapid City Leah Braun, Pennington County Health and Human Services Director Barry Tice and Pennington County Board of Commissioners Chair Ron Weifenbach.

Per previous reporting by the Journal, Tamang, Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun and Mueller sent a letter on Sept. 11, 2025, to South Dakota Attorney General Marty Jackley requesting $25 million from the state's opioid funds.

The South Dakota Department of Social Services opioid settlement fund dashboard shows the state has received nearly $33 million since disbursement. Of that, $9.6 million has gone to local governments. Rapid City and Pennington County have received a combined $1.5 million and reported about $300,000 in spending. The majority of the funds, 70%, are under the state’s purview. 

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office oversees the Care Campus, the county facility that provides addiction treatment services and safe beds at 321 Kansas City St. The facility is accredited by the South Dakota Division of Behavioral Health through the Department of Social Services.

"Mayor (Jason Salamun) and I have been very actively involved behind the scenes to try to work to get block grant funding for the Rapid City area and in the Sioux Falls area that could improve services, create better coordination with the services that we do have and also cut some of the taxpayer funding that goes into these types of services," Mueller said.

Mueller told the Journal specifics on how the grant funding will be spent are still up for discussion, but he is hoping to enhance services at the Care Campus as well as Rapid City. 

 Care Campus
The Care Campus is Pennington County's alcohol and drug treatment facility, located on 321 Kansas City St. 

Mueller said he would like to have government agencies in correspondence to more efficiently tackle the issue of addiction treatment. 

"It will improve public safety and that's my main goal. It will make our community safer, and it will reduce recidivism and it will give people who live here a higher quality of life," Mueller said.

 Opioid funding
The Pennington County Board of Commissioners were presented a letter by Mueller about $615,000 coming from the state's Opioid Advisory Committee on April 21. 

He's hopeful further funding from the state will trickle down to both Rapid City and Sioux Falls.

Local Opioid Committee

The local opioid committee is working to distribute both state and municipal opioid settlement funds to organizations in Rapid City working to expand community-based support programs, address the needs of criminal-justice involved individuals, and strengthen workforce development for addiction professionals. It's fielding requests from community organizations working in drug treatment, prevention or research. 

On April 20, the Rapid City Council approved the authorization for the mayor on behalf of the city to join an opioid settlement lawsuit with a total settlement of $97.62 million. 

The lawsuit contains six defendants consisting of associated pharmacies, JM Smith Corporation, Louisiana Wholesale Drug Company, Morris and Dixon Company, North Carolina Mutual Wholesale and more.

Assistant City Attorney Justin Williams told the Journal he couldn't estimate how much the city could get, but said it might be a small amount.

Tamang, who sits as chair of the committee, clarified to the Journal that this settlement funding would not come from the state.

"There's a whole bunch of smaller settlement suits that are going on. This isn't the first one. The city has joined many of them," Tamang said.

Rapid City Finance Director Danie Ainslie said the city currently has $682,266.25 in settlement funds. With recent distributions of funds going to Narcan ($53,460), Infinity Vial Sampler ($84,236), Care Campus ($250,000), Fire and EMS ($150,000), Addiction Recovery Center of the Black Hills ($50,000), and to Monument Health Foundation ($50,000).

title: Updated: Murphy wildfire near Hermosa now 10% contained
description: The Murphy wildfire near Hermosa is 10% contained, according to an afternoon update from the South Dakota Department of Public Safety .
_full_text:
The Murphy wildfire near Hermosa is 10% contained, according to an afternoon update from the South Dakota Department of Public Safety.

The Murphy wildfire began around midnight on Thursday, April 23. Watch Duty reports the fire affects 423 acres.

 042426-nws-fire-p1.jpg
There is a wildfire burning over 420 acres northwest of Hermosa. The Murphy wildfire started around midnight on Thursday, April 23. 

The fire is 3 miles northwest of Hermosa.

"Windy conditions continue as crews work to strengthen the fire line around the fire perimeter," DPS said in the afternoon update.

"Interagency resources continue initial attack on the Murphy Fire. ... Dozer line surrounds the perimeter but with gusty winds there is no containment," the department said earlier in the day.

Pennington County issued evacuation orders overnight and lifted them on Thursday morning.

 042426-nws-fire-p2.jpg
According to the South Dakota Department of Public Safety, the Murphy wildfire began around midnight on Thursday, April 23. Watch Duty reports the fire has affected 420 acres. 

Fire fuels burning include grass and timber, DPS said.

Strong winds are expected to continue Thursday, with gusts up to 40 mph, according to the National Weather Service's Rapid City office. There's a 20% chance of rain during the day and 70% chance of precipitation this evening. The high today is around 58, with a low around 31.

Friday, April 24, brings a chance of snow and a 60% chance of precipitation during the day, according to the National Weather Service. There's a 30% chance of evening precipitation, with a high around 50 and low of 27.

 042426-nws-murphy-fire-p3.jpg
Fire crews work on the Murphy wildfire near Hermosa.

title: Zach Bush announces run for seat on Pennington County Board of Commissioners
description: Zach Bush has announced his for candidacy for Pennington County Board of Commissioners for a seat as commissioner of District 1.
_full_text:
Zach Bush has announced his for candidacy for Pennington County Board of Commissioners for a seat as commissioner of District 1.

 Zach Bush
Zach Bush has announced his run for the Pennington County Board of Commissioners District 1 seat. 

Three of the five seats on the board are up for election on June 2. The current seats up for election are held by Commissioners Ron Rossknecht (District 1), Deb Hadcock (District 3) and Gary Drewes (District 5).

Hadcock is the only commissioner seeking re-election.
Mike Mueller is also running for District 1.
Per a press release, Bush is running on the values of "principles, transparency and fiscally responsible leadership."
"County government should work for the people, not the other way around," Bush said in the release. "I will fight to lower taxes, efficient and sustainable service and decisions that respect the hard-working families, farmers, and business owners in district 1. Together, we can keep our communities safe, prosperous and true to South Dakota's independent spirit."
According to the release, Bush is a veteran. His priorities include protecting taxpayer dollars, supporting responsible growth and infrastructure, promoting public safety, and preserving the rural and agricultural character.
For more information, visit zachbushdistrict1.com.
Absentee voting began on Thursday, April 24, after a delay because of ballot printing issues, and continues until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.  The delay is a result of state law timelines and is not caused by the Pennington County Auditor’s Office or the County Auditor.

The voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

title: Rapid City man pleads not guilty to rape and child sexual abuse material charges
description: A 23-year-old Rapid City man pleaded not guilty to two counts of fourth-degree rape and two counts of creating child sexual abuse material.
_full_text:
A 23-year-old Rapid City man pleaded not guilty to two counts of fourth-degree rape and two counts of creating child sexual abuse material.

Kaege Snyder appeared for his arraignment before Circuit Court Judge Matthew Brown at the Pennington County Courthouse on Thursday, April 23.

Snyder is charged with two counts of fourth-degree rape and two counts of child pornography for an incident that involved a 16-year-old in June 2023. 

In South Dakota, fourth-degree rape is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and $30,000 fine. Child pornography is a Class 4 felony with a maximum sentence of 10 years in prison and a $20,000 fine. 

Snyder could face the possibility of 50 years in prison if convicted of all four charges. 

Snyder's next court appearance is set at 1:15 p.m. on May 21.

title: Rapid City YMCA and South Dakota Mines partner together for Hardrocker Basketball Camps
description: The Rapid City YMCA is excited to announce an upcoming 6-week basketball skills camp, held at the Rapid City Y beginning Tuesday, May 5. The Camp has three levels of participation, based on age: Kindergarten-2nd grade, 3rd-5th grade, and 6th-8th grade. Cost is $58 for YMCA Members; $72 for Community Participants.
_full_text:
The Rapid City YMCA is excited to announce an upcoming 6-week basketball skills camp, held at the Rapid City Y beginning Tuesday, May 5. The Camp has three levels of participation, based on age: Kindergarten-2nd grade, 3rd-5th grade, and 6th-8th grade. Cost is $58 for YMCA Members; $72 for Community Participants.

The basketball clinic will be led by our local college basketball team – the SD School of Mines Hardrockers and Women’s Head Basketball Coach, Kyley Bachand. Players will focus on dribbling, shooting, passing and defense through drills, scrimmages and fun challenges. The camp will boost confidence, and attendees will have a blast learning from collegiate athletes in a safe, fun and supportive environment. It’s a chance for all skill levels to train with the best, right here at home!

 Hardrocker BB Camp Social Media Post - 1

“We’re thrilled to partner with the South Dakota School of Mines to bring this camp to the Rapid City YMCA,” said Kristy Lintz, Chief Operations Officer for the YMCA of the Black Hills. “It’s an incredible opportunity for young athletes to learn directly from a college-level coach, build their skills, and be inspired both on and off the court.”

For more information or to register, visit https://ymcabh.org/news-events/ or stop by your Black Hills-area YMCA in Rapid City, Liberty Center (Box Elder), Custer or Edgemont.

title: About a dozen countries represented at Rapid City Central High School's first cultural night
description: Rafia Mahmood, a junior at Rapid City Central High School and Pakistani exchange student, wanted to wear her sarree.
_full_text:
Rafia Mahmood, a junior at Rapid City Central High School and Pakistani exchange student, wanted to wear her sarree.

"I want you to do something, so I can present my culture and wear that," Mahmood said she told Rapid City Central Student Council Advisor Logan Mays. From there, the very first cultural night at Central was born.

 Rafia Mahmood
Rafia Mahmood explains the food items at her table during the first cultural night held at Rapid City Central High School on Monday, April 20. Mahmood is an exchange student from Pakistan.  

According to the South Dakota Department of Education, 48% of Central's students are white, over 23% are Native American, nearly 12% are Hispanic or Latina, over 13% are two or more races, 1.3% are Asian and 1.6% are Black or African American. 

"One of our strengths at Central is our diversity," said Callum Dewar, who teaches U.S. history at Central and is from Glasgow, Scotland.

The Monday, April 20, event included booths from nearly a dozen countries, where students and teachers shared items and foods from their cultures. Choir students performed songs from various cultures. 

"Isn't it fun?" said Mahmood, who is a foreign exchange student from Pakistan who is in Rapid City for a year before she returns home. 

"Like three years ago, I was one day sitting at my computer, and I'm like, 'These American high schools are so cool," Mahmood said. "...and God just listened to me. And the next year I got to know about this (exchange student) program."

Mahood served a potato salad with chickpeas and savory spices, not too far removed from the Midwestern potato salad, but spicier. She also made a fried dessert with semolina — a type of flour — and milk. 

A posterboard filled with photos of Pakistan sat on her table. Mahood explained the prevalence of truck art in her home country as she pointed to a photo of a heavily-decorated truck.

"All the details are hand-painted. If you would come to Pakistan, you won't see boring trucks on the roads," she said.

 Truck
A photo of truck art on display at the Pakistan table. 

Photos highlighted other notable Pakistanis, including musician Zayn Malik, Nobel prize winners Malala Yousafzai and Abdus Salam, and the diverse landscapes of Pakistan, which include high mountains, deserts, valleys and rivers. 

Countries represented at Rapid City Central High School's first culture night
Mexico
Greece
Japan
Nigeria
India 
Nepal
Pakistan
Spain
Vietnam
Philippines
Scotland

Dewar manned the Scotland booth. He moved to Rapid City with his wife after they married and has taught at Central for three years. The couple now have a 2-year-old daughter. 

Dewar held up a quaich, a small bowl with two handles, that he received as a gift for his wedding. 

 Rafia Mahmood
Rafia Mahmood explains the food items at her table during the first cultural night held at Rapid City Central High School on Monday, April 20. Mahmood is an exchange student from Pakistan.  

"You would normally put whiskey in there, and you would share a dram," Dewar said. "It's meant to be a sign of friendship, sometimes given at weddings."

His table also featured a map of Scotland — which he admits his mother-in-law, an elementary school teacher, made — showing the country's more than 700 islands. He also offered shortbread.

Natalie May, senior class vice president, helped organize the area and set up tables for the event.

"What truly brought it together, though, is all of the cultures coming in and celebrating and showing all of their diversity," May said. "And I love seeing people's culture and what I can learn about it, especially coming from a leadership standpoint."

 Photos from Rapid City Central High School's first cultural night
Rapid City Central Student hosts its first cultural night earlier this week, featuring food, song and more highlighting the school's diversity.

title: Locals invited to vote now for third Thriving Communities investment
description: Black Hills Area Community Foundation invites area residents to vote now for the third Thriving Communities Initiative. The community can choose an issue to receive $200,000 in financial support.
_full_text:
Black Hills Area Community Foundation invites area residents to vote now for the third Thriving Communities Initiative. The community can choose an issue to receive $200,000 in financial support.

BHACF’s Thriving Communities Initiative is a recurring program that lets Black Hills residents have input on a regional investment. Through May 31, area residents can vote online at bhacf.org/thriving-communities for an issue they believe needs more attention and financial support. The Thriving Communities Initiative supports nonprofits across the northern, central, and southern Black Hills.

 The Inside Out Tree installation (copy)
Artist Mickey MacKaben holds a branch of the Inside Out Tree on Oct. 9, 2025, as she and Noah Geiger of Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City installed the tree. The art project was displayed throughout October. It was funded as part of the 2025 Thriving Communities Initiative. Black Hills Area Community Foundation responded to community input and invested $145,000 in youth mental wellness nonprofit programs. BHACF partnered with the Rapid City Arts Council to host the Inside Out Tree, which was designed to reduce stigma, encourage empathy, and spark local conversations about mental health. 

This year, Black Hills residents can choose between four causes:

Community Information: Support local libraries, journalism, museums, and other information hubs.

Quality Care for Elders: Help our senior generation thrive in this phase of life.

Arts for All: Cultivate community and build bridges by expanding access to the arts.

Childcare for Healthy Families: Expand families’ access to quality childcare services.

At the end of 2026, BHACF will award its first round of nonprofit grants based on the community’s survey responses. In 2027, BHACF will bring community members together to connect and learn about the issue area. At year’s end, a second round of grants will be awarded.

“The Thriving Communities Initiative is about more than giving back -- it’s about building community. We’re looking forward to engaging our community more deeply in their chosen issue area. This spring, each survey response is especially important because it will shape the initiative’s focus for two years,” said Chris Huber, president and CEO of BHACF.

To commemorate its 20th anniversary, BHACF has increased the investment that will be allocated to the third Thriving Communities Initiative.

In 2025, “Youth Mental Wellness” received the community’s popular vote in the second Thriving Communities Initiative survey. Locals emphasized a need for resources that encourage open conversations about mental wellness that reduce stigma. As a result, BHACF gave $145,000 in grant funds to nine nonprofits that strengthen youth mental wellness across the Black Hills.

In 2024, more than 600 people took the first Thriving Communities Initiative survey and selected Beautiful Black Hills — investing in the outdoor spaces that contribute to residents’ quality of life — as the cause to be funded. BHACF awarded $125,000 in grant funds to 10 nonprofits that promote recreation and care for outdoor spaces.

The Black Hills Area Community Foundation is a registered public charity. It funds charitable work, inspires donors to make meaningful gifts and manages initiatives around key issues. The Thriving Communities Initiative represents the Foundation’s third special initiative, in addition to its Beautiful Black Hills and Strategic Housing initiatives.

title: Jerry E. Smith
_full_text:
June 3, 1951 - April 15, 2026

Jerry Smith, 74, of Concord, NE, passed away peacefully on Wednesday, April 15, 2026, at his home surrounded by his family. The visitation will be held on Thursday, April 23, 2026, from 5:00 p.m. until 7:00 p.m. at First Lutheran Church in Allen, NE. A Celebration of Jerry's Life will be held on Friday, April 24, 2026, at 2:00 p.m. at First Lutheran Church. The burial with military honors will be held in Eastview Cemetery in Allen, NE. Memorials are suggested to First Lutheran Church in Allen, NE, Allen American Legion, Eastview Cemetery, and the Logan Valley Campers. The family requests that anyone attending the services please wear patriotic attire. Arrangements are under the direction of Munderloh-Smith Funeral Home in Wakefield, NE.

Jerry was born in Stockton, California, on June 3, 1951, the son of Lyndal and Evelyn (Bates) Smith. Following his graduation from Custer High School in Custer, SD, in 1969, he earned his Associate's Degree in Engineering Drafting from Western Technical College. He entered the United States Army in 1971, serving until 1974. On May 6, 2011, he was united in marriage to Karen (Woodward) Claussen at Chadron, NE. They were an active couple that enjoyed life together. Jerry was a member of the Allen American Legion, First Lutheran Church, and the Logan Valley Campers. He enjoyed fishing, hunting, gardening, making jewelry, playing cards, camping, hiking, and biking.

Jerry is survived by his wife, Karen of Concord, NE; sons, Thaddeous (Gloria) Stanton of Lewistown, MT, Kaleb (Maggie) Smith of Detroit Lakes, MN, Jesse Smith of Omaha, NE, Jaron (Alexis) Smith of Severna Park, MD; step-son, Jared Claussen of Chadron, NE; step-daughters, Casie Williams of Omaha, NE, and Chandra Hans of Washburn, ND; brothers, Ed Smith of Las Vegas, NV, Darcy Smith of Custer, SD, and Charles Smith of Custer, SD; brother-in-law's and sister-in-law's, Kenny and Kathleen Hill, Keith and RoNelle Woodward, Kevin Oxner and Loraine Buckwald, and Kevin and Stacey Woodward; nine grandchildren; and four great-grandchildren.

Jerry was preceded in death by his parents, Lyndal and Evelyn Smith, and a brother, Ronnie Hill.
