title: ‘Maybe not ideal, but certainly a win:’ Rapid City schools raises employee salaries 2.5%
description: Negotiations have come to a close, and Rapid City Area Schools employees will receive 2.5% wage increases in fiscal year 2027, which starts on July 1.
_full_text:
Negotiations have come to a close, and Rapid City Area Schools employees will receive 2.5% wage increases in fiscal year 2027, which starts on July 1. 

“There’s no other process that impacts the district greater than negotiations,” said Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse. “So it’s something we put a lot of time and effort into.”

 Coy Sasse
Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse answers questions about employee negotiations at the Tuesday, April 21, Rapid City Area Schools Board meeting. 

The increase covers all district employees except executive leadership. Those negotiations continue, Sasse said.

The employee negotiations began in mid-March, and although what happens within them is confidential, Sasse shared the results publicly at the Tuesday, April 21, Rapid City Area Schools Board meeting. The cost for the district comes out to $2.51 million.

“It might not always look like what we want it to, but delivering 2.5%, $2.5 million more dollars into the pockets of our employees, as the district's overall resources are declining and as we're dealing with a really difficult deficit situation, in my opinion, is a big win for us,” Sasse said. “Maybe not ideal, but certainly a win for the district.”

In addition to all employee groups agreeing to the 2.5% increase, they also agreed to a two-year contract term, decreasing the frequency of negotiations. In the past, groups negotiated with the district each year on a full contract. Now, unions can only open negotiations with wages and one other topic of their choosing each year.

 RCAS Board
From left: RCAS Board Members Angela Rafferty and Christine Stephenson, Superintendent Jami Jo Thompson, Chief Financial and Operations Officer Coy Sasse and Assistant Superintendent Cory Strasser at the RCAS board meeting on Tuesday, April 21.  

“This is important and a bit of an understated inclusion of these agreements,” Sasse said, noting it adds stability.

Sasse said the negotiating process involved two different methods, the traditional and Interest-Based Bargaining (IBB). According to the Federal Mediation & Conciliation Service, IBB is a collaborative approach to resolving labor and management disputes. Through the process, parties proactively identify durable solutions to outcomes at the bargaining table. Agreements are based on mutual and individual interests rather than positions. This approach emphasizes problem-solving and enables mutual gain outcomes.

Traditional is less collaborative and team-based and more side-based, Sasse said, sitting on opposite sides of the table and exchanging pieces of paper.

Jennifer Macziewski, a teacher at Valley View Elementary, expressed concerns about the rising costs of insurance premiums, and how that impacts take-home pay.

"It remains our most pressing concern. Salaries are just not keeping pace with the cost of living and rising insurance costs," Macziewski said.

President of the Rapid City Education Association, Stephanie Hunter, echoed Macziewski's concerns and said she hopes for a collaborative conversation to continue.

"It truly is open conversation, so that is much better than the days long ago where you sat there and had a paper back and forth and you felt like you got nowhere," she said. "To every board member who has sat through negotiations over the time, thank you for your time. We absolutely know that it is not always easy to be in your situation."

Sasse said the district will absorb an additional $2 million in insurance costs, and premiums are expected to go up for district employees. Sasse said there will be a presentation at the May 5 board meeting with more accurate figures on how insurance premiums will impact district employees.

 RCAS wage negotiation results
A document included in the April 21, 2026 Rapid City Area Schools Board agenda laying out the results of employee negotiations. 

 Photos from the Rapid Valley Elementary expansion groundbreaking
Officials, students and teachers celebrated the Rapid Valley Elementary expansion and renovation groundbreaking on Monday, April 20. The addition will add 25,000 square feet and add classrooms and other facilities. The building, constructed in 1985, is 46,000 square-foot with a maximum capacity of 524 students, according to the district's master plan.

title: Updated: Rapid City pair plead not guilty to attempted first-degree murder
description: Two 18-year-olds pleaded not guilty to aiding and abetting attempted first-degree murder and five counts of aiding and abetting aggravated assault on Monday, April 20.
_full_text:
Two 18-year-olds pleaded not guilty to aiding and abetting attempted first-degree murder and five counts of aiding and abetting aggravated assault on Monday, April 20. 

James Lerat and Anthony Hill, both from Rapid City, appeared for their arraignment before Circuit Court Judge Joshua Hendrickson at the Pennington County Courthouse. 

Lerat and Hill are charged with aiding and abetting attempted first-degree murder and five counts of aiding and abetting aggravated assault for an incident involving a knife and two alleged victims that occurred on Wednesday, Feb. 18.

Two of the aiding and abetting aggravated assault charges are applied to one victim and the aiding and abetting attempted murder charge and three of the aiding and abetting assault charges are applied to the other alleged victim.

Lerat and Hill could each be convicted of one charge for each alleged victim, for a total of two charges — either aiding and abetting attempted first-degree murder or, in the alternative, one of the aiding and abetting assault charges for the one victim. For the other alleged victim, they could be convicted of one of the aggravated assault charges, but not all of them. 

In South Dakota, aiding and abetting attempted first-degree murder is a Class 2 felony with a maximum sentence of 25 years in prison and a $50,000 fine. Aiding and abetting aggravated assault is a Class 3 felony with a maximum sentence of 15 years in prison and a $30,000 fine. 

Lerat and Hill's next court appearance is set for May 18 at 3:15 p.m.

title: UPDATE: Rapid City Police release name of man arrested after stabbing incident
description: The Rapid City Police Department released the name of one of the men from Wednesday's stabbing incident and arrested him.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department released the name of one of the men from Wednesday's stabbing incident and arrested him.

Jesse Torres, 40, of Rapid City was arrested on suspicion of attempted murder. 

At around 5:50 a.m. Wednesday, April 22, police responded to a home in the 500 block of Waterloo Street for a report of a stabbing, the department said on Thursday, April 23.

Upon arrival officers found a man standing in the roadway with stab wounds to his upper torso, police said. While giving aid to the man, police were notified of another person who reported being stabbed. 

Police found Torres with a stab wound to the arm at a gas station in the 500 block of East North Street. Both men were transported to the hospital for non-life-threatening injuries.

During their investigation, police were able to determine that Torres was the alleged stabbing suspect and his injuries were "likely unintentionally self-inflicted" as he stabbed the other man. 

As of Wednesday afternoon, Torres was not found in the Pennington County Jail roster.

title: Firefighters contain structure fire in Rapid Valley
description: Firefighters successfully contained a structure fire in Rapid Valley on Wednesday, April 22.
_full_text:
Firefighters successfully contained a structure fire in Rapid Valley on Wednesday, April 22.

The Rapid Valley Volunteer Fire Department responded to a report at 2: 48 a.m. of a structure fire in the 2300 block of Cattle Drive. 

Upon arrival, firefighters found the home on fire and smoke conditions in the walls and roof, the department said.. Firefighters were able to contain the fire and protect surrounding buildings. 

The fire eventually escalated to two alarms. Two people were reported with minor injuries, while one firefighter was transported to Monument Hospital for further evaluation, the department said.

title: Pennington County Sheriff's Office says missing Hill City man's body has been found
description: The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said a missing man has been found dead.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Sheriff's Office said a missing man has been found dead.

The evening of Tuesday, April 21, Pennington County Search and Rescue found a deceased individual in a "wooded, rugged area a few miles west of Hill City," the sheriff's office said. The man has been identified as 86-year-old Ronald Deloss Blanchard of Hill City, who went missing on April 6. The office had initially said he was found north of Hill City.

 Ronald Deloss Blanchard
Ronald Deloss Blanchard 

Pennington County Search and Rescue had been conducting continued searches in the area. Based on the preliminary investigation, there is no indication of foul play. Cause and manner of death will be determined following an autopsy and the conclusion of investigative work, the office said.

The Pennington County Sheriff’s Office said it sends "our condolences to the friends and family of Ronald Blanchard. We thank the many agencies and volunteers who helped with the search and tonight’s discovery. We could not conduct these operations without dedicated volunteers that help with search efforts."

title: College student from China charged with illegally taking photos of US military planes
description: A college student from China has been charged with illegally taking photos of U.S. military planes in Nebraska during a multistate road trip that included a stop at an Air Force base in South Dakota.
_full_text:
A college student from China has been charged with illegally taking photos of U.S. military planes in Nebraska during a multistate road trip that included a stop at an Air Force base in South Dakota.

Tianrui Liang, 21, was arrested April 7 at a New York airport while trying to leave the U.S. for Glasgow, Scotland, where he attends school, the FBI said in a court filing.

Liang admitted that he got out of a car on a public road in late March and took photos of an RC-135, a reconnaissance aircraft, and an E-4B at Offutt Air Force Base near Omaha, the FBI said.

The E-4B, known as the “Nightwatch”, can serve as an airborne command center for a president and military officials in times of emergency, according to the Air Force.

The FBI said it's illegal to photograph or sketch defense installations without approval. Images of both planes are available online.

Liang told investigators that it was ”legal to take pictures of the sky, but he knew it was illegal to take pictures of the planes on the ground," the FBI said. He said they were for his personal collection, the FBI said.

Liang's attorney, Jeff Thomas, declined to comment Tuesday. Liang has not appeared yet in federal court in Omaha.

Liang flew to Vancouver, British Columbia, in Canada on March 26 and met a friend who is a college student in New York, the FBI said. They drove across the U.S. border in Washington state before Liang drove alone to see Ellsworth Air Force Base in South Dakota, according to the affidavit. The FBI said he was also interested in going to Tinker Air Force Base in Oklahoma.

Other cases involving military sites have been filed against college students from China.

Five men were charged with lying and trying to cover their tracks after they were confronted in the dark in 2023 near a Michigan military site where thousands of people had gathered for drills. They graduated from the University of Michigan and apparently returned to China months before they were charged and have never appeared in court.

In 2020, two Chinese nationals who were pursuing master’s degrees at the University of Michigan were sentenced to prison for illegally taking photographs at a naval air station in Key West, Florida.

title: South Dakota AG: Highway Patrol justified in shooting Box Elder man after pursuit
description: The South Dakota Attorney General's Office said a South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation review has determined that two South Dakota Highway Patrol troopers were justified in shooting a man holding an AR-15 rifle-style Airsoft gun.
_full_text:
The South Dakota Attorney General's Office said a South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation review has determined that two South Dakota Highway Patrol troopers were justified in shooting a man holding an AR-15 rifle-style Airsoft gun.

The March 21 incident began when the Summerset Police Department started the pursuit of a vehicle following an attempted traffic stop, the AG's office said in a release. The Meade County Sheriff’s Office also assisted in the pursuit. The officers involved terminated the stop after the vehicle left their jurisdiction.

Benjamin Milbrandt Jr., 31, of Box Elder, was later identified as the driver of the suspect vehicle, the release said. Two Highway Patrol troopers joined the pursuit east of Rapid City. The vehicle continued east before it stopped at a residence on U.S. Highway 1416.

According to the release:

Milbrandt Jr. left the vehicle holding an AR-15 rifle-style Airsoft gun. He pointed the gun at the troopers. The two troopers shot at Milbrandt. One fired six rounds. The other fired three. 

Milbrandt Jr. received a single gunshot wound and suffered non-life threatening injuries. Neither the two troopers nor the civilians at the scene were injured.

DCI processed the crime scene, conducted a forensic examination of evidence, interviewed those involved, and reviewed all available video. The Airsoft rifle used by Milbrandt Jr. was recovered at the scene.

Testing of Milbrandt Jr.’s blood showed a blood alcohol content of 0.207 percent, and methamphetamine and cannabinoids were found in his system. Blood and urine samples of the officers tested negative for alcohol or illegal drugs.

“The person involved pointed what appeared as a weapon at the troopers and later expressed his desire to be killed by law enforcement,” Attorney General Marty Jackley said in the release. “Based upon the investigation, the troopers were justified in the use of force.”

Milbrandt Jr. had a history of criminal offenses including DUI, ingestion, possession of controlled substances, and burglary. He was on parole at the time of this incident and had an active arrest warrant for being a parole absconder.

Law enforcement agencies that assisted were the South Dakota Forensic Lab, Box Elder Police Department, the Rapid City Police Department, and the Pennington County Sheriff’s Office. The Highway Patrol cooperated with the investigation.

Jackley said Milbrandt Jr. has been charged in Pennington County with two felony counts of Simple Assault on a Law Enforcement Officer and one misdemeanor count of Eluding. Other charges are possible. The Attorney General’s Office will prosecute.

The AG's Office said it was the first officer-involved shooting in South Dakota this year.

title: Rapid Valley Sanitary District installing new water main on North Valley Drive section
description: Effective next Tuesday, April 28, traffic will be restricted on a section of North Valley Drive as crews with Rapid Valley Sanitary District install a new water main.
_full_text:
Effective next Tuesday, April 28, traffic will be restricted on a section of North Valley Drive as crews with Rapid Valley Sanitary District install a new water main.

Traffic cones will be placed in the affected area, which will be maintained to through traffic along a stretch of North Valley Drive, including Eagle Drive. Drivers are advised to use caution in the affected area during the project period, which is scheduled until mid-June.

For more information, contact the Rapid Valley Sanitary District at 605-393-1050.

title: Rapid City Council approves joining Pennington County TIF district project
description: After concerns of oversight by the Rapid City Council that led to a delayed action on April 6, the council approved a resolution to join in a Tax Increment Financing District with Pennington County.
_full_text:
After concerns of oversight by the Rapid City Council that led to a delayed action on April 6, the council approved a resolution to join in a Tax Increment Financing District with Pennington County. 

The council voted 9-1 to approve the resolution at its Monday, April 20, meeting. Councilman Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) voted to deny. 

 Jason Salamun
Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun debuts new shaved head look at the April 20, City Council meeting. 

Councilman Kevin Maher (Ward 3) said he met with the developer of the TIF and approved the infrastructure and retail space the area could provide. No other comments on the TIF were made by the council at the meeting.

According to city documents, Pennington County is looking to create a Tax Increment Financing District on an estimated 172-acre lot located generally south of Catron Boulevard, west of Fifth Street and east of Black Hills Boulevard.

Part of the TIF area is within Rapid City limits, and the county has requested permission for that area to be used in the TIF district.

The City Council's resolution approval does not approve a TIF district or project plan.

The developer will still need to submit a TIF application before it goes through the county government approval process. 

 County TIF
Pennington County Buildings and Grounds Director Davis Purcell (left) and Dana Foreman with DGR Engineering (right) in the audience for the county TIF. 

According to city documents, Black Hills Marketplace would be used for major retail and restaurant developments, as well as single- and multi-family residential developments. The city limits section is on Catron Boulevard.

On April 6, the Rapid City Council voted 9-0 to delay action to the April 20 meeting, after council members expressed concern of oversight of the TIF, as approval of the TIF boundary and project plan will be made by the county. Council President Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5) was absent from the meeting. 
Pennington County Commissioner Ron Weifenbach came to the April 6 meeting to voice concerns about the oversight. He and Finance Director Daniel Ainslie clarified the TIF request was originally proposed to the city.

Ainslie confirmed with the Journal the city did in fact receive a TIF request from the developer of this TIF, but because the TIF area falls mostly within the county, the city reached out to the county for them to consider the TIF.
Ainslie clarified at issue is a request for the city to join in the TIF boundary with the county. There is no approval of a TIF project plan at this time.
Ainslie also said the city would have some oversight of the project. He said the city still controls the planning and zoning aspects of the project.

Ainslie could not give an exact figure of the city's property in the project site, but estimated it at less than 5% of the total 172-acre site. Ainslie also said it's too early in the TIF process for a project cost valuation.

title: South Dakota Wildland Fire celebrates new $2 million expansion at Rapid City campus
description: South Dakota Wildland Fire is happy to have a new building at its campus for crews to utilize.
_full_text:
South Dakota Wildland Fire is happy to have a new building at its campus for crews to utilize.

On Monday, April 20, officials hosted a ribbon-cutting ceremony — done with a pickaxe — for the space at 3305 W. South St. The agency held a ceremony the same day in Hot Springs for another new building as well. The Southern Hills location is dedicated to wildland fire division member Trampus Haskvitz, who died while working on the State Engine 561 at the Coal Canyon Fire in 2011.

"I'm very grateful today for his new facility, and part of the reason I'm grateful, I (can) share lots and lots of stories about our old facilities ... Several years ago, we had to take all this, we call it our cache, all of our firefighting equipment was stored in a loft. We had to take that out, because we found out we were at 180% of the rated load for that loft," South Dakota State Wildland Fire Division Director Jay Wickham said about his excitement for the new space in Rapid City.

A previous building at the Rapid City campus, Wickham said, became inadequate for wildland crews, leading to a new building needing to be constructed.

The two new buildings in the Black Hills came from appropriated funds during the 2023 and 2024 legislative sessions. The Rapid City cost an estimated $2 million.

The wildland division is a part of the state's Department of Public Safety, with four district offices in Custer, Hot Springs, Lead and Rapid City.

Wickham said the division has an estimated 45 permanent employees and adds an additional 45 seasonal employees in the summertime.

According to the wildland division website, the Rapid City District Office oversees responsibilities of suppression, management and investigations of all wildland fires on state and private lands in Meade and Pennington County.

The new building will house additional fire trucks and include a briefing room for fire crews.

"(It's) a big deal for them to have this new space. It's more of a family, the firefighting group here, and so they have more of a home base now, and it's going to that meaningful to them," Secretary for the Department of Public Safety Bob Perry said during the ceremony, which Gov. Larry Rhoden also attended.

"I recently visited the Qury Fire by Custer to see the action firsthand, and I was really blown away. It was an eye-opener for me to become head of wildland fire and see firsthand the talent and the dedication and just the knowledge that our firefighters have in fighting fires here in the Black Hills, day and night," Rhoden said.

The Qury Fire scorched an estimated 9,000 acres of forest in Custer County through mid-March. 

"We were really surprised that we got approved, and really so grateful because it helps us. It truly does when you have the right tools, helps you do your job better," Joannah Tornow with South Dakota Wildland Fire said.

Wildfire concerns

When discussing the mild winter in the Black Hills, and the warmer weather, Wickham said there has been concerns for further forest fires.

(I'm) never going to speculate on what to expect, because things can change faster in South Dakota. What we're seeing is really, if you travel around the Black Hills at all, really no green up right now because of the lack of moisture. So all the fuels are completely cured and available to burn. So the fires that we've had, Qury, 79, all those fires are a result of wind, wind-driven. So any day when we get wind, the possibility of a fire becoming large is very easy," Wichman said.

The 79 wildfire was reported April 11 and burned over 5,000 acres in the Buffalo Gap region in Custer.

Rhoden said he has concerns about the current conditions of the forest region due to a lack of moisture in the region.

"Firefighters are just a sample of the great people and the hard work and sacrifice that they're willing to make for their communities and for the state, and we owe it to them to show them the respect and give them the tools and the facilities that they need so they can continue keeping South Dakota strong and safe," Rhoden said on the support for the wildland division.

Rhoden is joined by state Speaker of the House Jon Hansen, businessman Toby Doeden and U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson in the Republican gubernatorial primary race. The election is on Tuesday, June 2.

title: Local law enforcement hosts National Prescription Drug Take Back Day
description: The Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff's Office are encouraging the public to turn in their old and unneeded medications, joining National Prescription Drug Take Back Day.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County Sheriff's Office are encouraging the public to turn in their old and unneeded medications, joining National Prescription Drug Take Back Day.

Take Back Day is a nationwide event to help prevent prescription drugs getting into the wrong hands. It gives the community the opportunity to clean out their medicine cabinets and turn in medicines safely and anonymously. 

According to the United States Drug Enforcement Administration, there is a total of law enforcement participation of 4,263. Nationwide they've collected 571,054 pounds of old and unneeded medications. 

The event is on Saturday, April 25, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. at the Public Safety Building on 300 Kansas City Street.

title: Lange II Generating Station: Black Hills Energy's new facility should be complete by end of 2026
description: Black Hills Energy ’s Lange II Generating Station should be complete by the end of 2026, bringing reliable electric service to its customers in South Dakota and Wyoming.
_full_text:
Black Hills Energy’s Lange II Generating Station should be complete by the end of 2026, bringing reliable electric service to its customers in South Dakota and Wyoming.

Construction on Lange II began earlier this year on Deadwood Avenue in Rapid City near Black Hills Energy’s Lange I facility. The community will see more steel beams going up in the weeks ahead as the new Lange II building is being constructed.

Lange II is a 99-megawatt natural gas facility that will replace the Ben French generating units, which have been in use since the 1960s and are slated for retirement in 2027.

Lange II will tie into Lange I, which generates about 40 megawatts to provide power service within the city limits for Rapid City, and for the Black Hills region. Lange I was built in the early 2000s.

 BHE engines
Lange II will be powered by six RICE (reciprocating internal combustion engine) engines, pictured sitting onsite on Deadwood Avenue. Each engine weighs about 386,000 pounds and can generate about 18 megawatts of power, enough to provide electricity to 15,000 to 18,000 homes. 

“Black Hills Energy is very excited because we’re about at the halfway point of Lange II, a milestone in our construction project with our Lange II generator project,” Wes Ashton, vice president for BHE South Dakota and Wyoming Utilities, said during a tour on Friday, April 17. “We are retiring about 105 megawatts (of power) at Ben French and bringing on new technology.”

Ashton emphasized Lange II is being built for its customers, and not to provide power for a specific large client or a specific project such as a future data center. Lange II is intended to support peak demand and reliability during events like extreme weather for BHE’s customers in western South Dakota, northeastern Wyoming, plus a portion of southeastern Montana. About 78,000 of BHE’s customers — the vast majority — are in South Dakota.

Lange II “will allow us to continue to serve Rapid City and the Black Hills region for decades to come,” Ashton said. “As we continue to focus on our customers, we know that reliability is something that matters very much to all of our customers. We want to make sure Black Hills Energy can provide safe, affordable, reliable energy, and that reliability comes with the addition of Lange II.”

Lange II will be powered by six RICE (reciprocating internal combustion engine) engines made by Wartsila, a company in Finland. The engines were transported by ship and railroad to Rapid City.

Each engine weighs about 386,000 pounds. The dual fuel-capable engines use some diesel to start the combustion process and then run on natural gas. The duel-fuel capability means that in case of a natural gas shortage, the engines can run on diesel to provide power for 4-1/2 days to BHE customers, according to Derek Silbaugh, BHE’s director of Generation Engineering and Environmental Services.

 BHE roof panels
Steel beams have started going up for a new building that will house Lange II's six engines. Pictured in front of the beams in this Friday, April 17, photo are roof panels. Black Hills Energy said construction on Lange II should be completed by the end of 2026. 

“They’re really quick to speed, so if there’s a call for energy in the market, the engines can be online and up to 99 megawatts in less than five minutes. They’re really good at responding to reactions on the grid,” Silbaugh said.

“These engines are what we call our quick-start capability, so if anything ever happened with the system or our grid, this is where we would begin restoration of our system. They’re online very quickly, so we’re really excited about having these units available for our customers,” Silbaugh said.

Each engine produces slightly more than 18 megawatts of power, meaning each engine will be capable of providing power for 15,000 to 18,000 homes in the region, Silbaugh said.

He said each engine is low in water usage, consuming about nine gallons of water per hour. That’s comparable to the amount of water an average household uses.

These Wartsila-made engines are being used by about 80 projects throughout the United States including Lange II, Ashton said.

Lange II’s engines are on site on Deadwood Avenue now, and they will be moved into the building that’s under construction.

 BHE construction site
An overview of construction on Deadwood Avenue for Black Hills Energy's Lange II Generating Station on Friday, April 17. It will replace the Ben French station, which is being retired in 2027.  

“The engines will be housed in an acoustically controlled building so it reduces the decibel levels, the sound produced by these very large engines so outside of the building, you probably wouldn’t even know they were inside of there,” Silbaugh said, adding that being good neighbors to the community is important to BHE.

The mild, dry winter has allowed construction on Lange II to proceed more quickly than expected, Ashton said.

“We didn’t have the moisture you would usually expect, or the muddy spring conditions, so that’s actually allowed construction to proceed very well,” Ashton said. “We’re actually ahead of schedule to get this service online by the end of the year.”

Ashton and Silbaugh said BHE is pleased to be working with several local contractors and companies on the construction of Lange II: Avid4; AET; Zandstra Construction; Pete Lien & Sons; Quinn Construction; Simon sanitary sewer; Rinker Materials; Horsley Specialties; and the City of Rapid City’s Planning and Zoning Department.

Lange II is providing local economic benefits, such as long-term property tax revenue for Pennington County and ongoing jobs at the site. Silbaugh said about 150 employees and contractors have been working onsite, and in the next month, Silbaugh said that number will grow to about 225 people.

title: Rapid City parole operation results in 7 arrests, 4 parolees arrested
description: With the Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County State's Attorney banding together last week to create a parole task force , the department highlighted Friday's, April 17, efforts that resulted in seven people arrested, four being parolees.
_full_text:
With the Pennington County Sheriff's Office, Rapid City Police Department and the Pennington County State's Attorney banding together last week to create a parole task force, the department highlighted Friday's, April 17, efforts that resulted in seven people arrested, four being parolees. 

Christian Degeest, 28, of Rapid City was arrested Saturday, April 18, on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance, possession of paraphernalia and a parole hold. According to court records, Degeest is out on parole for being a habitual offender for three or more felonies. He was sentenced to eight years in prison with four suspended. 

Degeest was convicted of several drug charges between 2016 and 2019. He also has several pending charges for possession of a controlled substance in Pennington County.

Nikki Richards, 23, of Pierre, Bruce Bunkelman, 33, Rayla Habben, 33, both of Rapid City, were arrested Friday, April 17 on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance and a parole hold. 

Richards was convicted in 2023 for manufacturing, distributing, or possession of fentanyl and was sentenced to three years in prison. Bunkelman was convicted in 2024 for possession of methamphetamine and was sentenced to seven years in prison with three years and six months suspended. Habben was convicted in 2024 for possession of methamphetamine and was sentenced to four years in prison. 

Other arrests from Friday's incident included 36-year-old Samanthe Dewitt of Rapid City, Hayden Hensrud, 35, of Box Elder and Echo Blodgett. 

Dewitt was arrested on suspicion of possession of a controlled substance. Hensrud was arrested on suspicion of false personation and possession of a controlled substance. Blodgett was arrested for a Pennington County warrant, for alleged forgery and grand theft charges. 

At around 2:55 p.m. on Friday, members of the interagency parole task force arrived at a residence in the 300 block of East Indiana Street for a parole compliance check for Degeest. 

Upon arrival, officers saw a woman exit a car at the residence and recognized her as a known parolee, Richards. Police said Richards was found in possession of methamphetamine. Officers made contact with the driver of the car, who initially gave a false name, but was later identified as Hensrud. 

Habben answered the door to the home, where police were able to determine she was also on parole. 

The task force executed the parole check inside the home and found numerous drugs and associated paraphernalia inside the house. A search warrant of the home was requested and approved by a judge. 

In a detached garage, police found Bunkelman and Dewitt allegedly in possession of methamphetamine. During the search, police also made contact with Blodgett and determined she had an active warrant. A camper, reportedly stolen in 2022, was found on the property. 

At around 12:20 a.m. on Saturday, police received a report of a man with an active warrant at 225 Federal Ave. Police found Degeest standing in a nearby truck bed. A search of his person turned up methamphetamine and associated paraphernalia, police said. 

As of Monday, April 20, Degeest, Richards, Hensrud, Habben and Bunkelman are being held at the Pennington County Jail with no bond. Blodgett and Dewitt were not found in the jail records.

title: Have you seen this man? Police release photos of Rapid City casino robbery suspect
description: The Rapid City Police Department is looking for a man after he allegedly robbed a casino in Rapid City.
_full_text:
The Rapid City Police Department is looking for a man after he allegedly robbed a casino in Rapid City.

Police said they are looking for a Native American man, between 20 to 35 years old, wearing a light blue hooded sweatshirt, jeans, a blue bandana, a large chain necklace and a black backpack. 

 image010.png
Police said they are looking for a Native American man, between 20 to 35 years old, wearing a light blue hooded sweatshirt, jeans, a blue bandana, a large chain necklace and a black backpack.  

At around 1:35 a.m. Monday, April 20, police responded to a report of a robbery at 777 Casino South at 2110 Jackson Blvd. The employee told police that a man entered the business and handed them a note directing them to give the man money.

The employee said the man was positioned where he appeared to be holding a handgun through the pocket of his sweatshirt. The employee gave the man the money and he was last seen leaving the building, heading north. 

Anyone with any information about the incident or the identity of the man should contact Det. Brendan Lenard at 605-394-4134. An anonymous tip can also be submitted by texting the letters "RCPD" and the information to 847411.
