title: The hens have it: Rapid City Council approves backyard chickens ordinance
description: Urban hens are here.
_full_text:
Urban hens are here.

The Rapid City Council voted 6-4 on Monday, April 20, to approve an ordinance legalizing backyard chickens. Council members Stephen Tamang, Callie Meyer, Lindsey Seachris, Josh Biberdorf, Kevin Maher and Bill Evans voted in favor. Council members John Roberts, Rod Pettigrew, Greg Strommen and Lance Lehmann voted no.

The ordinance takes effect 20 days after the legal notice runs in the Journal.

The ordinance legalizes having up to six hens on residential property, excluding any roosters.

The item has been proposed to the council many times over the last decade, but with additional regulations and stronger enforcement, the pro-hen community and Councilman Stephen Tamang (Ward 1) were able to get the effort over the finish line.

The ordinance requires containment for chickens through an enclosed coop and chicken run, an enclosed outdoor space, to keep predators out. The coops must be ventilated and provide a minimum area of at least 2 square feet per chicken. Runs must be attached to the coop and have a minimum area of at least 10 square feet per chicken.

Coops, runs and exercise yards must be 25 feet from any residential dwelling, church, school, or place of business, excluding the residence on the subject property, and 15 feet from any lot line.

The ordinance also defines violations and lays out enforcement guidelines.

"It is unlawful to keep hens where the provisions of § 6.08.021 are not met. Each day a violation occurs is a separate offense. In addition to any other remedies or causes of action the city may possess, the keeping or maintaining of a hen in a manner inconsistent with § 6.08.021 is declared to be a public nuisance and may be abated by the city as provided by state law and City ordinance," the ordinance reads.

Violations include owning roosters, failing to provide feed and water at all times and/or keeping poultry for commercial use.

The council voted 7-2 on the first reading of the ordinance two weeks ago, with Roberts and Lehmann noting no and Pettigrew absent. Strommen voted yes initially before voting no on the second reading.

title: War, climate change and AI: What’s at stake at this year’s UN Indigenous forum
description: Hundreds of delegates are arriving at the United Nations this week for the world’s largest gathering of Indigenous peoples. But they arrive against an increasingly hostile global backdrop, facing an artificial intelligence boom driving new extraction on ancestral lands, a U.S. administration that has made it increasingly difficult for Global South delegates to secure visas to attend, and the twin challenges of climate change and green energy projects that have frequently run afoul of Indigenous land rights.
_full_text:
Hundreds of delegates are arriving at the United Nations this week for the world’s largest gathering of Indigenous peoples. But they arrive against an increasingly hostile global backdrop, facing an artificial intelligence boom driving new extraction on ancestral lands, a U.S. administration that has made it increasingly difficult for Global South delegates to secure visas to attend, and the twin challenges of climate change and green energy projects that have frequently run afoul of Indigenous land rights.

This year’s United Nations Permanent Forum on Indigenous Issues is focused on the grim topic of survival in the midst of war, with its official theme “Ensuring Indigenous Peoples’ health, including in the context of conflict.” Experts emphasize that Indigenous peoples already face health inequities from colonialism and climate change, and these harms are compounded by armed conflicts and militarization that risk ecological degradation and further displacement of Indigenous peoples from their lands. Experts say that health for Indigenous peoples is directly tied to the environment, land, and sovereignty, and can’t be siloed into clinical discussions about medicine or public health. Warfare isn’t the only concern — advocates are seeing the extraction of critical minerals for the green transition drive Indigenous rights violations, and are echoing a long-standing call to make climate financing directly available to their communities, instead of through state or foreign intermediaries.

But before diplomatic conversations can even begin, many delegates must confront the practical barrier of visa restrictions put in place by the Trump Administration. Mariana Kiimi Ortiz Flores, who is Na Ñuu Savi from Mexico and works as an advocacy assistant at Cultural Survival, said last year, her organization prepared Indigenous representatives from Africa to attend the forum, but their visa applications were denied, and this year, one of their Indigenous staff members from South America was denied her visa as well.

“It’s getting harder and harder to access the United States, not only because of the visa (issues),” said Flores. “People from the Global South, especially Indigenous peoples that have a certain look like brown skin and certain characteristics, we feel threatened because of the general climate of insecurity and hate speech against Latin people and Indigenous peoples.”

Last year, Flores’ organization helped Indigenous leaders from Bolivia attend the forum to protest mining in their traditional lands. They left the forum after being harassed by the leader of a political party in Bolivia, and, coupled with health issues, have decided not to return. “The forum is meant to be for Indigenous peoples, but we really felt that that’s not what’s happening anymore and that at the end of the day the states are the ones who have more power over our lives,” Flores said. “This struggle of defending their land against this extractive industry is really affecting them not only physically but also mentally, spiritually.”

That comprehensive toll is one of the central focuses of a key report by Geoffrey Roth, a Standing Rock Sioux descendant, former vice chair of the Permanent Forum, and board chair of the Indigenous Determinants of Health Alliance, an international Indigenous health advocacy nonprofit. “You can’t separate human health from the health of the environment, or our culture, or our language,” Roth said. “Indigenous people view health from a holistic perspective.”

In his report, Roth outlines the Indigenous determinants of health, ranging from land tenure and governance authority that strengthen Indigenous well-being to risk indicators like land dispossession and exclusion from decision-making. Roth argues that fragmented approaches to Indigenous health frequently embraced by the U.N. system and state governments fail to adequately address health problems and underlying causes.

For example, biodiversity policies that ignore Indigenous rights miss opportunities to restore Indigenous land tenure that can both improve ecosystem outcomes and strengthen access to traditional foods. Mental health interventions that ignore state-sanctioned Indigenous language erasure overlook the potential to improve Indigenous mental well-being through language revitalization. “Indigenous health is not just about healthcare, it’s about land, culture, food systems, and community,” Roth said.

The Coquille Indian Tribe in Oregon adopted the Indigenous determinants of health by ordinance last year, and Roth has been working with them as chairman of their executive health board to incorporate the determinants of health across their agencies. “They understand that when they take elders out on a monthly basis to do fishing activities, that is health for those elders,” he said. “It’s continuing their tradition as Coquille people, and it improves the mental health, behavioral health of those elders that are able to participate in that, let alone the food they catch.”

Roth also calls on the U.N. to recognize the value of Indigenous midwifery, which has been frequently banned in favor of Western practices, forcing Indigenous women into conventional institutions where they often face racism and “obstetric violence,” such as procedures performed without their consent. “Indigenous people have been doing this for thousands of years, not only midwifery, but also caring for the environment and caring for our culture and preserving these food systems,” he said.

In another report to UNPFII, former Permanent Forum chair Hindou Oumarou Ibrahim, who is Indigenous Mbororo from Chad, warns that AI acts as a double-edged sword for Indigenous communities. While she urges governments to help Indigenous peoples develop AI tools to revitalize endangered languages and monitor their territories, she also warns of a looming era of digital extractivism as generative AI systems and tech companies actively scrape cultural content, such as medicinal knowledge, traditional stories, and even genetic data.

Lydia Jennings, citizen of the Pascua Yaqui Tribe (Yoeme) and Huichol (Wixáritari), is an assistant professor of environmental studies at Dartmouth College. She said her advocacy for Indigenous data sovereignty — the movement ensuring communities retain the right to own and control their own data — began after a troubling discovery. She noticed a mining company had pulled information about Indigenous cultural practices from an environmental impact statement and was using it on its website to promote a mining project. “That was very alarming to me,” she said. “How much information do we share in efforts to protect our sacred homelands? And what are the ways that we can govern how and who uses that data?”

Like Ibrahim, she says AI can be an opportunity for tribes, noting some might be interested in hosting data centers or using AI to help with language preservation or synthesizing information. However, she remains wary of how much Indigenous data AI systems may be co-opting without consent, as well as the severe risks that massive data centers pose to tribal lands and water resources. “Who has the power and how do we redistribute that power?” she asked. “It can be a tool to power and a tool to harm, but how do we choose to wield it?”

Jennings said there’s a growing movement to incorporate best practices of Indigenous data sovereignty on multiple levels, ranging from academic research to national and international policies.

Another focus of this year’s Permanent Forum is the climate crisis. In a February report focusing on nomadic peoples, experts warned that rigid state borders and exclusionary “fortress conservation” models are curbing the traditional mobility of pastoralists, hunter-gatherers, and seafarers, even as they deal with the fallout of both climate change and increasing lack of access to ancestral lands and waters.

The authors argue that mobility is a deliberate, knowledge-based climate adaptation strategy that state policymakers are actively erasing, citing an example of the Tuareg people in the Sahara Desert. “While the desert knows no borders, contemporary militarized frontiers increasingly restrict ancestral routes and undermine pastoral systems and access to services, rendering these lived realities of Indigenous Peoples invisible in official data and policy frameworks,” the authors describe.

That echoes sentiments expressed by Samante Anne, who is Indigenous Maasai from Kenya and recently spoke at a virtual panel on pastoralists’ legal rights on behalf of the Mainyoito Pastoralists Integrated Development Organization. Anne said although 60 percent of land in Kenya is considered communal, land is increasingly being subdivided for developments and claimed for carbon offset projects that limit pastoralists’ access to land and movement.

“Mobility has everything to do with us adapting to climate change,” Anne said. “Mobility has everything to do with ensuring our livelihoods are secure, our food security is good.”

Making progress on Indigenous health, artificial intelligence, and territorial rights is complicated by a persistent trend within the U.N.: lumping Indigenous peoples together with “local communities.” In official policies and initiatives, the two groups are frequently merged under the acronym “IPLCs”—Indigenous Peoples and Local Communities. But while local communities represent a broad category of stakeholders, Indigenous peoples hold distinct, legally recognized rights under international law. Roth from the Indigenous Determinants of Health Alliance said he recently confronted this issue at the World Health Organization when the agency categorized an Indigenous initiative merely as an “equity” issue.

“This is not an equity issue,” Roth said he told the agency. “We are not just another one of your minority populations. We are rights holders, and this needs to be approached from a rights-based approach.”

“Conflating us with other populations really diminishes our rights and diminishes our ability to maintain our health in our communities,” Roth said. This grouping also actively hinders participation, Roth said, pointing to the U.N. Convention on Biological Diversity’s IPLC working group as an example.

“I’ve tried to participate in that group several times, and as an Indigenous person, I don’t feel welcome and I’m not able to participate,” he said. “These (IPLC) institutions are a way to lessen or dilute the voice of Indigenous peoples in these global mechanisms, and that, to me, is unacceptable.”

He is far from the only one who feels that way. In 2023, the U.N.’s three top Indigenous rights bodies — the Permanent Forum, the Special Rapporteur on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, and the Expert Mechanism on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples — issued a joint statement demanding that U.N. environmental treaties stop using the IPLC acronym entirely. “Indigenous Peoples should not be grouped with an undefined set of communities that may have very different rights and interests,” they wrote.

For advocates on the ground, that debate is just one part of a growing disillusionment with the U.N. system itself. Cultural Survival’s Mariana Kiimi Ortiz Flores said that the institution has suffered from a willingness by member states to simply disregard its laws.

“The United Nations as an international institution has been losing its influence and its power,” said Flores. But despite its bureaucratic hurdles, visa denials, and geopolitical hostility, she said she’s among the many Indigenous peoples determined to show up this week.

“If we as Indigenous peoples don’t do it,” said Flores, “No one else will speak for us and defend us.”

This story was originally published by Grist and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.

title: Roads, Libertyland TIF, housing among topics covered at Rapid City Council candidate forum
description: Around 180 people packed into the Canyon Lake Senior Center to hear from 10 candidates running for five seats on the Rapid City Council. This year every ward is contested for the first time since 2002.
_full_text:
Around 180 people packed into the Canyon Lake Senior Center to hear from 10 candidates running for five seats on the Rapid City Council. This year every ward is contested for the first time since 2002.

The current seats up for election on June 2 are held by Josh Biberdorf (Ward 1), Lindsey Seachris (Ward 2), Kevin Maher (Ward 3), John Roberts (Ward 4) and Rod Pettigrew (Ward 5).

Pettigrew is the only council member not seeking reelection.

 Rapid City Council candidates
Ten Rapid City Council candidates participated in a Citizens for Liberty forum on Thursday, April 16, at Canyon Lake Senior Center. 

Those throwing their hat in the race who are not currently on the council are Murray Lee (Ward 1), Christopher Vanderhoof (Ward 2), Andrea Schaefer (Ward 3), Valeriah Big Eagle (Ward 4), Pat Roseland (Ward 5) and Laura Armstrong (Ward 5).

Ward 4 candidate Ardin Cychosz announced on April 15 that he would be dropping out due to work-related schedule conflicts.

 Crowd
About 180 people attended Thursday's forum. 

The nearly two-hour forum on Thursday, April, 16, was hosted by South Dakota Citizens for Liberty.

Introductions

Every candidate was allotted a two-minute introduction to start the forum.

Ward 1 candidates:

Alderman Biberdorf said, "My slogan is be part of the solution or be part of the problem." He said he's a Christian conservative committed to faith, family and service.

"For me, in truth, I'm really passionate about, number one, making this a great place to have business. Rapid City should be open for business. I believe small businesses and large businesses drive us and give us a great place to raise a family."

 Josh Biberdorf
Ward 1 candidate and current Councilman Josh Biberdorf. 

Lee said he is from Rapid City, but traveled and lived in Arizona, California and Texas for work. In 2005, he moved back to the area to stay closer to his and his wife's family.

"Family is central to who I am. It's central to who my wife is. We've always been deeply involved in our family's lives.... So I understand how important a strong support system is, whether that's family, whether that's the people you call your neighbors, whether that's strangers," Lee said.

He said he is running on issues related to the city's growth, community safety and fiscal spending.

Ward 2 candidates:

Vanderhoof said, "You're all my neighbors. That's how I look at this. We all need to do this together."

He said he comes from New Jersey and has been living in Rapid City for 16 years. He is running on helping out the community and treating residents like neighbors.

 Christopher Vanderhoof
Ward 2 candidate Christopher Vanderhoof. 

"That's what I believe in. We need to help each other out. Knock on a neighbor's door. 'Is everything OK?' 'How are you doing?' I think that's where the city needs to be again. I'm here to help. I'm here to be here for you and listen," Vanderhoof said.

Alderman for Ward 2, Seachris said, "One of the biggest challenges we face today is getting out ahead of growth. Priority isn't just growth, it's coordinated economic development, where institutions work together, resources are aligned and we are able to diversify our local economy."

She said through being a parent, volunteer, coach and council member, she is involved in the community.

Ward 3 candidates:

Schaefer said she is a business owner and former dance studio owner, which makes her connected to the needs of the community.

"Being a small business owner, I think, is a great strength for somebody that wants (this) job. Small business owners, as many of you know, are connected to the community in a real, authentic way," Schaefer.

 Andrea Schaefer
Ward 3 candidate Andrea Schaefer. 

Alderman Maher described himself as fiscally conservative, with a background in business.

He said on seeking re-election, "There's a lot of talk about taxpayers should not support certain things. So I really believe that growth should pay for growth, and that is something that I will work on. I also think we need to strengthen our relationship with the (South Dakota) School of Mines."

 Kevin Maher
Ward 3 candidate and current Councilman Kevin Maher. 

Ward 4 candidates:

Big Eagle said she comes from Lake Andes, South Dakota and received her doctorate in education leadership to serve the community.

She said growing up fishing, farming and in a single-parent household made her the person she is today.

"My motto is our community knows what our community needs, and that's each and every one of you, your perspectives, your work experiences and the working class. That's where I came from, and so just wanting to be able to share a little bit of that with you," she said.

Alderman Roberts said he's been on the council for over a decade, originally running after seeing how the county was spending money. He worked for the county, so he couldn't run for a county office. He said the council works best when people work together.

One area he said he had worked on is safety in the northside of the city. He said city staff and Rapid City Chief of Police Don Hedrick have aided in creating police and fire precincts in the ward to address crime and safety.

"I think everybody knows that we have a crime problem in Rapid City. We have for quite a while. I think it's getting better," Roberts said.

Ward 5 candidates:

Roseland said he is a U.S. Navy veteran and worked as a registered nurse in anesthesiology for 30 years before retiring in 2016. He previously served on the council for Ward 5 from 2022 to 2025.

"I want Rapid City to be the best Rapid City it could be. I want safe neighborhoods. I want responsible growth. I want Rapid City to be open for all businesses and new investments," Roseland said.

 Laura Armstong
Ward 5 candidate Laura Armstrong. 

Armstrong said she has served on the council for Ward 5 for two terms, and twice served as council president.

She is currently the executive director for the Minneluzahan Senior Center.

"In recent years, I feel City Hall has shifted (to) less transparency, less collaboration and far too many decisions have been made without public input," Armstrong said.

She noted issues of nonprofits in the city closing, bus stop removals, Vision Fund projects and an increase in costs of city services as concerning.

Q&A

Citizens for Liberty President Tonchi Weaver opened the public questions portion of the event by asking the candidates what the main issue they've heard about in their ward.

Ward 1:

Lee said roadways, aging infrastructure, safety, housing and affordability in general are key issues in the community.

Biberdorf discussed crime, homelessness and roads.

Ward 2:

Seachris said she's heard of issues on aging infrastructure, roadway conditions and aging park systems and neighborhood amenities.

Vanderhoof said roads and housing upkeep for residents are the main two issues he's heard.

Ward 3:

Maher said roadway improvements have been an issue, stating the city has three projects underway to address issues on Wonderland Road, Carriage Hill Drive and Skyline Drive.

 Forum
From left, Rapid City Council candidates Andrea Schaefer, Kevin Maher, John Roberts, Pat Roseland and Laura Armstrong. 

Schaefer noted safety concerns about the Chapel Lane Bridge reconstruction being delayed and the impacts on residents. She also noted concerns about wildfires and roadways.

Ward 4:

Roberts said roadways, aging infrastructure, property taxes and crime are main issues.

Big Eagle said the biggest issue is housing and affordability in the area. She added public safety and livable wages are common issues.

Ward 5:

Roseland said roadway potholes, population growth and homelessness are key issues.

Armstrong named infrastructure, development, education, accessibility and social spaces as areas she wants to focus on.

Libertyland TIF

One resident asked the candidates whether they were in favor of the Libertyland USA Tax Increment Financing District, which was rejected by voters in January.

The Destination District TIF, which included the Libertyland project, was a proposed $125 million TIF in infrastructure development for the future erection of above-ground construction.

The project site was located north of Rushmore Crossing off East Mall Drive.

 Forum
From left, Rapid City Council candidates Josh Biberdorf, Murray Lee, Christopher Vanderhoof, Lindsey Seachris, Andrea Schaefer and Kevin Maher. 

The TIF for the Destination District was first approved in July 2025, then, after an administrative error, the council voted and reapproved the TIF and project plan again in September 2026.

Thereafter, a bipartisan group, made up of state Sen. Taffy Howard, R-Rapid City, who represents District 34, Weaver — of South Dakota Citizens for Liberty — Democracy in Action and other volunteers, collected signatures to take the TIF to a vote.

On Tuesday, Jan. 20, the "no" side received 7,965 votes (compared to 3,415, yes votes), shooting down the TIF.

Ward 1:

Lee said he was a "no" vote, saying, "In my development work (I) learned about TIFs and how they're supposed to be used, what parameters are supposed to follow, all the qualifications that they're supposed to meet to be considered for a TIF. ... But I don't think that it qualified to the letter for a TIF."

 Murray Lee
Ward 1 candidate Murray Lee. 

Biberdorf said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I would just say that for me, the lens and the optics always is, is this going to be something that's going to help low-income workforce housing when it comes to a TIF, or is it going to be an economic driver for this town. Everybody wants our roads fixed. The fact is, none of us up here going to do that because there's not enough money in the coffers."

Ward 2:

Seachris said she voted "yes," saying, "We knew this was going to be a hot topic tonight. I'm in support of Libertyland. I'm in support of TIFs in general as an economic development tool. So long as there is a community benefit tied into what we would see as a return after the period of time has elapsed," Seachris said.

 Lindsey Seachris
Ward 2 candidate and current Councilwoman Lindsey Seachris. 

Vanderhoof said he was a "no" vote.

"I believe that the TIF was a little too big. They made all these claims where they could make all this money for us. (My) question is, 'why can't that money go back into what they were doing?' If they were going to make that money, they can have the infrastructure and everything done on themselves," Vanderhoof said.

Ward 3:

Maher said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I voted for the Libertyland TIF, but I really, really respect the voters' decision. It was two to one to kill the TIF, and so I support that."

 Trio
From left, Councilman Josh Biberdorf, Councilman John Roberts and council candidate Pat Roseland. 

Schaefer said she was a "no" vote, saying, "I voted no, sort of as an uninformed voter. I think for me, the way it was marketed just felt a little off the mark. It felt a little dishonest to me, and I felt like the public was a little underestimated on what we knew about what a TIF was."

Ward 4:

Roberts said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "I voted yes on this TIF. I voted yes on quite a few TIFs, and I voted no on quite a few TIF and I've stopped quite a few TIFs before they even got to that process."

 John Roberts
Ward 4 candidate and current Councilwoman John Roberts. 

Big Eagle said she was a "no" vote saying, "It was seeing our community struggle not having a place to live, the high crime rate, all of these things going on in Ward 4. Then seeing Libertyland right there, I feel that we could have used a TIF in a different way. If it was for responsive, affordable community development, then yes, I would. I would definitely be interested in supporting a TIF like Libertyland."

Ward 5:

Armstrong said she was a "no" vote

"I felt like it was rushed and pushed, and that's why I voted no. I will say I'm grateful that we did have the Libertyland vote because it unified people that usually work against one another, and brought people together."

 Line to ask questions
Attendees formed a long line to ask questions of Rapid City Council candidates during a forum on Thursday, April 16. 

Roseland said he was a "yes" vote, saying, "North Rapid needed some development out there. It seemed like that was the last place that people wanted to go and wanted to develop. And with this came up, I thought, why not? The (Pete Lien) family wanted to put in lots of money to help develop in that area ... We the People, had their choice to say yes or no. They said no, and I voted yes for it, and I'm not ashamed of that."

 Pat Roseland
Ward 5 candidate Pat Roseland. 

Voting information

Absentee voting is delayed because of ballot printing issued but was set to begin on April 17 and continue until 4 p.m. on Monday, June 1, at the Pennington County Administrative Building at 900 Concourse Drive.

The county's auditor's office released a statement notifying voters absentee ballots will not be available on the first day of the absentee voting period.

"Under South Dakota law, absentee voting is scheduled to begin 46 days prior to an election pursuant to SDCL 12-19-1.2. However, the candidate petition filing deadline was March 31st, as established in SDCL 12-6-4, which determines the final list of candidates to appear on the ballot. This statutory timing results in a brief delay between the start of the absentee voting period and the availability of ballots," the release said.

This delay is a result of state law timelines and is not caused by the Pennington County Auditor’s Office or the County Auditor.

The voter registration deadline is May 18, ahead of the June 2 election day.

 PHOTOS and VIDEO: Citizens for Liberty Rapid City Council candidate forum
Citizens for Liberty hosted a Rapid City Council candidate forum on Thursday, April 16, at the Canyon Lake Senior Center. All 10 candidates participated.

title: 11 local Democrat candidates tout their priorities at joint event
description: BOX ELDER — Housing, education and an affordable cost of living for South Dakotans were among priority issues for state and local Democratic candidates who officially announced their candidacies during a press conference.
_full_text:
BOX ELDER — Housing, education and an affordable cost of living for South Dakotans were among priority issues for state and local Democratic candidates who officially announced their candidacies during a press conference.

Gubernatorial candidate Dan Ahlers and 10 candidates running for local positions and South Dakota State Legislature seats each briefly shared their campaign platforms during the press conference at The Box in Box Elder. The event was sponsored by the Pennington County Democratic Party.

 Group
Jeff Kingsbury with the Pennington County Democratic Party, back left, stands with candidates after a press conference officially launching their campaigns on Monday, April 27, at The Box in Box Elder. 

Ahlers is currently the executive director of the South Dakota Democratic Party. His experience includes time as a small-business owner, an administrator for the Dell Rapids Chamber of Commerce, a state legislator and a volunteer.

“I have been able to build teams and work collaboratively. I don’t care what party you are. I believe South Dakota has had one-party rule for too long,” Ahlers said, noting that the Legislature has been ruled by Republicans for 30 years, and the governor’s office has been occupied by Republicans for nearly 50 years.

Ahlers said long-standing successes that still benefit South Dakota, such as establishing the four-year medical school at the University of South Dakota and the South Dakota Retirement System (for 105,000 state employees), were created by Democrats.

“You have to ask yourself, are you better off today than you were just five or 10 years ago?” Ahlers said, noting South Dakota is facing struggles in areas such as healthcare and education.

Ahlers is the lone candidate in the Democratic primary and will advance to the November general election. On the Republican side, businessman Toby Doeden, state Speaker of the House Jon Hansen, U.S. Rep. Dusty Johnson and Gov. Larry Rhoden face off in the June 2 primary.

 Dan Ahlers
South Dakota Democratic gubernatorial candidate speaks during a press conference on Monday, April 27, at The Box in Box Elder. 

At the press conference, Ahlers also said he wants to find solutions to the cost of property taxes — and he believes the state should use its surplus funds to help the state’s residents. He said the state has programs that can give immediate property tax relief to citizens, but those programs need state funding and better promotion so people can access them and receive help. Among those programs, he noted, are relief programs administered by the South Dakota Department of Revenue for veterans, the disabled and the elderly.

He also said the Native community needs a leader who will recognize them as sovereign and work with their leadership to address problems.

“When everyone does well, we all do better. That’s the type of South Dakota that I’m talking about,” Ahlers said.

“Democrats will represent you. I’m asking you to give me a chance at four years to show you what real leadership that cares about people in this state can do for you,” Ahlers said.

City, county and Legislature candidates

In addition to Ahlers, candidates at the press conference were: Murray Lee, Ward 1, Rapid City Council; Valeriah Big Eagle, Ward 4, Rapid City Council; Timothy Williams, District 5, Pennington County Commission; Bret Swanson, House District 30; Jeff Shelton, Senate District 32; Christopher Baumgartner, Senate District 33; Kari Hall, House District 33; Michael Calabrese, Senate District 34; Ken Jeakins, House District 35; and Rep. Nicole Uhre-Balk, who is running for re-election in House District 32.

All of the Legislature candidates are the only Democrats running in their respective race and will advance to the November general election. The candidates below are listed in the order in which they spoke at the press conference.

Lee and Big Eagle are each running for City Council for the first time.

Lee, who grew up in Rapid City and is raising his family here, said he is running for council to represent all local citizens in Ward 1, not a particular political party or point of view. He emphasized his desire to have constructive conversations and listen to people of varying opinions about their concerns.

 Murray Lee
Rapid City City Council candidate Murray Lee speaks at the press conference. 

Affordable housing, affordability in general and smart approaches to growth topped his concerns, especially as the city and Ellsworth Air Force Base continue to grow, Lee said.

“We need to expand the tax burden so it doesn’t fall on the same homeowners again and again,” he said.

“What I want to bring to the council is the ability to put in the work, to listen, and to get things done and bring people together,” Lee said. “I’m very excited to be running.”

Lee faces Councilman Josh Biberdorf in the Ward 1 race.

Big Eagle said she brings “an educated, fresh perspective” to the council. She is the director of He Sapa Initiative for NDN Collective in Rapid City. She earned a baccalaureate in Sociology, a Master of Education, and her Doctor of Education in Education Leadership. Yet she said she struggled to find affordable housing when she moved to Rapid City in 2016. She ultimately became a homeowner by partnering with Habitat for Humanity.

 Valeriah Big Eagle
Rapid City City Council Ward 4 candidate Valeriah Big Eagle speaks at the press conference. 

“I have the lived experience to be able to navigate the challenging, and multi-faceted, complex issues in our community,” Big Eagle said.

In addition to advocating for affordability and housing, Big Eagle said she wants to see improvements in public safety, improved infrastructure and roads, safe parks and recreational opportunities in north Rapid City.

She wants to learn from community members and their experiences. “Let’s find the best option that would benefit for our whole community, not just to live, not just to survive, but to thrive,” Big Eagle said.

Big Eagle takes on Councilman John Roberts in Ward 4.

Williams said he will bring a “working class sensibility” to the Pennington County Commission. He is a veteran, an electrician by trade and a former factory worker.

 Tim Williams
Pennington County Board of Commissioners District 5 candidate Tim Williams speaks at the press conference. 

“I’ve seen everything from the bottom up, not from the top down,” he said. “The people I want to serve is the people of Pennington County, not just the rich. I want balance. I want the county to benefit everybody.”

Williams is running uncontested in the Democratic primary.

Swanson, of Hermosa, is a third-generation South Dakotan. He is an educator who taught in Rapid City and internationally, and who served on the board of the Rapid City Area Schools and Humane Society of the Black Hills. Swanson said his main issue is protecting the watershed and preventing corporations from destroying it.

He also emphasized the need for diversity of ideas and perspectives in the state Legislature.

“Our state is 50% non-Republican, but the Legislature’s 89% Republicans,” Swanson said. “We need to be more proactive in trying to get more balanced so alternative views are heard more often.

Republican Reps. Tim Goodwin and Trish Ladner currently hold the House seats in District 30 and are running for re-election. They're joined in the Republican primary by Stephen Saint. Two will advance.

Shelton introduced himself as an “archeologist, a homeowner and a neighbor,” all of which influence what he represents, he said. He lives in Rapid City’s Robbinsdale neighborhood and he has worked at locations statewide. His top issues are affordability, property tax relief, and transparency in the medical community to ensure patients get a clear picture of their medical costs.

Shelton also praised Senate Bill 135, which would protect residents from increased utility costs and utility shortages caused by data centers, and would clarify authority to regulate data centers.

“I think we can have responsible and measured growth in our community that doesn’t come at the expense of our natural resources and doesn’t come at the expense of our constituents,” Shelton said.

Republican Sen. Helene Duhamel is running for re-election in District 32 and faces challenger Karen McNeal in the Republican primary. One will advance.

Baumgartner said he is running to represent Rapid City, Black Hawk and Summerset. He said he decided to run for office because he believes people who care about issues need to fight for them.

“I believe government works best when it … listens to its people it represents and focuses on real problems, not political theater,” Baumgartner said. “I will be a voice of common sense, community and courage.”

His priority issues include affordability, housing costs, utilities, child care, and making sure communities are accessible to all.

“People with disabilities deserve a full participation in public life – our sidewalks and buildings, our services and our government. That’s not special favor, that’s just basic dignity, and we have work to do,” he said.

In District 33, Republican Sen. Curt Voight is seeking to retain his seat and faces David Johnson in the primary. One will advance.

Hall of Rapid City has a background in athletic training, education, and the nonprofit sector. Her primary issues are affordability, education, health care, the local work force and the economy.

She emphasized the need to protect Medicare and rural health care, and the importance of investing in teachers and administrators to strengthen education. She also believes in protecting the region’s clean water supply.

“I want to fight for practical solutions that help families stay here and get ahead. … I believe our communities are our greatest strength and when we invest in people, we all do better,” she said.

 Candidates
Pennington County Democrats hosted an event launching candidate campaigns on Monday, April 27, at The Box in Box Elder. 

Republican Reps. Phil Jensen and Curt Massie currently hold the District 33 House seats, and are joined in the primary by Jeff Marlette and Sandra Comer. Two will advance.

Calabrese said he is running because he believes voters deserve choices. He co-owns Black Hills Vinyl in Rapid City with his wife, Jennifer. He said he’s heard concerns from locals who are struggling to get ahead, and “there is a quiet sense (the Legislature) has stopped listening to Rapid City.”

He said his agenda is housing that working families can actually afford, keeping and strengthening health care, schools families are proud to send their kids to, and good jobs that will keep people in Rapid City.

“Candidates have to show up, listen and answer for what they do,” he said. “I’m willing to work with anyone, in any party, who is serious about delivering for this district.”

Republican Sen. Taffy Howard is running to retain her seat in District 34 and faces Jason Green in the primary.

Jeakins of Rapid City said his priority is investing in people through focusing on affordable and safe housing, public education that are accessible to everyone, public safety, and wages that allow people to afford housing, higher education and saving for the future.

“We have to have wages people can actually do things on,” saying inflation is outpacing wages and causing people to struggle.

“If you don’t have the people, nothing else is there. You can say AI can do this, AI can do that. Without the actual people who are there to get things done, you have nothing. So how do we take care of those people?” he said.

In District 35, Republican Reps. Tony Randolph and Tina Mulally termed out after the 2026 session.

Uhre-Balk said she believes strong schools are the foundation of strong communities, and she reviewed her successes during her first term in the legislature. Those include issues affecting education and the education funding formula, protecting victims of human trafficking, ensuring responsible oversight of mining to protect water, lowering costs where possible, and “trying to make sure state government is working for people.

“Voters, especially in District 32, have shown strong support for … leadership that brings different perspectives to the table, focuses on practical solutions and prioritizes results over partisanship,” she said. “Democracy is a team sport. If you believe in that kind of leadership, collaborative, accountable and getting things done, I respectfully ask for your support."

In District 32, Republican Rep. Steve Duffy is seeking re-election and is joined by Zachary Martin in the primary. Both will advance.

The municipal and primary election will be held on June 2, 2026.

title: Sharon Mayer
_full_text:
June 9, 1948 - April 24, 2026

Sharon Lucy (Jensen) Mayer, 77, of Rapid City, passed away on April 24, 2026 at Monument Health. A funeral will be held on April 29 at 10 am at Divine Lutheran Church in Black Hawk, SD. Visitation 1 hour before.

title: Candlewood Place homicide trial: Family testifies to the loss of 24-year-old McGarland Walker VI
description: A mother's cries for her son echoed in a Pennington County courtroom Tuesday, April 28, as 14 jurors listened to her recount the worse day of her life. On March 8, 2024, Cindy BoJack's son McGarland Walker VI was shot and killed inside Candlewood Place, an apartment building in Rapid City.
_full_text:
A mother's cries for her son echoed in a Pennington County courtroom Tuesday, April 28, as 14 jurors listened to her recount the worse day of her life. On March 8, 2024, Cindy BoJack's son McGarland Walker VI was shot and killed inside Candlewood Place, an apartment building in Rapid City. 

"He was my son, he was my everything, he was my best friend," BoJack cried. "... It was like my heart being ripped out."

It was the first day of trial for Luke Birch, 23, of Rapid City, who is charged with first-degree murder and, in the alternative, second-degree murder for Walker's death. Birch can only be convicted of one or the other, not both. 

"Angry words doesn't justify taking someone's life. Pushing and shoving doesn't justify taking someone's life," Senior Deputy State's Attorney Olivia Siglin said. "...Luke Birch didn't need to take McGarland Walker's life. He wanted to." 

However, one of Birch's attorneys, Joanna Lawler, argued Birch was defending himself from a person he no longer recognized. She said they were best friends before that day, but, "that bond was shattered by a violent and irrational change from Mack."

Walker, also known as Mack, was found dead inside Candlewood Place in west Rapid City near the front entrance with six bullet wounds after police responded to a call around 4:45 p.m. on March 8, 2024.

Siglin said during opening statements that morning started with two friends having an argument, with Walker eventually asking Birch if he could come over to his apartment at Candlewood Place. She said the evidence will show Walker didn't force his way into the building, but Birch let him inside. 

Lawler argued during the defense's portion of opening statements that Birch may have unlocked the door to speak with Walker and de-escalate their argument, but he had no intention of letting him inside. She claimed Walker pushed and shoved Birch and himself further inside, continuing to threaten Birch. 

Lawler said evidence will show Walker sent numerous threatening messages, promising on his children's lives that he will harm Birch. 

Siglin criticized the self-defense argument, highlighting Birch's attire before the shooting — a black hoodie, black jeans, black shoes and a black face covering up to his eyes. 

'Who goes into an argument with a friend wearing essentially a ski mask?" Siglin asked during opening statements.

She further argued that Birch never called 911 after the shooting to claim self-defense. 

Lawler said Walker left his blue Jeep parked at a curb, running, when he went to confront Birch. 

"He arrived for a fight and he arrived with a loaded gun," Lawler said. 

Walker carried a concealed handgun for work, Siglin said. She said the gun remained holstered on Walker's waistband with a loaded magazine but no bullet in the chamber after the shooting.  

Lawler said during Walker and Birch's argument, Birch put his hand on his own holster, warning Walker to leave. She said Walker told Birch, "I'll show you how to do that (explicative) for real,"  before Walker allegedly reached for his own gun. Birch, however, was able to pull out his gun faster. 

"When Mack reached for his weapon, the best friend was gone," Lawler said. "Replaced by a threat."

Siglin criticized Birch's actions, stating the evidence will show how Birch fled from the scene after the shooting to Wilderness Park, where he would allegedly dispose of the murder weapon and several clothing items.

Police body camera footage played in court showed Birch returning to the apartment complex, claiming he was out on a jog and denying owning a firearm. Birch was later taken into the police station for questioning, where Siglin said he continued to lie about his involvement through hours of interrogation. 

However, she said Birch eventually admitted his involvement to law enforcement by claiming self-defense. 

A best friend lost: Mother and wife testify

Serene Verhulst — her last name was Walker at the time of the shooting — was living her day like any other day on March 8, 2024. Verhulst said she and Walker were both working that day while BoJack watched their two-year-old daughter and 10-year-old son. 

She told the jury that Walker would usually get off of work before her and relieve his mother of the kids around 5 p.m. But when BoJack called Verhulst asking where Walker was, she said felt something was off. 

When Verhulst rushed home, she found police cars parked in front of her home. Later that day, Verhulst and BoJack were informed Walker was dead. 

BoJack testified Walker and Birch were close friends, and Birch had lived with her for some time before he moved to Candlewood Place with a girlfriend. She said Birch was struggling to find a place to live at the time and Walker provided a place to stay, but BoJack insisted on staying with her instead. BoJack said her son always helped his friends.

Verhulst said Walker and Birch would often hang out, even make music with one another. She said Walker had a passion for music, loved basketball and had an adventurous spirit. 

Lawler asked Verhulst if Walker had a gun and she said yes and that he carried it regularly. 

Neighbors and police testimonies

On Tuesday, the jury also heard testimony from residents inside Candlewood Place, all of whom testified to hearing two men arguing, followed by a volley of gunshots, then silence. 

One resident said he watched from his window a man dressed in all black running north towards Wilderness Park, before retrieving his own handgun and investigating the hallway. 

He said he found Walker laying on his back unresponsive and another resident calling 911. Together, they reported the shooting and he told the operator about the fleeing man. 

Afterward, he went back to his apartment, where he put on a bulletproof vest and pulled out his rifle, where he waited out in the hallway "to be a sentry," until law enforcement arrived.

He relinquished his guns to the police and spoke with them. His guns were later returned to him. 

Another resident testified to seeing a man wearing all black and a partial face covering rushing past with what looked like a handgun. He said he went into his apartment debating to call 911, until he heard the gunshots. 

The jury also heard police officers who responded to the scene testify and watched police body cam footage as they secured the crime scene and questioned Birch as he returned to the building. 

"I chose this area because this I thought this was a good safe place," Birch told the officers on scene.

Birch's trial continues at 9 a.m. on Wednesday, April 29.

title: Two Rapid City elementary schools put on secure status after man brandishes gun in neighborhood
description: General Beadle Elementary and Knollwood Elementary were both placed on secure status on Tuesday, April 28, after police received a report of a man brandishing a handgun in the 200 block of Knollwood Drive.
_full_text:
General Beadle Elementary and Knollwood Elementary were both placed on secure status on Tuesday, April 28, after police received a report of a man brandishing a handgun in the 200 block of Knollwood Drive. 

The report came into police at 1:15 p.m. The schools were placed on secure status at about 1:25 p.m. and taken off secure status at 1:30 p.m., according to a Rapid City Area Schools spokesperson.

"The reporting party stated they were concerned (the man) was about to shoot somebody," the department said in a press release at about 3:40 p.m.

The call generated a large law enforcement response and prompted the precautionary secure statuses at the two elementary schools. General Beadle Elementary and Knollwood Elementary are each about a mile from the 200 block of Knollwood Drive.

When police arrived, they found a man who began to run away. He was observed attempting to discard the firearm onto a nearby roof, police said. Officers caught and arrested the man, who they identified as 27-year-old Ziad Belhage of Rapid City. Belhage is held on no bond at the Pennington County Jail on suspicion of possession of a firearm with a prior felony drug conviction and receiving stolen property. 

According to federal court documents: In 2017, Belhage was convicted of a felony unauthorized ingestion of a controlled substance and felony failure to appear, according to court documents. Because of those charges, he is not legally allowed to possess a firearm.

In 2019, a federal judge in Rapid City sentenced Belhage to 18 months in prison and three years of supervised release for possession of a firearm by a previous offender. In 2022, Belhage was once again caught with a firearm. In that instance, that gun was manufactured in the Philippines and stolen. He was sentenced for the 2022 incident in 2023 to two years in prison and three years on supervised release for possession of a firearm and ammunition by a prohibited person.

Federal Bureau of Prisons records show Belhage was released on Feb. 6. 

The Rapid City Police Department said the gun found in the Tuesday incident was stolen from a February vehicle burglary in Rapid City.

title: After Sioux Falls officer shot, Pennington County, Rapid City law enforcement express support
description: Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller and Rapid City Police Chief Don Hedrick sent out a joint statement on Tuesday, April 28, expressing their support for the Sioux Falls Police Department and one of its officers who was shot and injured on Monday, April 27.
_full_text:
Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller and Rapid City Police Chief Don Hedrick sent out a joint statement on Tuesday, April 28, expressing their support for the Sioux Falls Police Department and one of its officers who was shot and injured on Monday, April 27.

"Right now, our thoughts and prayers are with the Sioux Falls Police Department and the officer seriously injured in yesterday’s shooting. We are thankful he is in stable condition," the agencies said. "This incident highlights the inherent danger of the work that we do. Our brave men and women know the risks they face every day. We are appreciative to live in a community and state that supports their law enforcement."

According to reporting from the Sioux Falls Argus Leader, officials with the Sioux Falls Police Department confirmed Monday afternoon, April 27, that two suspects are now in custody after the officer was shot at about 2 p.m. that day near the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds in Sioux Falls. 

 Sioux Falls shooting
Law enforcement on the scene of an officer-involved shooting on Monday, April 27, 2026, just south of the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds in Sioux Falls.  

The officer was last reported to be in stable condition at the hospital with multiple injuries. The two suspects have not been publicly named. Minnehaha Sheriff's Capt. Josh Phillips said both suspects, a man and a woman, were on parole holds. Sioux Falls Police Department and Minnehaha County Sherriff's officials emphasized what they characterized as a need to stronger parole laws. The Journal reached out to the Sioux Falls Police Department to ask the names of the suspects and what charges they were paroled on. 

Mueller and Hedrick also took the incident as an opportunity to push for parole reform.

"This officer-involved shooting also highlights the value of addressing violent re-offending parolees in our communities," Hedrick and Mueller said. "This has been a topic the Sheriff, Chief  and several partners across the state have been active and vocal on over the past few years. Although we are making some progress, incidents like this highlight the importance of making immediate changes at the state level necessary to help us continue to keep our community and state safe."

The law enforcement leaders said they hope to release statistics later this week on the work of the Parolee Accountability Task Force, which the agencies launched on Wednesday, April 15.

title: SD Supreme Court upholds conviction of man who threatened Trump ahead of Rushmore appearance
description: The South Dakota Supreme Court has affirmed the conviction and sentence of a man who made threats against President Donald Trump ahead of his appearance at the 2020 Mount Rushmore National Memorial Independence Day fireworks celebration.
_full_text:
The South Dakota Supreme Court has affirmed the conviction and sentence of a man who made threats against President Donald Trump ahead of his appearance at the 2020 Mount Rushmore National Memorial Independence Day fireworks celebration.

The South Dakota Attorney General's Office announced the decision on Tuesday, April 28, a week after the Supreme Court heard oral arguments. Lucian Celestine was sentenced to two-and-a-half years in prison for attempted threat of felony terrorism in March 2025 in Pennington County Court.

“Threats against the president of the United States and public officials are taken seriously,” Attorney General Marty Jackley said in a release. “We will continue to prosecute individuals who seek to harm or intimidate public officials and their families.”

 Trump talks at Rushmore 2020
President Trump spoke in 2020 at Mount Rushmore ahead of the fireworks display. 

Per the AG's office: Court records show Celestine first contacted the Federal Bureau of Investigation in September 2019, claiming he was hearing voices instructing him to kill Trump. In June 2020, Celestine attempted to purchase a sniper-style rifle through an online firearms marketplace and requested a scope capable of shooting up to 600 yards along with ammunition. The seller, a Brookings police officer acting as a private citizen, became concerned about Celestine’s behavior and reported the interaction to the South Dakota Division of Criminal Investigation.

Law enforcement later contacted Celestine as authorities prepared for Trump’s visit to Mount Rushmore National Memorial for the 2020 Independence Day fireworks celebration, the AG's office said. During the encounter, Celestine acknowledged he had contacted the FBI and admitted he had recently obtained a rifle. Officers located a Browning X-Bolt .300 Winchester Magnum rifle, ammunition and targets in the trunk of his vehicle.

After being taken to the Rapid City Public Safety Building and advised of his rights, Celestine told investigators that voices in his head instructed him to kill the president, the AG's office said. He described a plan to go to Mount Rushmore and position himself above the monument. He was subsequently arrested and indicted for threat of felony terrorism.

Celestine was initially indicted for threat of felony terrorism, a Class 5 felony with a maximum sentence of five years and a $10,000 fine, according to previous Journal reporting.

According to court records, a Pennington County jury heard the case July 5-6, 2023, and couldn't reach a decision following deliberations, leading to a mistrial.

From there, Celestine tried to represent himself but was denied on mental competency grounds by then-Pennington County Seventh Circuit Court Judge Robert Gusinsky. A new trial was ordered, but at a Nov. 14, 2024, hearing Celestine entered an Alford plea, which lets the defendant maintain their innocence while admitting the prosecution has enough evidence to convict at trial.

Celestine then filed a motion to remove his guilty plea and a hearing was held on Dec. 10, 2024. Gusinsky dismissed the motion, ruling "a knowing and voluntary plea took place," according to a description of the case in Supreme Court filings.

 Keystone | Mount Rushmore | Trump 2020
Members of the South Dakota National Guard stand behind riot shields as they await orders while lined up on Highway 16 near Keystone, S.D., where protesters blocked the road to stop access to the state visit by President Donald J. Trump and the fireworks display at Mount Rushmore National Memorial.  

Celestine later appealed his conviction and sentence to the state Supreme Court. His attorney, Matthew Mirabella with the South Dakota Office of Indigent Legal Services, argued the circuit court erred in denying the motion to withdraw the guilty plea. During written and oral arguments, the attorney argued a new jury trial should be ordered.

"Celestine demanded a jury trial at his initial appearance, fought his case before a jury that would not convict him three years later, and took only a week to request withdrawal for leave to proceed to a second jury trial," Mirabella argued in Supreme Court filings, later writing, "Celestine's undisputed mental illness casts significant doubt as to the knowing and voluntary nature of the plea, thus weighing strongly in favor of withdrawal."

Mirabella also argued his client presented a "credible and compelling assertion of innocence" that weighed strongly in favor of him being allowed to withdraw his plea. 

Mirabella pointed to the trial, which he argued had "an abundance of evidence" that established Celestine did not have the specific intent required for a conviction. "And that defense was compelling enough to result in a hung jury," Mirabella wrote. 

The Attorney General’s Office represented the state at the appeal. The state argued the circuit court's denial of the motion to withdraw was proper because Celestine didn't have a "fair and just" reason to withdraw his plea.

"Celestine's plea was knowing, voluntary, and not the product of any misapprehension of fact or coercion," the state argued in Supreme Court filings.

The South Dakota Supreme Court affirmed the circuit court’s judgment. Gusinsky, now a state Supreme Court justice, did not hear or rule on the case. Second Circuit Judge Jon Sogn sat in his place.

 Celestine appeal

title: Natural Grocers in Rapid City set to open in summer 2026
description: Natural Grocers in Rapid City is starting to take shape, with the company expecting to open sometime this summer.
_full_text:
Natural Grocers in Rapid City is starting to take shape, with the company expecting to open sometime this summer.

The grocery store will be housed in the former Albertson's building at 855 Omaha St. (located at the corner of Omaha Street and Mount Rushmore Road). The 47,000-square-foot building will also house Rapid City's second Jersey Mike's location, Juna Sleep Systems and a Monument Health clinic.

Dream Design International's Hani Shafai confirmed the businesses on March 6 and said the grocery store will occupy roughly 16,000 square feet. Dream Design in the property's developer. 

 Natural Grocers
Construction workers on site of the Natural Grocery building on Omaha Street on Tuesday, April 28. The location is set to open sometime in summer 2026. 

On Tuesday, April 28, the company announced it will host a hiring event in Rapid City from 10 a.m. - 4 p.m. on Tuesday, May 5, followed by a community meet-and-greet on Monday, May 11, from 4 - 6 p.m. at the Best Western Ramkota on North LaCrosse Street in Rapid City. The event will include a presentation and a question-and-answer session. Those interested in registering for the hiring event can do so on indeed.com

According to Natural Groceries, employees can expect to earn $17-$23 an hour with the assistant store manager making up to $60,000 annually. Headquartered in Lakewood, Colorado, Natural Grocers has 169 stores in 21 states.

"It's a critical intersection in our downtown and in Rapid City because most of our tourist traffic comes through there, and we hope that will enhance the gateway to Rapid City at that corner," Shafai said in March. "It fits very well with the civic center (The Monument) and the business community downtown."

title: 'Amazing': Japanese outreach coordinator embraced by Black Hills locals
description: Over the past two years, amid the headlines of strife, war and government shutdowns, a nice story has taken place in Spearfish and other Black Hills pockets.
_full_text:
Over the past two years, amid the headlines of strife, war and government shutdowns, a nice story has taken place in Spearfish and other Black Hills pockets.

It stars Yukari Tadano, a bespectacled Japanese woman with a soft smile and voice. Nearly two years ago, Tadano arrived at Black Hills State University to begin a two-year program as the Japanese Outreach Initiative coordinator through the Japan Foundation. The goal was to teach Black Hills locals about Japanese culture and to tell the story of how she was severely affected by the 2011 earthquake in her home of Fukushima.

Excited? Sure. Nervous? Oh, big time.

While Black Hills State University and Kaitlin Palmer, BHSU’s coordinator of the International Relations and Global Engagement Office, were thrilled to host her, Tadano couldn’t shake some of the initial angst.

 042826-nws-bhsu-p24.JPG
Yukari Tadano, middle, delivers a presentation at Black Hills State's Joy Center on Saturday, April 25.  

Everyone was a stranger. Spearfish’s Asian population, according to a 2020 U.S. Census Bureau report, is 1.13%. The landlocked Black Hills represent a stark contrast to Japan’s ocean-bordered landscape.

But over the next two years, Spearfish and the Black Hills have welcomed and embraced Tadano.

“Amazing to have watched the journey,” Palmer said.

 042826-nws-bhsu-p21.JPG
Renowned filmmaker and photographer Jake Price speaks at Black Hills State's Joy Center on Saturday, April 25.  

Tadano was hosted for dinner parties and holidays. Adults and kids loved the workshops on Japanese culture and munching on sweet Japanese candies. Tadano began taking trips to South Dakota Mines for language and culture courses.

Tadano was even a guest speaker at the Literary Council of the Black Hills, a program where community members help non-English speakers learn how to read, speak and write English.

“The audiences normally listen to me very warmly and those audiences make me feel more comfortable to give a speech,” Tadano said.

This was particularly evident at a Japanese Culture event on Saturday, April 25, that marked the conclusion of her program.

 BRIGHT SIDE LOGO.jpg

Dozens packed inside BHSU’s Joy Center, including students, BHSU faculty and Spearfish Rotary Club members, who helped fund it.

In the first half, they listened closely as Tadano offered gratitude for the program and how the Fukushima earthquake, tsunami and nuclear accident upended her childhood, but she didn’t want it to define the community forever.

“Beautiful ocean, mountains,” noted Tadano, who spent years building the community through disaster relief efforts. “People who live in those places who have the power to rebuild their communities.”

Tadano then introduced Jake Price, who’s a renowned filmmaker and photographer, who recently documented how some communities in Fukushima are regenerating themselves 15 years after the disaster struck.

After a Q&A session with Price, attendees made Daiyamaki, a giant sushi roll — affectionately referred to as a sushi snake — that stretched well over 10 feet long. First, they combined sheets of seaweed, then packed a bed of rice, added veggies, and rolled it all together. When completed, it looked like, you know, a giant sushi snake.

 042826-nws-bhsu-p2.JPG
Attendees hold the "sushi snake" they made at Black Hills State's Joy Center on Saturday, April 25.  

Following that, Tadano smiled as people sampled a buffet of traditional Japanese cuisine: Pickled eggplant, seaweed covered in peanuts, butter monaka and washed it down with sake, an alcoholic beverage consisting of fermented rice.

“Today, watching her give her presentation and kind of like, really come out of her shell and be able to really connect this community and connect to the community, was really cool for me to watch,” Palmer said. “As a kid I would have loved something like this.”

 042826-nws-bhsu-p16.JPG
Yukari Tadano, left, speaks with an attendee at a Japanese culture appreciation event at Black Hills State's Joy Center on Saturday, April 25.  

A few feet away from Palmer, as the event was winding down, a few locals gathered to take a group photo with Tadano.

And instead of saying “cheese!” they all went, “Sushi snake!”

Then they hugged, smiled and said goodbye.

“I’ll miss the communities here,” Tadano said.

 PHOTOS: Black Hills State hosts Japanese culture appreciation event
Black Hills State hosted a Japanese culture appreciation event on Saturday, April 25. Attendees made sushi, ate traditional Japanese cuisine and listened to renowned documentarian Jake Price tell the moving story of how a Fukushima community regenerated itself 15 years after it became a ghost town following the Great East Japan Earthquake in 2011.
