title: Rapid City Area Schools installing weapons detections systems at Central, Alternative Academy
description: Rapid City Area Schools announced on Monday, April 27, that it will be implementing weapons detection systems in two of its three high schools — Rapid City Central High School and Rapid City Alternative Academy, starting on May 4.
_full_text:
Rapid City Area Schools announced on Monday, April 27, that it will be implementing weapons detection systems in two of its three high schools — Rapid City Central High School and Rapid City Alternative Academy, starting on May 4.

The district said it will utilize OpenGate weapons detection systems, designed for high-traffic environments such as schools. The systems allow students to walk through at a natural pace without stopping or significantly altering their normal arrival routine, according to the district.

"The technology is discreet and efficient, helping maintain a steady flow of students while supporting a positive school climate," the district said. "If the system alerts, trained staff will respond calmly and privately. The system is preventive, not punitive, and does not track students or store personal data."

“This is one part of our broader approach to school safety,” said Jami Jo Thompson, superintendent of Rapid City Area Schools. “Our goal is to ensure every student feels safe, supported, and ready to learn each day.”

In its announcement, the district said the implementation is in partnership with the Rapid City Police Department. The systems were acquired through a federal COPS (Community Oriented Policing Services) grant, secured through collaboration between RCAS and RCPD.

"This partnership reflects a shared commitment to proactive, community-based school safety efforts," the district said. 

Stevens will not have a weapons detection system at this time. The district said because of the building's layout and number of entry points, adjustments are needed before implementation. Stevens High School remains part of the district’s long-term plan, and systems will be added following necessary construction and remodeling to support a smooth rollout.

The district said staff training and preparation are underway to ensure a smooth implementation.

Families with questions are encouraged to contact their school’s main office for more information.

title: Rapid City teen arrested for drinking and driving incident
description: A Rapid City 19-year-old was arrested Sunday, April 26, after running a red light while drinking and driving.
_full_text:
A Rapid City 19-year-old was arrested Sunday, April 26, after running a red light while drinking and driving. 

The man was placed under arrest on suspicion of DUI, open container in a motor vehicle, reckless driving, underage consumption of an alcoholic beverage, no proof of insurance and driving without a valid license.

At around 3:25 p.m. Sunday, police responded to a report of a disturbance inside a passenger car. While attempting to locate the car, police found it speeding southbound on Mountain View Road where it ran a red light. 

A traffic stop was initiated where the car was pulled over to a parking lot at 1029 Jackson Boulevard. The occupants of the car were ordered out of the car, where the 19-year-old man was arrested. 

Police smelled a strong odor of alcohol coming from the man and found an open container inside the car. 

The 19-year-old was not on the Pennington County Jail roster as of Monday afternoon, April 27.

title: Rep. Mary Fitzgerald to receive SDNA open government award
description: Rep. Mary J. Fitzgerald’s work to improve South Dakota’s open government laws has earned her recognition as the 2026 recipient of the South Dakota NewsMedia Association’s “Eagle Award.”
_full_text:
Rep. Mary J. Fitzgerald’s work to improve South Dakota’s open government laws has earned her recognition as the 2026 recipient of the South Dakota NewsMedia Association’s “Eagle Award.”

The award is given by SDNA to an individual or group demonstrating outstanding efforts to protect and enhance open government and the public’s right to know.

 Mary Fitzgerald mug
Mary Fitzgerald 

Fitzgerald, a Spearfish Republican representing District 31, has sponsored various bills during her time in the South Dakota Legislature that have strengthened open government. This year, she sponsored a bill that helped clarify language in the open records laws to mirror similar language in the state’s open meetings laws. It becomes law on July 1.

In 2025, she sponsored a bill that requires governing boards to annually review the open meetings laws and a bill that clarified the use of teleconferencing in the open meetings laws. Both bills became law.

 Mary J. Fitzgerald
Rep. Mary J. Fitzgerald, R-31, Spearfish, stands and applauds during the State of the State Address on Tuesday, Jan. 13, at the State Capitol in Pierre. 

“Rep. Fitzgerald has been a true advocate for open government issues during her time in the Legislature,” SDNA Executive Director David Bordewyk said. “She is always willing to work for solutions that are reasonable, balanced and ultimately protect the public’s right to know. We are appreciative of her commitment to these issues and are proud to honor her with the SDNA Eagle Award.”

Fitzgerald is completing her third term as a state representative. She is a member of the House Judiciary Committee and the House Local Government Committee.

Fitzgerald will receive the Eagle Award during the association’s annual convention on May 1 in Deadwood.

SDNA started the Eagle Award in 2002 to honor those who support and advocate for the First Amendment and open government issues in South Dakota. The first recipients were the 2002 state Supreme Court justices for their work to bring cameras into their courtroom proceedings. Among other recipients over the years have been the late journalist and educator Chuck Baldwin (2024), Rep. Tim Reed (2022), Sen. Arthur Rusch (2019), Gov. Dennis Daugaard (2018), Sioux Falls resident Gordon Heber (2010) and Attorney General Larry Long (2004).

South Dakota NewsMedia Association, based in Brookings, represents the state’s newspapers and digital news outlets.

title: Pennington County seeks Planning Commission members
description: The Pennington County Board of Commissioners will be accepting applications to serve on the Pennington County Planning Commission.
_full_text:
The Pennington County Board of Commissioners will be accepting applications to serve on the Pennington County Planning Commission.

Currently, there are two positions open for a three-year term. All residents of Pennington County are eligible to serve. Individuals who have backgrounds in planning, zoning, development, construction, real estate or other related fields are encouraged to apply.

The Pennington County Planning Commission is a seven-member board whose members are appointed by the Pennington County Board of Commissioners. The job of the Planning Commission is to plan the long-range growth of Pennington County and review development requests in the unincorporated areas of the county. Regular meetings of the County Planning Commission are held on the second and fourth Mondays of each month at 9:00 a.m. Board members are reimbursed for mileage and are paid $50.00 per day, per diem for attendance at official meetings and functions.

Those who want to be considered for an appointment are asked to submit a citizen interest form describing their qualifications and background to the Pennington County Board of Commissioners. Please include your agency or community affiliation (if applicable), your particular interests as related to a board of this kind, and what you can contribute to such a board. You may also submit supporting information such as a resume. Citizen interest forms can be found on the County website at https://pennco.rja.revize.com/forms/13164.

All citizen interest forms must be received in the Commission Office located in the County Administration Building at 900 Concourse Drive, Rapid City, SD by 4:00 p.m., Tuesday, May 12th, 2026 in order to be considered.

Public interviews are scheduled to be conducted on Tuesday May 19th during the Board of Commissioners regular meeting. The appointments may be at that or any later Board of Commissioners Meeting. The applicants appointed by the board will start with their first official Planning Commission meeting to be held on Monday, July 13, 2026.

For more information, contact Planning Director Brittney Hand at (605) 394-2186, ext. 1408.

title: Rhoden appoints Black Hawk man Fourth Circuit judge
description: Gov. Larry Rhoden announced the appointment of Judge Chad Callahan as a circuit judge in the Fourth Circuit, a vacancy created by the retirement of Judge Mike Day.
_full_text:
Gov. Larry Rhoden announced the appointment of Judge Chad Callahan as a circuit judge in the Fourth Circuit, a vacancy created by the retirement of Judge Mike Day.

“Judge Callahan is a man of integrity who communicates clearly, calmly, and professionally,” Rhoden said in a release. “I have no doubt that he will uphold the rule of law, show care to everyone in his courtroom, and keep our state strong, safe, and free.”

 042726-nws-judge-p1.jpg
Chad Callahan 

Callahan grew up in Johnstown, Pennsylvania. He graduated from the University of Pittsburgh-Johnstown in 1994 with a bachelor’s degree in political science and graduated from Ohio Northern University in 1998 with a juris doctorate. Callahan began his career in private practice and later served as a public defender in Pennington County. From 2013-2017, he served as an assistant attorney general under Attorney General Marty Jackley. He currently serves as a Magistrate Judge in the Fourth Circuit.

“I am truly honored and grateful that Gov. Rhoden has chosen me. I will do my best to continue to earn his faith in me,” Callahan said in the release. “I’d also like to acknowledge the Judge that I will replace, Michael Day, who has served the 4th Circuit as an attorney since 1983 and as a Circuit Judge for the last 13 years. He will be greatly missed as he begins his well-earned retirement.”

Callahan and his wife live in Black Hawk, SD. They have three children.

title: PIT Count for 2026 shows 18.7% decline in reported of homelessness in Rapid City compared to 2025
description: The 2026 state-wide Point in Time (PIT) Homeless Count , conducted by South Dakota Housing's Housing for the Homeless Consortium, reported a total of 343 homeless people in Rapid City, an 18.7% decrease from last year's numbers.
_full_text:
The 2026 state-wide Point in Time (PIT) Homeless Count, conducted by South Dakota Housing's Housing for the Homeless Consortium, reported a total of 343 homeless people in Rapid City, an 18.7% decrease from last year's numbers.

"(If) Rapid City wasn't doing what we're doing, those numbers would be a lot worse. (It's) because of the teamwork and cooperation and efforts from various agencies or nonprofits. It's good to see that we're trending in the right direction," Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun told the Journal.

 Homeless Survey
Volunteers of America conducts a homeless survey in January 2023 outside the Volunteers of America office building in Rapid City. 

This year's one-day PIT count for the state found a total of 1,154 people were experiencing homelessness. Of those counted across the state, 675 men and boys were identified, compared to 479 women and girls. In total, 22% of those surveyed were unsheltered, a decrease of 25% compared to last year's data.

Additionally, 26 veterans were homeless, the lowest total recorded since the standardization of PIT counts in 2009.

For 2026, Sioux Falls had a total PIT count of 603, a 3.8% decrease from 2025's 625.

According to the report, the PIT count accounts for those staying in public and private shelters, including domestic violence shelters, transitional housing or motels paid for by an agency and unsheltered individuals who are staying in vehicles or on the streets.

"I'm pleased to see the trend on the PIT count. It does not tell the full story. Obviously, if you want a more accurate picture, come back here in the summertime for a week, you probably would get a better picture," Salamun said. "It's good to see the trend going down. I don't think that's by accident."

 Jason Salamun
Rapid City Mayor Jason Salamun at a press conference in March 2026.  

Salamun attributes the decrease in local partnerships to address homelessness in the city, naming the county's Care Campus, Journey On, Cornerstone Rescue Mission, Great Plains Tribal Health, Volunteers of America, OneHeart and more. 

Salamun said through the city and county's recent efforts to request and utilize both state and municipal opioid settlement funds, he hopes to further tackle the issue of substance addiction.

A look at trends of homelessness from PIT count data for Rapid City: From 2022 (458) to 2023 (499), there was an increase of 8.95%, from 2023 (499) to 2024 (428), a decrease of 14.2% and from 2024 (428) to 2025 (422), a decrease of 1.4%.

The PIT Count data for Rapid City only goes back as far as 2021, which only provides data on homeless people who were sheltered. 

Journey On: There's more to the numbers

Journey On Executive Director Stephanie Kor said the numbers don't paint the full picture of homelessness in Rapid City.

"I think (PIT) count numbers are really important to have and to show statistical trends, but I think it's not easy to draw specific conclusions about the increase or reduction of homelessness based on just those numbers," Kor said.

 Glow Walk
Attendees participate in the Glow Walk for youth homelessness awareness on Thursday, Nov. 7, 2024, in downtown Rapid City. The event is held during National Youth Hope Month. Kia Overturf with Youth and Family Services and co-chair of the Youth Task Force explained HOPE stands for homeless outreach, prevention and education. 

Journey On is a Native American-led nonprofit organization focused on community street outreach, case management and resources for at-risk individuals experiencing substance abuse, mental or behavioral health crises, crime victimization, and chronic or potential homelessness.

Journey On is one of the participants in the PIT count, with teams surveying all day and training other volunteers on how to conduct the study.

Kor said factors for the survey can change with the one-day timeframe, mentioning it's conducted in the winter month of January (where weather can play a factor), along with potential variances with the number of volunteer service providers participating in the count and shelter locations participating each year. The PIT count is done in the month of January each year.

"One of the things that the PIT Count measures is what we call literally homeless. It doesn't account for people who are doubled up living in their vehicles, couch surfing, things like that," Kor said.

According to the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, an individual is considered chronically homeless if they have been homeless for at least 12 months continuously or on four separate occasions in the past three years, totaling at least 12 months, with each break in homelessness lasting at least seven consecutive nights.

In Rapid City in 2025, 90 people were reported to be experiencing chronic homelessness, compared to 53 in 2026, a 41% decrease.

Despite this survey having some drawbacks, Kor said it's still one of the reports organizations look at when assessing homelessness. 

"I think we're really lucky to have a community where our providers work together. Over the last few years, we have seen an increase in collaboration amongst community partners. I think that's maybe why we see when we interact with fewer people on the day of the PIT count, it's because those people are actively engaged in those resources," Kor said.

title: Gerald “Jerry” Smith

title: Sonja Love
description: October 3, 1976 - April 27, 2026
_full_text:
October 3, 1976 - April 27, 2026

Sonja Love, 49, of Rapid City, passed away surrounded by her family after a courageous battle with cancer.

A celebration of her life will be 10 a.m. Saturday, May 2, 2026 at Fountain Springs Church 2100 North Plaza Dr. Rapid City, SD 57702. Visitation will begin one hour prior to the service at the church. Online condolences can be left at www.osheimschmidt.com.

She was born on October 3, 1976, in Rapid City, to Diane and Shelby Burrus Sr. Sonja was a devoted wife, an extraordinary mother, a beloved daughter, sister, and a trusted nurse whose care extended far beyond her profession. She had a rare gift, she made people feel seen. Whether speaking with a lifelong friend or someone she had just met, Sonja created connection. She was often the last one at a gathering, taking the time to truly know people.

Her greatest pride and deepest joy was her family. As a wife and mother, Sonja gave everything she had, quietly, selflessly, and without expectation of recognition. She built a home filled with love, strength, and unwavering support. She raised her daughters to be strong, to know their worth, and to stand up for themselves, lessons that will carry forward as part of her lasting legacy.

Sonja was a nurse by profession, but a caregiver by nature. She was confident in her abilities and deeply committed to helping others, yet those closest to her knew that her greatest passion was caring for her family. She did countless unseen things every day, the small, steady acts of love that define a life well lived.

She faced her illness with remarkable strength. Even in her most difficult days, she chose hope. Even in pain, she continued to care about others—asking about their lives, offering empathy, and lifting those around her. Her fight was not loud, but it was powerful. She showed what true strength looks like: resilience, courage, and the decision to keep going, even when it was hard.

Sonja is survived by her loving husband, Jason; their daughters, Gabbie and Finley, who were the center of her world; her parents, Diane and Shelby Burrus Sr.; and her brother, Shelby Burrus Jr. She is also survived by extended family and many friends who loved her deeply. She leaves behind a legacy of love, strength, and connection. Those who knew her will remember her warmth, her ability to bring people together, and the way she made others feel valued and cared for. Her influence lives on in her daughters, in her family, and in the many lives she touched.

Though her time here feels far too short, Sonja often shared that she had lived a blessed life—one filled with the love of her family and the connections she cherished most.

Her family finds comfort in knowing she is at peace, free from pain, and that her love remains present in their lives every day.

title: Beth Carter
description: August 20, 1932 - April 23, 2026
_full_text:
August 20, 1932 - April 23, 2026

Beth Carter, age 93, formerly of Bryant, SD, passed away on Thursday, April 23, 2026, at the Good Samaritan Center in De Smet, SD.

Visitation will be held at Kuhler Funeral Home, Huron, Saturday, May 2, at 9:00 AM with funeral service following at 10:00 AM, then burial at Sansarc Cemetery, rural Hayes.

Beth Joan Walker was the third of five children born to Henry and Anna (Fosheim) Walker on August 20, 1932, at Sansarc, SD. She began her education at Sansarc's one-room schoolhouse. The family moved to Bryant in 1941, where Beth finished her education, graduating in 1950 from Bryant High School. She graduated from Huron College with her teacher's degree; in 1979 she went back to school, earned a degree and worked as a dental lab technician.

In 1952, Beth was united in marriage to Tellef Carter. They moved to Everett, Washington and had five children before moving back to South Dakota in 1975.

Beth is survived by her five children, Terry Carter of Rapid City, Mark Carter (Evelyn) of Tacoma, Wash, Julie Carter of Black Hawk, Kathy Haarstad (Tony) of Iroquois, and Michael Carter (Janet) of Rapid City; one sister, Lily Lund of Madison; one sister-in-law, Lucy Walker of Syracuse, New York; eight grandchildren; six great-grandchildren; and numerous nieces and nephews.

Beth joins in Heaven her parents; one infant sister, Mary Anna; one sister, Margaret and brother-in-law, Pete; one brother, Paul; one brother-in-law, Floyd; three grandsons, Jared, Ryan and Jonathon and their grandfather, Tellef Carter.

www.kuhlerfuneralhome.com

title: Sharyl Glader
_full_text:
May 22, 1949 - April 26, 2026

RAPID CITY - Sharyl Glader, 76, died Sunday, April 26, 2026, at her residence.

Her funeral services are being scheduled with Osheim & Schmidt Funeral Home.

Sharyl Ann Glader, 76, Rapid City, entered into the presence of her Lord and Savior Jesus Christ on Sunday, April 26, 2026, at her home.

Sharyl was born May 22, 1949, in Lincoln, Neb., to Leonard and Margaret Walla, the second born of four children. She graduated from Rapid City High School in 1968. She attended SDSM&T, where she was a cheerleader and first runner-up Miss SD Universe. She transferred to Black Hills State College and graduated in 1972 with a double major in Physical Education and Speech as well as a BS of Education. She began her teaching career in Pierre, S.D., teaching Junior High PE and coached track and cheerleading. While in Pierre, she met Steven W. Glader, and the two were married in Rapid City on July 13, 1974.

Upon returning to Rapid City, she taught PE from 1975 until her retirement in 2010, taking a brief pause to raise her children. In 1998, she was awarded as the South Dakota PE Teacher of the Year.

After retirement, Sharyl was an avid bowler, hiker, biker, and even competed in several half marathons. She was part of Prima's Adult Tap group and was planning on performing in their spring recital yet again this year. She loved her Bible study group, her bunco girls, her happy hour gatherings, and, of course, her Friday pontoon days in the summer with her friends.

Above all else, Sharyl was a devoted mother to her three children, Tate, Brant, and Lindsey, and never missed an opportunity to support them in their activities. She loved fiercely, served selflessly, and was the cornerstone of her family. She treasured holiday traditions and created lasting memories for those she loved. Her deep faith in Jesus Christ guided her life and inspired her service to her family, friends, and her church community at Rimrock Evangelical Free Church.

She is survived by her three children, sons, Tate (Demetria) Glader and Brant (Mika) Glader both of Rapid City; daughter, Lindsey (Shay) Lundvall of Gillette, Wyo.; sister, Kathy (Kevin) Leui; brothers, Roger (Margaret) Walla and Chris (Diana) Walla; six grandchildren, Paige, Olyn, Eli, Lily, Skylin, and Breckin; and many friends whom she loved dearly. She was preceded in death by her husband, Steven; and both of her parents, Leonard and Margaret; as well as her mother and father-in-law, Sidney and Delores.

A memorial service celebrating Sharyl's life will be at 10:30 a.m. Saturday, May 2, at Rimrock Evangelical Free Church on 12200 HWY 44 West with Pastor Nic Ewing delivering the message. There will be a reception at the church immediately following the service.

A memorial fund in memory of Sharyl is being established at Rimrock Evangelical Free Church.

An online guestbook may be signed at www.osheimschmidt.com

title: Talkin' trash: Rapid City waste collection changes to take effect May 1
description: Rapid City trash changes are fast-approaching.
_full_text:
Rapid City trash changes are fast-approaching. 

Starting Friday, May 1, the city will no longer pick up yard waste for free, and starting on May 4, recycling pickup will transition to an every-other-week schedule, rather than weekly pickup.

Customers will continue to receive service on their regular collection day with recycling collections every other week. Trash pickup days, on a weekly basis, will remain the same. 

The changes come after the Rapid City Council last year approved a resolution — with a tie broken by Mayor Jason Salamun — to increase waste service fees, raising the 65-gallon trash can rate from $28.83 to $32.28 starting Jan. 1, with plans to revisit the issue in a year.

 Bins
A Rapid City Solid Waste Division truck contains tan yard waste bins, blue recycling bins and black trash bins.  

The changes include the $3.45 fee increase, schedule changes, the yard waste reduction and limiting free landfill dumps from 12 to 6 annually and increasing landfill tipping fees by $7 per ton.

The city's Solid Waste Division is an enterprise fund, meaning it operates as a business underneath the city and doesn't receive tax dollars to support it. 

 Cody the can technician
Cody Garnica, can technician with the Rapid City Solid Waste Division, unloads a yard waste container on Baldwin Street on Wednesday, April 29. Starting Friday, May 1, the city will no longer collect yard waste in brown paper bags at the curb.  

Ria Hannon, solid waste outreach coordinator, said at a Wednesday, April 29, demonstration of the new yard collection bins, "Last year, we had to look at ... what does the future look like? How do we move forward, and so we looked at a few different options. This was one of the options that was voted for."

Recycling collection schedule:

Monday/Wednesday/Friday routes: Recycling will be collected the weeks of May 4 and May 18.
Tuesday/Thursday routes: Recycling will be collected the weeks of May 11 and May 25.
Recycling will then continue on an every-other-week schedule.

Residents are encouraged to stay informed by subscribing to Solid Waste email and text reminders, and by following along on social media for updates during this transition.

If residents would like a larger recycling can due to the change or wish to order a yard waste bin, contact rcrecycles@rcgov.org or 605-355-3496.

Yard waste services go under subscription model 

Beginning Friday, May 1, the city will no longer collect yard waste placed in brown paper bags at the curb. Yard waste collection will only be provided through a new subscription-based container service at the cost of $15 plus applicable tax. A deposit on the container is no longer required. Previously, the city was going to charge a $100 deposit for the bins. The city ordered 7,000 of the bins, Hannon said, with a few hundred people signed up so far. 

"We're off to a good start," Hannon said.

 Bin address
Cody Garnica, can technician with the Rapid City Solid Waste Division, writes down an address on a new yard waste container on Baldwin Street on Wednesday, April 29. Starting Friday, May 1, the city will no longer collect yard waste in brown paper bags at the curb.  

Containers will be delivered to those who sign up at the end of April. Yard waste can be placed loose in the container — material does not need to be placed in a brown paper bag.

The new seasonal yard waste subscription service will be available to residents beginning this Friday. May 1, going through Nov. 30. The service is optional, and residents can sign up anytime, but enrollment constitutes commitment through November. Curbside yard waste service will begin Monday, May 4.

 Yard waste bin rules
A sticker on the top of the new yard waste bins shows what can and can't go in the bins. 

"Once you sign up, you are committed to the end of the season. So you can sign up in June, you can sign up in July, but at that point, you will be staying until the end of November. You cannot only sign up for two months, like, for example, you can't sign up for just June and just July," Hannon said.

 Hannon
Ria Hannon, city solid waste outreach coordinator, speaks with media about Rapid City trash changes on Wednesday, April 29, on Baldwin Street. 

Residents interested in subscribing to the yard waste service must contact the Solid Waste Division to request service. Once enrolled, a yard waste container will be delivered to the residence.

Residents who choose not to subscribe may continue to use the remote yard waste sites at no cost. Large yard waste containers will be available at Fitzgerald Stadium and 1117 Nickel St. beginning May 1.

At the end of the season, residents may cancel service and return their container, or keep it over the winter. If the residents choose to keep the container over the winter, the monthly billing will pause December–April and resume when service restarts in May. 

"At the end of the season, you have to give us a call to cancel. Otherwise, we will assume that you are going to start service again at the beginning of the following season, in May," Hannon said.

title: Rapid City pair charged for Sioux Falls shooting that injured officer, both have lengthy records
byline: SHALOM BAER GEE and MIKE BROWNLEE Journal staff
description: The Minnehaha County Sheriff's Office said two suspects from Rapid City are charged in connection to a shooting in Sioux Falls that left a police officer injured.
_full_text:
The Minnehaha County Sheriff's Office said two suspects from Rapid City are charged in connection to a shooting in Sioux Falls that left a police officer injured.

Darren Reuben Richards, 30, of Rapid City, was arrested in connection to the Monday, April 27, incident. Richards is charged with attempted first-degree murder of law enforcement, first-degree robbery, two counts of aggravated assault against a law enforcement officer, aggravated assault, possession of a firearm by a violent or drug-related convicted felon, possession of a firearm by a person with a prior drug conviction, second-degree eluding and failure to furnish information in an accident with vehicle damage.

Loretta Elizabeth Bettelyoun, 22, of Rapid City, was also arrested and is charged with first-degree and accessory to a crime.

Richards is being held on a $1 million cash-only bond, according to the Minnehaha County Sheriff's Office, while Bettelyoun is held on a $500,000 cash-only bond.

According to reporting from the Sioux Falls Argus Leader and information from authorities, Richards allegedly shot the officer at about 2 p.m. on Monday near the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds in Sioux Falls.

The officer was last reported to be in stable condition at the hospital with multiple injuries.

The shooting incident

Per reporting from the Argus Leader:

According to Sgt. Aaron Benson, officers responded to a business at about 2 p.m. south of 12th Street and Lyons Avenue, after reports that someone at that location had pulled out a gun on another person.

As officers arrived, Benson said they made contact with two suspects who were walking away from the scene. At some point during that contact, one of the suspects took out a gun and shot the officer.

Soon after, Minnehaha County Sheriff Mike Milstead and Sioux Falls Police Chief Jon Thum were on the scene. By 3 p.m., a SWAT team had also arrived, according to an Argus Leader reporter who was on scene.

Both suspects, one male adult and one female adult, then got into what Benson described as a gold vehicle and drove west. According to bystanders, the suspect was seen driving west on 12th Street in a Kia Optima.

 Law enforcement is on the scene of an officer-involved shooting on Monday, April 27, 2026, just south of the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds.
Law enforcement is on the scene of an officer-involved shooting on Monday, April 27, 2026, just south of the W.H. Lyon Fairgrounds in Sioux Falls. 

A witness, Tommy Heuer, said he was driving northbound on Interstate 29 when he saw the car with the two suspects inside drive through a fence near Northern Tool and Equipment, just south of the interstate. Heuer said they then drove onto the northbound on-ramp then crossed the median of the interstate until they could drive southbound on Interstate 29.

Benson said both suspects were apprehended in a neighborhood just south of 49th Street and Marion Road.

Officials call for reform

Minnehaha Sheriff's Capt. Josh Phillips said both suspects were on parole holds, the Argus Leader reported, and Sioux Falls Police Department and Minnehaha County Sheriff's officials emphasized what they characterized as a need for stronger parole laws.

Pennington County Sheriff Brian Mueller and Rapid City Police Chief Don Hedrick took the Sioux Falls incident as an opportunity to push for parole reform.

"This officer-involved shooting also highlights the value of addressing violent re-offending parolees in our communities," Hedrick and Mueller said in a joint release sent out Tuesday, April 28. "This has been a topic the sheriff, chief and several partners across the state have been active and vocal on over the past few years. Although we are making some progress, incidents like this highlight the importance of making immediate changes at the state level necessary to help us continue to keep our community and state safe."

The law enforcement leaders said they hope to release statistics later this week on the work of the Parolee Accountability Task Force, which the agencies launched on Wednesday, April 15.

"Right now, our thoughts and prayers are with the Sioux Falls Police Department and the officer seriously injured in (Monday's) shooting. We are thankful he is in stable condition," the local agencies said in the release. "This incident highlights the inherent danger of the work that we do. Our brave men and women know the risks they face every day. We are appreciative to live in a community and state that supports their law enforcement."

Suspects on parole, and have other pending cases

According to Department of Corrections records, a Pennington County judge sentenced Richards in December 2022 for possession of a controlled substance by an inmate and grand theft. The grand theft sentence was five years with one year suspended to run concurrently to the possession sentence of five years with two years suspended. He was on parole at the time of his arrest for the Sioux Falls incident. His parole office is listed as the Rapid City office.

He was also arrested in Pennington County in November 2025 for domestic abuse simple assault in connection to a July 5, 2025 incident. He was sentenced to 180 days in the jail, all suspended, and one year of probation. His probation started on Dec. 18, 2025, and he was on probation at the time of the Sioux Falls incident.

Richards also has other pending cases in Pennington County. He was arrested on March 25 for driving while under the influence, no driver's license, failure to maintain financial responsibility, improper use of signals and a seat belt violation. He's due in court on the case on May 13.

Bettelyoun was sentenced to five years in prison on drug charges on June 26, 2023, in connection to an arrest in Pennington County. She was sentenced the same day to two years on identity theft charges. The court suspended the sentences on both charges and placed her on probation for four years.

About four months later, Bettelyoun admitted to probation violations in both cases, and the judge sentenced her to five years in prison with three suspended for the violation in the drug case and to two years, none suspended, for the violation in the identity theft case. Less than a year later, Bettelyoun was let out on parole. She was under the supervision of the DOC at the time of the Sioux Falls shooting.

Bettelyoun also has a pending Pennington County case on charges of domestic abuse-simple assault and false imprisonment. She was arrested on March 14 in connection to a Sept. 5, 2025, incident. A status hearing in the case is scheduled for June 1.

title: Affordable housing investments opening new doors in Rapid City
byline: BART PFANKUCH South Dakota News Watch
description: Brianna Brave Heart sat on a curb outside the Sagebrush Flats apartment complex on a recent afternoon in April, waiting for her 8-year-old son to get home from school.
_full_text:
Brianna Brave Heart sat on a curb outside the Sagebrush Flats apartment complex on a recent afternoon in April, waiting for her 8-year-old son to get home from school.

As she sat amid rays of warm sunshine, Brave Heart reflected on how her life — and her potential future — feels a little brighter since she and her son moved into a new two-bedroom apartment in the complex that opened in 2025 along Omaha Street on Rapid City's east side.

The 179-unit complex was built in part with funding from the Strategic Housing Initiative, a partnership of philanthropic groups that has shown great success in expanding access to affordable housing for low-income individuals and families in the Black Hills region.

The initiative that began roughly a decade ago has resulted in development or retention of 645 apartments totaling more that 1,230 bedrooms for people who have difficultly obtaining quality housing in Rapid City, where there is a dire shortage of affordable housing and average rental rates are the highest in the state.

Brave Heart, 42, is a single mother and Army veteran who worked as an X-ray technician prior to suffering disabling injuries in a car accident a few years ago.

 2026-4-15 Brianna Brave Heart RC tenant.jpeg
Brianna Brave Heart sits outside the Sagebrush Flats apartment complex in Rapid City on April 15, 2026. 

She was among the first residents to move into Sagebrush Flats in April 2025, and while weekend nights can sometimes be noisy at the two-building complex, she is overall pleased with the affordable rent and the high quality of living space.

“This place is way better than where I lived before, and the management is a lot nicer and more caring,” Brave Heart told News Watch.

Brave Heart said the rent for a two-bedroom Sagebrush apartment would be $1,263 a month. But she pays only $763 due to support from the Veterans Affairs Supportive Housing program run by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development.

Other tenants who qualify for subsidized rents pay on a sliding scale based on their incomes, pushing Sagebrush rents below market rate and to an affordable level for a clientele with a wide range of incomes.

Brave Heart said she appreciates the opportunity to live in a new building and have a sense of stability as she looks for work while raising her son.

“The rents are good, but if I didn’t have the (VASH) program to help me, I wouldn’t be able to live here,” she said. “You have to have two people working in the household to afford rent in Rapid City.”

Response: A program built on partnerships

The Strategic Housing Initiative is a program managed by the Black Hills Area Community Foundation and fueled by the Strategic Housing Trust Fund. The fund launched in 2020 as a revolving loan fund to provide financial assistance to developers to encourage them to build multi-family housing that can be offered at affordable rental rates.

The fund so far has brought in about $15 million in investments, including $5 million from the John T. Vucurevich Foundation, $3 million from the Bush Foundation, $5 million from the sales tax-driven Vision Funds within Rapid City government and private donations.

The fund finances up to 10% of the cost of a housing project and along with federal affordable housing tax credits, enables developers to build projects cheaper and therefore require lower rents from tenants to still turn a profit.

 2026-4-15 Radiant apartments RC.jpeg
The Radiant apartment building in Rapid City, S.D., shown on April 15, 2026, is an affordable rental complex developed under the Strategic Housing Initiative. 

"Developers are not going to build projects if they can't make money, so we come in at a happy medium and give them cheap financing on a small portion of their project if they agree to stay affordable for up to 30 years," said Chris Huber, CEO of the Black Hills Area Community Foundation. "We win as a community by getting those units online, residents can rent a place that is affordable within their incomes, and developers win because they get a project completed."

The goal is to provide quality housing for people who make 60% or less of the area median income, which in the Black Hills includes individuals and families that make roughly from $20,000 to $55,000 a year, Huber said.

With average market rate rents for a one-bedroom apartment in Rapid City as high as $1,250, a family would need to bring in about $62,000 a year in order to avoid being considered "rent burdened," Huber said. Enabling families to afford rent in a stable housing environment is a primary building block to self-sufficiency and a brighter future, he said.

"When they have an affordable unit, they are able to pay their medical bills, they're able to put food on the table, they're able to think about what the next phase of their life could be," he said.

Evidence: Family takes a step up in housing

Chris Meadows, 40, found out about The Radiant affordable housing apartments developed through the housing initiative after making a DoorDash delivery to the complex. After discussions with the property manager, she and her two teen boys moved there in December.

Meadows, who is a disabled single mom, pays about $1,150 per month for a two-bedroom apartment that she said is newer, nicer and much larger than the more expensive apartment she used to rent.

“It’s amazing because the space is massive,” she said during an interview in the complex parking lot. “Every room is larger than the place I had before.”

One drawback is that The Radiant has shared laundry facilities, though Meadows said that minor inconvenience is overshadowed by the complex elevator, which she needs due to chronic pain in both knees.

 2026-4-15 Sagebrush Flats exterior.jpeg
The 179-unit Sagebrush Flats complex in Rapid City was built in part with funding from the Strategic Housing Initiative. 

“It’s so helpful for people who are disabled and have a hard time finding housing on the ground floor,” she said.

She said the facility also offers useful information for residents on how to navigate finances, employment opportunities and relationships.

"We've had some tough times, so this feels like a new start for us," she said.

Insights: Housing part of a holistic approach

The Vucurevich Foundation was an early entrant into the efforts to generate more affordable housing options in the Black Hills.

In addition to sharing funds with other philanthropic groups, the foundation found a great partner in CommonBond Communities, a Minnesota nonprofit that has developed low-cost housing for more than 50 years. It built and owns The Radiant complex and recently purchased the Maplewood apartments in Rapid City.

The sharing of knowledge, resources and money is a critical component of the housing program in the Black Hills or any other community that wants to expand access to affordable housing, said Jacqui Dietrich, CEO of The Vucurevich Foundation.

“The model of collective funding, and how you bring multiple players together is absolutely necessary if you want affordable housing,” she said.

Now and in the future, Vucurevich is focused on generating affordable housing as a foundation of a larger, more holistic approach to helping people rise out of poverty and thrive in their communities. The foundation continues to look for ways to help people obtain transportation, education and job skills, access to medical care and other basic needs that can lead to a more prosperous life, she said.

“It's all about how we provide that upward mobility through the interconnectivity of all the systems that are helping to provide people with stability,” Dietrich said. “And that really starts with affordable housing.”

Limitations: High costs create challenges

Obtaining funding to help make apartment developments affordable is a challenge in many communities. The financial challenges are heightened by the rapidly rising cost of inputs needed to make a project viable, including costs for land, materials and labor, Huber said.

For many South Dakotans, it is becoming increasingly difficult to make enough money to pay for rent, even in an affordable housing complex, because wage increases have not kept up with the rising cost of living, he said.

 2026-4-15 Sagebrush flats leasing sign.jpeg
The Sagebrush Flats apartments in Rapid City, S.D., shown on April 15, 2026, are an affordable rental complex developed under the Strategic Housing Initiative. 

But interest in the cooperative funding model that has driven the success of the Strategic Housing Initiative remains high, including among officials of Abilene, Texas, who visited with Huber in mid-April to learn more about the program.

The foundation also received a $30 million appropriation from Congress in 2024 to expand the affordable housing initiative into other cities in the Black Hills, starting with a project proposed for Spearfish, Huber said.

During a recent conversation with the property manager at The Radiant, the housing initiative property in Rapid City that includes 30 affordable rental units, the manager told Huber that five tenants had moved out and bought their own homes.

"It's easy to zoom out and say, 'Wow, this is such a huge problem,' and maybe get bogged down in the weeds," Huber said. "But when you zoom in and think about those individual families, it's worth the effort to continue trying to change their lives."

title: "I shot and killed my best friend": Jury listens to accused gunman's interrogation with police
description: A Pennington County jury watched a video of Luke Birch initially speaking about McGarland "Mack" Walker VI with the warmth of a brother, describing a caring, nonconfrontational best friend who had supported him in his darkest times.
_full_text:
A Pennington County jury watched a video of Luke Birch initially speaking about McGarland "Mack" Walker VI with the warmth of a brother, describing a caring, nonconfrontational best friend who had supported him in his darkest times. 

But as the hours of questioning bled into the night, Birch's depiction of his best friend's disposition on March 8, 2024, began to shift from a gentle soul to an "aggressive," "angry" and "demonic" person he didn't recognize. 

 Luke Birch
Birch 

Birch, 23, of Rapid City, is charged with first-degree murder and, in the alternative, second-degree murder for Walker's death. Walker was shot and killed inside Candlewood Place, an apartment building in west Rapid City. Birch can only be convicted of one or the other, not both.

"I shot and killed my best friend," Birch's voice echoed into the courtroom as the video of his interrogation with police played for the jury over Wednesday, April 29, and Thursday, April 30. 

Trial testimony from both lead investigators on the case also took place over the two days. 

First interview: The run

Pennington County Sheriff's Office Investigator Rio Shearer testified on the stand Wednesday afternoon. She said she was the first person to interview Birch on March 8, 2024, after the shooting. 

Shearer said Birch was taken to the police station initially as a potential witness. She said Birch wasn't considered a suspect until co-lead Rapid City Police Department Detective Dan Trainer texted her after her hourlong interview with Birch – "That's your guy."

In the video, Birch said him and Walker instantly clicked through a mutual passion for music and built a long-lasting friendship over the years. He said they would hang out on a nearly daily basis. 

"You don't have to be blood to be family and he was the truest definition of that," Birch told Shearer, highlighting how Walker supported him while he had family issues. 

When asked the last time he saw Walker, Birch told police he saw him on March 7, 2024, where they watched movies from 2 p.m. to 7 p.m. at Birch's girlfriend's apartment at Candlewood Place. 

Shearer asked if Walker had any enemies, Birch said he couldn't think of anyone wanting to harm Walker, highlighting his character. 

"Mack was a good dude," Birch said. "... He's had a rough life, but he's a good dude." 

Birch did mention a handful of names, however prefaced he didn't think any of those people would legitimately harm Walker. He told police Walker smoked weed and suggested it could've been someone who Walker buys from. 

Birch told police he last heard from Walker the day prior and had spent his Friday, March 8, 2024, straightening out his girlfriend's apartment, watching shows and preparing for his scheduled run around Wilderness Park. Birch said he goes on that run daily from 4 p.m. to 5 p.m.

Birch's ex-girlfriend took the stand Wednesday and testified she had never seen Birch go on a run for as long as she's known him. 

Birch said he didn't hear any gunshots on his run and didn't see any suspicious people at the park. He said he didn't know Walker was the person shot until police told him, and he insisted on seeing his best friend. 

The ex-girlfriend testified Birch had called her before he was taken to the police station, but he didn't specify what had happened, saying only that the police would be searching her apartment. 

Second interview: Denial 

As Shearer interviewed Birch, Trainer was further securing the crime scene. He testified on Wednesday, he and another officer recovered Walker's phone which revealed several text messages between Walker and Birch minutes before the shooting. 

Trainer said the majority of the messages were from Walker and appeared to be, "threatening in nature." 

One of Birch's attorney's, Ole Oleson, criticized how law enforcement didn't look into Walker's agitated demeanor by interviewing his coworkers. Oleson highlighted how it was unusual for Walker to not be home immediately after work and was instead at Birch's residence "behaving in a threatening manner."

When Oleson asked Trainer if sometimes innocent people lie when they are terrified, he said "possibly." However, when Deputy State's Attorney Adam Shiffermiller asked if it was normal for guilty people to lie, Trainer said, "absolutely." 

Trainer said the messages debunked Birch's claim of last seeing Walker the day prior and immediately labeled him as a suspect. 

In the second interview with Shearer and Trainer, Birch repeated how he last saw Walker yesterday and hadn't heard from him since. He retold how he went on a run around 4 p.m. and returned around 5 p.m. to find police cars surrounding the building. 

In the video, Trainer asks Birch to be honest, saying he knows Birch is lying. 

"I don't understand. My best friend was shot," Birch said.

Trainer began to read the text messages between Birch and Walker. Birch told police he doesn't know how those messages got there because he didn't hear from Walker all day. 

Birch continued to deny seeing Walker, insisting he was out on a run. Trainer continued to criticized Birch's story, asking why he would go running in slip-on shoes. 

In the video, Birch proceeded to get more emotional, denying ever meeting Walker on March 8, 2024, let alone shooting him. 

"I don't know what you guys want to pin on me," Birch said. "... I don't shoot or kill nobody, I don't like guns."

After another hour talking to law enforcement, Birch requested to have a lawyer. 

Third interview: Friend or foe?

Over two hours later, Birch requested to speak with Trainer again, this time with "the truth." 

Birch began the interview apologizing for lying and wanted to make it clear he wasn't lying about Walker being his best friend. 

Birch told police he and Walker were attempting to make plans earlier that day, but due to Walker's busy schedule, Birch suggested they hang out another day instead. He said that was when Walker's demeanor completely changed. 

After Walker insulted Birch's intelligence, Birch hung up, ensuing multiple text messages from Walker, swearing on his children's lives he will harm Birch. 

Birch said Walker pulled onto the curb of the apartment around 4 p.m., continuing to pressure Birch through text messages to meet him outside. He said when he answered the front entrance, he was attempting to deescalate the situation and calm Walker down. 

"I didn't do it because I was mad," Birch said. "He just kept getting aggressive. He kept going and going."

Birch said Walker pushed and shoved Birch further inside the building as he yelled at him. Birch said he placed his hand on top of his holster to warn Walker and to calm the situation. 

He said Walker said, "I'll show you how do that (explicative) for real," before he allegedly reached for his own gun. Birch, however, was able to pull out his gun first and fire. 

Birch said he initially fired two shots and watched Walker stumble back for a moment. He said Walker had an "evil look to him, he looked demonic." Walker then allegedly stepped forward to Birch, and Birch fired several more rounds until Walker eventually collapsed to the ground. 

Birch said he picked up two shell casings near his feet and ran. He told police he hid the gun inside a crack of a rock formation at Wilderness Park and threw his black hoodie and jeans somewhere in the woods. Birch denied wearing a face covering. However, police found a black face covering with the rest of the clothes. 

Trainer asked why Birch went to the door to meet a friend with a gun. Birch said he already had the gun on him all day. 

Trainer asked if he had to do it all over again, what would he do differently? Birch said he wouldn't have messaged Walker to begin with. And if Walker still showed up?

"I wish I could've hugged him and get him to calm down," Birch cried in the interview. "... I'm the reason that little girl and little boy doesn't got a dad no more." 

Between Wednesday and Thursday, the jury also heard testimony from forensic experts who collected the weapon, Walker and Birch's clothing, and took pictures of the crime scene.

They also heard from Forensic Pathologist Donald Habbe who detailed Walker's injuries — three gunshot wounds in the chest, one in the forearm, one to the upper lip and one at the top of his head. 

Birch's trial is set to continue on Friday, May 1 at 9 a.m.

title: NDN Collective, partners on site protesting exploratory drilling in the Black Hills
description: In the early morning of Thursday, April 30, protesters gathered at two Rochford Mineral Exploration Drilling Project sites in the Black Hills, an area held sacred for Indigenous tribes. The sites are operated by Pete Lien & Sons under permitting from the U.S. Forest Service.
_full_text:
In the early morning of Thursday, April 30, protesters gathered at two Rochford Mineral Exploration Drilling Project sites in the Black Hills, an area held sacred for Indigenous tribes. The sites are operated by Pete Lien & Sons under permitting from the U.S. Forest Service. 

"Pe’ Sla is one of the sacred sites, sacred places in the Black Hills to the Lakota. It's not the only one but it is one of the most sacred. Pete Lien & Sons mining company knew this. The U.S. Forest Service knew this, in violation to what is right and yet they applied to drill here and yet the U.S. Forest Service granted that permit, in violation to U.S. Law," NDN Collective President Wizipan Garriott said in a livestream.

 Protest
On April 30, NDN Collective announced action against the Rochford Mineral Exploratory Drilling Project based in the Black Hills. 

On site are members of NDN Collective, the Oglala Lakota Nation Youth Council and other community members.

Valeriah Big Eagle, director of He Sapa initiatives at NDN Collective, said they were able to block off the two active drilling sites and asked supporters to call the Black Hills Mystic Ranger District office and Pete Lien & Sons to halt the drilling. She said no one was working the sites when the group arrived.

In the livestream of the protest, Pennington County Sheriff's deputies asked the group their reason for being there and how long they would stay. One group member said they would stay until the court decides to stop the drilling. 

The Pennington County Sheriff's Office in a statement at 6:40 p.m. on Thursday confirmed, "an activist group has shown up at a drill site in the Black Hills west of Rochford in Pennington County attempting to stop legally permitted drilling activities through illegal means."

The statement continued: "We are working with local, state and federal law enforcement to continue the criminal investigation and potential restitution related to this illegal activity. Law enforcement from the sheriff's office and U.S. Forest Service have been in contact with the protestors."

 PE SLA
The Rochford Exploratory Drilling Project is a drilling project for graphite material in the Black Hills by Pete Lien & Sons. 

NDN Collective, Black Hills Clean Water Alliance and Earthworks filed a lawsuit in federal court against the U.S. Forest Service on April 1 because of the exploratory mining permit. According to a press release by NDN Collective, the drilling in the area has started despite public opposition, no environmental review, and the active lawsuit.

A day before the protest, on Wednesday, April 29, the plaintiffs against the project filed motions for a temporary restraining order and preliminary injunction in the lawsuit, asking a judge to stop the drilling. The defendants have not yet filed a response, according to federal court records.

The organizations said the move comes after the Forest Service granted a permit for exploratory drilling in Pe’ Sla, a sacred and protected Indigenous site.

 Pe Sla
A protester at a drilling machine in the Black Hills. 

The exploratory drilling by Pete Lien & Sons was permitted by the U.S. Forest Service on Feb. 27, for a large mineral exploration drilling project on USFS-managed public lands near Rochford in west Pennington County. The permit allows the company to use its federal mining claims to look for graphite in the area. 

A decision memo from the Forest Service allows for 18 boreholes at a maximum depth of 1,000 feet.

The memo notes access to the drilling land would be made by USFS roadways and roughly 5,060 linear feet of new, temporary overland access trails. "Water used for drilling will be sourced from approved municipal supplies and recirculated on-site using infiltration galleries or tanks. No water will be withdrawn from streams or wetlands," the Forest Service said in the memo.

 Pe Sla
Protesters disrupt machinery used in the drilling to prevent workers from continuing to drill.  

But NDN and partners argue the move is a violation of the NEPA (National Environmental Policy Act).

"Our call to action is to halt all drilling operations and to follow the law, and with that law, when it comes to this type of work and exploratory drilling project that is taking place, the categorical exclusion that they were approved for is not applicable because they did not follow the NEPA (National Environmental Policy Act) process, and they did not follow the national historical preservation process with tribes. We're asking for a full environmental review to be conducted," Big Eagle said.

In a court filing, the plaintiffs said the federal government violated the National Environmental Protection Act by incorrectly using a "categorical exclusion" to avoid factoring in concerns from tribal communities, clean water organizations and others.

According to the Department of Energy's NEPA website, "a categorical exclusion (CE) is a class of actions that a federal agency has determined, after review by (the Council on Environmental Quality), do not individually or cumulatively have a significant effect on the human environment and for which, therefore, neither an environmental assessment nor an environmental impact statement is normally required. The use of categorical exclusions can reduce paperwork and save time and resources."

 Pe Sla
NDN Collective protesting a exploratory drilling project located near Pe’ Sla in the Black Hills. 

Pe' Sla (Bald Spot) is an area in the Black Hills marked by a rocky peak overlooking a large meadow, according to the National Park Service. It sits in the Black Hills west of Rapid City.

According to the NDN Collective release, 10 drill pads are actively operating and some are within the 2-mile buffer zone protected by a memorandum of understanding between the U.S. Forest Service and Lakota Tribes.

A 2014 Memorandum of Understanding between the U.S. Forest Service and representative tribal nations of the Oceti Sakowin (meaning seven council fires made up of the Lakota, Nakota and Dakota tribes) acknowledged and agreed that Pe’ Sla was of great cultural and religious importance to the Oceti Sakowin, the lawsuit claims.

The hearing for the lawsuit is slated for Monday, May 4. Pete Lien & Sons did not immediately respond to an inquiry for comment.

title: Updated: Douglas High School bomb threat determined to be a hoax
description: A Douglas High School bomb threat on Thursday, April 30, was determined to be a hoax after local authorities investigated the situation, the Douglas School District said on Thursday afternoon.
_full_text:
A Douglas High School bomb threat on Thursday, April 30, was determined to be a hoax after local authorities investigated the situation, the Douglas School District said on Thursday afternoon. 

"Out of an abundance of caution, students and staff followed established safety protocols while law enforcement conducted a complete assessment of the building," the district said in a social media post. "At all times, the safety and well-being of students and staff remained the district’s top priority. Douglas School District takes any threat to students and faculty seriously and will always respond with appropriate safety measures."

The district extended its thanks an appreciation to the Box Elder Police Department, Ellsworth Air Force Base 28th Security Forces Squadron and other personnel, Pennington County Sheriff’s Office, South Dakota Highway Patrol, and the Rapid City Fire Department for their prompt response and assistance in ensuring the building was safe.

Students were released early from Douglas High School after the morning bomb threat. The district posted at 10:39 a.m. that students were evacuating the building. 

Per that post, "At this time, we believe the threat may be a hoax, consistent with similar incidents reported across the country. However, out of an abundance of caution, all students and staff at the high school are being safely evacuated while authorities thoroughly check the building."

While the investigation was underway, the district's other schools were under secure status. High school students will be able to retrieve their belonging starting at 7:45 a.m. on Friday, May 1.

title: Dahl Art Center to host 6th annual Black Hill Indian Artist Market on Saturday
description: The sixth annual Black Hills Indian Artist Market will feature a fashion show, ribbon skirt contest, live music, vendor booths and more.
_full_text:
The sixth annual Black Hills Indian Artist Market will feature a fashion show, ribbon skirt contest, live music, vendor booths and more.

 Art Market
Travis Rattler answers a customer's questions at the Black Hills Indian Artist Market Holiday Show in December 2024. 

On Saturday, May 2, the Dahl Arts Center will open its doors at 10 a.m. for guests to enjoy a day of Indigenous art. 

Lorri Ann Two Bulls (Lakota), a committee member for the market, told the Journal it is an event about celebrating and connecting with the artists.

 Black Hills Art Market
The sixth annual Black Hills Indian Artist Market inside the Dahl Arts Center in Rapid City includes a fashion show, ribbon skirt contest, elder storytelling, live music, vendor booths and more. 

"I like the way the community comes together. I was born and raised here in Rapid City the city, I went to school here and so the community really responds and comes down," Two Bulls said.

The event is free admission and will run until 4 p.m.

Two Bulls said at 1 p.m. they will start the live music, fashion show and other performances.

She said the schedule and times for these events is not set in stone.

"In our group, we're all in charge of one area. It's like the Lakota way of working together. By that, I mean that there's no chairman or boss or anything," Two Bulls said.

 Prairie Dust
Prairie Dust plays on the Dahl Arts Center stage during the annual Spring Black Hills Indian Artist Market and Fashion Show on Saturday, April 5, 2025. 

This year they will be honoring the former announcer for the BHIAM, Christopher Eagle Hawk, who passed away in December, as well as local musician John Miller, who passed in April.

The ribbon skirt contest will have its own booth for participants to sign register their skirts. First place will receive $200; second place will receive $150 and third place will receive $100.

Booths selling art will include Jennifer White, Jim Yellowhawk and Martin Red Bear. They will have booths for beadwork, paintings, leather, textile, and silver work.

Performances will include Sequia Crosswhite, The Three Chili Peppers, and the Mighty Arrows.

"We came up with this (event) and it's been helpful ever since. People want to keep it going," Two Bulls said on the market.

She invites the community to experience Indigenous culture, creativity and make connections.
